

The Gospel According to St. Matthew

28 Chapters  1071 Verses  23,684 Words

The first book in the New Testament introduces the reader to the coming of Christ (the Messiah) to Israel as their King. There can be no doubt as to the author's intention in presenting Christ in this fashion, nor is there any doubt, to the open-minded reader, that this gospel occupies a unique place in the canon of Scripture. It has a [Jewish flavor]{.class_4257} of such intensity that little in it can be applied [doctrinally]{.class_4257} to the Gentiles or to the Gentile believer. Matthew's Jewish "slant" is apparent on every page of the book, and the beginning of the genealogy with Abraham, in the very first chapter, is a dead "give-away" as to the purpose of the author. He is giving "the gospel" from the standpoint of an Old Testament Hebrew who is anxiously awaiting the appearance of the "anointed one," the Messiah.

Literature on the book is voluminous and dense. The commentary by John Peter Lange contains a complete bibliography for anyone interested in tracing the chronological data or the critical theories regarding authorship. As we have stated elsewhere, this is a "Bible Believer's Commentary," not a series of articles written to destroy the word of God by innuendo, implication, supposition, or sophistry. All the data on the "Aramaic Gospel" according to Matthew or the "Q" document theory is available to any simple-minded person who wants to waste the time to investigate it. Our purpose, here, as always, is to believe the revelation that God has given us and find out what that revelation says. The author of Matthew's Gospel is Matthew. We know this conclusively, since 1,500 years of investigation by the most brilliant and critical minds of unregenerate scholarship and dead orthodoxy have failed to disprove it or have failed to produce another author. [The Bible is innocent until proved guilty.]{.class_4257} Since the laws of the credibility of a witness in a modern court are considered "scientific" in the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw78.xhtml_page_4}twentieth century, we may conclude positively that the author of Matthew is Matthew until someone has proved otherwise, [conclusively.]{.class_4257}

If the reader would like to waste several days in worthless investigations, he can drill through the library shelves of the works of Bauer, Strauss, Graf, Wellhausen, Dillman, Scholtmann, Neander, Wetstein, DeWette, Alford, Hort, Schleiermacher, Ewald, et al., and come to the conclusion that he would have learned more reading the gospel according to Matthew than he would have learned reading these men in an effort to get rid of Matthew's autograph. The "International Standard Bible Encyclopedia" has some more information regarding critical theories, but our purpose is not to classify ourselves with "great minds" who have the intelligence to "criticize" the Bible. Our purpose is to let [God speak to us]{.class_4257} and criticize us where He sees fit.

The Gospel according to Matthew was written sometime around A.D. 64--66, by ["Matthew the publican"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 9:9, 10:3). It is inspired by God through Matthew (2 Tim. 3:16) and, as such, [may or may not]{.class_4257} use other sources, according to the prerogative and good pleasure of the "Divine Author." There are no contradictions in the book. The Received Text has preserved for us, [without proven error,]{.class_4257} the words of the Holy Spirit through Matthew. (For further discussions on the quality and merits of the Received Text, spend a little time with Dean Burgon, Kirsopp Lake, and Von Soden, and ignore Westcott and Hort for a spell. It is bad for people to become dogmatic and narrow-minded about the Received Text, simply because two men smuggled a corrupt text into the Revision Committee of 1881.)

Matthew, then, is placed first in the New Testament as the logical link between Malachi and the events that are about to take place around the first appearance of our Incarnate Lord. We are coming from thirty-nine Jewish books which concern the "calling-out" of a chosen people from Abraham, to twenty-seven books which deal primarily with Gentiles called out from among the nations, "to be a people for God's name" (Acts 15:14). It is certainly fitting and proper, therefore, that a "gospel" should be inserted which would give us a bridge, or transition, from a Jewish economy to the Church composed of Jew and Gentile. As a [transition]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw78.xhtml_page_5} book, then, Matthew is a dangerous place for the believer to rest [doctrinally.]{.class_4257}

It will be observed that the three great stumbling blocks that God has placed in the Bible all concern [the Jew.]{.class_4257} Matthew is the first of these blocks, Acts is the second, and Hebrews is the third. Any man who has had extensive, practical experience in doctrinal controversy and denominational convictions recognizes immediately the import of this statement. [Every division in the Body of Christ is based on verses found in one of these three books.]{.class_4257} We shall note the passages in Matthew that deal with these divergences, as we study the book.

In conclusion, it may be said that Matthew is the author of this book, writing under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit. The ancient and archaic "two-document theory," still taught in denominational seminaries (and in pay stations of the National Council of Churches), was long ago proved inconclusive, if not downright childish. The Bible believer may reject it at once and overlook the arguments about Matthew using Mark or some mythical "Q" document. This theory, propounded by C. H. Weiss (1838) and championed by Streeter (1924), is still extolled by "pseudo-scholarship" and "unregenerate theology," which does not have access to (or else ignores) the treatise by B. C. Butler (1951) on "The Originality of St. Matthew." The Bible believer may rest assured that though Union Theological Seminary and Yale Divinity School "assail" him, he can rest in the sweet thought that [thorough investigation]{.class_4257} of the problem proves Matthew wrote Matthew, as God directed him to write it.

With such an orientation, and with a humble mind "weaned on the sincere milk of the word" (Psa. 131:2; 1 Pet. 2:1--3), let us open the covers (never mind all the talk about "vellum scrolls" until you [find out what God says]{.class_4257}), and be [taught of God]{.class_4257} through his human instrument, St. Matthew.

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml}

# Chapter 1 {.heading_84-0}

1:1 The book of the generation of Jesus Christ, the son of David, the son of Abraham.

The beginning of Matthew's gospel introduces the Messianic line from Abraham to Joseph. This is the TENTH TIME that we find the expression: "the gen­erations" in the Bible. The last time we read of ["the book of the generations" ]{.class_4258}in the Bible was in Genesis 5:1. This is significant, for [Adam is ]{.class_4257}said to be a type of Christ in Romans 5:16--21 and 1 Corinthians 15:45--47. Here, then, are two lines of two men representing two types of people. In Adam's line, we read ["and he died," "and he died," "and he died," ]{.class_4258}right through the chapter (Gen. 5); but in Matthew 1 we find that [no record is given of anyone dying! ]{.class_4257}It may be objected that this is accidental, but this can hardly be---too many "coincidences" overdraw a scholar's bank account. Adam represents Death, for ["in Adam all die"]{.class_4258} (1 Cor. 15:22), and Christ represents Life, for He said, ["I am the way, the truth, and the life." ]{.class_4258}If it be objected that Matthew could not have foreseen this undesigned coincidence in making his genealogy fit the comparison between Adam and Christ, it may be answered that [God foresees what neither you nor Matthew could foresee, ]{.class_4257}and we are dealing here with divine inspiration.

Verse 1 gives an insight into the correct Bible usage of the word ["son,"]{.class_4258} and this insight will help the student out when confronted with numerous "apparent contradic­tions," drummed up by the "scholars." A ["son"]{.class_4258} can be a great-great-great-great‑great-grandson, or a "son‑in‑law" (Luke 3:23), or even an [adopted son ]{.class_4262}(see Matt 1:1--18). Jesus was not even [David's son by descent, literally; ]{.class_4262}yet here he is called, ["the son of David,"]{.class_4258} as also in Matthew 22:45. The Bible believer should be careful not to be drawn into the rigid, logic‑tight compartments of critical scholarship which force or superimpose their "definitions" on Bible words. The Bible is its own authority and dictionary, and will define for you what it means when it says ["son."]{.class_4258}

1:2Abraham begat Isaac; and Isaac begat Jacob; and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_7}Jacob begat Judas and his brethren;

The verse is self‑explanatory, with only one or two prominent points. It is to be observed that the word for [Judah ]{.class_4257}used here is transliterated ["JUDAS."]{.class_4258} This is instructive, for the reader will note in the passages dealing with the Antichrist (Rev. 13:18; John 17:12; 2 Thess. 2:2--3) that the Antichrist has even imitated the [tribe from which the "Lion" ]{.class_4257}came (Rev. 5:5). The ["his brethren" ]{.class_4258}following "Judah" immediately emphasizes Matthew's position---he is going to present the King that came from the Kingly tribe (Gen. 49:10), and the chance, ["his brethren,"]{.class_4258} shuttles the eleven tribes aside to call the reader's attention to the ["Lion of the tribe of Juda."]{.class_4258}

1:3And Judas begat Phares and Zara of Thamar; and Phares begat Esrom; and Esrom begat Aram;

The reader is suddenly surprised by the appearance of a Gentile woman in the Hebrew genealogy! This woman springs up in Genesis 38:6, and although it is not stated that she is a [Canaanite, ]{.class_4257}the implication is too strong to resist. As a matter of fact, it is impossible to resist when we continue the genealogy and find an [Amorite ]{.class_4257}([Rahab]{.class_4257}),[ a Moabite ]{.class_4257}([Ruth]{.class_4257}),[ a Hittite ]{.class_4257}([Bath‑sheba]{.class_4257}),[ and an Ammonite ]{.class_4257}([Rehoboam's mother]{.class_4257})[ ]{.class_4257}in the list. The slight difference in spelling of the proper names in the New Testament (notice Matt. 1:5--7) should not upset the believer. Pedro, Pierre, Pietro, Peter, and Petros are all the same name, but transliterating from Spanish, French, Italian, and Greek makes the words appear different. "Phares," here, is "Pharez" of Genesis 38, and "Esrom" is the descendant of Ruth 4:18 (Hezron).

1:4 And Aram begat Aminadab; and Aminadab begat Naasson; and Naasson begat Salmon;

In Ruth 4, we find Ram for ["Aram"]{.class_4258} and Nahshon for ["Naasson,"]{.class_4258} the same problem being involved. (The names are the same but coming from different languages the spellings make them appear different.) We are still following Abraham's line to David. Since David was the King whom God chose (Saul was chosen by permissive will on request of the people), it is fitting that the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_8}genealogy should run from Abraham to David, and thence to Christ. ["Amminadab"]{.class_4258} occupies a peculiar position, in that he is called the [prince ]{.class_4257}of the children of Judah, and his "chariots" are mentioned in the Song of Solomon (6:12) in connection with a [rapture. ]{.class_4257}Amminadab's son (Num. 1:7) was a leading warrior in the tribe.

1:5 And Salmon begat Booz of Rachab; and Booz begat Obed of Ruth; and Obed begat Jesse;

"Boaz" is here noted as ["Booz,"]{.class_4258} and we find ["Rachab"]{.class_4258} for the ["Rahab"]{.class_4258} of Joshua 2:1. It is assumed by nearly all commentators that the "Rahab" of Joshua is the woman referred to in this chapter; however, there is no conclusive evidence. That a professional harlot should be in Christ's line is of some wonder, and yet not so shocking when we consider the activity of Thamar, who presented herself as a harlot, Bath‑sheba, who stepped out on her husband, and Solomon, who had 1,000 wives! In Clarke's commentary, we find a bold and useless effort to convince the reader that the word ["harlot"]{.class_4258} in Joshua 2:1 is really "innkeeper" (Clarke, of course, designing to protect our Lord's genealogy). However, this is a bad blunder showing ignorance of the Scriptures, for James, in citing the incident of Joshua 2:1--8, uses the word [porna ]{.class_4257}(James 2:25) which is a far shot from "innkeeper." The word [never means an innkeeper; ]{.class_4257}it means a professional prosti­tute---sacred or secular.

1:6 And Jesse begat David the king; and David the king begat Solomon of her that had been the wife of Urias;

The verse affords a very interesting study in the superiority of the [Authorized Version ]{.class_4257}to the [Westcott and Hort]{.class_4257} Greek text. In their effort to "strain out a gnat and swallow a camel," the revisers of the [ASV, RSV, RV, ]{.class_4257}and subsequent mutilations have overlooked the plain English that [gave a morally correct reading, ethically, ]{.class_4257}and have inserted a conjecture which violates a teaching found in both Testaments, even in the Greek and Hebrew! Note how accurately the [King James ]{.class_4257}gives ["of her that HAD BEEN the wife of Urias."]{.class_4258} Uriah [was dead when Solomon was born ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 7:1--3). At this time Bath‑sheba [was truly David's wife. ]{.class_4257}But when the [RSV ]{.class_4257}reads: ["by the wife of Uriah" ]{.class_4257}(as the Westcott and Hort Greek Text), it invents an absurdity: [Uriah had no wife when Solomon was begotten---]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_9}he was dead. Furthermore, the Holy Spirit is careful to point out that Bath‑sheba was not really considered David's "wife" till the first [child ]{.class_4257}was dead (2 Sam. 12:15). Bath‑sheba [conceived the first child while Uriah was living. ]{.class_4257}How marvelously the [King James ]{.class_4257}"translation" points this out in 2 Samuel 12:24, by calling Bath‑sheba [David's wife after the first child is dead! ]{.class_4257}The unsearchable riches of the Authorized Version are always superior to the "original," when manhandled by a gnat‑strainer. An interpolation is needed in the Greek text, which stands as [ek tas tou ouriou. ]{.class_4257}However, lack of fidelity to the remainder of the Bible (which contains the full body of the truth) renders the [RSV ]{.class_4257}and [RV ]{.class_4257}and [ASV ]{.class_4257}translators incapable of an intelligent and reasonable interpolation. The [Authorized Version]{.class_4257} is far superior.

1:7 And Solomon begat Roboam; and Roboam begat Abia; and Abia begat Asa;

Continuing the Messianic line from Abraham to Christ, Matthew continues down through the line of Jewish kings that followed David's reign. A careful study of 1 and 2 Chronicles, at this point, will help the reader to understand what is taking place. The Anointed Messiah will be a King over Israel, and as such, it is essential that He have a genealogical record of His descent from the Kings. \[As someone has aptly said: "In Matthew 1, we have the Genealogy of the King; in Matthew 2, we have the Birth of the King; in Matthew 3, the Herald of the King; in Matthew 4, the Preparation of the King; in Matthew 5--7, the Constitution of the Kingdom; in Matthew 8--9, the Credentials of the King; in Matthew 10, the Message of the kingdom; in Matthew 11, the Rejection of the Kingdom; in Matthew 12, the [unpardonable sin in Rejecting the King." Notice the anointing of the Holy Spirit is involved ]{.class_4257}(Mark 3:29--30). In Matthew 13, there is an abrupt change, which sidetracks 90 percent of the commentators into hopeless confusion, for in Matthew 13, the whole matter of the kingdom goes into a parabolic state described as "mysteries," and the postmillennial and amillennial commentators lose track forever of the intelli­gible order of events. They insert a [spiritual Church‑Kingdom ]{.class_4257}for a Jewish Monarchy, [which is still in abeyance []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_10}and will continue exactly as it began till it culminates in God's original plan to put a King from David on the throne at Jerusalem ]{.class_4257}(Rev. 11:15; Luke 1:31--33).\]

["Roboam"]{.class_4258} (Rehoboam) is a half‑breed from an Ammonitess mother (l Kings 14:21), and the ["Abia"]{.class_4258} here is the "Abijah" of 2 Chronicles 14.

1:8 And Asa begat Josaphat; and Josaphat begat Joram; and Joram begat Ozias;

The reader can readily identify Jehoshaphat from the list. Joram is listed as "Jehoram" in 2 Chronicles 21:1, and the ["Ozias"]{.class_4258} of Matthew 1:8 turns out to be "Uzziah" of 2 Chronicles. Now the student is faced with a serious problem, which unregenerate scholarship has complicated as much as possible. In the genealogy of the kings in 2 Chronicles, we find the monarchy running---J[ehoshaphat ]{.class_4257}(21:1),[ Jehoram ]{.class_4257}(21:1),[ Ahaziah ]{.class_4257}(22:1),[ Joash ]{.class_4257}(24:1)[, Amaziah ]{.class_4257}(25:1),[ and then Uzziah]{.class_4257} (26:1).[ ]{.class_4257}Where Matthew inserts only ONE King between Jehoshaphat and Uzziah, Chronicles lists [four. ]{.class_4257}Surely this must be a contradiction! No, there is no contradiction. The student noticed in the first verse of Matthew that a ["son"]{.class_4258} does not have to be the actual, [physical ]{.class_4257}son of his immediate father. But there is another fact to consider. In Matthew 1:17, the Holy Spirit tells us that there is a purpose in cutting down the genealogy to the number of names found in chapter 1. If all [four kings ]{.class_4257}of 2 Chronicles were listed, the "fourteens" of Matthew 1:17 would not come out even; it would be "fourteen generations from Abraham to David" and "seventeen generations from David until the carrying away," etc. The Holy Spirit has deliberately omitted Ahaziah, Joash, and Amaziah in Matthew for a purpose.

The purpose becomes apparent when we tangle with the critical problems in regard to [Ahaziah's age, ]{.class_4257}which is the classic "contradiction," supposedly, found in the AV 1611 Bible. In 2 Chronicles 22:2 we find the statement: ["Forty and two years old was Ahaziah when he began to reign," ]{.class_4258}and yet (!) in 2 Kings 8:26 we find: ["Two and twenty years old was Ahaziah when he began to reign." ]{.class_4258}This is the classic example of ["contradictions" in the Authorized Version, ]{.class_4257}and is one of the reasons why the majority of scholars in the world look upon the [King James ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_11}Bible with a light or sneering attitude. Accepting the fiats of their denominational scholarship, blindfolded, they are ready to believe the Bible contains "contradictions," without even examining the evidence, or questioning their leaders. \[There are some Greek manuscripts with all intervening Kings listed: Joram, Ahaziah, Joash, etc.\]

Now to clear the air, let it be noticed that Ahaziah [is a break in the kingly line and is not a direct descendant of Judah's kings. ]{.class_4257}Ahaziah was not Joram's (Jehoram's) son by his wife, but Ahaziah's mother was [Joram's mother‑in‑law! ]{.class_4257}(2 Chron. 22:9--10), whereas Joram had Ahab's daughter for a wife (2 Kings 8:18), [Ahaziah's mother was not Ahab's daughter---]{.class_4257}she was Ahab's [sister ]{.class_4257}(2 Kings 8:26), the daughter of Omri, a wicked king of [Israel---not JUDAH! ]{.class_4257}Again, we find the unsearchable riches of the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 clear a lot of obscurities resulting from mishandling of the Hebrew and Greek "originals" (see Exod. 20:5--6 for [why]{.class_4257} three generations were left out).

Why shouldn't there be a break in a Jewish genealogy presenting a Jewish King Messiah, when the succession from Jehoram (Matt. 1:8) goes to a Baalite from the northern tribes, whose mother was Jehoram's MOTHER­-IN‑LAW!? There is probably an act of immorality here, violating the Levitical law (Lev. 18:17), and there can be no doubt about the consequences; for Jehoram dies of [an incurable, internal disease ]{.class_4257}(2 Chron. 21:15--18), [and he had no son but Jehoahaz. ]{.class_4257}Ahaziah, then, was probably an illegiti­mate birth through immorality with a pagan "Baal‑worshipper," whose relationship is forbidden in Lev. 18:17. The Bible's critics do not have access, evidently, to the scientific and historical facts, which were available to Matthew! Matthew omits Ahaziah, and does not take up the line until [Uzziah. ]{.class_4257}The gap comes out happily at an exact seventy years, for upon the death of Ahaziah, Joash reigns forty years (2 Chron. 24:1). His successor runs twenty‑nine years (2 Chron. 25:1), and on the even "70," Uzziah ascends the throne and Matthew takes up the genealogy again. (The devout reader has no trouble with the seventy, for the even series of seventy times seven, found in Daniel 9 and in relation to the passages in 1 Kings 6:1, show []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_12}God's purpose in calling the number to his attention. From Abraham to the Exodus is 490 years, and from the Exodus to Solomon there are 490, and from Joshua to David there are 490, and from the dedication of the first temple under Solomon to the dedication of the second under Ezra and Nehemiah is another 490 years.)

The apparent "contradiction" in Ahaziah's age (42 and 22) is a knotty problem with more complexities than the omissions in Matthew 1. The most logical explana­tion is that since Ahaziah was a Baalite usurper from illegitimate ascendancy, he must have reigned sometime before becoming King over Judah. Checking the facts, this becomes apparent. Omri reigned in 832 B.C. and was probably succeeded by Ahaziah, who represented Ahab's house for twenty years before he took the throne of Judah. He was twenty-two years old when he took over the northern kingdom, [or at least represented it, ]{.class_4257}but as Jehoram's "son" (by his mother‑in‑law), [he had a title to the throne of Judah. ]{.class_4257}He took this title [at forty‑two years of age, ]{.class_4257}being counted as a king before he took over the throne---see 1 Samuel 13:1 and 1 Samuel 27:6 for a comparative case. A little study in the English often clears up obscurities in the LXX and Syriac, where they contradict the Hebrew text.

1:9 And Ozias begat Joatham; and Joatham begat Achaz; and Achaz begat Ezekias;

The reader can check the Old Testament spelling in the passages in 2 Chronicles 26--31. There are no deviations from the Old Testament list.

1:10 And Ezekias begat Manasses; and Manasses begat Amon; and Amon begat Josias;

The parallel passages in history are 2 Chron­icles 33:1, 20--21, 34:1.

1:11 And Josias begat Jechonias and his brethren, about the time they were carried away to Babylon:

The passage has several difficult problems in it which critical scholarship would call to our attention. (And it must be remembered that since God uses the "wrath of men to praise Him," and uses Satan []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_13}himself as an instrument to accomplish His purposes, we should not quake and tremble before the "questioning" of the Bible critics. None of their questions are unanswerable with a little study, and most of them can be answered without any study. Common sense can often fill in a gap left blank by a complex mind which is seeking nothing but a picture of itself.)

In 2 Chronicles, the line from Josiah runs as follows: Jehoahaz takes over the throne when twenty-three years old (2 Chron. 36:1). He is captured by the king of Egypt, who deposes him and crowns in his stead Eliakim (2 Chron. 36:4), and Eliakim has his name changed to Jehoiakim. Jehoiakim is followed in turn by Jehoiachin (notice the change of the "m" to "n"; (2 Chron. 36:9), and Jehoiachin is followed by the last Hebrew monarch, Zedekiah, who is subsequently carried captive to Babylon. We have a "set up," therefore, in which Zedekiah and Jehoiachin are said to be [brothers: ]{.class_4257}both of them being [sons of Jehoiakim ]{.class_4257}(2 Chron. 36:8), who in turn was a [son ]{.class_4257}of (2 Chron. 36:1--4) Jehoahaz. The Jechonias of Matthew 1:11 must refer to Jehoiachin, the last monarch before Zedekiah, for we read in Matthew 1:11 ["about the time they were carried away\...." ]{.class_4258}How then can we read that Josias [begat ]{.class_4257}Jechonias? The question is important, for we find that Jechonias is listed in Jeremiah 22:24 as "Coniah," the son of Jehoiakim, and there is a prophecy given concerning him that makes it certain[ that Christ had to be virgin born!]{.class_4257} (If Joseph were Christ's father, [Christ had no legal right to the throne, ]{.class_4257}according to the prophecy on Coniah's line in Jer. 22:30.)

In an effort to solve the problem, Griesbach (1771) and Clarke insert "Jehoiakim" in the verse, so the missing link is supplied: ["Josias begat Jehoiakim; and Jehoiakim begat Jechonias, etc."]{.class_4257} However, this tampering with the word is responsible for so much false doctrine, personal prejudices, perverted truth, and twisted teaching, we would do well to avoid it. There are two more logical Christian answers: (1.) Jeconias could refer to Jehoiakim (final "in"), in which case the "they" in Matthew 1:11 would point to Zedekiah and Jehoiachin (final "n"), being carried away captive---which they were (2 Chron. 36:10, 20). (2.) If Jeconias refers to Jehoiachin, which it probably does, we face the alternative of applying what we []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_14}learned from Matthew 1:1 and from the study of Ahaziah. A "son" is not always a direct son. David is said to be the "son" of Abraham, yet fourteen generations intervene. Ahaziah was said to be [the son of Jehoram ]{.class_4257}(2 Chron. 22:1), yet he is only a "son" by his father's mother‑in-law (or else not a real son at all). David is called a "son" by Saul, who was his father‑in‑law (1 Sam. 24:16). When it says that Josias begat Jeconias (who was really a grandson), it can mean that he begat him indirectly, exactly as we find that [Levi was "in the loins of Abraham," before Abraham BEGAT Isaac, and before Isaac BEGAT Jacob, who BEGAT Levi ]{.class_4257}(Heb. 7:9--10). This is the most logical explanation, although it can not satisfy the prejudiced mind that comes to Matthew with a preconceived and fixed opinion that the genealogy has to be [exactly and scientifically ]{.class_4257}accurate, as judged by modern "standards." The Bible has its own standards. Josiah could have raised up seed "to his brother" (Ruth 4:17), and this child could have been called Jehoiakim's son exactly as [Obed was said to be Naomi's son when he was really Ruth's! ]{.class_4257}(Ruth 4:17).

1:12 And after they were brought to Babylon, Jechonias begat Salathiel; and Salathiel begat Zorobabel;

13 And Zorobabel begat Abiud; and Abiud begat Eliakim; and Eliakim begat Azor;

At this point in the genealogy, the Messianic line begins to become obscure. No one who has ever tried to trace the Messiah through the books of Ezra, Nehemiah, and Chronicles has failed to notice the obscurity. Jeconias to Salathiel is plain (1 Chron. 3:17). The line can be traced with ease from Jeconias to Salathiel to Zerubbabel (1 Chron. 3:17, 19), but [beyond Zerubbabel ]{.class_4257}the Holy Spirit, for purposes of His own, nearly obliterates the trail of descendancy. Again, we can find abundant evidence for an intentional "break" in the line, for in Zechariah 4:6--10, Zerubbabel is plainly pictured as [a type of Christ. ]{.class_4257}In Matthew 1 the genealogy runs Zorobabel, Abiud, Eliakim. That this Zorobabel is the one of the returning captivity, mentioned in Ezra 5:2, is evident by the time element, for seventy years (the length of the captivity) is plenty of time for Jeconias to have a grandson named Zerubbabel. Clarke, in his commentary, makes an error at this point---and it is an error in []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_15}common sense---in supposing that the Zerubbabel of 1 Chronicles 3:19 and Ezra 5:2 could not have been a grandson of Jeconias. Seventy years is ample time for any man to be a grandfather! However, the problem is not solved completely, for the Ze­rubbabel of 1 Chronicles 3:19 is said to be the son of Pedaiah, while the Zerubbabel of Ezra 5:2 is said to be the son of Shealtiel, [and the Zorobabel of Matthew 1 is said to be the son of Salathiel! ]{.class_4257}Now Shealtiel and Salathiel are obviously the same man (remembering, of course, the differences between "Booz" and "Boaz," "Esrom" and "Hezron," etc., which you studied earlier in the list of names), but who is Pedaiah? (1 Chron. 3:19). And if the next man to follow Zorobabel is Abiud, then where is Abiud in the list of 1 Chronicles 3?

1\. There are exactly [ten names ]{.class_4257}given between Zorobabel and Jesus Christ in Matthew 1[. ]{.class_4257}We shall list them as part of the commentary text.

1:14 And Azor begat Sadoc; and Sadoc begat Achim; and Achim begat Eliud;

15 And Eliud begat Eleazar; and Eleazar begat Matthan; and Matthan begat Jacob;

16 And Jacob begat Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ.

Counting Eliakim and Abiud from verse 13, we have exactly ten names between Zorobabel and Jesus Christ, his anti‑type. To finish our observation, we learn from a study of 1 Chronicles 3 that there are exactly [ten names]{.class_4257} between Zerubbabel and the word Judah (the kingly genealogy---(1 Chron. 3:24), which brings us to the second observation.

2\. [The last man in the line of the kings of Judah, before the word Judah itself reappears]{.class_4257} (1 Chron. 4:1), [has the name of "Anani,"]{.class_4257} which means: "the clouds of heaven" (Matt. 26:64)! If this interpretation seems too "far­fetched," let the real scholar make note that the [Jerusalem Targum ]{.class_4257}says of this man: ["He is the King Messiah, who is to be revealed!"]{.class_4257}

With these thoughts in mind, let us approach our text humbly, and not with the arrogant, subjective attitude of a man determined to make the Bible lie, or to correct its supposed "corruptions." The names in 1 Chronicles, which are as follows, [could be ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_16}other names for the same ones in Matthew 1, although it [seems improbable. ]{.class_4257}Here are the two lists:

MATTHEW [​]{.class_4272}1 CHRONICLES

Zorobabe[l]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Zerubbabel

Abiu[d]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Hananiah

Eliaki[m]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Pelatiah

Achi[m]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Obadiah

Eliu[d]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Shemaiah

Eleaze[r]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Neariah

Mattha[n]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Elionenai

Jaco[b]{.class_4263}[             ]{.class_4264}Anani

Joseph

The next man in Matthew's list is "Jesus," and the next man in the listing of 1 Chronicles 3--4 is ["Judah"]{.class_4258} (1 Chron. 4:1), immediately following ["Anani."]{.class_4258} With consid­eration of Revelation 5:5 and Genesis 49:10 before us (and we should never be so foolish as to abandon [what the Scripture says about another passage of Scripture), ]{.class_4257}we find a definite intention of the Holy Spirit in the apparent discrepancies between Matthew and Chronicles. However, to make the two lists of names match seems to be stretching our liberty in Christ a little too far. There are numerous cases of "double naming" in the Bible: Gideon, Jerubbaal, Barak, et al., but we can hardly make a list of ten names all conform to an opposing list. The only thing to do is admit our foolishness and confess that from Zerub­babel onward, the [exact genealogy ]{.class_4257}through the captivity is lost to us, except [as Matthew has recorded it. ]{.class_4257}Lest someone should suppose the Bible believer to be "naive," who adopts this attitude, let it be called to his attention, that when the library at Alexandria was burned to the ground, [every ]{.class_4257}official record of Jewish descendancy to the throne of David was lost, and to this day [no Rabbi ]{.class_4257}or [Jewish official ]{.class_4257}has any official record of the line of kings [after Zedekiah ]{.class_4257}(2 Chron. 36). We have recorded the line from David to Mary (Luke 3), and Matthew records it to Joseph from David. Without these records, no Jew could ever put [any king on the throne of David, ]{.class_4257}for he has no record of [who followed Zedekiah! ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_17}The list of kings in 1 Chronicles 3 ends at verse 16: ["Zedekiah his son"]{.class_4258}; and Jeconiah's genealogy winds up with[ "Anani"]{.class_4258}---beyond this, total obscurity, [unless we believe what God told Matthew to write.]{.class_4257} Luke takes you to Zerubbabel---but beyond that, obscurity (Luke 3:27).

["And Jacob begat Joseph the husband of Mary, of whom was born Jesus, who is called Christ" ]{.class_4258}(vs. 16).

The way the Holy Spirit has worded the text, it is obvious that Joseph was not the [father ]{.class_4257}of Jesus Christ; however, Theodotian (A.D. 150) an Ebionite who wrote in the ancient commentary "The Hexapla" \[Origen's (A.D. 180) compilation of guesswork\], intimates most strongly that Joseph was Christ's father. In keeping with this blasphemous tradition, the [RSV ]{.class_4257}has, in the footnote, Theodotian's reading, with the curious and naive annotation that it is an "ANCIENT AUTHORITY." Exactly what this means is a great mystery, for Theodotian's reading [is not a GREEK manuscript. ]{.class_4257}The reading of the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 is the reading of the five oldest Greek manuscripts in existence. Exactly why the naive translators of the [RSV ]{.class_4257}should call Theodotian's reading an ["authority"]{.class_4257} is mystify­ing. The importance of this reading, however, can not be overestimated in regard to the [RSV]{.class_4257}'s use of it in a footnote, for Dean Weigle is cited by Kenneth Dole, in the [Washington Post ]{.class_4257}(Oct. 1, 1952), as saying: "We wouldn't put [anything ]{.class_4257}in a footnote that was [remote ]{.class_4257}from the truth." So here we have an honest confession that the [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators believe in the illegitimate birth of Christ by an adulterer and an adulteress---a nice piece of "scholarship."

The words ["Jesus, who is called Christ"]{.class_4258} strike our attention. One is the human name, as in verse 25, while the other is His title: "Anointed One." Since Satan is said to be "anointed" also, the Holy Spirit (being much more exacting in scholarship than the commentators) is careful to point out that [there is more than one "Christ" ]{.class_4257}(Luke 2:26; Acts 4:26). The use of this title "Christ" instead of the name "Jesus" is a key to the apostasy in the Roman and Protestant churches. It is at the name of "JESUS" that every knee is to bend, and it is this name "JESUS" that is above EVERY name (Phil. 2:9--10), not "Christ." Satan is "a" Christ, but not the "Lord's Christ" (Ezek. 28:14). As "a" Christ, he will "take over" the position given to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_18}Jesus Christ, at least for a period of three and one-half years (see 2 Thess. 2:3--10; Rev. 13:1--18).

1:17 So all the generations from Abraham to David are fourteen generations; and from David until the carrying away into Babylon are fourteen generations; and from the carrying away into Babylon unto Christ are fourteen generations.

Matthew has admittedly arranged his table so that it comes out to an even forty-two generations, by the omission of names in verses 8 and 11, which we have already studied. Thus, it is evident that forty-two generations take up a period of approximately 1921--1992 years, or about 45--47 years to the generation. There are other ways of figuring "generations" in the Bible. In 1 Chronicles 6, we find the generations from Abraham to David, through the priestly line of Aaron, figure to exactly fourteen generations. Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Levi, Amram, Aaron, Eleazar, Phinehas, Abishua, Bukki, Uzzi, Zerahiah, Meraioth, Ama­riah, Ahitub. The Bible believer recognizes immediately the fourteenth name, ["Ahitub,"]{.class_4258} for this is the priest during David's early years (1 Sam. 22:12). This list of fourteen would again confirm the teaching that God's reckoning of a "generation" is somewhere around 45--47 years. However, in Genesis 15 (where we are dealing with the progenitors of the Hebrew race, as they appear in future history), we find the curious bracketing of a generation in 100 years (Gen. 15:13, 16).

Scholarship (Matthew Henry, foremost, and others) has done it's best to convince us that Aaron was not the great‑grandson of Levi, and that there must have been intervening generations (Exod. 6:16--20). However, the list makes it clear that from Abraham to Aaron we are going to run six generations: 1. Isaac, 2. Jacob, 3. Levi, 4. Kohath, 5. Amram, 6. Aaron. But Abraham and Isaac [did not go down into Egypt. ]{.class_4257}The statement in Genesis 15:16 is that the ones who go down into Egypt to [serve ]{.class_4257}shall serve for a nation, and the fourth generation [of that group]{.class_4257} will come out. And so it comes to pass exactly and literally, for [Jacob goes down into Egypt, ]{.class_4257}and following Jacob comes 1. Levi, 2. Kohath, 3. Amram, and 4. AARON! [And Aaron goes up out of Egypt with Moses! ]{.class_4257}By such figuring, a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_19}"generation" is equal to 100 years. The problem, of course, lies in the fact that men lived to 175 (Abraham) and 120 (Moses) in these times, while the [average ]{.class_4257}age of men from Abraham to Christ, including the period of the kings and the captivity, appears to be much less; David, for example, not much over sixty years, and Amaziah (54 years), Jotham (41 years), Hezekiah (54 years), etc.

1:18 Now the birth of Jesus Christ was on this wise: When as his mother Mary was espoused to Joseph, before they came together, she was found with child of the Holy Ghost.

The verse professes to tell you the exact manner of the birth of the most remarkable man who ever lived. It would be wise to believe it, since the writer was an eye‑witness to three and one-half years of His ministry, and since the weight of scientific evidence is against [Christ's literal resurrection,]{.class_4257} if his father was an Adamic creature like Joseph. The ["before they came together" ]{.class_4258}rather puts the damper on the modern, neo‑orthodox talk of a "DIVINE CONCEPTION" instead of "A VIRGIN BIRTH." There was no human man involved for any divinity to "divinely conceive by"! Exactly why unbelieving scholars keep [making an issue of this matter, ]{.class_4257}when the Scriptures are clear on it, is another great "mystery of iniquity." After all, if one writer says [virgin ]{.class_4257}(vs. 23) and uses a Greek word [parthenos, ]{.class_4257}which never means anything [but a literal virgin, ]{.class_4257}and if the same writer tells you the conception took place [before the man and woman came together, ]{.class_4257}and another writer (a physician!) tells you [that Mary had no relation­ship with any man ]{.class_4257}(Luke 1:34), one would think a person would either take the Bible at its word or leave it alone and try to laugh it off. This everlasting, inconsistent meddling with the Bible, while [not believing it, ]{.class_4257}has to have a stop at some place. Here (Matt 1:18, 23) is a good place to stop. The [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators, forever disregarding the Greek text, render a slovenly and sloppy, ["I have no husband" ]{.class_4257}in Luke 1:34 for ["I know not a man"! ]{.class_4258}What we are supposed to believe from this perversion of Greek is an enigma. Do the [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators suppose their readers are so naive and gullible as to believe that Mary [could not have a child unless she had a HUSBAND? ]{.class_4257}What kind of crackpots do these []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_20}"scholars" take us for? Do they think we are unaware of their mental complex about the virgin birth that makes them [distort ]{.class_4257}the Greek and the English to rid themselves of it? The Bible believer has to resort to one path or the other. Either change the verses and say they don't mean what they say, or leave them as they stand and believe them. He believes them. The ["child" ]{.class_4258}of verse 18 was by the Holy Ghost. This Religious Leader is unique. He is God's ["begotten," ]{.class_4258}and He is God's ["only begotten"]{.class_4258} (John 3:16; Rom 1:4; Matt. 11:27), and so say [all ]{.class_4257}the New Testament writers (Mark 14:61, etc.).

To further slander His Deity and degrade Him to the level of Buddha, Marx, Huxley, Spinoza, Zoroaster, and Socrates, the [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators have committed the final act of [Greekicide; ]{.class_4257}they have taken the compound Greek word [monogenas ]{.class_4257}in John 3:16 and have translated only [half of the word ]{.class_4257}as "only." Exactly what the [RSV ]{.class_4257}does with the ["gena,"]{.class_4257} begotten, is another one of the endless mysteries connected with this obscure translation. The word "begotten" has been shelved as "archaic," yet in the [RSV ]{.class_4257}in Psalm 2:7, one will find the word standing there in all its archaic glory! Let us help our sterile scholarship out by suggesting that "only begotten" is a much more accurate translation of John 3:16, and much truer to the "original Greek."

1:19 Then Joseph her husband, being a just man, and not willing to make her a public example, was minded to put her away privily.

The verse contains a tremendous spiritual truth, for here we find a perfect example of a righteousness that goes [beyond the law, ]{.class_4257}even though it is performed under the law, regulated by Old Testament conditions. By all legal rights, under the law, Joseph had more than a right to make a public example of Mary---he had a right to have her [killed ]{.class_4257}(Deut. 22:17--21). Yet Joseph applies (or was ready to apply) Deuteronomy 24:1--3, without bringing a scandal upon his fiancée. The word ["privily" ]{.class_4258}is our word for "private" or "secret." A further study of the wording reveals that a Jewish man was often called a ["husband"]{.class_4258} before he had actually had a relationship with his bride. Joseph is called ["her husband"]{.class_4258} in verse 19, and she is called ["his wife"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_21} in verse 20 and verse 24. The ruling is from Deuteronomy 22:24 where a betrothed woman is said to be the wife of the man to whom she is engaged---notice ["neighbour's wife" ]{.class_4258}near the end of the verse (compare Gen. 29:21).

The [RSV ]{.class_4257}reads "divorce" for ["put\...away,"]{.class_4258} which again is reading something into the text that is not there. The word "divorce" is used by the Pharisees in Matthew 19, while discussing the regulations of Deuteronomy 24:1--2, but notice, please, that in Deuteronomy 24:1--2 [the man has already had a relationship with his wife, before he "divorces" her. ]{.class_4257}Joseph is not getting a divorce [yet, ]{.class_4257}as he has not had any relation with his wife yet. The [RSV ]{.class_4257}anticipates too much in its effort to prove that [Mary and Joseph were more than "engaged." ]{.class_4257}The translator's slip is showing.

1:20 But while he thought on these things, behold, the angel of the Lord appeared unto him in a dream, saying, Joseph, thou son of David, fear not to take unto thee Mary thy wife: for that which is conceived in her is of the Holy Ghost.

Again, the Holy Spirit emphasizes the fact that there was no human, male agency in the "Divine conception." (The term "divine conception" is a Catholic merger, being a compromise toward the immovable Roman supersti­tion that Mary was "immaculately conceived," i.e., "the immaculate conception.") The Angel of the Lord occupies a unique position here, for the term is found in both Testaments as applying to the Lord Jesus Himself (note Gal. 4:14; Acts 27:23; Gen. 32:30; Judg. 13:18). The word ["angelos" is ]{.class_4257}used in classical Greek as meaning "messenger"; however, we should be wary of this type of teaching, as the New Testament is written not in classical Greek primarily, but in "koine" or "common Greek." The Attic Greek of Sophocles (495 B.C.), Plato (427 B.C.), and Euripedes (480 B.C.) is a sorry standard by which to find the meaning of Greek words, in the New Testament, for the "Hellenic" or common dialect (with the Attic as a base dialect) is the correct standard. The Holy Spirit [changes the meaning of some Greek words from their use in classical Greek. ]{.class_4257}For example, in 1 Peter 4:11, we find the Greek ["korage," ]{.class_4257}which in the classical Greek means only the "supplying" or []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_22}"furnishing of a chorus." Classical Greek is not an infallible guide to the lexicon or to Bible usage. Certain laws operate in any language to change the use, and the papyri in some cases are more reliable on the meaning of Greek words than the great mutilated perver­sions of the LXX, passed down by Origen. Other Greek words that you might study if you have time are [zoopoieo, praxtor, ]{.class_4257}and [para.]{.class_4257}

The word ["angel,"]{.class_4258} then, means a great deal more in Scripture than it does in the secular musings of Aeschylus (525 B.C.), Aesop (521 B.C.), Acreon (572 B.C.), Simonides (556 B.C.), and Herodotus (413 B.C.). In the Bible, it has a definite meaning of ["an appearance," ]{.class_4257}or ["apparition," ]{.class_4257}which we would liken to a [ghost. ]{.class_4257}Notice carefully the following [inspired ]{.class_4257}uses of the word---Matthew 18:10 and Acts 12:15. This opens up a line of thought closed to students who waste too much time with the "unsearchable riches" of the Greek. It poses the thought that there is a good reason for the use of the word Holy [GHOST ]{.class_4257}in the New Testament, in many instances, instead of the Holy SPIRIT. The "uniform" translating of the liberals, and the technical gymnastics of Bullinger, only serve to obscure the truth. Christ Jesus, as a Spirit, [has a bodily shape ]{.class_4257}(Gal. 4:19; Phil. 3:10; 1 Cor. 4:15), and this bodily shape is the bodily shape ascribed to [HIM; and He is "the Angel of the Lord." ]{.class_4257}An angel is an "appearance," not merely a "messenger," as we find it in classical Greek.

1:21 And she shall bring forth a son, and thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins.

It would be profitable to compare the message with the one delivered to Mary in Luke 1:30--33. Joseph is only told the bare essentials. However, we learn from the passage that [Joseph did the naming of the child ]{.class_4262}(Matt. 1:21, 25), [not Mary. ]{.class_4262}The word ["JESUS"]{.class_4258} is full of meaning. This is the Old Testament Joshua, with variations such as Jehoshaphat, and Jehoshua, and Joash, et al. \[The word is inserted for "Joshua" in two passages in the [AV ]{.class_4262}1611, and both times the scholars call it to our attention as a mistake. It is no mistake, however. The word ["Jesus"]{.class_4258} for "Joshua" in Hebrews 4:8 points out the fact that the wilderness journey will be [repeated in the future ]{.class_4262}(see Rev. 11; Micah 7:14--15; Hosea 2:14--20). And the occurrence in Acts 7:45 points out the same thing. The []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_23}[book of Joshua then is a type of the Second Advent of Jesus. ]{.class_4262}To fail to observe this fact is to lose a tremendous amount of rich prophetic information.\] Jesus, then, means "salvation" or "saviour," and thus we find the [AV ]{.class_4262}1611 defining the word for us in Matthew 1:21, ["for he shall SAVE his people."]{.class_4258}

The ["his people" ]{.class_4258}needs some consideration. Although the verse may be applied [inspirationally ]{.class_4257}to anyone who will accept Jesus as Saviour, the verse here, historically and doctrinally, can have only one meaning: Jesus will save [the Jews ]{.class_4257}from their sins. This is reaffirmed constantly throughout the history of this period. Luke speaks of it (Luke 1:68--77---especially verse 77). Peter speaks of it (Acts 3:26), and it is not applied to [Gentiles ]{.class_4257}until Acts 10:43. The ["his people"]{.class_4258} is positive identification, for the Old Testament references are too numerous to list (Deut. 32:36, 43, Psa. 44:12, 83:3, 85:8, etc.). A sermonic note can be found in the use of the word ["from."]{.class_4258} He is not going to save anyone [in ]{.class_4257}their sins; He is going to save you [from them. ]{.class_4257}You may be dead IN [trespasses and sin ]{.class_4257}(Eph. 2:1--3), but when He saves you, you will be saved FROM them.

1:22 Now all this was done, that it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying,

["By the prophet" ]{.class_4258}is omitted in the [RSV]{.class_4257}, and we shall see later that the translators of this glorified telephone book have a special bone to pick with [Old Testament prophecy. ]{.class_4257}They have carefully attacked and changed 75 percent of the verses in the prophets that deal with the First Advent of Christ. Those who are familiar with the antiquated and fanciful guesswork of Poteat, Wieman, Knudsen, Harkness, Cadoux, Van Dusen, Crane, Dahlberg, Chappell, Bultmann, Heidegger, and others, know their eternal thesis is---that [there are contradictions in the Bible.]{.class_4257} To prove this, they change verses in the Old Testament to [force them to contradict with verses in the New Testament, ]{.class_4257}thus "proving" their thesis---to no one but themselves.

1:23 Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel, which being interpreted is, God with us.

Here "the Greek" catches up with its devotees, and with a resounding ["parthenos" ]{.class_4257}(Greek) settles forever the dispute about whether Isaiah is saying "virgin" or "young woman" (Isa. 7:14). Since any author is allowed the liberty of freely quoting [his own work, ]{.class_4257}and since any author knows what he [intended ]{.class_4257}when he wrote his work, we may safely assume that the word ["VIRGIN"]{.class_4258} in Isaiah 7:14 means "virgin," and by no means "maiden" or "young woman." That a YOUNG WOMAN should conceive would hardly be a [sign, ]{.class_4257}anyway, and we are distinctly told in Isaiah 7:14 that ["The Lord himself shall give you a sign."]{.class_4258} If it were only "young woman," that would be a pretty sorry demonstration of God's powers to produce a miraculous sign.

Where does all this nonsense come from? It is VIRGIN in Isaiah 7:14 in the Septuagint (220 B.C.? or A.D.!), it is VIRGIN in the Vulgate (A.D. 382), it is VIRGIN in the [RV ]{.class_4257}(1881), it is VIRGIN in the [ASV ]{.class_4257}(1901). Whence, then, this irreverent nonsense about "young woman"? The student will find it in the [Babylonian Talmud. ]{.class_4257}The "authority" is not an Old Testament authority. It is one of the ["Jewish fables" ]{.class_4258}warned about in Titus 1:14. The Rabbis make this woman the mother of Hezekiah, which is a terrific blunder, for at this time Hezekiah was [already twelve years old ]{.class_4257}(Isa. 7:1). The word ["Almah" ]{.class_4257}for ["virgin,"]{.class_4258} here, is found to be [virgin ]{.class_4257}wherever else it occurs, as in Genesis 24:43 ([RSV ]{.class_4257}"maiden") and Song of Solomon 1:3, 6:8. When Harry Emerson Fosdick made his dogmatic statement that he did not know a single intelligent Christian minister that believed in the virgin birth, he showed his own lack of intelligent training, for Robert Dick Wilson of Princeton believed it, and so did Dr. A. T. Robertson of Louisville, and so did J. Gresham Machen, and R. A. Torrey. Any man in that list could put poor Fosdick out of the pulpit when it came to Greek or Hebrew scholarship. It is ["virgin,"]{.class_4258} you can be sure.

The ["they shall call"]{.class_4258} indicates that although Joseph calls Him Saviour (Jesus), the people of Israel will know Him as ["God with us"]{.class_4258} (Immanuel, Isa. 7:14). Why any natural‑born baby of human parentage should be called ["God with us"]{.class_4258} is another problem the modernist will have to work out when he wakes up from his pseudo‑scholastic hangover.

From the Hebrew ["Almah" ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_25}(Isa. 7:14), we find a curious twentieth-century custom which bears testimony against the rendering of "young woman." The expression is "my old ALMA MATER," meaning of course, "virgin mother!" (The expression also indicates the extent of Roman Catholicism's brainwashing. "Mater" is Latin for [Mother.)]{.class_4257}

1:24 Then Joseph being raised from sleep did as the angel of the Lord had bidden him, and took unto him his wife:

For notes on the use of the word ["wife,"]{.class_4258} see comments on verses 19--20.

1:25 And knew her not till she had brought forth her firstborn son: and he called his name JESUS.

The word ["firstborn" ]{.class_4258}is omitted in the Westcott and Hort Pro‑Catholic text, used for the [RV, ASV, RSV]{.class_4257}, and the modern translations. The pressure of Roman tradition is felt quite strongly in all committees subsequent to 1611, and we are not surprised to find the word ["firstborn"]{.class_4258} omitted, as such a reading carries with it the implication (and fact) that [Mary had other sons after Jesus Christ. ]{.class_4257}That these are the facts is apparent to any sane reader, for in Mark 6:3 the brothers are listed! The standard Jesuit objection is that the "brothers and sisters" here are really "cousins," and since the Bible does occasionally "mix" terms which refer to blood relationships (as we have learned in our study of the genealogies), the objection [could be valid. ]{.class_4257}However, in this case, there has been a definite error, for the word "COUSIN" is [used in the Bible when referring to a cousin! ]{.class_4257}In Luke 1:36 the Holy Spirit does not have to use the word "sister"; He uses COUSIN. And again, in Luke 1:58 He does not use the word "brothers and sisters," but the word COUSINS. If the "brothers and sisters" of Mark 6:3 are cousins, then why is the word COUSIN not found in the text? [It was available.]{.class_4257}

But there is even a greater reason why the Bible believer can be absolutely certain that the [King James ]{.class_4257}text is superior to the Greek of Westcott and Hort and the superstitious errors of the pagan priests. In John 2:17 we are told that the reference is to the Lord []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw101.xhtml_page_26}Jesus Christ. Yet when we check the reference in the Psalms, it is found in Psalm 69:9; and right in the context, we find these peculiar words: ["I am become a stranger unto MY BRETHREN, and an alien unto MY MOTHER'S CHIL­DREN"! ]{.class_4258}(Psa. 69:8). The New Testament confirms this, for in Galatians 1:19 (and again in 1 Cor. 15:7) we find a reference to the ["Lord's brother."]{.class_4258} The ["brethren"]{.class_4258} of the Lord could be cousins, but the ["Lord's brother"]{.class_4258} is a dead giveaway, for this [James had a special post‑resurrection appearance]{.class_4257} (1 Cor. 15:7)[ for the purpose of converting him from his unbelief, recorded in John 7:5! ]{.class_4257}This James is not the apostle, for all the apostles are given in 1 Corinthians 15:7, and the "twelve" are given in 1 Corinthians 15:5. Mary most certainly DID have other children after Jesus Christ, and the superstitious, un‑biblical tradition of Mary's "perpetual virginity" is just another Roman wreck to finish the wrecking work of Irenaeus, Eusebius, Constantine (A.D. 313), Augustine (A.D. 354), and Jerome (A.D. 340).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml}

# Chapter 2 {.heading_84-0}

2:1 Now when Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judaea in the days of Herod the king, behold, there came wise men from the east to Jerusalem,

["When Jesus was born"]{.class_4258} indicates the time near His birth, but not the exact date, for if the student will give his careful attention to the material in Matthew 2:8--11, as compared with the account in Luke 2:15--16, he will discover that Jesus is a [young child ]{.class_4257}at the time of the visit by the Magi, while He is a [babe ]{.class_4257}at the time of the shepherd's visitation. There are several "Herods" in the New Testament times, and the information on them is available in any Bible dictionary. This Herod is not the Herod of Acts 12:1, since a son, Archelaus (Matt 2:22), reigns between them.

The wise men (Greek---m[agi) ]{.class_4257}from the east are called by the name of our word "magician." The "mag" of the first part of the word is found attached to Babylonian names in Jeremiah 39:13. The expression ["from the east"]{.class_4258} is significant, as nearly all movements from this direction are good movements in the Bible. Hitler (1889--1945) and Napoleon (1769--1821) both ended their careers through failure to observe this Bible phenomenon, and Horace Greeley (1811) coined it accidentally, without knowledge, when he said, "Go West, young man." This is one of the rich treasures of the [Authorized Version]{.class_4257} (1611) overlooked by the students who waste their time in the vertical studies of the corrupt Westcott and Hort Greek. Note:

1\. When God drove Adam and Eve out, He drove them out to the East (Gen. 3:24).

2\. When Cain left God's presence, he went out toward the East (Gen. 4:16).

3\. When Jacob "backslid" and ran from Esau, he went Eastward (Gen. 28:10).

4\. The first tribes to go into captivity settled East of Jordan (Num. 32).

5\. When the Jews were carried captive, they went Eastward (Jer. 52).

6\. When David ran for his life from Absalom, he went []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_28}Eastward (2 Sam. 16--18).

7\. [West to East is the direction of the rotation of the world against the sun, and it is the direction one would take leaving the Holy of Holies and going out of the Tabernacle! ]{.class_4257}(Exod. 27:13).

1\. When God called Abraham to the land, he called him to go West (Gen. 12:1--6).

2\. When Jacob got right and came home, he traveled Westward (Gen. 32--35).

3\. When the Jews entered Palestine, God made them traverse the Dead Sea so they could come in East to West (Num. 19--25).

4\. When the Jews returned from captivity, they traveled Westward (Ezra--Nehemiah).

5\. [God forbade the gospel to go Eastward to Asia, but made it go Westward to Europe ]{.class_4257}(Acts 16:6--10).

6\. East to West is the direction of the sun, and the sun is said to be a type of Jesus Christ [moving in opposition to the world ]{.class_4257}(Mal. 4:2; 1 John 2:15).

7\. When you enter the Tabernacle, the movement to the Holy of Holies is East to West; [the direction of Jesus' entry into Jerusalem!!]{.class_4257}

A little Bible study in the Textus Receptus (the text of the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 and Martin Luther's German Bible) would have saved Hitler an empire. Russia is the wrong direction. He should have crossed the channel, and so say [all ]{.class_4257}military experts, saved or lost. [The wise men came from the East.]{.class_4257}

2:2 Saying, Where is he that is born King of the Jews? for we have seen his star in the east, and are come to worship him.

This is the first question in the New Testament. It is interesting to compare it to some other questions. The first question men ask before Christ's baptism is ["Where is he that is born King of the Jews?"]{.class_4258} Immediately after His baptism He asks, ["What seek ye" ]{.class_4258}(John 1:38), and in the Old Testament the first question God asks is, ["Where art thou?" ]{.class_4258}Somebody is looking for something! It is God []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_29}who is seeking for men, and then in response SOME men seek Him (Luke 19:10). It would be no great thing for Orientals to come to seek the King of the Jews, for every [magician ]{.class_4257}among the Orientals knew of the prophecy of the Messiah from Genesis 9:26. They knew he would be a [Shemite, ]{.class_4257}and if they had access to Genesis 49, of course they knew He would be Jewish and would be marked by a Star (Gen. 49:10; Num. 24:17). Modern commentators (liberals foremost) are extremely naive and egotistical in their attitude toward the ancients' ability to obtain knowledge. A slight look at the information the pagan Babylonian had access to (Jer. 40:2--5) or the heathen Abimelech (Gen. 20:2--6) or the knowledge that a fisherman had access to, in regard to the Second Coming of Christ (Matt. 17:10), should convince someone that [common people in Bible times ]{.class_4257}had much more information available than modern scholars do, since the majority of them [believed and read the Scriptures as though they were revelations from God. ]{.class_4257}The common people did not [miss His first coming ]{.class_4257}(Mark 12:12, 37). It was the educated, broad-minded, religious leaders who muffed the ball, as usual.

The ["wise men from the East"]{.class_4258} had access to further information which no postmillennial scholar or amillen­nial scholar of the twentieth century has. They had access to Daniel 9, [which was written while Daniel was in the East. ]{.class_4257}In Daniel 9, you will find that Christ's triumphal entry into Jerusalem is given to the very day, dating from the decree of Artaxerxes (445--446 B.C.) in Nehemiah 2:1. Exact information can be found in Sir Robert Anderson's [Coming Prince]{.class_4257} or Larkin's [Daniel]{.class_4257}. It would be a small matter for a smart man, if [he believed the Bible was the word of God, ]{.class_4257}to figure the [birth ]{.class_4257}of Christ within two or three years. In Numbers 4:43 and 1 Samuel 2:35 it is clear that Christ would have to be thirty years old before He could appear officially as an anointed Messiah. And why would not the Magi (wise men) study Numbers? Does not a "Magi" in Numbers prophesy of a [star ]{.class_4257}that will mark the Jewish King? Look at it---Numbers 24:17---and notice that this is the star of a [King, ]{.class_4257}for a [scepter is ]{.class_4257}connected with it!

The trouble with our gullible postmillennial and amillennial "scholars" is they don't have the sense that God gave to a B.C. Babylonian. They presume the [Bible is limited to history ]{.class_4257}in its information. They suppose it is all [past, ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_30}except with an occasional vague, figurative, spiritual reference to some ethereal future state, which is a "floating opportunity." A little study in the Bible and a little faith in what it says would bring them where it brought the Magi---to the feet of the Saviour to worship [Him, ]{.class_4257}not the library shelf.

Notice further that the ["him"]{.class_4258} is prominent in verse 2. They do not come [to worship Mary. ]{.class_4257}If the reader will check the verses in the chapter, he will notice how carefully the Holy Spirit has drawn attention to the fact that Mary is only a secondary standby in the scene. Verses 8--9, 11, 14, and 21 all put the emphasis on the ["young child,"]{.class_4258} and always put the ["mother"]{.class_4258} in the second place.

2:3 When Herod the king had heard these things, he was troubled, and all Jerusalem with him.

The visit seems to have caused quite a stir, but then you will find Jesus causing quite a stir whenever He shows up (Matt. 21:8--15). His consecrated followers seem to be afflicted with the same plague, for wherever we find them they seem to be "apostles of discord," "schismatics," and "trouble makers," etc. (Acts 7:54, 5:40, 13:50, 14:14, 16:20, 17:6, etc.) Undoubtedly our Lord is guilty of [troubling people---]{.class_4257}notice the text! He troubled Judas till he committed suicide; He troubled Peter till he wept; He troubled Paul till he fell off a horse, blinded; He troubled Belshazzar till his knees knocked together; and He troubled the Pharisees till they murdered Him. Our Lord is a "troublemaker" (see Luke 14:26, 12:51--53). To call Him the "Prince of Peace" and the "Lowly Galilean" and the "Nazarene" and the "Essene," without pointing out His talent as a [Revolutionist, is ]{.class_4257}an unforgivable oversight for a man who professes to be educated. None of His revolutions, however, concerned [social and class issues. ]{.class_4257}They crucified Him for [blasphemy against the established religious setup of the COUNCIL ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 10:17; Mark 13:9; Matt. 12:14; Acts 5:41, 6:12, etc.). \[An unbelieving Communist is hardly the correct anti‑type of Christ Jesus. Jesus professed to believe the entire [Old Testament, as it stood ]{.class_4257}(Luke 24:27, 44), and used it constantly with the "prooftext" method for His authority.\]

2:4 And when he had gathered all the chief priests and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_31}scribes of the people together, he demanded of them where Christ should be born.

Notice the king's acquaintance with the title "Christ" [(Christos), ]{.class_4257}which he immediately associates with a King. Notice further that he [expects the Jews to know the answer. ]{.class_4257}Herod expects the Jewish scriptures to be able to accurately foretell the future, and is so convinced they can, [that he is willing to murder on the accuracy of that information! ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 2:16).

Again, we are impressed by the fact that kings, fishermen, and magicians in these times had more respect for the word of God than the average seminary professor does today. And Herod is not disappointed, for in the next verse the information is given.

2:5 And they said unto him, In Bethlehem of Judaea: for thus it is written by the prophet,

6 And thou Bethlehem, in the land of Juda, art not the least among the princes of Juda: for out of thee shall come a Governor, that shall rule my people Israel.

The quotation is from Micah 5:2, and since it is scientifically accurate, one would expect the unscientific [RSV ]{.class_4257}to mutilate the prophecy if possible---which it has done. In the [RSV ]{.class_4257}in Micah 5:2, we find the [eternity of Christ ]{.class_4257}attacked by the changing of the expression ["from everlasting" ]{.class_4258}to ["from ancient days." ]{.class_4257}The [ASV]{.class_4257}, the grandfather of the [RSV]{.class_4257}, renders "of ancient days," but is too scared to put it into the text in 1901, so the [ASV ]{.class_4257}thrusts it into a footnote. Moffatt comes home in fine fashion with "of long decent"; other assorted attacks on Jesus Christ by these fine "broad‑minded liberals" continue to tear up the verse relating to the place of His birth. The Hebrew reads, ["mime olam," ]{.class_4257}referring to the ["days of eternity"]{.class_4257}---never as the sloppy rendering of the [RSV]{.class_4257}.

There is a rather humorous note in regard to the way in which the scribes misquote the verse in Matthew 2:6. The text in Micah reads that Bethlehem is ["little among the thousands of Judah." ]{.class_4258}It was so little that it is missing in the catalogue of Joshua (in the Hebrew), although it is found in the so‑called "LXX" in Nehemiah 7:26. It is not mentioned among those rebuilt in Nehemiah 11:25 at []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_32}the return of the captivity. The scribes, however (being questioned by Herod), are in the [capital of Judah, ]{.class_4257}Jerusalem---see verse 1. So to impress Herod, their foreign satrap, with their own importance, they say that Bethlehem is "NOT [the least among the princes of Juda"! ]{.class_4257}Note, too, the insertion of the word ["governor" ]{.class_4258}for "ruler." This also is designed to impress Herod, for the next "governor" of Judaea was [Pontius Pilate ]{.class_4257}(Luke 3:1). The complexity of this prophecy develops. Nothing could indicate more clearly the depravity of the human heart than its attitude toward [Divine revelation of the future which proves to be true, ]{.class_4257}for the very men who quote this verse to Pilate, or at least their students, are yelling in John 7:42 that it is so, and yet in John 7:27 they are saying ["no one knows where Christ is from!" ]{.class_4257}They are perfect "Bible-believing Agnostics." They profess to believe it, but when it comes to a [concrete application, ]{.class_4257}they only "surmise." They are like our modern "liberals" who still stand in Trinitarian pulpits, which [profess ]{.class_4257}to believe the Apostles' Creed, but on the golf course they joke about the "myths" of the New Testament. Herod is not so deceived; he takes action.

2:7 Then Herod, when he had privily called the wise men, inquired of them diligently what time the star appeared.

Notice that the Roman king is an ardent student of prophecy (2 Pet. 1:19), exactly as his later successors (Acts 26:27). The Old Testament had a much wider circulation in the Greek‑speaking world than our modern commenta­tors imagine. Hercules is an imitation of Jonah, according to Lycophron (in the courts of Ptolemy). Philadelphus translated some of the Hebrew into Greek before Christ, and no one who has given careful attention to Ecclesiastes (written hundreds of years before Greece had any philosophers or poets) would escape the thought that all "great" literature is just watered‑down Bible, diluted through two and one-half pints of human brains.

The time of the appearance of the star is question­able, depending upon the exact distance of the journey the wise men had to travel. If they set out as soon as it appeared (and if it appeared at the time the Lord Jesus was born), it would hardly have taken them a year or []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_33}more to get to Bethlehem, unless of course, they travelled from China. Again, it may be that the star appeared sometime after the birth, for it leads them to the [house ]{.class_4257}where the ["young child" ]{.class_4258}was (vs. 11), not to the [manger ]{.class_4257}where the [babe ]{.class_4257}was (see Luke 2). However, this star is a very peculiar star. For it [moves. ]{.class_4257}And in moving it moves less than ten miles! Notice the exposition on verse 9.

2:8 And he sent them to Bethlehem, and said, Go and search diligently for the young child; and when ye have found him, bring me word again, that I may come and worship him also.

The word ["Bethlehem"]{.class_4258} means, "The House of Bread," a fitting designation for the birthplace of Him who was the Bread of Life. Herod uses the words ["young child"]{.class_4258} here for the first time, which indicates that the wise men had already told him that the birth had been some period [before this time. ]{.class_4257}This is not the night that the shepherds were heralded by angels. This is a night much later.

Again, notice how Herod calls the wise men aside [privately ]{.class_4257}(see vs. 7) to talk with them. He would not have dared talk about [worshipping this "King of the Jews" ]{.class_4257}in front of the Jewish scribes and priests! They would have laughed him out of the throne room. But before unsus­pecting foreigners from an eastern land, the King feigns humility and stealthily seeks to find the exact location of the coming King: ["that I may come and worship him also." ]{.class_4258}The "worship" of a king is a common thing in the mouth of the one speaking, for Herod was under Caesar, and one of Caesar's titles was "Lord." (This, of course, is the standard Roman practice: the deification of a "Father" at Rome. It is most observable in the dictator‑complex found among the Catholic people. Franco, Batista, Castro, Trujillo, Hitler, deGaulle, Mussolini, Stalin, all were "Catholics" before their ideological rebellions.)

2:9 When they had heard the king, they departed; and, lo, the star, which they saw in the east, went before them, till it came and stood over where the young child was.

10 When they saw the star, they rejoiced with exceeding []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_34}great joy.

The problem here is how one could track the distance that a star moved when the "moving" was only from Jerusalem (vs. 1) to Bethlehem (vs. 11), for the distance is [less than ten miles. ]{.class_4257}The proverbial "Star of wonder, star of night," is certainly a "wonder"! There are two explanations. One is that the second clause beginning, ["the star, which they saw in the east," ]{.class_4258}is a repetition going back over the history of the past weeks in which they travelled to Herod. The ["when they saw the star"]{.class_4258} would indicate this interpretation, unless of course they saw the star in the East, and then for a while it disappeared and [reappeared ]{.class_4257}upon their departure from Herod. Another explanation is a little more farfetched, but fits the requirements of the text much better. (The Bible believer should never allow himself to be tight‑laced by the "broad‑minded" scientists who limit the meaning of a ["star" ]{.class_4257}to a circular, light‑bearing, heavenly body.) The Bible, for example, plainly tells you that ["angels" ]{.class_4257}are ["stars" ]{.class_4257}(Rev. 12:4, 12:9, 9:1). Notice the ["him"]{.class_4258} when referring to a star. Notice further that Jesus, who is a "Him," is called a star in Revelation 22:16, and if that were not enough, in Revelation 1:20 we are told, by [definition, that stars stand for angels!! ]{.class_4257}Surely if we have a revelation of Jesus Christ (Rev. 1:1) by which to determine words, we should use it.

When Satan is called[ "an angel of LIGHT"]{.class_4258} (2 Cor. 11:12--14), we should not forget that, the stars are "light bearers." (Study Isa. 14:12 in relation to the word "Lucifer" from the Latin, ["LUX‑FERO": ]{.class_4257}"light bearers" or "shining ones.") The [RV]{.class_4257}, quaintly enough, gives Satan the title reserved for Jesus Christ in this passage and says "Day‑star" for Lucifer. \[This perversion could not be classified as the unforgivable sin of Mark 3, but there may be some relation, for Jesus is the DAY STAR (2 Pet. 1:19--20)---not Satan.\]

The text in Matthew would indicate that if the star is not a star as the sun (somewhere in the galaxies and constellations), that it is the Angel of the Lord, [standing over the spot ]{.class_4257}where the Messiah is, exactly as He once stood over Jerusalem before to indicate the place of burnt‑offering and sacrifice (1 Chron. 21:15--16). It is instructive to notice that the ["Star out of Jacob," ]{.class_4258}prophesied by Balaam, is said to be a "him" (notice Num. 24:17, the ["he"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_35}). Let us not forget the comments on chapter 1, verse 20; an angel is not merely a [messenger, ]{.class_4257}but he is an [appearance. This star ]{.class_4257}turned some to righteousness, instead of to Hollywood.

2:11 And when they were come into the house, they saw the young child with Mary his mother, and fell down, and worshipped him: and when they had opened their treasures, they presented unto him gifts; gold, and frankincense, and myrrh.

The passage is rich and suggestive. To begin with, it is a[ "house," ]{.class_4258}not a "manger." The quaint and archaic Catholic conception of the birth of Christ, with the Magi and shepherds both present, is typical of Roman dogma, along with such myths as the dogwood cross and the red‑haired Christ, etc.

Notice that the [young child ]{.class_4257}is always mentioned [before Mary---]{.class_4257}see verses 13--14, 20--21. How meticulously the Holy Spirit puts[ "and his mother"]{.class_4258} after [Him. ]{.class_4257}The [worship ]{.class_4257}of verse 11 is to [Him, ]{.class_4257}not her.

The treasures they open have tremendous doctrinal and spiritual significance, but the practical significance is even more significant. At this time, Mary and Joseph were so poor that they had not been able even to offer the required sacrifice for Mary's cleansing as a sinner. Mary, as any female sinner (Luke 1:47), needed to obey the laws of purification given to every woman in the Old Testament (Luke 2:21--24; Lev. 12:1--6). But when she came to have Jesus circumcised, she and Joseph were so poor that they could not bring the required [Lamb ]{.class_4257}(see Lev. 12). So instead, they offered the turtle doves and pigeons. Marvel of marvels, they [had the required Lamb in their hands, but did not recognize Him, since He was human ]{.class_4257}(John 1:29). Being this poor, there was no way on earth they could have taken the long trip into Egypt from Bethlehem, that they were going to have to take, at the time of the Magi's visit (Matt. 2:13--14). But now with the appearance of the wise men, Joseph and Mary are [RICH! GOLD! ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 2:11). They can make the trip (Phil. 4:19).

Now, to come to the treasures themselves: they represent the gifts that would be given to three types of individuals. Gold is for a [king ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_36}(1 Kings 10:18). As gold represents the highest material value, it represents the highest spiritual value; hence, the articles in the tabernacle were all covered with gold. A Messiah‑King of divine origin would need this gift. Gold is given to the King of the Jews to acknowledge He is God's chosen ruler. Frankincense concerns a priesthood (Exod. 30:34--38), and especially the duty of the priest to pray for [the people ]{.class_4257}(Rev. 8:3--5; Luke 1:10). We have here, then, an indication that this king is a King‑Priest, and He is typified by His right name "Joshua" (see Matt. 1:21), as a Priest who reigns as a King in Zechariah 6:11! His other type is Melchisedec: a King­-Priest (Heb. 7:2).

There is a Bible basis, therefore, for the peculiar, dictatorial obsession manifested by the Roman Catholic Church. To combine the kingly office with the religious office of a priest is the obsession (and was the obsession of Constantine) of all popes.

However, this integration of priest and king is the [unholy ambition of Saul]{.class_4257} (1 Sam. 14:35, 13:10--11), and it is not the holy authorization, which David had in 2 Samuel 6:14. The Devil [always has a counterfeit. ]{.class_4257}The counterfeit always precedes the genuine article. Cain before Abel. Esau before Jacob. Reuben before Joseph. Ishmael before Isaac. Saul before David. Moses before Christ. [Antichrist on this earth seven years before God's Priest‑King comes to reign!]{.class_4257}

The third gift is unique. Like the song says: "Myrrh is mine, it's bitter perfume, breathes a life of gathering gloom. Sorrowing, sighing, bleeding, dying, sealed in the stone‑cold tomb." Myrrh is a Bible "embalming fluid" (Mark 16:1; John 12:3--7, 19:39), and it is associated closely with Christ Jesus (Song of Sol. 5:5). We are left to some conjecture about the office it represents, but no one who has read the "weeping prophet" Jeremiah fails to see the significance. [Jesus' first coming, to die, was in the office of a Prophet ]{.class_4257}(Deut. 18:15--17; Matt. 21:11). There is not too much conjecture. This [Man is a King‑Priest‑Prophet.]{.class_4257}
