The myrrh, given here for His prophetic office, comes from a trifoliated Abyssinian plant. The bruised tree produced a high‑priced gum which is used for the perfume. Myrrh is the first and last gift that is given to Christ during His first coming as Prophet []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_37}(Mark 15:23).

2:12 And being warned of God in a dream that they should not return to Herod, they departed into their own country another way.

13 And when they were departed, behold, the angel of the Lord appeareth to Joseph in a dream, saying, Arise, and take the young child and his mother, and flee into Egypt, and be thou there until I bring thee word: for Herod will seek the young child to destroy him.

On both of these occasions an angel reveals God's will in a dream. This is a consistent method of revelation in the Old Testament (Gen. 20:3, 28:12), and it is the common method, in contrast to the direct method of revelation given to Moses (Num. 12:6).

Again, we observe the wording ["young child," ]{.class_4258}first, and the mother next. The Holy Spirit is continuing to keep the reader's mind on Christ, and not on His mother. A difficulty is involved in the appearance of the angel of the Lord---who is said to be Christ (Gal. 4:14; Acts 27:23; Gen. 32:30), while Christ Himself is present as a child. But the situation is not strange when we keep constantly before us the Bible use of the word "angel"; again, it is not merely a [messenger, but an appearance. ]{.class_4257}An appearance of the Lord is not the Lord Himself. Study this distinction in the appearance of God to Moses, in the bush, [by an angel ]{.class_4257}(Gal. 3:19--20; Acts 7:30; Exod. 3:2).

2:14 When he arose, he took the young child and his mother by night, and departed into Egypt:

15 And was there until the death of Herod: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken of the Lord by the prophet, saying, Out of Egypt have I called my son.

The quotation is from Hosea 11:1, where the historical reference is to Israel. We learn then from this application that a verse can have more than one meaning. Historically, Hosea is referring to a past event, but doctrinally, he is referring to [a future event. ]{.class_4257}Inspirationally, we have a teaching on the love of God. This threefold application is the key to the divisions in the Body of Christ. [History, doctrine, ]{.class_4257}and [inspiration ]{.class_4257}can be found in every verse, but to confuse these and teach [doctrinally, ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_38}something that is only true inspirationally, sabotages the purpose of the Holy Spirit. This will be studied more when we take up the Sermon on the Mount.

The constant allusions to the Exodus from Egypt points out to the believer that the event must have some tremendous [doctrinal ]{.class_4262}significance. Moses is called out of Egypt, Aaron is called out, Israel is called out, Christ is called out, and the "church" or ["ekklesia" ]{.class_4262}(Gk.) is a "called‑out" assembly. All believers are familiar with the fact that, [spiritually, ]{.class_4262}Egypt represents the world or world system, controlled by Satan (Rev. 11:8; Heb. 3:16; Matt. 4:1--8; Luke 4:5--7). Numerous sermons have been built on the idea that the Exodus represents salvation (Exod. 12), water baptism (1 Cor. 10:1--3), deliverance from Satan (Exod. 13--14), the pilgrim life on earth in the "wilderness" (Exodus--Numbers), and the crossing of Death (Jordan---Josh. 2--4) into life, while pointing out that Palestine was [a battlefield, ]{.class_4262}not Heaven, even after the children of Israel entered. This all makes fine preaching. But what is the [doctrinal ]{.class_4262}application? Don't forget, every verse in the Bible has [three applications: ]{.class_4262}Historical, Doctrinal, and Inspirational. Without going into a detailed exegesis, the verses that the believer will need to study to work this thing out are as follows: Exodus 14:13, 15:3, 10, 14; Psalm 68:1, 17, 21--24; Habakkuk 3:15--18; 1 Thessalonians 4; 1 Corinthians 15:50--54; Psalm 148:4; 2 Kings 2:8--16. If these verses are carefully and prayerfully studied, you will find that the Exodus is a type of a yet great future [exodus, ]{.class_4262}which has not yet taken place. God foresees something, yet unrevealed to unregenerate "science." He foresees the [exodus ]{.class_4262}of millions of people off this planet, through the universe, through a [layer of water above ]{.class_4262}the firmament (see Commentary on Genesis, Gen. 1:1--4), and into His presence. It is fitting that the Author and Finisher of their faith should be called[ "out of Egypt."]{.class_4258}

2:16 Then Herod, when he saw that he was mocked of the wise men, was exceeding wroth, and sent forth, and slew all the children that were in Bethlehem, and in all the coasts thereof, from two years old and under, according to the time which he had diligently inquired of the wise men.

This act, one of the cruelest recorded in history, is typical of "Roman Peace," and finds it parallel in []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_39}[Foxe's Book of Martyrs,]{.class_4257} where Roman atrocities surpass Communist persecutions as far as the Pacific exceeds the Atlantic. One can imagine the screaming mothers trying to hide their babies and protect them, and there is no doubt that when the babies were murdered, many a mother and father lay beside them, slaughtered by soldiers who were unable to carry out the execution without killing the parents. The age limit is given at [two years, ]{.class_4257}which helps us to understand that the Lord Jesus was well over six months old at this time. He may not have been two years old, as Herod would have allowed a little "leeway" to make sure he snuffed out the life of the "king"; six months to eighteen months would be the probable age of Christ at this time. However, the king had enquired [diligently ]{.class_4257}(Matt 2:7, 16), and his age bracket may have been more accurate, in which case one year to two years old would be the correct age.

2:17 Then was fulfilled that which was spoken by Jeremy the prophet, saying,

18 In Rama was there a voice heard, lamentation, and weeping, and great mourning, Rachel weeping for her children, and would not be comforted, because they are not.

Herod's act was evidently anticipated by Jeremiah the prophet. The act shows again the old adage that "the mountains of worldly honor are always covered with a perpetual snow." Herod, though a king, could not sleep until he'd killed a few babies. We find Henry II in a rage at Thomas á Becket, and we find Napoleon tormented in old‑age by a disease he contracted from using too much snuff. Alexander dies in a drunken rage because he can't find any more land to conquer. Haman can't sleep because a Jew won't say "Hello" to him, and Ahab can't eat because a man owns a vineyard that he covets! Herod can't sleep because an unknown [baby, ]{.class_4257}or young child, [somewhere, is ]{.class_4257}marching to the throne across his pillow.

The quotation is from Jeremiah 31:15, and Matthew, by the Holy Spirit, gives a free quotation. To reconcile these differences in New Testament and Old Testament texts, the scholars have hit upon the ingenuous idea of supposing the New Testament writers are quoting [a Greek translation of the Old Testament ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_40}(the LXX), and not the Hebrew "originals." However, this type of tomfoolery fails completely on a number of comparisons, and it elevates the corrupt manuscripts of Eusebius and Origen to an undue place of prominence. \[Aleph, B, and other "Hesychian" or "Egyptian" manuscripts (which are the final authority in all modern translations) contain such tripe as "Bel and the Dragon," "The Shepherd of Hermas," the "Epistle to Barnabas," and "Judith," etc., [which were never accepted by any orthodox Jew as being part of inspired scripture at any time]{.class_4257}.\] It is stretching things a little too far to suppose Matthew had the reverence for these North African "Latin" corruptions which Jerome, Eusebius, Westcott and Hort, Alford, Trench, and Tischendorf manifest.

Taking up the text, the quotation in Jeremiah has an application to the Second Advent (and we will find the further we get into Matthew how the setup is an ideal setup for the fulfilling of the prophecies in the Old Testament on the "Day of the Lord"), as well as the first. Notice, Jeremiah 31:13, 21. Rachel is involved, as she dies at the exact location of Bethlehem (Gen. 35:16--19). The word ["Rama"]{.class_4258} means a "high place," spelled[ "Ramah"]{.class_4258} in Jeremiah 31. If the "Rachel" means Rachel literally, we have here an indication that the departed know what is going on [on this earth, ]{.class_4257}for the children are said to have [representative appearances in heaven ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 18:10). However, this is squeezing too much from a verse, for Rachel at this time is [not in heaven; ]{.class_4257}she is in Paradise (note Luke 16:19--22, 23:43; Heb. 10:4). Old Testament saints were forgiven, but not "cleared" (Exod. 34:6--7), and consequently, we find them in an intermediate state called "Abraham's bosom" until the resurrection of Christ (see Matt. 27:50--53). The use of Rachel, here, is undoubtedly figurative, picturing [all the women in Bethlehem ]{.class_4257}by their ancestor, Rachel.

2:19 But when Herod was dead, behold, an angel of the Lord appeareth in a dream to Joseph in Egypt,

20 Saying, Arise, and take the young child and his mother, and go into the land of Israel: for they are dead which sought the young child's life.

For the dream, note verses 12--13. Again the ["young child"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_41} is placed ahead of his mother---see verses 11, 13--14, 21. ["The land of Israel"]{.class_4258} has now become synonymous with the whole land of Palestine, and not the ten Northern Tribes only, for if it were the ten Northern Tribes only, then Joseph [did ]{.class_4257}go into the land of Israel. However, the next verses (vss. 21--22) say that he did not go into the land of Israel, but went to Galilee. Galilee is in the ten Northern Tribes (2 Kings 14:25; Josh. 19:13). Dan, Asher, and Naphtali all had land north of this point. There is a curious notation in Isaiah 9:1, which should be kept in mind in dealing with Galilee. The statement is ["Galilee of the nations"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}(Gentiles). It is significant for a number of reasons, one of which is the appearance of Greek proselytes from this region to see Jesus [before His death ]{.class_4257}(John 12:20--21).

2:21 And he arose, and took the young child and his mother, and came into the land of Israel.

22 But when he heard that Archelaus did reign in Judaea in the room of his father Herod, he was afraid to go thither: notwithstanding, being warned of God in a dream, he turned aside into the parts of Galilee:

Like father, like son. Joseph could certainly have no assurance that Herod's son was a better man than Herod, and on the other hand, he could be absolutely sure that Archelaus would be watching like a hawk for the appearance of any Jewish young man who professed to be a "king." Herod would not have died without giving a fair warning to his household, if they were in danger of losing the throne. For the ["dream"]{.class_4258} see notes on 2:12--13. Archelaus was the son of Herod's fourth wife. He was reared at Rome and banished (in A.D. 6) to Vienna, where he died.

The movement to Galilee signals something ominous for the "chosen people." Galilee is called "of the Gentiles" (nations) in Isaiah 9:1, so here we have another slight intimation that the coming "King" is not going to be limited to the House of Jacob in His rulership. Note that Luke limits it to Jews (Luke 1:33), in speaking of the time of Christ's rule, but Isaiah includes all when speaking of the territory over which he shall rule (Isa. 9:6--7). Already in Matthew, we find two veiled wordings which purport to go beyond Israel. The first is []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw197.xhtml_page_42}the mention of five Gentile women in the genealogy (see Chapter 1), and the second is the providential guidance of Joseph around Jerusalem and Judaea, into the northernmost province of Palestine, in close proximity to Tyre, Zidon, and Caesarea.

2:23 And he came and dwelt in a city called Nazareth: that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophets, He shall be called a Nazarene.

Undue criticism has been heaped on the verse, by failure of critical minds to observe the English text (also in the Greek), which says: ["which was SPOKEN"]{.class_4258}---not "WRITTEN." We find many scholars have trouble reading English. (An excellent example is the invention of a mythical "Book of Enoch" in Biblical times to satisfy the demands of Jude 14. These Catholic inventions are numerous, and all smack of the mystical, magical, extra­canonical inventions of men too stupid to believe what they read when they are able to read it. Nowhere does the Holy Spirit ever intimate that [Enoch wrote anything. ]{.class_4257}The "book of Jubilees" and other assorted rubbish found with the Isaiah scroll only prove conclusively that man will [believe anything ]{.class_4257}that will absolve him from believing the revelation that God has given him, [as it stands]{.class_4257}.)

The Greek is clear if a man is REALLY worried about it. (Most Greek‑men are not worried about it; they merely go to the Greek to [enforce ]{.class_4257}a tradition they have already been taught.) He says ["harthen" ]{.class_4257}in Matthew 2:23. The prophets [spoke ]{.class_4257}it; they did not write it. (Rome will produce it in writing, though, after a while. She has to, to keep before the public the eternal lie that there [are other authoritative decrees beside the sixty-six books of the Bible]{.class_4257}. Rome's hand is not hard to recognize in Protestant seminaries once you get used to her tricks.) "The Naza­rene" is found in the [Analytical Lexicon ]{.class_4257}to mean: "A Nazarite" (p. 275), in which case the reference is obvious. This Prophet (see Matt. 2:11) will fulfill the function of Numbers 6, and it is instructive to notice that [not one single time in His life is it ever said that He drank wine or grape juice! ]{.class_4257}The Pharisees accused Him of it (Matt. 11:19), and He offered it at the Supper, but notice the words to the disciples for [THEM to drink of it ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 26:27).

When the wine and myrrh of Proverbs 31:4--6 was offered to Him on the cross (Mark 15:23), [He did not take it.]{.class_4257}

Perhaps, however, this is stretching a point too far. If He were a Nazarite, He could have had access to new wine under certain conditions (note Num. 6:20). If the reference is not to a Nazarite, the chances are the prophets spoke of His residence verbally, in their prophesying. There are some attempts by commentators to make the Nazarene be another wording of the root word for "branch," a ["netzer" ]{.class_4257}(Hebrew); there may be some connection, as Isaiah the prophet (in the great passage on the Second Coming, Isa. 11:1--11) refers to the Lord Jesus with this word. Although a different word is used for ["the Branch" ]{.class_4258}in the other passages referring to Christ, the analogies are fantastic; for in Jeremiah 23:5 we have [a Branch presented as a Jewish "King" for Matthew, ]{.class_4257}in Zechariah 6:12--13 we have[ a Branch presented as the "Son of Man" for Luke, ]{.class_4257}and in Isaiah 4:2 we have [a Branch presented as "the Son of God" for John! ]{.class_4257}With an occasional minor help in Hebrew and Greek we can find the full, rich, truths of the plain English even easier.

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml}

# Chapter 3 {.heading_84-0}

3:1 In those days came John the Baptist, preaching in the wilderness of Judaea,

2 And saying, Repent ye: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.

The[ "in those days"]{.class_4258} can be dated within six months. The entire information is given in Luke 3:1--2. Augustus Caesar died August 29, 767. (John, here, has to be thirty years old to begin his work, his birth being six months before Jesus.) Tiberias Caesar had a joint-reign with Augustus two years, which would give him thirteen years beyond the date of Augustus' death in 767. This would bring us to the Roman year 781. It would also give us the date of John's birth in the Roman year of 751, which would date within six months the birth of Jesus Christ. \[Further details may be obtained in Lindberg's [Christian Dogmatics, ]{.class_4257}p. 392.\]

["The Baptist"]{.class_4258} introduces a curious note to the narrative. The word about which there has been endless argument, signifies "to DIP," as in dyeing materials in a tub. The way the word is used in the Bible signifies its doctrinal meaning (see Matt. 3:16). Baptism is found in the Old Testament, both in sprinkling, dipping, and immersing. The references are Heb. 6:1--2; Mark 7:4, the word "washing" being baptismous, Heb. 9:19; Ezek. 36:25; 2 Kings 5; 1 Cor. 10:1--3.

["Repent ye: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand."]{.class_4258} At this juncture, 85 percent of the colleges and seminaries step off the gangplank into the flood and never bob up again. ["The kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} is the weight that drowns the swimmers. ["The kingdom of heaven,"]{.class_4258} in Matthew, is the grand bulldozer that shoves every student and scholar off into the bushes, unless he can find the controls. The literature on it is so extensive it could not even be listed. The perversions of the doctrine are so numerous it would be a waste of time to study them, and the two camps of thought on the subject are so clearly divided that reconciliation is hopeless, unless there is a man somewhere that will believe what he reads, the way it stands.

Now, before considering the doctrine and inspiration contained in this passage (and remember, every verse has three applications), let us consider the history. Here comes a Judaean-Jew, out of the Judaean wilderness, with no New Testament and no knowledge of the New Birth, the Crucifixion, or the Resurrection (other than what God showed him in the Old Testament). This Judaean-Jew does not come preaching the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ (1 Cor. 15:1--4), and he plainly states his mission is to manifest Christ to Israel, not to the Church or to the Gentiles (John 1:31).

Let us get our bearings before we hoist sail into the wind (Eph. 4:14). (If a man breaks his theological noodle, he will break it in Matthew, Acts, or Hebrews.) We are in Matthew. John is preaching a Kingdom. The Kingdom he is preaching is so plain to any Bible believer, who has enough sense to read, that no one could mistake it. You will find the first "king" in Genesis 2 and his dominion outlined. You will find how he lost that "kingdom" in Genesis 3. You will learn who took it over from there on in Luke 4:6--7. You will find it controlled by seven kings under Satan (Rev. 12:1--4), and they are all listed in order: Nimrod, Pharaoh, Sennacherib, Nebuchadnezzar, Darius, Alexander, and Caesar. You will find God giving sole dominion to Noah, after the flood, and you will find God calling Abraham out to make him the progenitor of two lines of people: a spiritual seed (Rom. 4), who will be born into the Kingdom of God (John 3:3), and a literal seed, which shall inherit Palestine (Gal. 3; Amos 9:15; Isa. 55:12; Joel 2:19--24; Deut. 28:10). \[See [Commentary on Genesis,]{.class_4257} Gen. 12:1--4.\] Suffice it to say that at this point in Matthew we reach a crucial stage in understanding the Bible. You can't get the Bible right till you get the Jew in his right place, and the Kingdom promised the Jew (Luke 1:32--33) is either going to come or it isn't. The kingdom was convenanted in 2 Samuel 7:11; it was prophesied in Jeremiah 23:5; it is announced in our text as ["at hand."]{.class_4258} Up until now, there is not the slightest hint that it is anything but Jewish, unless there is an occasional reference of blessing to the Gentile through the Jew (Gen. 12:1--4; Isa. 56:3--8, 49:5--13). The church is not involved in any way, shape, or form (Eph. 3--4), and to equate this Kingdom with the Gentile-ecclesiastical-political setup of apostate Romanism and Protestantism is a stunt impossible for a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_46}sideshow juggler.

[The Theocratic Kingdom,]{.class_4257} by Peters (Funk and Wagnall, 1884), is a thorough and exhaustive study of the subject, going far beyond "kingdom-scholars" in either Fundamentalist or Modernist camp. The crux of the matter is simple. Either God meant what He said to Israel in the Old Testament and will fulfill it in the Day of the Lord at the Second Coming, or He was teaching spiritual "parallels" like a "saton" in Buddhism and is gradually working it out, invisibly, through the Church instead of Israel. This is the heart of the matter and further discussion is useless. You either believe it or you don't. When a man rejects the Book of Revelation, he loses the key that unlocks the prophecies in the Old Testament on "the Day of the Lord." And when he loses this key, he drops the whole ring, for the Book of Acts begins with this prophecy (Acts 2:16--20), and the Book of Matthew, until Matthew 13, proceeds as though the day were ["AT HAND."]{.class_4258} One by one, we observe the pitiful spectacle of unbelieving religious leaders driving their flocks to the pit by their unbelief in these passages. The Campbellite hits Acts 2:38 and perishes there without even looking at the context. The Catholic gags on Matthew 16:16--19 and chokes to death on Matthew 18:34 (purgatory). In Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews, God neatly decapitates the unscriptural heads of every man who refuses to believe what God said about Israel in the Old Testament, as revealed by the Book of Revelation, in the New.

If John's ["kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} is not located, exactly, immediately, in its proper place, then the remainder of Matthew is unintelligible. No Bible believer has any trouble locating it, for it is already located for him (Dan. 2:44--45, 7:13, 27). In the first passage we are told that ["the dream is certain, and the interpretation thereof sure";]{.class_4258} there is nothing "figurative" or "apocalyptic" about it (2 Pet. 2:18--20) .

To make this literal, Messianic, Jewish kingdom a spiritual effusion of "churchanity" is to collapse the Empire State Building into a match box. The alibi of the postmillennialist and amillennialist is pitiful. Their alibi is that since the thing did not come to pass literally at the First Advent, that is proof it is spiritual and never will happen literally!

And here we must leave our subject, for the proof that it will happen literally is found in 300 verses in the New Testament and 570 in the Old Testament. We cannot wait for unbelieving scholars to rightly divide the word to the point where they can distinguish between the two advents, for if the Old Testament prophets could not (1 Pet. 1:10--11), we can be certain that Dabney, Berkhof, Strong, Hodge, Matthew Henry, Lange, Ramm, Barth, Brunner, Kierkegaard, Tillich, Niebuhr, and Pope Johnny 234 3/4 can never make it. Let the reader return to a man who knows what he is talking about---Matthew the publican.

Matthew says that John proclaims the ["kingdom of heaven is at hand."]{.class_4258} The reader will find the identical references in Christ's first message (Matt. 4:17), and the import of the message is the "good news of the kingdom" (Matt. 4:23), not the "good news" of 1 Corinthians 15:1--4 and Galatians 1:4--10. The reader should rightly divide the "gospel" \[Moffatt, in keeping with postmillennial superstition, reads the Greek, "basileia" [(]{.class_4263}βασιλεί[α]{.class_4263}) in Matthew 3:2, to subtly imply that it is a spiritual operation, rather than a geographical dominion. The [RSV ]{.class_4257}acquiesces to this by making the dying thief say, "kingly power" (Luke 23:42), instead of ["thy kingdom."]{.class_4258} Neither reading is scholarly, neither is accurate, neither is correct, and neither is true to the body of revelation. Both readings (Moffatt and the [RSV]{.class_4257}) are true to the postmillennial-church-kingdom theory of Constantine and Augustine in the Roman church.\]

3:3 For this is he that was spoken of by the prophet Esaias, saying, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.

The citation is from Isaiah 40:3--8. Luke 3:4 says that these are the words of Isaiah (contrary to much of the literature still published exalting the outmoded "second Isaiah" theory). That Isaiah wrote the passages in Isaiah 40--66 is evident by the fact that the New Testament writers (with more accurate information and access to more authoritative manuscripts than modern "scholars") cite Isaiah as author of a dozen passages in the section---Romans 10:16, 20; Matthew 3:3, 12:17; Luke 4:18, etc. Many seminaries in the United []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_48}States still teach as FACT (like Darwin's theory) the archaic and often disproved "Deutero-Isaiah" theory---the theory that an "unknown" wrote Isaiah 40--66 and lied about the authorship. The attempt, of course, is to unseat the prophecies about Jesus in Isaiah 40. It is a little too obvious and crude for a Bible believer to swallow. Why not make Isaiah 1--39 a "second Isaiah"? Why pick on the blood atonement (Isa. 53)!?

Matthew 3:3 is given in other words by Mark in Mark 1:2--3. Here again we see the deft manipulation of the scholars in an effort to destroy faith in the word of God. Moffatt renders "Isaiah the prophet" right in the middle of Mark 1:2, cutting out the plural ["prophets."]{.class_4258} This is a bad mistake, however, for one more prophet, besides Isaiah, is represented in the quotation. The quotation is not merely from Isaiah 40, but also from Malachi 3:1, and the correct reading is the Received Text of the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611: ["prophets,"]{.class_4258} not "the prophet Isaiah." \[This textual corruption is found in Nestle's Greek text, representing the Hesychian (Egyptian) text of the Catholic-African church.\]

3:4 And the same John had his raiment of camel's hair, and a leathern girdle about his loins; and his meat was locusts and wild honey.

The reader is struck by the similarity of John's appearance to that of Elijah the prophet (2 Kings 1:8). Now, keeping in mind that up until here the conditions are coming to pass to fulfill the Day of the Lord, prophesied by Joel (Acts 2), we should here find an exact type of Elijah, who will herald the Second Coming. Hence, the double reference in Mark 1:1--3 to ISAIAH AND MALACHI, for Malachi 4:5--6 is plain on Elijah's appearance. Jesus confirms this in Matthew 17:11, but adds hastily that John the Baptist can fulfill this prophecy under certain conditions (Matt. 17:12). It must be kept in mind the fact that up until here there is no intimation or revelation of a Church Age at all. In Matthew 3, we are sailing head-on into the fulfillment of the Old Testament prophets, previous to a literal, Messianic Kingdom; anything else "read into the text" will confuse the exegesis and exposition. John's office is distinctively different from Elijah's (compare Luke 1:16--17 with Mal. 4:5--6), yet both are []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_49}beheaded (Rev. 11:6, 8, 20:4; Mark 6:27); both are forerunners of Christ; both dress alike and; both face a "type" of the Antichrist. For all practical purposes, if the Jews had received their Messiah and His "kingdom," all the prophecies in regard to the First and Second Coming in the Old Testament could have been fulfilled. In Acts 7 (and look out for Acts!), this offer is made. All the conditions have been met, in TYPE. It is true that the crucifixion and resurrection had to take place (Psa. 22; Isa. 53), but once this has happened, the kingdom can come immediately to Israel---read Acts 2:22--23, 36, 3:18--26; and Matthew 10:23. Failure to recognize this crucial Bible teaching will forever close three books to the student---MATTHEW, ACTS, and HEBREWS. John, in our text, is heralding the first coming as things work out, but for all practical purposes and on the knowledge he has (that God had given), he is heralding a literal king for a literal kingdom, which is ["at hand."]{.class_4258}

3:5 Then went out to him Jerusalem, and all Judaea, and all the region round about Jordan,

6 And were baptized of him in Jordan, confessing their sins.

The expression ["in Jordan"]{.class_4258} is a strong implication that they went into the river for the ceremony. They were not sprinkled "BY" Jordan. ["Confessing their sins"]{.class_4258} carries with it tremendous implications. In the first place it gives the idea that "confessing of sins" has something to do with salvation. This idea is carried out at length in the Roman church until it becomes a condition for keeping salvation. Failure to observe the Bible teaching on "confessing of sins" leads not only to this perversion, but to subsequent "penance," "contrition," "absolution," and God knows what.

Now, there is a New Testament passage that makes it plain that God forgives and cleanses on the confession of sin (1 John 1:9), but failure to observe the context again leads to disaster. For this person, confessing has a basis already on which God can forgive them (1 John 2:1--2); furthermore, if they do not confess, they are chastened (1 Cor. 11:30--33; Heb. 12:4--8), not "sent to hell."

Simply confessing, repenting, and making restitution doesn't accomplish anything in connection with salvation, in the Bible; for []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_50}here the man Judas does just that, and does it to a priest (Matt. 27:3--4), and goes to hell (John 17:12; Acts 1:25). (Technically, the "bottomless" pit.)

Notice how the confession and remission of sins is here connected with water baptism (Mark 1:4), and observe further how that, long before any New Testament books were in print, the Jews are still following this theology in the Book of Acts. Ananias speaks of it to Paul on his conversion in Acts 9 (recounted in Acts 22:16). Peter continues to preach it in Acts 2:38, as though the water were an agency in the cleansing of sin. That this could be natural is apparent, for until Acts 8--9 there are no New Testament books in print and every Jew is still going by the rituals described in Hebrews 9:13, 19. Note how clearly this is brought out about the time of Jesus' baptism, where the Jews are worried about the relation of water to "PURIFYING"! (John 3:25--26). We still have these Old Testament "Judaizers" in our midst. They stunted their growth in Matthew 3 and never developed beyond the stage of a dwarfed Pharisee. They still persecute and harass the saved and lost alike with compressed and powerful blasts of hot air, to the effect that water baptism is "essential" to salvation. They never learned even what Peter learned in Acts 10, and they never followed Peter through Acts to find out the New Testament condition of salvation (Acts 15:11). (If you break your neck, you'll bust it in Matthew, Acts, or Hebrews!)

3:7 But when he saw many of the Pharisees and Sadducees come to his baptism, he said unto them, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come?

There can be no doubt about the ["wrath to come."]{.class_4258} John is speaking from Malachi 3:2--5 and Malachi 4:1--6. He is speaking of the Second Coming. (The thought then is: "Well, he was wrong." No, he was not wrong. He was going by what God had given till this point, and that is what God expects any man to do. God could have worked it out up until Acts 7. (Keep your eye on Matthew and Acts carefully.)

The ["generation of vipers"]{.class_4258} is suggestive. The references will []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_51}be found in Proverbs 30:11--13; Job 20:16; Psalm 58:4; and Micah 7:17. Each reference will be found near a passage that speaks of the Second Coming in Glory and the time of "Jacob's troubles."

3:8 Bring forth therefore fruits meet for repentance:

9 And think not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, that God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham.

["Meet for repentance"]{.class_4258} implies something "equal to," or "matching." When two things come up to meet each other, they are equal (Gen. 2:20). If the fruit cannot be produced, it is proof the tree is rotten (vs. 10, Matt. 7:17--20). Martin Luther used to say: "If you don't know what the fruit is and you can't see it, shake the tree and you'll see it." ["Fruits"]{.class_4258} in this case (which prove real repentance) are described in the list in Luke 3:8--14. Again, we find the Roman custom of the priest prescribing "what penance to do," to manifest real "contrition." Rome seldom gets out of the Old Testament times into the Pauline epistles; she has a "legalistic fixation." John reads some thoughts when he warns them about bragging on Abraham, for they say this very thing to Jesus (in spite of the warning) in John 8:33, ["We be Abraham's seed"]{.class_4258}! The covenant theology of the Pharisees was their alibi for rejecting the New Testament. "The faith of our Fathers" is sometimes a "stinking mess" instead of a "spiritual blessing."

3:10 And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: therefore every tree which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire.

The ["trees,"]{.class_4258} mentioned, can refer to individuals as in Matthew 7:17--19, or they can refer to the nation of Israel in regard to other nations as in Luke 21:29--30. Either way, the threat is the same. Satan (who has the power of death) is addressed in the person of the King of Babylon in Jeremiah 51, and there he is called God's axe (Jer. 51:20). As such, he is in a measure responsible for what took place the night before the crucifixion, for in Jeremiah 51:23 we find the cross-reference to Zechariah 13:7, which Jesus quotes the night of His betrayal (Matt. 26:31). When the ["axe"]{.class_4258} is laid to the roots, down []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_52}goes the tree. That John may have a reference to Israel is obvious, for in Luke 13:8--9 we have Christ's confirmation of the impending rejection of Israel, and in Romans 11 we have a detailed description of the same thing. (Isaiah 27:11 has a curious reference to the rejection of Israel in regard to people being "burned in the fire." The same thing is found in Mal. 4:1--2.)

3:11 I indeed baptize you with water unto repentance: but he that cometh after me is mightier than I, whose shoes I am not worthy to bear: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost, and with fire:

12 Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and gather his wheat into the garner; but he will burn up the chaff with unquenchable fire.

The passage is of great importance, for it outlines John's ministry and some of its relationship to Jesus Christ; it also accurately foretells the termination of events before the Second Advent. Comparing Scripture with Scripture, which is the proper method of Bible study (1 Cor. 2:13), we find that John's water baptism unto "repentance" is primarily a Jewish ministry. Even Christ Himself is said to be first ["a minister of the circumcision"]{.class_4258} (Rom 15:8), and John declares his baptism has nothing to do with New Testament salvation or the New Birth; it is to "manifest Christ to Israel" (John 1:31). Surely John's word about his ministry is of much greater importance than the private interpretations of the Catholic and Campbellite dignitaries.

John is careful to contrast his water baptism ["unto repentance"]{.class_4258} with Jesus Christ's "baptism of the Holy Ghost unto salvation." Notice that at all future dates, Christ never baptizes anyone in water (John 4:2), and none of the apostles but Paul are baptized in water under the New Covenant. When Paul is baptized, he is baptized by a Jew before any New Testament books are in print, and he follows the Old Testament "purification" idea taught in Leviticus and Numbers (Acts 22:17--18).

The verse (Matt. 3:11) is interesting because it clearly defines three separate baptisms---a thought all students should ponder before they try to define the ["ONE BAPTISM"]{.class_4258} of Ephesians 4:4. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_53}There are seven baptisms given in the New Testament: three here, one in 1 Corinthians 10:1--3, one in Matthew 20:22, one in 1 Corinthians 12:13, and one in Matthew 28:19. Since the one in 1 Corinthians 12:13 may be equated with the baptism of the Holy Ghost here, the seventh baptism will be found in 1 Peter 3:21. The peculiar obsession that makes people say "WATER" everytime they see the word "BAPTISM" (and vice-versa) is another tremendous testimony to the power of Rome to insert her superstitious decrees into the body of divinity of any church.

Careful attention needs to be paid to the expression ["and with fire"]{.class_4258} near the end of verse 11. We often hear it misquoted as the "baptism of the Holy Ghost and fire," but this is a tragic error. I say tragic, for the ["FIRE"]{.class_4258} part of the baptism is defined in the next verse as the wrath of God! This is hardly a baptism to pray for, or to be desired! The mistaken people who read it this way use as a prooftext the passage in Acts (Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews. That will do it!) where it reads: ["cloven tongues like as of fire"]{.class_4258} (Acts 2:3). But this is not fire, it is ["like as,"]{.class_4258} and not the real thing. Furthermore, wherever the baptism of the Holy Ghost is mentioned by name, in the Book of Acts, the expression ["and with fire"]{.class_4258} is always omitted (see Acts 1:5, 11:16).

Someone can spiritualize the passage and speak of a "fiery trial" for the Christian, but where John is speaking he can only have reference, directly, to Old Testament passages. Two such passages suit his speech admirably---Isaiah 4:4 and Malachi 3:2. The fire is the fire of God's wrath, historically, to take place at the Advent (2 Thess. 2:7--9)---doctrinally, referring to the Old Testament passages---inspirationally, referring to fiery testings or to punishment of the lost in hell.

The ["fan"]{.class_4258} of verse 12 will be the "winnowing fan" of Old Testament times and is used to separate the wheat from the chaff after the threshing machine has ground-out the grain. (A Bible dictionary will give the exact operation. The grain is thrown up into the air, and the wind blows away the chaff, leaving the grain only to fall on the floor \[Ruth 3:2\].)

The ["garner"]{.class_4258} of verse 12 is the same word used in 13:30 for the ["barn,"]{.class_4258} and Bible believers will recognize immediately in the use of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_54}the word, a reference to some kind of a rapture (Rev. 14:14, 16).

The passage then (vss. 11--12) gives a remarkable summary of the entire ministry of Jesus Christ. He comes to get them to repent (first eight words); He then ushers in the day of Grace (next twenty-six words); He then appears in glory and wrath (remainder of vs. 11), and before He does, there is a rapture (first twenty words in vs. 12); then His enemies are consumed at the Advent and cast into Hell (Matt. 25:41--46).

3:13 Then cometh Jesus from Galilee to Jordan unto John, to be baptized of him.

14 But John forbad him, saying, I have need to be baptized of thee, and comest thou to me?

15 And Jesus answering said unto him, Suffer it to be so now: for thus it becometh us to fulfil all righteousness. Then he suffered him.

John has a revelation that Jesus is coming to Him (John 1:33), and Jesus, of course, has His own orders from the Father to go to John (John 1:6--12). Notice that when John says that he has a need to be baptized by Jesus, he has already indicated that this baptism is not a water baptism, but a baptism of the Holy Spirit. Perhaps John is implying that Jesus should baptize him in water, but the context would question the meaning. The word ["suffer"]{.class_4258} here is an excellent word, which anyone understands as meaning to "allow." If you "put up with something" you allow it, and you "suffer" from "puttin' up with it." The common American can solve many problems obscure in the Greek. The transition from SUFFER in 1611 English, to American of 1960, is not so great that the "poor common people" can't "understand their Bibles." We have only to look at the absurdities and fatuities in an [RSV ]{.class_4257}to see such an alibi is a lame one for changing the [Authorized Version.]{.class_4257} Imagine "slime of the purslane" ([RSV]{.class_4257}) for ["white of an egg"]{.class_4258} ([AV]{.class_4257}); tut! tut! (Job 6:6).

["For thus it becometh us to fulfill all righteousness."]{.class_4258} The words indicate the purpose of WATER baptism. Clearly it is not absolutely necessary, since Jesus had no sins to confess and no need of repenting. However, for the sake of manifesting Himself to Israel, He submits himself to the ceremony. The act is significant, for it []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_55}shows that water baptism after the resurrection has a spiritual meaning not associated with literal Jewish purification. (Simon Peter doesn't learn this till Acts 10, but God interrupts his message to show him that water baptism is not essential for ["remission of sins"]{.class_4258} \[see Acts 10:44, 47\].) In our dispensation where the believer is ["in Christ,"]{.class_4258} it is more than apparent that he is in someone who has already fulfilled all righteousness (Rom. 10:1--4). The believer's water baptism, then, is not essential to salvation, but is a ceremony performed to manifest the believer's unity in Christ, publicly (Rom. 6:1--6); thus, his baptism in water is said to be a ["figure,"]{.class_4258} and not salvation itself (1 Pet. 3:21).

3:16 And Jesus, when he was baptized, went up straightway out of the water: and, lo, the heavens were opened unto him, and he saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove, and lighting upon him:

17 And lo a voice from heaven, saying, This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.

The phrase ["went up straightway out of the water"]{.class_4258} nearly fixes the meaning of this particular baptism as an "immersion." Throughout the Old Testament the picture is there (Psa. 42:7, 88:5--7, 66:12), and it becomes so obvious in the book of Jonah that little doubt is left. Jonah, as a type of Christ, is submerged and buried in water (Jonah 2). The fact that some denominational leaders refuse to recognize the historicity of Jonah, or "fight" over the apparent teaching of the type, is of no consequence whatsoever to a man who is earnestly desiring to know the truth. Baptism is BURIAL, where it pictures the identification of the Believer with Christ (Col. 2:12; Rom. 6:2--6); no amount of sophistry or "greekifying" of texts can erase the looming, preponderous, salient, obvious fact: baptism, where it pictures salvation, IS A BURIAL. We may argue about "pouring," "dipping," "sprinkling," and "dunking" in some other passages, but ["up straightway out of the water" ]{.class_4258}is a little too plain to misread if one has 20-20 vision.

There is a "Western reading" found in the Old Latin writing of the fourth century saying: ["and a great light shone around\...,"]{.class_4257} right before the sixteenth verse begins. A Latin manuscript from the ninth []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw285.xhtml_page_56}century has the same reading. The reading is of no consequence except that its absence from the Byzantine text of the Textus Receptus is one of the numerous proofs that Westcott and Hort didn't know what they were talking about when they imagined the Received Text was a compilation by editors of [earlier manuscripts.]{.class_4257} How they failed to put into their text this addition (and Westcott and Hort insist the Byzantine text was created to put in as many additions as possible) is a puzzle to which no one has yet been "clued-in."

Three persons in the Godhead appear in the verse. The [Father's]{.class_4257} voice is heard from heaven, as the [Spirit]{.class_4257} descends in bodily shape on the [Son,]{.class_4257} who is coming up out of Jordan. The ancient Cappadocian teaching of the three distinct persons has some basis for thought. The reader can find lengthy and exhaustive discussions of the Trinity in nearly any Systematic Theology. It will suffice to say that man is a Trichotomy (1 Thess. 5:23) created in the image of God, who has a Body---Jesus Christ, a Spirit---the Holy Spirit, and a Soul---the Father.

Surpassing the transcendentalism of Nirvana or Samahdi, God is able to [separate]{.class_4257} His constitution of Body, Soul, and Spirit so that all Three can appear in separate manifestations without losing their identity.

["This is my beloved Son"]{.class_4258} again places the Lord Jesus in a unique position occupied by no other man. The heavens open here for this voice, and this is not without significance, for the heavens open [three]{.class_4257} times in the Gospels: here, in Matthew 17:5, and in John 12:28. These three openings stand for [three]{.class_4257} "raptures" marked three times by the expression ["come up hither,"]{.class_4258} which occurs three times in the Bible: Revelation 4:1; Revelation 11:12; and Proverbs 25:7. If the student would like further study, he may investigate the three Jewish feasts in Deuteronomy 16:16 which mark these "raptures." The ["beloved Son"]{.class_4258} is a unique Son. He is the first Son born of God (Rom. 1:4) and the ["only begotten"]{.class_4258} in the sense that the New Birth is not mentioned until He appears as the first in a line of "sons" (Heb. 3:6). He is also the only-begotten in the sense that, although others after Him are "born of God" (John 1:13), they are not physically born of God. The best saints have human fathers according to the flesh, whereas Christ had only a mother (Heb. 1:6).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml}

# Chapter 4 {.heading_84-0}

4:1 Then was Jesus led up of the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted of the devil.

Since the Jews were taught to pray ["lead us not into temptation,"]{.class_4258} an inquiring mind would have the right to ask: "Why was Jesus led into it?" The answer is twofold, the most obvious being from the English text---He had to be tempted to fulfill the requirements of a Saviour (Heb. 2:16--17). The Greek, however, suggests a different solution. The word in Matthew 6:13 in the disciples' prayer is "eisenegkas" (εἰσενέγκῃς), while the one for "lead," here, is "anechthe" [(]{.class_4263}ἀνήχθ[η]{.class_4263}) Abraham was "tempted" (Gen. 22:1), but the "tempting" was not the tempting forbidden in James 1:12--14, but a "testing," as is clear from the English reading (Heb. 11:17), where the word ["tried"]{.class_4258} occurs.

At any rate the word "anechthe" (ἀνήχθη) means "to be conducted up," in distinction from "eisenengkas" [(]{.class_4263}εἰσενέγκῃ[ς]{.class_4263}) which carries more "pressure" with it. Tradition fixes the site of the temptation at Quarantaria in Judaea, but this is probably in error. The close association of Jesus with Elijah and Moses in a number of respects pretty well identifies the mountain as Sinai. Elijah and Jesus and Moses appear there together (Matt. 17), all of them fasted forty days and nights, all of them are mentioned in connection with the Second Coming (Mal. 4), all face a type of Antichrist (Ahab, Pharaoh, Herod), all have a succeeding minister (Elisha, Joshua, Paul---Deut. 34; 2 Kings 2), and where Elijah crosses is where Jesus was baptized (John 1:28) and where Joshua crossed! The Mount of Temptation is too hard to overlook; it is Mount Sinai where Christ will come again before He journeys up the "King's highway" to land on the Mount of Olives (Deut. 33:2; Judg. 5:4; Hab. 3:3; Num. 21:22).

Since the Bible (in either Testament) always takes for granted the literal, personal existence of Satan (the devil), the Bible believer should also. The references to his personality and work are too numerous to list. Refusal to admit his presence and his work in the world is to court disaster in the spiritual realm. Jesus refers to him constantly (Matt. 12:26, 16:23; Luke 10:18, etc.), and never refers to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_58}him as only an "influence" or "principle" (Matt. 25:41) of evil.

4:2 And when he had fasted forty days and forty nights, he was afterward an hungered.

The Bible student recognizes the "time of probation" given.

This is the time of the wilderness wandering, in years; it is the time that Goliath tested Israel in the valley of Elah; and strangely enough, it is the period from Eli to the division of the kingdom, coming in exact quantities of "forties." Eli is priest forty years, Samuel is judge forty years, Saul reigns forty years, David reigns forty years, and Solomon reigned forty years. ["Forty"]{.class_4258} stands in Scripture for "testing" and the expression "life begins at forty" is connected with the Scriptural truth. Elijah and Moses both spend forty days and nights without food when they appear before the Lord on the Mount.

Although Matthew reads: ["And when he had fasted forty days and forty nights"]{.class_4258}---giving the impression that the FIRST temptation occurs at the end of this period, Luke supplies an additional thought by saying: ["Being forty days tempted of the devil. And in those days he did eat nothing"]{.class_4258} (Luke 4:2). The three major temptations, then, come after a period of TEMPTING that has already continued forty days, during the fasting.

4:3 And when the tempter came to him, he said, If thou be the Son of God, command that these stones be made bread.

4 But he answered and said, It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God.

The verses are filled with doctrinal and spiritual meaning. ["If thou be\...."]{.class_4258} Satan, in keeping with his "scholarly" character, is a natural born skeptic. Every time he speaks within Scripture he is questioning (Gen. 3:1; Job 1--2; Matt. 4:3). An ["if"]{.class_4258} is found in all three speeches of Matthew 4, verses 3, 6, and 9. The Pharisees also represent this attitude of Satan toward the Lord's Christ---["If thou be the Son of God\..."]{.class_4258} (Matt. 27:40). An inquisitive and inquiring mind can be used of God if it is consecrated in full faith and belief, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_59}but left alone to the machinations of higher criticism and "enlightenment," it does nothing but mimic the founder of the "Yea-Hath-God-Said-Society" (Gen. 3:1).

["Command that these stones be made bread."]{.class_4258} Commentators miss the meaning of the statement. To underestimate the devil's ability and accuse him here of merely trying to take advantage of Christ's natural hunger is a slander on the devil. Moses got through this period three times without asking God for food (Deut. 9:9, 17--18, 25), and one can hardly suppose that the devil figured he was dealing with a man weaker than Moses. No, our commentators have overlooked something which is going to be overlooked if the book of Revelation is treated lightly. In Revelation 12:1--12, we find Israel (defined as Israel, not "Mary" or the "Church," in Gen. 37:9--10) being fed in the wilderness by supernatural means (compare Micah 7:14--15; Jer. 50:19--21; Ezek. 20:35; 1 Sam. 25:2).

The first temptation of Satan, then, is at least ten times as subtle as anyone has given him credit; it is not merely something Christ could have done---it was something the Scriptures commanded to do, but at the wrong time! A close study of the three temptations reveals that ALL of them bear this stamp. They are all Second Advent phenomena which the Scripture commands Christ to perform. Satan's temptation is so well aimed and so in line with Scripture that he reveals himself here as an adversary who can tempt you to do the right thing---at the wrong time.

This is a real revelation when applied to contemporary Christianity, for lo and behold, we find that 90 percent of the denominational schools, Catholics included, have all fallen "sucker" for this hook on the devil's trotline. They are busy "SPREADING A KINGDOM," pretending the reign of Christ is PRESENT, when it is yet future. This theology, patterned after Satanic fashion, manifests itself immediately in the actual lives of its adherents, for we find them busy reigning with crowns instead of traveling up mountains with crosses on their backs (Rom. 8:17; 2 Tim. 2:12).

The pope himself is a monument forever to the devil's power to deceive, and the temptations of Matthew 4 stand as evidence that the power of the Saviour far surpasses that of His followers to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_60}discern truth and error. The Modernists, with their everlasting harping on "our example," fail the first test, even before the words of Jesus are found in the New Testament. Jesus does not speak in the New Testament until Matthew 4:4, and when He does, He refuses the Second Advent privileges which accompany a crown. He takes the wilderness road with a cross.

["Man shall not live by bread alone."]{.class_4258} Every time that Christ answers the devil, He uses the "prooftext" method of refutation; that is, He quotes the Old Testament without amending the text. This is of tremendous importance, for it shows the huge gap between modern preaching and teaching and the correct example of "our example." Strangely enough, the liberals who yap the loudest about Christ as an Example are the last to follow Him in the matter of His attitude toward Scripture. Notice that Jesus uses the Old Testament constantly, without ever criticizing the translation or finding fault with the authorship! (Matt. 9:13, 12:5, 12:41--42, 15:7--8, 19:18, 21:16, 22:31, etc.). Especially in Matthew 15:7--8, Jesus "lets the cat out of the bag" concerning the religious leaders who foam at the mouth about "following our example," while they flatly reject to believe about the authority of Scripture. On all three temptations, Jesus answers: ["It is written."]{.class_4258} Since the word of God is a fire, hammer, and sword (Jer. 23:29; Heb. 4:12), you do not have to defend it---you are to use it to fight (Eph. 6:10--18)! Jesus uses it. ["But by every word\..."]{.class_4258} is a corollary to Job's lament that he had esteemed God's word more than his necessary food (Job 23:12).

4:5 Then the devil taketh him up into the holy city, and setteth him on a pinnacle of the temple,

6 And saith unto him, If thou be the Son of God, cast thyself down: for it is written, He shall give his angels charge concerning thee: and in their hands they shall bear thee up, lest at any time thou dash thy foot against a stone.

Again, the temptation concerns the Second Advent. Notice the reference to Christ coming down from the skies (Acts 1:11; Matt. 24:30) and the place of the Temple in this prophecy (Mal. 3:1). Again we repeat, if a man rejects the doctrine of the Second Advent as it is revealed in the book that is said to be the revelation of Jesus Christ []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_61}(Rev. 1:1), he is unable to unlock the Old Testament, and Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews are unintelligible without a proper understanding of the Old Testament. Satan even answers this time from the Old Testament. Not to be "outdone" by the Saviour, the devil quotes his own Scripture, and herein is a marvelous lesson: every false doctrine in the world is based on a verse of Scripture; all heresies appeal to Scripture for help.

With all that has been said, then, about the authority of Scripture, are we to faint at this point and draw back under the accusation that "everyone thinks they're right so how can you be sure? They think they're right too, and you don't have any corner on the market," etc. These outworn Catholic rebuttals to Bible truth need to be answered even in the twentieth century, and there is only one answer---["Study to shew thyself approved unto God, a workman that needeth not to be ashamed, rightly dividing the word of truth"]{.class_4258} (2 Tim. 2:15). A lazy or wicked man cannot find the truth; furthermore, God will send him a lie if he trifles with what truth he has (2 Thess. 2:10--12; Ezek. 14:2--11). In either Testament, the principle is the same. A man who will not diligently, sincerely, resolutely, and steadfastly search the Scripture has no chance whatsoever of finding out "who is right" and "who is wrong." He will die in the ignorant conceit of supposing that because he didn't criticize anyone else, the word will not criticize and condemn him---but it will (Heb. 4:13; John 12:48).

Now note: every heresy, every false doctrine, every perverted truth, every Roman superstition, every modernistic fable, comes from one of three ways of mishandling Scripture. The first way is by taking words out of the verse that belong there. The second way is by adding to the verse words that are not there. The third way is by taking the verse out of the immediate context in which it appears. In our passage (Matt. 4:6) Satan "shows his hand," and it is four short of an "inside straight." He quotes Psalm 91:11, but is careful to leave out ["in all thy ways."]{.class_4258} This "subtracting" from the word is the first sin ever committed on this earth, for if the reader will give his careful attention to Eve's misquoting of God's commandment (before she ate the fruit), he will find that she subtracts one word---["freely"]{.class_4258} (Gen. 2:16 with 3:2). The sin of the "salvation-by-works" crowd is []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_62}this sin. It is the "original sin," if you please. It is the sin of denying the free grace of God (Rom. 5:18).

Satan quotes, but he misquotes. He quotes Mark 16:16; Acts 2:38; Matthew 16:16--18; Hebrews 6:4--6, Hebrews 10:26--30; Galatians 3:26, and the Sermon on the Mount, but he is careful to add and subtract where necessary, and he assiduously removes the passages from their context: subtraction, addition, and distortion from context are the three standard, universal, and eternal means of man's self-damnation (2 Pet. 3:16).

4:7 Jesus said unto him, It is written again, Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God.

What a rebuke, in view of verse 1! God is not to be tempted, and yet here, God in the flesh (1 Tim. 3:16) is being tempted. Jesus answers from Deuteronomy 6:16. (He also answers the third temptation from Deut. 6:13.) The first temptation was answered from Deuteronomy 8:3, so we see that Jesus answers all of Satan's temptations from the Mosaic Law. ["Thou shalt not tempt the Lord"]{.class_4258} is illustrated well by the disobedience of Israel in Exodus 17:1--7.

4:8 Again, the devil taketh him up into an exceeding high mountain, and sheweth him all the kingdoms of the world, and the glory of them;

9 And saith unto him, All these things will I give thee, if thou wilt fall down and worship me.

The high mountain is not named, but it does not have to be high enough to view Asia, Europe, and Africa, for Luke 4:5 adds that He saw them in ["a moment of time."]{.class_4258} The vision then is supernatural, although modern television could produce the same affect in a few hours.

There is a ["mountain"]{.class_4258} in Scripture from which the entire physical globe could be seen (Heb. 12:22), but the "glory of the kingdoms" implies a "passing-in-review" before the eyes of the sum total of worldly honor.

Satan's offer to give the kingdoms to Christ is a bonafide offer, but again it is the wrong time. In Revelation 11:15, the correct time []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_63}transpires, but at the time of the temptation Jesus is looking for a CROSS, not a crown (see remarks on 4:3). That Satan is the temporary despot of this world is certainly true, for in Jeremiah 27:5--10 and Luke 4:6 the Holy Spirit records that God has given it to him, and also with it, the power of death (Heb. 2:14). Such a "world-view" of humanity and its "progress" puts an entirely different set of glasses on the believer's face than we find on the face of the "betterment-of-all" humanists who roll up their depraved sleeves with an optimistic grin and set in to tackle a job they don't understand and were not called upon to perform. As a ["god"]{.class_4258} (2 Cor 4:4), Satan wants WORSHIP. Failure to recognize this fact (and believe it) handicaps the "religious leaders" of the world from the start of the race to the impossible finish. It is no great thing that Satan here demands worship from "God in the flesh," for before the incarnation it took one member of the Trinity (the Father) to rebuke the devil, whereas one member (the Son) could not undertake it, until the incarnation---study Zechariah 3:1--2.

Satan's sphere then is primarily religious. The Bible believer should never lose sight of this fact. Although Communism and liquor may take their toll, it should never cloud the mind or brainwash the sincere believer into thinking that this is the devil's "domain." The devil's sphere of activity is religious. He is called an angel (2 Cor. 11), a prince (Eph. 2), a "christ" (Ezek. 28), a ["god"]{.class_4258} (2 Cor. 4), and a king (Job 41). He desires worship and will get it before the mess is over---see 2 Thessalonians 2:1--10. You will not find the devil in the liquor store or the juke joint or the dance hall---that is "the flesh." You will find Satan primarily interested in church councils, "business meetings," conclaves, conferences, ecumenical efforts, and "ex cathedras."

To sum up the temptations practically, it is obvious that the three represent the three of John's first epistle and the three confronting Eve in the garden.

1\. The lust of the flesh---the tree was ["good for food"]{.class_4258}---bread (1 John 2:16).

2\. The pride of life---desired to make one wise--- jump off the temple roof.

3\. The lust of the eye---pleasant to the eye (the fruit)---rule the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_64}kingdoms.

(The references are in 1 John 2:16 and Gen. 3:6.)

In the first temptation, Satan is asking you to distrust God and do it for yourself. In the second temptation, he is tempting you to presume on God and make God do something for you, and in the last temptation, he is asking you to abandon God and let the devil do something for you.

4:10 Then saith Jesus unto him, Get thee hence, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve.

The quotation is again from the Law (Deut. 6:13). Here, in His carnate state as a man, the Lord Jesus proves His Deity and also proves His sinless Humanity, for He rebukes the devil directly without referring him to the Father (as He did in Zech. 3:1--2). In a sense, until Christ had proved Himself in the flesh as a man, the devil "had a point" in his contention with God about the creation. This contention ends in Colossians 2:14--15 with a complete victory on Jesus Christ's behalf.

4:11 Then the devil leaveth him, and, behold, angels came and ministered unto him.

Mark 1:13 notes the ministry also and adds that during the time Jesus was in the wilderness that ["wild beasts"]{.class_4258} were with Him. The wild beasts can be literal, or they can represent the demons of Isaiah 34. The angels are specifically said to be ["ministering spirits sent forth to minister"]{.class_4258} (Heb. 1:13--14). We find specific instances in Acts 5:19 and Psalm 34:7, and the classic example is the ministry in Gethsemane (Luke 22:43); which, incidentally, is not found in the text of the pope's manuscript, [Vaticanus]{.class_4257} (A.D. 360), nor is it accepted by Origen (A.D. 184) the original Bible "translator."

4:12 Now when Jesus had heard that John was cast into prison, he departed into Galilee;

13 And leaving Nazareth, he came and dwelt in Capernaum, which is upon the sea coast, in the borders of Zabulon and Nephthalim:

14 That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet, saying,

15 The land of Zabulon, and the land of Nephthalim, by the way of the sea, beyond Jordan, Galilee of the Gentiles;

16 The people which sat in darkness saw great light; and to them which sat in the region and shadow of death light is sprung up.

The Lord Jesus now begins His public ministry. To get the order of events correctly in conjunction with the other gospel accounts, the student can avail himself of any study of the synoptic gospels (for example, A. T. Robertson's [Harmony of the Gospels]{.class_4257}). Capernaum is found on the northwest shore of Galilee, and Nazareth is about twenty miles southwest of it.

The quotation from Isaiah is interesting, for it takes the reader smack into the prophecies on the Second Advent. The quotation is from Isaiah 9:1--2, and only a slight study of the context points out both Advents. Note the Battle of Armageddon in Isaiah 9:5, and the famous Messianic verses 6 and 7, speaking of the victorious "Government" of Christ on the throne of David---not up in heaven. (Compare Luke 1:30--33; Matt. 25:31, 19:28; Ps. 102:16; Jer. 14:21.) The battle of Gideon with the Midianites (Isa. 9:4) is pointed out as a similitude or type of the last battle. Yet here, in Matthew 4, the Holy Spirit is pointing to a preliminary fulfillment at the First Coming. This is the kind of thing that throws all amillennial and postmillennial scholars off-base, and they never get back on till the ball game is over. Acts 2, with its tribulation prophecy from Joel, is another perfect example of the intricate, complex, and exacting organity of the word of God. It cannot be trifled with, and it cannot be limited to one application, and it will not be forced into a system that contradicts other verses.

Now, what can we find here in the line of spiritual application? The ["great light"]{.class_4258} must be the light of the world (John 1:4--9), and the ["shadow of death"]{.class_4258} must have something to do with man's sinful condition (Eph. 2:1--2); but to say this is the doctrinal meaning of the passage is a hasty and irreverent "hustling" of the Almighty. The "vexation" of Isaiah 9:1 has a definite reference to the time of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_66}Jacob's troubles (Rev. 6, 8, 16), and the context of Isaiah 8:22 speaks all too plainly of a time far ahead of Matthew 4, historically. (Scholars have had trouble with the ["not"]{.class_4258} in Isa. 9:3, which you will find changed from the [Authorized Version ]{.class_4257}to a positive statement: "hast increased their joy." This perversion is found in most of the newer "translations," but the [King James ]{.class_4257}["not"]{.class_4258} is the correct reading of Kittel's Hebrew text. The "positive" reading is a perversion found in Origen's "LXX" and the Syriac---the reading coming from Jonathan's Targum. Light on the verse, as usual, is found in the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 English of Isaiah 26:15, where the increase of the Jews \[previous to their conversion\] is not accompanied with joy, but with the sorrows of a travailing woman.)

4:17 From that time Jesus began to preach, and to say, Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.

Here is another important passage drawing the clear distinction between the New Testament gospel of Christ's accomplished work for sinners (1 Cor. 15:1--4) and the Messianic promise of Daniel 2:44, which is Jewish primarily. We have already remarked before about the futility of trying to twist this clear Old Testament teaching (and there are no New Testament books in print at the time of this declaration) to make it fit into the Catholic-African-world-political system under the "church." That the Kingdom of Heaven is not the church is so plain that a small child could see the difference in the spelling of the words. Jesus Christ, as a minister of the circumcision (Rom. 15:8), is heralding a literal, visible kingdom which is promised to Israel; so all understood it, defined it, and all believed it (Acts 1:2--8); all except the amillennial and postmillennial loggerheads of the twentieth century. To try to manufacture an ethereal effusion of vaporous "spirituality" and then jam it down our Lord's throat (when He speaks in Matt. 4:17) is certainly not common courtesy, if indeed it is not sheer blasphemy. Study carefully the notes on Matthew 3:2 in regard to this (Matt. 9:35, 16:19).

4:18 And Jesus, walking by the sea of Galilee, saw two brethren, Simon called Peter, and Andrew his brother, casting a net into the sea: for they were fishers.

The significance of the first disciples being "fishermen" is not apparent to the person who reads through Genesis 1:4--8 lightly. The Bible teaching of water beyond (northward) the galaxies and constellations is an "undiscovered" scientific truth which scientists may or may not discover later. That our Lord comes down through water to reach us is apparent by John the Baptist's ministry and the references to Jonah (Matt. 12:40), but the further teaching of an exodus up through that water to land on "shore" is clouded to the carnal believer. (For complete information, study carefully Eph. 3:18; Amos 9:6; Psa. 148:4; Job 9:8; Psa. 33:7, 68:33, 48:2, 75:6--7; Isa. 14:13; and Job 26:7, 38:30.)

These fishermen can be found (nearly as we find them here) in the Gulf Coast section from Panama City to Port St. Joe. Two kinds of nets are used in this section: a round throw net, varying from six to fourteen feet, and a large seine, or sweep net, sometimes running 400 yards. The Greek "sagana" of Matthew 13:47 is the seine used by fishermen off the boats to catch a school, and the Greek "amphiblastron" is the net thrown from the shoulder. This net is lined with lead weights; one weight is held between the front teeth (at least on the Gulf Coast where it is used primarily for mullet) and is thrown with a right-handed, backhand cast in shallow water.

Matthew writes from the future, already knowing Simon Peter's surname from the events which transpired in Matthew 16.

4:19 And he saith unto them, Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men.

20 And they straightway left their nets, and followed him.

There is some confusion in the minds of critics and scholars as to the reconciling of these events with those recorded in Luke 5:1--10 and John 1:40--42. (This is often the case with dense minds. They overcomplicate a simple situation because they have phobias about "oversimplification." Down South they say: "He can't see the woods for looking at the trees.")

That Luke 5 is a future calling is obvious, for here Peter already is calling Him "Master," and He has already healed Peter's mother-in-law (Luke 4:38), which Matthew does not place until Matthew 8:14. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_68}Again, it is apparent that this is so, for they forsake all in Luke 5:11 and not just their nets, as in Matthew 4:20. A blessed truth! You can follow Him without forsaking all, but all is the call to true discipleship! Mark's account (Mark 1:16--20) is the account in Matthew---the first call. Luke's is a deeper and closer test of "calling." John gives a detailed account of the exact personal nature of the first meeting of Matthew 4:18. Andrew had heard John the Baptist preach previous to this (John 1:40), and so when the Lord Jesus is walking by the shore, Andrew approaches, bringing his brother with him. The detail is a spiritual lesson teaching the duty of Christians to bring their "brothers" to Christ.

4:21 And going on from thence, he saw other two brethren, James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother, in a ship with Zebedee their father, mending their nets; and he called them.

22 And they immediately left the ship and their father, and followed him.

(Still dealing with the first calling, they are "mending their nets," not "washing them" as in Luke 5:2.) James and John, ["the sons of thunder,"]{.class_4258} put in their first appearance here. Strangely enough, the first four men Jesus chooses are in pairs (see Matt. 21:1, 11:2; Mark 6:7). Two more brothers beside these four are found among the apostles (see Acts 1:13).

4:23 And Jesus went about all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness and all manner of disease among the people.

Again, we run into a doctrinal problem that must be settled if we are to intelligently read the coming "Sermon on the Mount." We must give the Hebrews credit for at least believing what God gave them. The Old Testament scribes, saved or lost, were much more meticulous and reverent in handling the "oracles of God" than such slipshod interpolators as Tischendorf (1869), Tregelles (1857), Weiss (1877), and Westcott and Hort (1881). The Hebrew knew what Jesus was heralding. He understood it. If the modern pseudo-[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_69}psychologists in our pulpits have confused the "gospel of the kingdom" with the self-annihilation of Zen or the meditations of Kahlil Gibran, that is their business, but our business is to find out what God said and believe it.

Now, this is the "gospel of the kingdom." It says it is, so it must be. This gospel includes the "signs and wonders" of miraculous healing that make their first appearance in Scripture with the calling out of Israel as a nation (Exod. 4:1--9). No one gets sick and gets healed until then. You can't find a disease named or implied in fifty chapters of Genesis. Healing is a sign associated with Israel (Exod. 15:26). As such, it teaches the Jew to look for a sign (1 Cor. 1:22). He is justified in looking for one, according to his national history, for that history begins with signs (Exod. 4:30) and ends (before the present age) with signs (Mark 16). But the one sign he was told to believe, many a Hebrew missed (Matt. 12:39), for it was a sign Jonah missed since it was connected with Gentiles getting in on the blessing too!

This blessing is a spiritual blessing and leads us into such subjects as the "body of Christ" and the "new birth" and "the kingdom of God," but ["the gospel of the kingdom,"]{.class_4258} here, is plainly (in the context, historically and doctrinally) the announcement of a literal, visible, Messianic Kingdom with God's ruler set up to do "signs and wonders." (Check Matt. 11:5 and notice that although John had some doubts, he was not as stupid about the kingdom as Reformed Theology and Catholic tradition.) The Kingdom is "set up" (Dan. 2:44); the "church" is "built up" (Eph. 2:20). The Kingdom has "heirs" (James 2:5), while the church has no "heirs." The Kingdom has "children" (Matt. 8:12), while the church has none. Whoever read "children of the church" in a Bible? The Kingdom can be received (Luke 19:12), while the church receives people! The Kingdom is never a "body" or "holy temple" or "house," but the Church is. If that were not enough, the Jew is taught to pray ["thy kingdom come"]{.class_4258} (along with the followers of Weatherhead, Peale, Poling, and Jones), but what nut would pray "thy CHURCH come." The church is going! It is a "called-out" ASSEMBLY by definition!

How foolish and supercilious to prepare Christians with the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_70}"Sermon on the Mount" in the next ten verses, after the Holy Spirit has just told you that this is ["the gospel of the kingdom."]{.class_4258} No Christians are present. All are Israelites, no one even knows the gospel of the New Testament, and no one is in a spiritual kingdom or body, by a new birth to receive it. (We can be charitable, but we cannot be profane; Scripture forbids it \[2 Tim. 1:13, 2:14\].)

4:24 And his fame went throughout all Syria: and they brought unto him all sick people that were taken with divers diseases and torments, and those which were possessed with devils, and those which were lunatick, and those that had the palsy; and he healed them.

25 And there followed him great multitudes of people from Galilee, and from Decapolis, and from Jerusalem, and from Judaea, and from beyond Jordan.

(Syria, at this time, is northwest of Galilee and a little north, just east of Phoenicia.) Note, in particular, the difference between a demon-possessed person and a "lunatic." The Scriptures are always reasonable. We read of "comforting the feeble minded" in 1 Thessalonians 5, and here we see that the Holy Spirit recognizes the difference between demoniac influence and ordinary lunacy, debility, incompetency, or whatever you may call it. The monstrous slander raised against the Bible, which blames the treatment of the demented in the Dark Ages on its teaching, is a monstrous slander indeed. The Holy Spirit knows the difference between organic troubles and spiritual troubles, between psychosomatic relations which arise through malfunction of the body and those which arise through sin against God in the inner life. The ["and he healed them"]{.class_4258} indicates no failures. Not even where the unbelief was rankest did Jesus ever find anyone who "didn't have enough faith to be healed," and where they didn't, He healed them on someone else's faith (Mark 6:5, 2:4--5). Not even Peter found any folks without enough "faith" to get healed; his results in "The Acts" were one hundred percent (Acts 5:16). The Bible reader observes quite a gap between the Messianic Jewish signs given to Israel, while they still could receive Christ (Notice, no miracles or healing in Jerusalem after Acts 7!), and the modern Gentile church "full-gospel" healing []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw344.xhtml_page_71}program, which has duds, short rounds, blown gaskets, short circuits, and "leakage of power." (Someone undoubtedly has busted their theological neck in Matthew, Acts, or Hebrews \[probably Matt. 8:17 and Heb. 13:8\].)

The crowd includes the entire area around the Sea of Galilee, south into Judaea and Jerusalem, and east of Jordan into what is now "Trans-Jordan."

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml}

# Chapter 5 {.heading_84-0}

5:1 And seeing the multitudes, he went up into a mountain: and when he was set, his disciples came unto him:

2 And he opened his mouth, and taught them, saying,

What follows is the famous "Sermon on the Mount," made nearly "infamous" by its perversion and distortion in the malformed and unskilled hands of the modern "liberal." A few points may merit attention before we launch into the sermon.

1\. If the other events in Matthew are not true (and many are not, according to devotees of the "sermon on the mount"), then this is only a heroic romance---nothing but a pagan discourse. This everlasting, subjective choosing of what to believe and what not to believe, of what God has told you to believe and what not to believe, should come to a screeching halt. Why, the idea of a man taking only what [doesn't offend him ]{.class_4257}out of the Bible and calling this the real "meat" or the real "truth" or the real "word of God"! If Matthew is reliable in chapters 5--7, then he is just as reliable in chapters 13, 23--24, and 27. The fact that these chapters [offend ]{.class_4257}the self‑righteous humanitarians of this day and age does not disqualify them from being equal to, or superior to, the Old Testament, Hebrew "sermon on the mount" (primarily for Jews only) before there was a church (Matt. 16) or any Christians (Acts 11).
