2\. There are no Christians present. There are no born‑again believers present. There are no New Testament writings at this time. The discourse is not addressed to the church; it is not even addressed to apostles, as the "twelve" have not yet been chosen (Matt. 10:1--4).

3\. To attempt to apply it [doctrinally ]{.class_4257}to the Christian is suicide, and the very liberals who adore the "sermon" are responsible for some of the greatest suicidal schisms in the church that have arisen. Imagine accepting Matthew 5:17--20 as New Testament doctrine and then [refusing to believe the Seventh-day Adventist teaching against the Roman church! ]{.class_4257}Or imagine rejecting the Jehovah Witness teaching on the 144,000, [while refusing to recognize their "sermon-on-the-mount-conscientious-objecting" ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_73}(5:39). When you attempt to apply the Sermon on the Mount to Christian people you [split ]{.class_4257}the Body of Christ, for no reasonable, sane Christian in his right mind could reconcile the passages, [doctrinally, ]{.class_4257}with the Pauline epistles. This is the liberal's purpose: [to destroy the belief that the Pauline epistles are of equal authority with the gospels! ]{.class_4257}He does it by everlastingly harping on the "sermon on the mount," one of the less significant messages for Christians, if not the least significant in the entire Bible.

4\. It is at Calvary that you get the power to live the "Sermon on the Mount" in a [spiritual ]{.class_4257}sense; you cannot live Matthew 5:22 because you were never tempted to call anyone "Raca"! An Old Testament Jewish sermon, given under the law by a Messiah (before His atonement), is an impossibility to live without the New Birth, and Romans 13 commands the [ignoring of the Jewish sabbath recommended in 5:19. ]{.class_4257}(If you get messed up it will be in Matthew, Acts, or Hebrews!)

5\. To correctly understand what God has given us, all we have to do is believe what we have read in the Old Testament until here. God had promised a kingdom; God promised a literal kingdom with His ruler on the throne of David (see 2:6, 3:2). The ruler appears. He is heralded, and as a king, He SITS (Matt. 5:1) and delivers the constitution for the kingdom, which is [an earthly, Messianic, Jewish kingdom on earth---]{.class_4257}remembering[, ]{.class_4257}of course, that up until here, all conditions for the [Second Advent ]{.class_4257}of Christ are being met. The further revelation given (that it turned out to be the [First Advent) is ]{.class_4257}not an excuse or alibi for us to fly in the face of God with an amillennial and postmillennial "abomination of desolation." At this point (Matt. 5), the "day of the Lord" approaches rapidly. Let us believe what we read as we read it. Quit rushing things. No man seated on the "Mount" had any idea anything would happen except exactly what God said would happen, [which will happen. ]{.class_4257}The Church Age and "gap" is another thing (see Eph. 3). Unsaved scholars fail to see this lay‑out, as their churches are all Old Testament, Jewish, works-rules‑sacrament setups, which avoid the revelation of Romans and Galatians, this side of the open tomb.

5:3 Blessed are the poor in spirit: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.

Notice that "Heaven" is not the subject of the discourse, but the ["kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4258} No salvation by "penitency" here. In the unsearchable riches of the Authorized Version we find the Greek, ["Makarioi," ]{.class_4257}translated for us (without reference to the Greek) as "happy" in Genesis 30:13, so we do not need a lexicon. "Blessed" means "happy." The wording is ["poor in spirit" ]{.class_4258}also, not just "poor." This, spiritually speaking, would be the "contrite or broken spirit" referred to many times in the Psalms.

5:4 Blessed are they that mourn: for they shall be comforted.

Notice again how futile it is to apply the passage [doctrinally ]{.class_4257}to a situation involving salvation. The liberal's choice of the "Sermon on the Mount" is not without purpose. The entire Sermon does not say enough about [salvation ]{.class_4257}for an average man to find it in three night's reading. A lot of people [mourn ]{.class_4257}who are never comforted (Matt. 13:42), but in John 15:16, we have a Jewish example of what Christ means. \[To assume that every reader reading the "sermon" is a "Christian" is a disaster. Christians often "stick up" for the sermon on the mount after having read some nice, smooth, mild‑mannered commentary on it, which applies it to Christians only; but such an exposition is treacherous to the gospel trust. A commentary on the "Sermon on the Mount" that doesn't warn its reader of hell‑fire (see Matt. 5:22) is a shambles.\]

5:5 Blessed are the meek: for they shall inherit the earth.

This saying is borrowed from Psalms 37:11, with reference to Zephaniah 2:3, and it brings us head‑on into the real nature of the sermon on the mount, for the meek [have never inherited the earth and never will till the Second Advent! ]{.class_4257}Furthermore, every verse in the Bible on an "inheritance" deals with [the millennial possession of Palestine]{.class_4257} (Col.[ ]{.class_4257}3:24; Eph. 5:5; Gal. 5:21; James 2:5), and this possession of land is [merited by the meek, suffering in this age ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 8:17; 2 Tim. 2:12). [It is not a gift; it is an earned reward. ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_75}It is described in detail in Luke 19:12--27.

5:6 Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness: for they shall be filled.

Proverbs 21:21 is a good commentary on the verse. To the unsaved man (speaking inspirationally, not doctrinally), it can mean that he is to thirst after the righteousness of God, which is Jesus (Matt. 6:33; Rom. 10:1--4; John 4:14). To the saved, it can mean future rewards for doing right now. To the audience then present in history, it is an indication that those listeners whose hearts are right are going to [understand the First Coming and have a chance to receive the Messiah, ]{.class_4257}who is to be rejected by the religious leaders. Note how this "filling" comes literally to pass before the Pauline epistles (Acts 4:31).

5:7 Blessed are the merciful: for they shall obtain mercy.

The general spiritual truth is apparent and is thor­oughly explained in detail in other passages such as Romans 12:8, 12:19--21; James 2:12--13. However, to teach mercy [at the Last Judgment, ]{.class_4257}on the basis of [mercy that humans showed to humans, ]{.class_4257}is over‑reaching the "sermon" too far, and it will surely land the self‑righteous Bible‑perverter in Hell. (This over‑reaching is a common failure of liberal theology in trying to deliver the souls of men.)

5:8 Blessed are the pure in heart: for they shall see God.

Again, the Lord is repeating an Old Testament doctrine well‑known to every Old Testament Saint, although it may come as a novelty to some New Testament "Greek scholars." The doctrine is stated plainly and in detail in Psalm 24:4. (The context again is the Second Advent, and Psa. 24:6 shows the word "Jacob" inserted for the word "God.") Note that the Psalm (which defines the scope and meaning of ["Blessed are the pure in heart"]{.class_4258}) deals entirely with a literal, earthly, visible reign of a Messianic King (vss. 1, 3, 8, 10).

To spiritualize the sermon on the mount without recognizing this []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_76}logical, doctrinal intention of the Holy Spirit is to slight the Almighty. We can spiritualize the passage to those "born again" and emphasize internal purity rather than outward righteousness, but the teaching will hardly match the Scripture. For every person in the universe will [see ]{.class_4257}God at one time or another (Rev. 20; Rom. 2:16, 3:19). The Christian is said to have a "pure heart," however (1 Pet. 1:22), and also a soul that has been "purified." The "pure heart" is unobtainable without the new birth, which occurs in the next verse in Peter's epistle (1 Pet. 1:23; John 3:3). A "sermon on the mount" exposition that neglects to tell its readers that they have a hopeless task in this age, [without the new birth, ]{.class_4257}is[ ]{.class_4257}therefore a mutilated exposition which does more harm than good (see Acts 15:9--11 for an exact exposition).

5:9 Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God.

Again, the expositor who fails to take the premillennial position is up to his neck in quicksand. To spiritualize the passage is fatal, for the ["peacemakers"]{.class_4258} will have to be juggled and balanced till no one knows who they are, and the "children" of God will not fit into the rest of the New Testament unless every ambassador, diplomat, attache, and intercessor between Rome and Moscow is classified as a "child of God."

Unfortunately, any [doctrinal ]{.class_4257}application to this age is just as fatal. Christ Himself was not a "child of God" by the statement (if the Sermon on the Mount is to be applied doctrinally), for notice that [He was not a "peacemaker" ]{.class_4257}(Luke 12:51--53; John 7:43, 10:19). [He gave peace to those who believed the revelation that God spoke through Him ]{.class_4257}(John 14:27), but for the type of scholarship which disbelieved the Old Testament, His most peaceful retort was ["fools"]{.class_4258} and "judgment" (Luke 24:25; John 5:44--47). The "peacemaker," represented by the mushy, maudlin synthesizer of doctrinal leaven in the ecumenical realm, hardly fits the verse. The entire address is relative to the Kingdom‑Age when the King will reign on the throne of David. The Old Testament makes this abundantly clear (Psa. 72:7, 3, 85:8, 125:5).

5:10 Blessed are they which are persecuted for righteousness' sake: for theirs is the kingdom of heaven.

11 Blessed are ye, when men shall revile you, and persecute you, and shall say all manner of evil against you falsely, for my sake.

To people who believe that "righteousness" is a [relative ]{.class_4257}matter ("all depends on how you look at it"), the verse offers considerable confusion. The "righteousness" here is identified in verse 11 as being synonymous with Jesus Christ Himself (Rom. 10:1--4), and thus it is also found in the Old Testament, carefully tucked away right in the middle of the Law---see Deuteronomy 30:20 (which is the identical context of Rom. 10:5--9). The introduction of Jesus Himself, by Himself, as "righteousness," offers another problem to the liberal who denies the absolute Deity of Christ. If Jesus was not God, He was not even ["good,"]{.class_4258} let alone "righteous" (Luke 18:19). God's prophets are not sugar sticks; they are "salty" (Matt. 5:13), and they are persecuted for Christ's sake, not because they are trying humanitarian efforts to "make the world a better place to live in" (and DIE in, it should be added). If the student is in doubt about the exposition, due to the loose generalities published by Buttrick, Fosdick, Carnell, Weatherhead, Ferre, and other assorted Scripture‑babies, let him give careful attention to the literal fulfillment of the verse in Acts 4:18 and 5:40. [Those being persecuted are persecuted for doctrinal preaching on the literal death and literal resurrection of a Saviour, ]{.class_4257}not for approving of sit‑down strikes at lunch counters, court‑orders to integrate, government helps for housing projects, and CARE packages sent overseas.

5:12 Rejoice, and be exceeding glad: for great is your reward in heaven: for so persecuted they the prophets which were before you.

["Rejoice"]{.class_4258} is the hard part of the verse, in view of some things they will be called upon to endure. A Christian is admonished with the same commandment in Romans 12:12 and 1 Peter 3:14--18. The reward for [this ]{.class_4257}group will be at the White Throne Judgment (Rev. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_78}11:18, 20:12); whereas, the Christians' rewards in this dispensation will be at the Judgment Seat of Christ (1 Cor. 3; 2 Cor. 5; Rom. 14:10--12).

["The prophets which were before you" ]{.class_4258}must be qualified by an additional statement found in Luke 6:26. This distinction must be noticed, for "false prophets" are among the Bible's most outstanding targets for criticism and the references run into the scores (Matt. 7:15; 2 Pet. 2:1; 1 John 4:1; Rev. 16:13, 19:20, etc.). The false prophet and God's prophet are to be discerned and identified immediately by their fidelity or lack of fidelity to the revelation of Scripture. The sure test is given in Deuteronomy 18:21--22, and by this standard (and Isa 8:20), we learn that preachers who teach that God is through with Israel or that Israel would not return to Palestine or that the world is getting "better" or that there will be no more wars or that all men can learn to live peacefully together are, without a doubt, [phonies.]{.class_4257}

The terrific emphasis on social and class issues today is a dead give‑away to the distorted mental condition of the neo‑orthodox and liberal class of "prophets." Borrowing their authority from Hosea and Amos, they claim to be the [reformers ]{.class_4257}for the Church Age; but alas, in the social message of the [true prophets, the sinful heart is ]{.class_4257}recognized as the source of the world's woes, not "capitalism." In the prophets, the only hope is that people [repent, ]{.class_4257}not "re‑think." In the prophets, the emphasis is placed on God's judgment on [sinful nations, ]{.class_4257}not God's approval of a "Brotherhood" of nations. In the prophets, the only hope for "society" is a Messianic Person; not reformers, decrees, bills, ideologies, and proclamations. And if that weren't enough, you will find that Isaiah, Amos, Hosea, and Micah are relentlessly, if not viciously, opposed to [all forms of integration, ]{.class_4257}ritualism, formalism, [sacramentalism, ]{.class_4257}and ecclesiastical organization. The modern "brain‑washed" liberal is a sad‑sack and a sorry‑sot [as a "prophet," ]{.class_4257}when you compare him to the true prophets.

5:13 Ye are the salt of the earth: but if the salt have lost his savour, wherewith shall it be salted? it is thenceforth good for nothing, but to be cast out, and to be trodden []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_79}under foot of men.

The verse is good sermonic material as ["salt"]{.class_4258} has been and always will be a valuable commodity. Leviticus 2:13 commands its offering with every sacrifice, and Numbers 18:19 speaks of a ["covenant of salt."]{.class_4258} Job 6:6 makes it plain that God cannot "swallow" some things without salt, any more than a man can. The salt is also used for [destruction, ]{.class_4257}as in Ezekiel 47:11.

There are several spiritual applications which can be made to Israel or to the Christian. Israel has always been the "salt of the earth," at least that part of Israel that believed what God said. This Remnant of Israel is always connected with [the condition of the earth ]{.class_4257}(Gen. 12:1--4, Mal. 4:6, Matt. 13:44, Rom. 11:1--20). God uses Israel to bless the earth and to destroy it (Jer 50:20, 31:36). And when the salt loses its ["savour" ]{.class_4258}or "saltiness," then sure enough, it is cast out (Jer. 52). The word ["cast out"]{.class_4258} occurs thirteen times in the book of Jeremiah, which precedes the Babylonian captivity, and it typifies the captivity of Israel under the Antichrist (Rev. 11:1--4). The Christian also can be likened to salt, inspirationally speaking. He is to make people "thirsty" for Christ, and his witnessing should have a ["bite" ]{.class_4257}in it (Col. 4:6). As a preservative, we are told to have "salt in ourselves" and be at peace with one another (Mark 9:50).

5:14 Ye are the light of the world, A city that is set on an hill cannot be hid.

There can be some application to the believer, for Philippians 2:15 applies the name to the Christian in the church. Christians are even called ["children of light]{.class_4258}" (1 Thess. 5:5), which is also applied to Jews before the crucifixion in Luke 16:8. Thus, we learn an excellent principle in studying the Bible: we learn that there can be a [spiritual application in the Gospels to the Christian, if the exact similitude occurs again in Paul's writings ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 15:8, 15--16).

Now as ["the light of the world,"]{.class_4258} we reflect Christ's light, for He is ["the light of the world"]{.class_4258} (John 9:5). This leads our thoughts to consider the tremendous similitude God has given us to learn about ["the light."]{.class_4258} Christ is this LIGHT; yet this Light is not literal sunlight, for when God made the "lights of heaven," He already had a ["light" ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_80}superior to them (Gen. 1:3--17). That this "light" is much greater than solar light (and of a different origin) is evident from Psalm 74:16, even though the sun is a [type of Christ ]{.class_4257}(see Psa. 84:11, 19:2--5, and Mal. 4:2). We have then in the Bible the mystery of the Trinity, clearly explained in the unsearchable riches of the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 Bible, without the benefit of Cappadocians, Monotheletes, Hoomousians, Monarchianists, Saballeans, Nestorians, Apollonarians, or Dynamic Monarchians. Since the Godhead can be [clearly seen ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 1:20), we do not have to waste unnecessary time in Theology to find out what is going on. Jesus Christ is likened to the "sun." As such, He is the source of all spiritual life, all spiritual warmth, all spiritual growth, and all spiritual sight in this world. The sun has alpha, beta, and gamma rays (or rays which [can be seen, ]{.class_4257}but not felt; [felt, ]{.class_4257}but not seen; and actinic rays, [which can neither be seen nor felt]{.class_4257}).[ ]{.class_4257}Thus we find God the Son visible, but unfelt; God the Holy Spirit felt, but not seen; and God the Father unfelt and unseen (1 Tim. 6:16). One Sun with three rays, separate or unseparate according to operation; one God in three persons, separate (Matt. 3:16) or unseparate (Isa. 9:6), according to operation! All of this clearly revealed without any reliance on the mutilated texts of Sinaiticus and Vaticanus!

The Christian, then, would have to have a corollary to match the similitude. In Song of Solomon 6:10, we find the mate. The body of Christ is here spoken of: ["fair as the moon." ]{.class_4258}As the ["light of the world,"]{.class_4258} the moon reflects only the light of the sun, and so should a Christian. As the light is a lesser light for "night," so this age is called "night" wherever you find it (Mark 13:35; Matt. 24:43; John 11:9--10). The believer is DEAD in Christ, since the moon is a dead planet. He sometimes shines brightly and at other times does not. He has no light of his own, but reflects Christ's (a scientific fact known to Job but unknown to "scientists" till recent years---Job 25:5). The believer still bears the marks of sin ("craters and spots") and occasionally, he suffers a total eclipse [when the world ]{.class_4257}(1 John 2:15) [comes between him and his "Sun." ]{.class_4257}["Ye are the light of the world."]{.class_4258}

[​]{.class_4259}["A city that is set\...." ]{.class_4258}The immediate reference is to Jerusalem, which is set on a hill with other hills about it: the Mount of Olives on the east ridge across Kidron, the valley of Jehoshaphat and Mt. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_81}Scopas on the north, and the hill of Evil Council on the south, which are all higher than Mt. Zion on which the city is built. But still it cannot be hid, ["for it is the city of the great king"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 5:35), God's chosen place (2 Sam. 24:18), and the focal point of international politics (Psa. 122:6).

There may be a Scriptural reference to the "cities of refuge" in the Old Testament, for all of these were placed in conspicuous places whereunto the refugee could flee (Josh. 21:13--39).

5:15 Neither do men light a candle, and put it under a bushel, but on a candlestick; and it giveth light unto all that are in the house.

16 Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.

The candle ["under a bushel"]{.class_4258} can also be misplaced ["under a bed,"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}according to Mark 4:21, which indicates that there are two dangers in regard to an effectual witness for Jesus: [commercialism and laziness! ]{.class_4257}["The spirit of man"]{.class_4258} is said to be ["the candle of the ]{.class_4258}[Lord]{.class_4267}["]{.class_4258}; the "light" that shines is supposed to come through the man's eyes (Prov. 20:8, 27; Matt. 6:22). The applications are richer even than this, for as the Christian reflects Christ's light in the "nighttime" (see vs. 14), the woman who typifies believing Israel, or the Church, [does not let her light go out by night! ]{.class_4257}(Prov. 31:18). A candlestick can have some application to the church, for in Revelation 1:20 a church is defined as a "candlestick."[ "The house"]{.class_4258} of Matthew 5:15 can be Christ's house (Heb. 3:2--6) or the "house of Israel," in the context. There is a woman who has to sweep house to find [a believing remnant ]{.class_4257}in Luke 15:8, and she [has to light her candle ]{.class_4257}to get the job done!

Notice, the works do not shine; the light does. We are to get people to [observe our works ]{.class_4257}by witnessing for Christ. If we witness, then don't you worry about it, we'll be watched! Many a man alibis his way out of witnessing by saying: "Well, I know a lot of folks that talk about it that don't live it. I just let my life speak for itself." This however is an error and a subterfuge. No one really [notices your works ]{.class_4257}till you begin to speak up for Jesus, and then they begin to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_82}watch you like a chicken hawk. That is why most men don't "speak up"! They don't want to be watched that close, or their "works" would not say as much as they suppose they do! Furthermore, it would be sinful for a man to do good works and take credit for them if he were a real Christian in the Bible sense, for his works are wrought by "Christ in him," through the Holy Spirit, and that is where the credit should go (John 3:21; Phil 2:13)!

The ["your Father"]{.class_4258} of verse 16 introduces again the ancient teaching given to Moses that [God was a Father to ISRAEL ]{.class_4257}(Exod. 4:22--23; Deut. 32:6, 18--19; Isa. 46:3). The "Fatherhood‑of‑God" idea is even older, being the ancient, pagan concept of the superstitious Greek gnostics and intellectuals (Acts 17:28), as represented in the Bible quotation from Aratus, a native of Cilicia. Since Jesus never taught the universal "Fatherhood of God and Brotherhood of Man" to anyone but believing Israelites, we may safely reject the whole idea as intellectual horseplay from the start. The Bible is sufficiently clear in both Testaments that God is not responsible for all the people that profess to be His children (Eph. 2:1--3; Matt. 23:33; John 8:44--47; 1 Sam. 1:16).

Again, it should be noticed that Jesus is very careful never to address an audience using the term ["Our Father" ]{.class_4257}as inclusive of Himself. The shallow and lazy mind of the "Modernist" has failed to observe the Sermon on the Mount in this respect, and somehow overlooks entirely the fact that Jesus [never ]{.class_4257}calls God ["Our Father."]{.class_4257} He calls Him "Your Father," as here, or "My Father" as in 18:35, and "Their Father" as in 13:43, but never "OUR FATHER," [except when telling SOMEONE ELSE how to pray!]{.class_4257}

5:17 Think not that I am come to destroy the law, or the prophets: I am not come to destroy, but to fulfil.

18 For verily I say unto you, Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled.

This passage is a crucial one, and it is one of the famous stumbling‑stones God has deliberately set in Scripture to dash out the religious brains of any insincere person who is trifling with the Scripture (1 Pet. 2:8).

1\. If He did not come to destroy the law, then it is useless to teach that what follows (vss. 21--48) is an [abridgment or changing of the Law. ]{.class_4257}The Lord surely knows more about what He means, than some Coke‑drinking professor fooling with the Graf‑Wellhausen theory.

2\. The passage shows His approbation of the Law and the Prophets; it classifies immediately those who do not believe them as "against Christ" ("anti‑Christ"; see John 5:46; Luke 24:44).

3\. The passage shows that the Law and Prophets (where they speak of righteousness) are fulfilled in Jesus Christ Himself (Rom. 10:1--4, 3:25). No one can deny that the "Sermon on the Mount" is a dissertation on "right­eousness" as God sees it. That "righteousness" is the issue is abundantly clear from the next three verses. Furthermore, the book of Matthew began with a definite statement on this when Jesus was baptized (Matt. 3:15).

The ["one jot or one tittle" ]{.class_4258}is a reference to the Hebrew characters in which the Old Testament is written. A classic example of the ["jot"]{.class_4258} is the Hebrew "Yod" which will be found in the heading of Psalm 119:73. The ["tittle"]{.class_4258} is the horn‑like projection that distinguishes Resh from Daleth, or Kaph from Beth (also illustrated in the headings of Psalm 119 in the [AV]{.class_4257}).

But by far the most important part of the passage is the statement in verse 18 that ["Till heaven and earth pass, one jot or one tittle shall in no wise pass from the law, till all be fulfilled!" ]{.class_4258}This is tremendous, for it indicates that the scope of Old Testament prophecy [does not end with the First Coming of Christ. ]{.class_4257}(Go back and read that again.) Heaven and earth do not pass away until 2 Peter 3 and Revelation 20, still far in the future. [The only part fulfilled at Christ's First Coming was the "righteousness" part. ]{.class_4257}There are at least 690 verses waiting [future fulfillment. ]{.class_4257}Again, the believer must part company with Augustine, Jerome, Origen, Eusebius, Calvin, Berkhof, and attendant palm readers. He will have to part company with these gentlemen regularly, for with all their Christian acquirements they have acquired the nasty habit of [disbelieving the Law and the Prophets where they speak of events beyond the First Coming. ]{.class_4257}All "the law," then, includes a good bit more than Christ dying to blot out the handwriting of ordi­nances. It also includes the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_84}time, manner, route, and purpose of the Second Advent through Palestine to the Mount of Olives (see Deut. 32--33, Gen. 49).

5:19 Whosoever therefore shall break one of these least commandments, and shall teach men so, he shall be called the least in the kingdom of heaven: but whosoever shall do and teach them, the same shall be called great in the kingdom of heaven.

Romans 3:31 makes it abundantly clear that the law is still in effect for [somebody. ]{.class_4257}The law is spiritual and good (Rom. 7:12, 14), but certainly the New Testament Christian is not under it (Gal. 3:22--26; Rom. 6:14--17; 7:3--6). This brings up a severe problem in what God requires of a Christian, and the modernist makes the matter even more complicated by insisting the "sermon on the mount" is "for" [the Christian. ]{.class_4257}Well, "all Scripture is for the Christian," [in the sense that he can learn something from it; ]{.class_4257}but to misapply a passage, [doctrinally, ]{.class_4257}through willful ignorance (2 Tim. 2:15), is another matter.

Ellen G. White (Seventh-day Adventism) had a nice solution. She pretended that the "Plan of Salvation" was in Matthew 5:18--20 and reinstated the Ten Command­ments to draw the Christian back into Old Testament, Jewish bondage (2 Cor. 3:1--10). Nice solution! The "Covenant‑theologians" hit upon a nice compromise; they reinstated the Ten Commandments in their churches, with the help of Cecil B. DeMille, yet still break the sabbath, [which is Saturday ]{.class_4257}(6 P.M. Friday to 6 P.M. Saturday), according to Christ's time and message! (Nice work.) The Catholics, not to be out‑hoodwinked, make ten commandments out of nine [by omitting the command to leave IMAGES ALONE ]{.class_4257}and by splitting the tenth into two commandments! (Despite the testimony of the Coptic, Arabic, Syriac, Hebrew, Samaritan Pentateuch, Chaldean, Vulgate, and Septuagint, scholars just get scholarly when it's convenient! Good pay too!)

Let us be more fair‑minded and reasonable, and a little more diligent in searching the Scriptures. At the risk of letting self‑deceived traditionalists say: "He thinks he's right and everyone else is wrong" (this is the standard retort of a man too lazy to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_85}investigate), let us read.

The commandments referred to, here, [are the ten of the Old Testament Law, ]{.class_4257}for a discourse on them follows---verses 21, 27, etc. That these commandments are still in effect and will judge the unsaved is also apparent from John 5:45--47 and 12:48, not only to the Jew but also to the Gentile (Rom. 2:15--16). However, a strange thing occurs when these TEN commandments reoccur in the local‑church epistles to New Testament "Christians," for when they appear, [the observing of the sabbath as a ceremony is distinctly omitted and is warned against ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 13:8--10, Col. 2:14--16). We find the First Day (Sunday) observed quite frequently (Acts 20:7; 1 Cor. 16:1--2), but the "Sabbath" is only found in the book of Acts up till the time that the New Testament books were printed.

Time after time we are told in the gospels that the Sabbath is unusual in that it was only a ceremonial observance, not a [moral command ]{.class_4257}(Mark 2:26--27, 3:1--5; John 9:16). If there were any doubts left, a cursory glance at Ezekiel 20:20 and Nehemiah 9:14 would settle the controversy. The Sabbath was a sign given to "Israel" and [unknown to anyone before Exodus 12:6. ]{.class_4257}It concerns Israel; so Adam, Enoch, Noah, Abraham, Isaac, Joseph, Abel, and Jacob didn't waste five minutes with it. The Christian is asked to observe two commandments (Rom. 13:8--10) which will enable him to observe seven more, but the Sabbath is out of the question. Paul and Jesus are careful to omit it when talking of righteousness (Luke 18:20; Rom. 13:8--10).

We are in a dilemma which can only be solved by admitting (whether we have been taught that way or not) that the commandment to observe and teach the "least of the ten commandments" [must have a primary doctrinal application to the Second Coming, ]{.class_4257}where, sure enough, we find the Sabbath being observed (Isa. 66:22‑24, Matt. 24:20). \[See Commentary on Revelation, Rev. 12:17 and 14:12.\] Jesus replaced the "ten" with two "new" commandments (1 John 3:23; John 14:21, 15:10--12, 14:23), and to fail to observe these and obey them is certainly to sin against your Lord and Saviour.

5:20 For I say unto you, That except your []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_86}righteousness shall exceed the righteousness of the scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven.

The verse introduces a new note into the sermon. Up until here the Old Testament Law and the Psalms are clearly in view; there is nothing of a "shocking" or revolutionary nature present. Any fisherman or farmer, in this age (remembering of course that the average "Peter" or "John" in this age is far better versed in the Scripture than the average seminary professor today), would have gotten the "gist" of the discourse. But here at verse 20, a peculiar note is sounded. The people had always accepted (and still do today) the office of their religious leaders as being nearly infallible. But suddenly our Lord speaks of a righteousness [beyond ]{.class_4257}the scribes and Pharisees. This righteousness must be quite an attainment, for nearly any Pharisee (Luke 18 for example) was more righteous than the majority of Catholic priests and Protestant ministers today. (Commentators of the twentieth century have a clumsy and irreverent habit of overlooking the achievements and advancements of people in Biblical times. When one reads the list of what a real Pharisee was \[Phil. 3:3--9\], one is struck with the fact that no Reformed "domini" or Catholic cardinal could say he obeyed the list without [lying somewhere]{.class_4257}.) A Pharisee is not to be overlooked. As an Arminian who believed in salvation by works, he certainly far surpassed in zeal and sincerity his modern counterparts, who prate about the "golden rule" and the "sermon on the mount." Of course these twentieth-century Pharisees are just as lost (Rom. 10:9--10), since the way that "seemeth right" usually ends in death (Prov. 14:12); nevertheless, these first century Pharisees had a righteous­ness not to be sneered at. To accuse them of being only [outwardly righteous ]{.class_4257}(because of Matt. 23:3--8) is to read too much into the text. A man who is not an extortioner or unjust (Luke 18:11) is slightly ahead of the priests and cardinals who organize lobbyists to get Congressional tax money for their own schools!

The subject is still the ["kingdom of heaven." ]{.class_4258}There has been no reference at all to the church, the new birth, the body of Christ, the death, burial, and resurrection, or the ["kingdom of God." ]{.class_4258}But []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_87}there is a shift in emphasis from the Old Testament Law and Psalms, as they stand "in the letter," to something that is going to "get rough." Jesus is going to analyze [the reaction of the heart to those laws; ]{.class_4257}He is going to fulfill [spiritually ]{.class_4257}His Second Advent prophecies described in the book right before Matthew (Mal. 3:2--5). It is interesting to note that of the eight "charges" filed against the Jews in Malachi 3:5, the Pharisees qualify on six counts!

The "righteousness" mentioned in the sermon on the mount is defined even in the Old Testament. Unfortunately, the modern liberal, with the reins of the International Sunday School Lesson slack in his frost‑bitten hands, has overlooked the passage, for it occurs NEAR a "salvation­by‑works text," which would appeal to any Pharisee. The passage is Micah 6:3--8. Notice the "liberal," in his narrow-minded and bigoted condition, always quotes verses 6--8 [which ends with a question mark ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 4:3)! Blinded, as usual, by his negative and unreasonable attitude, the Liberal fails to see the great teaching of verses 3--5, which defines [absolute righteousness. ]{.class_4257}The reference is to Balaam (the false prophet in Numbers), who could not understand why God would not curse a people who were [sinning. ]{.class_4257}Balaam, a stranger and an alien to grace (like any twentieth-century Arminian), thought that you [had to be good enough to get to heaven by your works! ]{.class_4257}This error (originating with Eve's refusal to quote the word ["freely"]{.class_4258} when she misquoted God in Gen. 3:2--3) comes right down through the Pharisees to our modern "fig‑leaf" age, where every blind leader of the blind is quoting verses to make people think they can develop themselves until they're good enough to "make it." Unfortunately, they will have to do [better ]{.class_4257}than Paul (Phil 3:3--9), and no one but a deluded egomaniac would suppose his life matched the life of a man who could say with the approval of the Holy Spirit, ["touching the righteousness which is in the law, ]{.class_4258}[blameless."]{.class_4268}[ ]{.class_4257}This perfect "ten commandment­golden-rule-righteousness" is defined as ["manure" ]{.class_4257}(see Phil 3:8), [when compared with the righteousness of Jesus Christ. ]{.class_4257}There is many a religious leader counting on this "goodness" to get him through the judgment; he is going to awaken to a catastrophe as he finds himself covered, not with the "pure gold" of God's absolute righteousness, but with the sediment of the city sewer lines! (Isa 64:6).

5:21 Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not kill; and whosoever shall kill shall be in danger of the judgment:

That there is [some ]{.class_4257}reference to the fourth commandment is apparent, but we find upon a study of this commandment that the "killer" was not merely in ["danger of the judgment,"]{.class_4258} he was to be [executed, ]{.class_4257}if two witnesses were available (Num. 35:16--31; Deut. 19:15--20). He is to be judged, and if he has killed someone, the ["danger"]{.class_4258} does not amount to being exposed to judgment; it amounts to capital punishment without mercy. The scribes and Pharisees had restricted the commandment, however, to the bare act of murder (for which there is some authority, for Jesus defines the fourth commandment as "murder" in Matthew 19:18). However, a "man‑slayer," whether by self‑defense, man‑slaughter, second‑degree murder, or by accident, still had to face a judge and judgment (see Num. 35:24; Deut. 19:18). A man was ["in danger of the judgment"]{.class_4258} if he even killed a man accidentally.

(If the word ["kill"]{.class_4258} here means "any killing," then the saying of ["old time" ]{.class_4258}was a correct saying; however, Jesus does not stop here but goes on to the heart of the matter.)

5:22 But I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause shall be in danger of the judgment: and whosoever shall say to his brother, Raca, shall be in danger of the council: but whosoever shall say, Thou fool, shall be in danger of hell fire.

Here, the "Sermon on the Mount" nearly explodes in the face of the Catholic, Modernist, Liberal, Neo‑Orthodox, and Orthodox amillennial and postmillennial schol­ars. (It had to happen sooner or later. You can't juggle a carton of TNT around all day, on a greased plank, without making a fool out of yourself somewhere.) When were you tempted to call anyone Raca? Where is the ["council"]{.class_4258}? How can it be ["hell fire"]{.class_4258} when the greatest writers on the "Sermon on the Mount" have no sermons themselves on ["hell fire"? ]{.class_4257}If the hell fire isn't literal, what madness is it to make the ["brother"]{.class_4258} literal or the ["fool"]{.class_4258} literal? What is ["the judgment," ]{.class_4258}when verse 21 refers to [earthly judgment; ]{.class_4257}and if it does [not, ]{.class_4257}how can []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_89}[anyone get by "the judgment" ]{.class_4257}when all [(regardless of killing or not)]{.class_4257} are said to have to face God? To avoid the dispensational application of the "Sermon on the Mount" to the millennium, and force it into the African‑Catholic‑Gentile church, produces more problems than it solves. Let us watch the "scribes" sweat it out as they try to jam the teaching into their "salvation­by‑works" schemes or their "anti‑dispensational" maze.

1\. The [RV]{.class_4257}, [ASV]{.class_4257}, and [RSV ]{.class_4257}go to work immediately and omit ["without a cause," ]{.class_4258}thus slandering the Lord and also adopting an unreasonable attitude towards law and jurisprudence. The source of this omission, as other corruptions in the new "Bibles," is the Afro‑Roman tradition. In "Vaticanus" (at Rome) and "Sinaiticus" (Aleph), along with the Vulgate and a few other minor manuscripts of no importance, we find ["without a cause" ]{.class_4258}omitted. Justinius (II) with Origen (A.D. 184--254) omits it, but Origen is not consistent. His logical, philosophical, gnostic mind knows it should be there to make sense, but his desire to be recognized as a "translator" and "critic" is so great that he must meddle with it; [so he leaves it out one time and puts it in another time---]{.class_4257}typical [RSV ]{.class_4257}work!!

2\. The correct text, preserved in the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611, is found in the Received Text (Byzantine) of the Greek New Testament church. It is in the Old Latin (A.D. 200), [which is older than Origen ]{.class_4257}(A.D. 251). It is attested to by the Syriac (A.D. 300), which is older than "Vaticanus." And it is in the majority of Greek witnesses, including Bezae Cantabregensis (D), and Koridethi (Theta). We may safely conclude that the [ASV, RV]{.class_4257}, and [RSV]{.class_4257}, as their mutilated predecessors, are not to be taken too seriously in the light of consistent, Christian, textual criticism.

3\. To return to our verse, the difficulties rapidly increase. In 1 Corinthians 15:36, Paul plainly says: "Thou fool," and Jesus Himself uses the appellation to two of His disciples (Luke 24:25). If we are going to sensibly expound the verse, the average "scholar" will be of scant help. In his desire to pose as a religious leader or exert himself as a "spiritual power," the amillennialist or postmillennialist snarls himself and his followers up in a hopeless mass of barbed‑wire theology. There isn't a record of a Gentile Christian calling another Christian "RACA" from A.D. 500 to A.D. 1960, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_90}[or even being "tempted" to do so.]{.class_4257}

We are dealing with the Kingdom of Heaven. It is Jewish. After all, "Raca" is a Jewish word. We are dealing with the constitution of a King which has been laid down for an approaching advent, which turns out to be delayed for two thousand years or more, but will, [without doubt, ]{.class_4257}come to pass. That this is the only correct [doctrinal ]{.class_4257}teaching of the passage is so apparent to a believer of the Old Testament that to "monkey with" the verse another minute would seem a waste of time.

Inspirationally speaking, of course, there are many fine spiritual applications. We shouldn't be angry "without cause," but to limit it to not ever being angry (as the [RV, ASV]{.class_4257}, and [RSV ]{.class_4257}do), is to accuse [Jesus Christ of SIN ]{.class_4257}(Mark 3:5; John 2:15). The Nestle's text, which omits ["without a cause," ]{.class_4258}has done a masterful job of straining out the gnat and swallowing the camel, hair, hide, and all; for even Christians are told to be angry [at times ]{.class_4257}(Eph. 4:26). The ["council"]{.class_4258} is peculiar, as every "council" in the New Testament, [with no exceptions, is ]{.class_4257}a group organized to slander Christians and fight against the word of God (see Matt. 10:17, 24:10, 26:59; Acts 4:15, 5:41). You cannot insert ["the council" ]{.class_4258}of Matthew 5:22 into the Church Age for love nor money. \["Raca" and "fool" (Gk. ["more"]{.class_4257}),[ ]{.class_4257}as used in either Testament, refers to more than a stupid person: rather a person of wicked or immoral character in relation to God. Lamsa's "nursemaid" is a little too naive to merit serious consideration. In an effort to invent an Aramaic gospel to concede to critical theories, Lamsa has short-changed the Greek.\] The ["hell fire"]{.class_4258} [(Geenan tou puros) is ]{.class_4257}worthy of note, as it appears in the favorite passage of men who profess [not to believe in it. ]{.class_4257}The "descended into hell" has recently been quietly removed from the famous "Apostles' Creed" in the churches of the National Council, and the poor sheep never even bleated. Sheep don't bleat even when a wolf gets in the flock and kills a few.

5:23 Therefore if thou bring thy gift to the altar, and there rememberest that thy brother hath aught against thee;

24 Leave there thy gift before the altar, and go thy way; first be reconciled to thy brother, and then come and offer thy gift.

The plot thickens. If the passages are to be applied to Christians in the church, where is [the altar? ]{.class_4257}It cannot be the one in the building, for the altar we are told to approach is not in a building (Heb. 13:10)! It is all right to have an ["altar,"]{.class_4258} but what are you doing bringing [gifts ]{.class_4257}to it, and when did you ever see anyone [leave a gift there? You ]{.class_4257}can find it in Leviticus 26:1--3. You can find it in the millennium in Ezekiel 44--48. You can find it in the tribulation (Rev. 11:1--3). But where can you apply Matthew 5:23, as Jesus taught it, to the [people He taught it to? ]{.class_4257}We are at an impasse again unless we apply it [literally ]{.class_4257}to the Second Advent. \[God is going to force you to it. Unless you recognize the fact that the "Day of the Lord" (ushering in Jesus Christ's victorious and glorious reign on earth) is [the central theme of the Bible, ]{.class_4257}you may as well quit trying to study the Bible and go back to something simple like Delitzsch, Keil, Strauss, Pavlov, Hemingway, Marx, Hegel, Zarathustra, or James Joyce.\]

Of course, you can make a sermon from anything.

The verse plainly teaches us, [inspirationally, ]{.class_4257}that we should not try to offer God anything until we have made things right with our brothers and sisters in Christ. However, even this application is strained, for all you own is said to "belong to God" (1 Cor. 6:20), and the term ["brother"]{.class_4258} can be applied to saved or lost, depending upon which Book you are reading (see 1 John 3:14--15; 1 Cor. 8:11).

["The altar" ]{.class_4258}([thusiastarion]{.class_4257})[ ]{.class_4257}really presents a problem, when the Greek pops up. This altar used is not for prayer or salvation or the mass or communion; [it is the altar built five‑by‑five in the Old Testament for the burnt sacrifice of animals! ]{.class_4257}Let us quickly put our little sermon­ettes on the "sermon on the mount" in File 13, where our Lord will want to find them should He return before Colgate-Rochester and Union Seminary learn how to read Greek!!

5:25 Agree with thine adversary quickly, whiles thou art in the way with him; lest at any time the adversary deliver thee to the judge, and the judge deliver thee to the officer, and thou be cast into prison.

26 Verily I say unto thee, Thou shalt by no means come out thence, till thou hast paid the uttermost farthing.

Now, remembering that the context of the discourse is the Law, we can approach this saying with some understanding. In John 5:45, the Lord Jesus presents the Mosaic Law, not only as an "adversary" but as an "accuser." \[The use of "adversary" in the New Testament, otherwise, refers to Satan (see 1 Pet. 5:8).\] The only other time that we find adversaries are "adversaries" to the gospel (Phil. 1:28). The parallel account of this saying will be found in Luke 12:58, and the entire setting has to do with a "debtor's" prison, as in the later parable of Matthew 18:23--35. Proverbs 6:1--5 is the perfect illustration of the saying, showing how a man has clapped his hands to bind a monetary transaction and (Prov. 22:26) is caught by his verbal agreement, and then bound by this agreement to pay the money. From the standpoint of the Law, the illustration is well typified by Israel, saying: ["All that the ]{.class_4258}[Lord ]{.class_4267}[hath spoken we will do" ]{.class_4258}(Exod. 19:8), and thus putting themselves [in debt ]{.class_4257}(Gal. 5:3).

That the story can be applied practically is obvious, since Proverbs teaches the wisdom of "out‑of‑court agreements" (as does 1 Cor. 6:1--6), but the [doctrinal‑Kingdom­application]{.class_4257} is all too clear. The Law [will be observed again in the millennium ]{.class_4257}(Isa. 66:20--24; Col. 2:16), and the Judge at that day will be Jesus Himself (John 12:48). Doctrinally, then, this teaching is in agreement with the verses found in Matthew 5:17--20. No attempt to avoid the strict adherence to these laws, outwardly or inwardly, will succeed. The ["thou"]{.class_4258} in verse 26 indicates that no priestly perversion into the pagan "purgatory" idea can be applied. The man pays it, not some relative having masses said for him. Further, there is no reference to life after death in the illustration. Furthermore, there is no "purgatory" mentioned, and still further, not even the pope himself refers to "purgatory" as a "prison."

5:27 Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not commit adultery:

28 But I say unto you, That whosoever looketh on a woman to lust after her hath committed adultery with her []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_93}already in his heart.

Note how the Lord is not abrogating the Law, but is reinforcing it by referring to the "heart-application" of the commandment. He does not say: "But I say unto you, ye [may ]{.class_4257}commit adultery"; He adds weight to the commandment by revealing the intent behind its giving. Since He Himself [gave it, ]{.class_4257}then certainly He knew what He intended when He gave it. Job, in the Old Testament, understood the commandment even if the Pharisees did not, for Job observed Christ's ruling in Job 31:1--3.

The ["in his heart" ]{.class_4258}offers some trouble to the exacting Liberal who insists the Bible use twentieth‑century terminology---a very selfish thought in itself, since it would hamstring 400,000,000,000 people from understanding the Bible, from A.D. 100 until the twentieth century. "The heart," in Scripture, seldom refers to the physical organ, but is used exactly as any sane, twentieth-century man uses the word "nerve" or "guts" or "backbone," when referring to "courage." The heart (since it is the source of the blood and regulates the blood, which penetrates every part of the body) is spoken of as the seat or source of the inner feelings and emotions. Any popular songwriter understands the expression even if the commentators don't.

["Adultery with her" ]{.class_4258}brings up an application, which, though it is not doctrinal, carries tremendous spiritual complications in regard to "Christian living," for the ["with her" ]{.class_4258}carries with it the idea that [two parties ]{.class_4257}are involved in this violation of the law. Although the Jewish women of Christ's day were modest to the extreme (if compared to American or European women today), still the "EYES" as means of flirtation or seduction were plainly used by the women (see Prov. 6:25; Ezek. 23:40), and it can hardly be supposed that the ["looketh on" ]{.class_4258}of Matthew 5:28 is always the fault of the man. When this commonplace fact is considered (in view of 2 Pet. 2:14) and also the commandment for women to dress modestly (1 Tim. 2:9), we understand that unless a woman is careful in her deportment and dress, she may find the charge of "adultery" waiting for her at the judgment, even where she has not been guilty of [the act ]{.class_4257}(cf. 1 John 3:15)!

5:29 And if thy right eye offend thee, pluck it out, and cast it from thee: for it is profitable for thee that one of thy members should perish, and not that thy whole body should be cast into hell.

30 And if thy right hand offend thee, cut it off, and cast it from thee: for it is profitable for thee that one of thy members should perish, and not that thy whole body should be cast into hell.

This is the third reference to ["hell"]{.class_4258} in the Sermon on the Mount. We see immediately why the published sermonettes on "the Sermon" usually end with the "beatitudes," for shortly after the Beatitudes, Hell appears in all its horrible reality. Its appearance shocks the refined sensibilities of the Neo‑orthodox and Neo‑liberal, until they must either skip the passages or condense them into a vaporous cloud of "indirect suggestion."

["Hell," ]{.class_4258}of course, is Jesus' favorite subject. Of the scores of verses that He quotes from the Old Testament, He quotes Isaiah 66:24 more times than any other verse. We learn from this a little about correct "balance" in Christian preaching, and take note that any man who believes that Jesus is our "example," and that we are to "follow in His steps," will spend considerable time talking about "hell fire," and "the worm that dieth not" (Mark 9:43--48).

The serious statements of Matthew 5:29--30 indicate also that Hell is not [the grave. ]{.class_4257}How can a man be warned of the advisability of cutting off a hand or eye to keep from going to the Grave, [when he is going there anyway? ]{.class_4257}(The Jehovah Witnesses will have to join the liberals here to avoid answering the question.) All go to the "Grave," so why is Jesus giving you an [alternative to "going"? ]{.class_4257}There is no alternative unless "Hell" is a different place. Since the Greek word for grave is [mnemeion ]{.class_4257}and the Hebrew is [keber, ]{.class_4257}while the word for Hell is [hades ]{.class_4257}in the Greek and [sheol ]{.class_4257}in Hebrew, we can be quite certain that Rutherford, Russell, Cady, Fox, and White do not know what they are talking about. Hell is a place, and it is [not ]{.class_4257}the grave.

The "offending eye" of verse 29 is connected with the ["adultery"]{.class_4258} of verse 28. On the basis of this passage, and the one in Mark 9, Origen (184--254), the original "Bible scholar" (and the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_95}source of errors in the transla­tions since 1885), castrated himself, claiming Scriptural authority. [(If a man gets messed up he will get messed up in Matthew, Acts, or Hebrews.)]{.class_4257}

Now, let us approach the verse from a sane, reason­able, Christian position, remembering, of course, that we are still dealing [doctrinally ]{.class_4257}with a passage showing the exact nature of the Law in relation to Israel, which will come into effect at the Second Coming of Christ.

1\. The passage does not say that any amputation will get you to [heaven! ]{.class_4257}The amputation would only remove one particular instrument of sin, which may or may not affect salvation. To reconcile the passage with the rest of the New Testament, one could only teach that if the hand or the eye was the member [that kept the person from receiving Christ, then ]{.class_4257}such an action would be justified---providing the person then [received Christ ]{.class_4257}(John 1:12--13; Mark 8:36).

2\. In Matthew 10:28 (where a parallel passage occurs), the setting is clear: He is referring to the Second Coming---for look at verse 23! We must never forget that the message is to Israel, in view of an approaching Messianic kingdom, headed by a Jewish King, with no Christians present, no one "born again," and no "gospel" in the New Testament sense of the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ (1 Cor. 15:1--4).

5:31 It hath been said, Whosoever shall put away his wife, let him give her a writing of divorcement:

32 But I say unto you, That whosoever shall put away his wife, saving for the cause of fornication, causeth her to commit adultery: and whosoever shall marry her that is divorced committeth adultery.

The highly controversial passage has a corollary in Matthew 19:9. Fundamentalists, who believe in "one marriage in spite of everything," have gone to great lengths to Greek-ify and Hebrew‑ify the parallel passages in order to limit every man to one public ceremony; of course, American jurisprudence has stretched the passage to include the very Old Testament law quoted---a man or woman [can ]{.class_4257}get rid of their mate for anything from drunkenness to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_96}"sleeping in bed with the cat."

A very popular exposition (taken up since 1950) is the peculiar teaching that the ["wife"]{.class_4258} of verse 31 is merely an [engaged woman ]{.class_4257}(like Mary in Matt. 1:20), and that the passage has nothing to do with a man who is [already married to his wife. ]{.class_4257}Rather it applies to a man who finds out that someone has had a relationship with his fiancee, [before the nuptials. ]{.class_4257}This fantasy ignores the quotation that the woman is not sent away because she has been [unfaithful; ]{.class_4257}she is sent away [after ]{.class_4257}she comes together with her husband, and she is sent away on any [arbitrary ]{.class_4257}"finding no favour" in his eyes, because he hath found ["some" ]{.class_4257}uncleanness in her. In Matthew 19:3, the Pharisees pose this question, and Jesus answers without denying that "any cause" was sufficient grounds in the passage they quote (Deut. 24:1--4). In an effort to prove the "one‑marriage" theory, many Fundamentalists have gone overboard here and have unwittingly denied the very Bible they profess to believe. No [engaged ]{.class_4257}man in Deuteronomy 24:1--4 has been putting away his wife for unfaithfulness (as the case of Mary, in Matt. 1:18--22). Here, a [married ]{.class_4257}man is divorcing his wife because she has [stepped out on him, ]{.class_4257}and this is given as legitimate, Bible grounds for divorce, in contrast to the divorcing of a wife for "any reason" (Deut. 24:1--4).

5:33 Again, ye have heard that it hath been said by them of old time, Thou shalt not forswear thyself, but shalt perform unto the Lord thine oaths:

The citation is from Deuteronomy 10:20 and Deuteronomy 6:13, remembering, of course, that the Divine Author of Scripture has the right to freely quote and interpret His own work (as any secular author is allowed the same liberty).

5:34 But I say unto you, Swear not at all; neither by heaven; for it is God's throne:

35 Nor by the earth; for it is his footstool: neither by Jerusalem; for it is the city of the great King.

The ["But I say unto you" ]{.class_4258}has led many expositors (liberals foremost) to suppose that Jesus is in opposition to the Old Testament teaching. The heretic Marcion (third century) went so far []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_97}as to suppose that the God of the Old Testament was a different God than the New Testament; this teaching is still accepted by the translators of the [RSV ]{.class_4257}(see Zech. 12:10) and the Jehovah Witnesses. Some say that the Nestorians (A.D. 428) also leaned this way. Briefly, the unreasonable theory is that the God of the Old Testament was a cruel, tribal God whose existence is mentioned in Holy Writ only to indicate that "Mono‑theism" is the correct religion. This "cruel" God allows terrible, poetic, imprecatory psalms to be written, and goes around ordering babies to be killed (2 Sam. 12), etc., while Jesus is supposed to reveal to us a loving, "Heavenly Father," who wants all men in a "brotherhood." The Bible believer can tell at a glance that this is undiluted tripe of the cheapest brand. The "Loving, Heavenly Father" of the New Testament still sends people to Hell (Matt. 25; Mark 9) and still destroys cities to the point where women in them eat their children (Matt. 24; Mark 13). \[See parallel accounts in Josephus (first century) on the destruction of Jerusalem.\] Conversely, the Old Testament God is spoken of as a "Good Shepherd," with loving‑kindness and mercy (Psa. 51, 23). The "dual‑God" theorist needs a short course in corrective reading---with good glasses, of course!

["Swear not at all" ]{.class_4258}is qualified in Matthew's gospel by a sworn oath, in court, which is legitimate (Matt. 26:63). This "swearing" is a private matter of "reinforcing" a statement with an oath. ["Neither by heaven; for it is God's throne." ]{.class_4258}The statement is sure proof of inspiration, for He who made the tongue \[and called it ["a world of iniquity,"]{.class_4258} and "untameable" (James 3:5--8)\] puts His finger on the exact expressions this member uses. Who has not heard: "Heavenly days!", "My heavens!!", "My stars!", "Thank Heavens!!", etc.

["Nor by the earth" ]{.class_4258}goes further into such ejacula­tions as "My lands!", "Land‑o‑goshen!!", "Land's sakes!", "Land‑o‑love!", etc. ["For it is his footstool" ]{.class_4258}points to two facts: first, that this earth is a very small and insignificant planet in God's vast universe and is only really the experimental site of a moral issue which must be resolved on one planet before it can corrupt the entire universe (Isa. 40:15, 17). Secondly, the statement is in line with such references as Isaiah 66:1 and Psalm 110:1, where the earth and its inhabitants are said to be in such a position (compared to Christ) that He can rest []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_98}His feet on them when He comes as King. In this connection the rest of the verse follows naturally: ["Neither by Jerusalem; for it is the city of the great King." ]{.class_4258}Jerusalem, as God's future capital (to be dominated seven years by the Antichrist before this event), is spoken of as synonymous with the earth; Jerusalem is also His "footstool" (Lam 2:1).

5:36 Neither shalt thou swear by thy head, because thou canst not make one hair white or black.

37 But let your communication be, Yea, yea; Nay, nay: for whatsoever is more than these cometh of evil.

These limitations to oaths confine the speaker to oathless conversation, for swearing (by oneself) is forbidden. Such remarks as "By my head" or "As sure as I live" or "As sure as you breath" or "I'll be d‑‑‑" come under this category. As a matter of fact, when man's tongue is analyzed under the microscopic scrutiny of Holy Writ, the most harmless utterances betray his depravity: ["Gosh, Goodness, Golly!" ]{.class_4257}for ["God"; "geeminny, gee whiz, gee whillikers," ]{.class_4257}for ["Jesus"; ]{.class_4257}"my goodness," "my gracious," "great goodness," "gracious me" all smack of polite, sanctified, "Christian cussing." The Christian's ["grace" is ]{.class_4257}God's, and his ["goodness" is ]{.class_4257}Jesus Christ Himself (Luke 18:19; 1 Cor. 1:30).

["Yea, yea\..."]{.class_4258} may be paraphrased as, "When you say 'yes,' let it mean 'yes,' not 'maybe' or 'no'; when you say 'no,' let it mean 'no,' and not 'yes' or 'maybe.'" In plainer words, if you mean what you say, you don't have to reinforce it with an oath. (They used to say "a man's word was as good as his bond." This is the idea. If the man is true, honest, trustworthy, and full of integrity, he doesn't have to bind himself with an oath; his "yes" is [the bond that binds him]{.class_4257}!)

As Talmage used to say: "All language can be reduced to the response of receiving or rejecting Jesus." The response will be answered by Him someday: ["Come, ye blessed," ]{.class_4258}or ["Depart\...ye cursed." ]{.class_4258}There is no ["maybe" ]{.class_4257}to it.

5:38 Ye have heard that it hath been said, An eye for an eye, and a tooth for a tooth:

39 But I say unto you, That ye resist not evil: but []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_99}whosoever shall smite thee on thy right cheek, turn to him the other also.

The quotation is from Deuteronomy 19:21, and the ruling is limited to a magistrate's court, which is clear from Deuteronomy 19:18. To misapply verse 39 in Matthew 5, then, to such issues as "no self‑defense," "attempted murder," and international war is to betray ignorance of the Scriptures. We have here a perfect test case to test the adherents to the sermon on the mount; for when we examine the actual, practical work of the National Council of Churches in their efforts to stamp out plain Bible­preaching on radio and TV, we suddenly learn that the people who brag the most about "passive resistance," "tolerance," and "turning the other cheek," are the most fanatically bigoted and narrow‑minded people on the face of the earth.

When it comes to a practical application of the doctrine of "passive resistance," we find the liberal in the [aggressive, domineering, controlling position, ]{.class_4257}with a vicious, anti‑Christian spirit of intolerance against Bible Christianity. It manifests itself in un‑Christian commentaries, slanderous books against the virgin birth of Christ, slanderous statements in regard to the trustworthiness of Scripture, and a ceaseless underground, political maneuvering to control the airwaves, so that every voice of Fundamental Christianity can be silenced. These subjective bigots can hardly be true devotees of Matthew 5:39! They want you to obey it so they can ride over you, but they have never obeyed it and never will! The Bible believer who practices Romans 12:17--20 has a much higher standard, anyway, than the Liberal who merely talks about "the sermon on the mount." "After all is said and done---more is [said ]{.class_4257}than [done!"]{.class_4257}

In the context (and a text without a context is a pretext), the audience is Jewish, the scene is contemporary, and the issues are issues that relate to the Kingdom of Heaven when Christ comes (see Matt. 3:1--2). That the discourse cannot be applied doctrinally, [to this age, is ]{.class_4257}obvious from Christ's statement in Luke 22:36 and the clear‑cut statement to "resist evil" and ["resist the devil," ]{.class_4258}in James 4:7 and Hebrews 12:4. Kings, governors, executors, and jailers are []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_100}still in power in the Church Age (Rom. 13:1--6; Acts 25:11), and they are there to punish wrong‑doers, not to "turn the other cheek." The "passive resistance" of Ghandi (which didn't work any more than Chamberlain's) and the opposition to "capital punishment" are both the fruits of this misinterpretation of Scripture as liberals try [to make everyone meek whom they want to step on!]{.class_4257}

5:40 And if any man will sue thee at the law, and take away thy coat, let him have thy cloak also.

41 And whosoever shall compel thee to go a mile, go with him twain.

42 Give to him that asketh thee, and from him that would borrow of thee turn not thou away.

The crowning fiasco of the liberal interpretation is found in this passage. The National Council of Churches [loans money and does demand it again, and demands it with interest! ]{.class_4257}How can a man preach what he can't practice? Furthermore, "law suits" are no new thing to Liberals and Modernists; far from [submitting, ]{.class_4257}they hire lawyers and fight it out. What a joke! These arrogant, blatant, Sermon-on-the-Mount bawlers don't even apply their own sermon, cannot put it into practice, do not understand it, and cannot even live up to it! In the great "church fights" over land, property, churches leaving "presbyteries" and "conventions," you may rest assured the greatest devotees of the "Sermon on the Mount" are the quickest to sue and counter‑sue till the ground is covered with subpoenas. They don't "give the cloke with the coat"; [they fight to keep their ear muffs.]{.class_4257}

The [borrowing, ]{.class_4257}referred to here, means [without ]{.class_4257}interest (Deut. 23:19; Exod. 22:25), which brings up the intriguing proposition---[if all men are brothers and should practice the Sermon on the Mount, why do they continue to charge interest on loans, insurance policies, debts, mortgages, savings, etc.? ]{.class_4257}Someone has slipped the hitch somewhere. It is the Liberal, as usual. It is nearly time for someone to admit that the Sermon on the Mount is a doctrinal statement for a Jewish Kingdom at the Second Advent. We cannot waste 3,000 sheets trying to apply the verses directly to the Christian; it [cannot be done. ]{.class_4257}Inspirationally, all that can be said is []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_101}that the Christian should be charitable in his dealings and go further than the law demands, but this same teaching is much plainer, much more emphatic, and much more specific in the Pauline Epistles (Rom. 12:11--21).

5:43 Ye have heard that it hath been said, Thou shalt love thy neighbour, and hate thine enemy.

The saying is the first commandment with a rabbinical addition attached to it. The addition is not as farfetched as it looks, in view of the commandments of God which attended the "ten," for they included the utter annihilation of all descendants of Ham in Palestine (Exod. 3:8; Num. 33:52; Deut. 7:1--3; 20).

The "hating" of an enemy is found strongly voiced in the "imprecatory" Psalms, such as Psalm 139:21 and others. Jesus' "correction" of the saying, however, is not a real correction. A clumsy and careless reader (like the Fatherhood-of-God-brotherhood-of-man type) would miss such passages as Job 31:29 and Proverbs 25:21--22, but these passages show clearly that the Lord Jesus is teaching nothing "new."

5:44 But I say unto you, Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you;

45 That ye may be the children of your Father which is in heaven: for he maketh his sun to rise on the evil and on the good, and sendeth rain on the just and on the unjust.

["Love your enemies" ]{.class_4258}is the only new note injected into the Old Testament teaching, for ["love"]{.class_4258} goes a little beyond tolerating him, or even beyond giving him bread and water (Rom. 12:20). When it comes to applying the verse, several difficulties appear.

1\. The verse cannot be forced into a geo-political situation because you do not know who [your enemies are ]{.class_4257}until you face them on a battlefield. You could pray for Russia when your [real enemies ]{.class_4257}might be [Jesuit priests in Baltimore and New York!]{.class_4257}

2\. When your "enemy" does appear on the battle­field, you have orders from the government to [shoot him ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 13:1--6). And you are to obey the powers that be---President to T/5 Corporal. We hear a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_102}lot of talk these days from pacifists about "obeying court-orders" to integrate the races on the basis of Romans 13:1--6, but the same pacifist will [disobey ]{.class_4257}the same authority when it comes to R.O.T.C., West Point, Polaris missiles, and atomic war­heads. You see, the contradictions are not in the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 Bible; they are [in the conduct of its critics.]{.class_4257}

3\. The "cursing" and "persecuting" is, undoubtedly, a personal thing between individual men, not nations.

["Your Father"]{.class_4258} introduces an element that must be dealt with, for up till here [no one in the Bible ever refers to God being the Father of anyone but Israel]{.class_4257} (Exod.[ ]{.class_4257}4:22--23; Deut. 32:6, 18; Isa. 46:3). No one in Christ's audience, at this time, would be so foolish as to think that the oracles ever taught that God was [everyone's ]{.class_4257}Father. Jeremiah 14:22 makes it clear that Christ's reference to the ["rain"]{.class_4258} is an application to a Jew's two worst enemies: [Gentiles and scalywags (Publicans!).]{.class_4257}

["That ye may be"]{.class_4258} (Gk. ["genasthe," ]{.class_4257}subjunctive) is certainly not a doctrinal statement on "how to be born again, as a child of God." There is no "become" here, no crucifixion, no atonement, no regeneration, no new birth, no "body" of Christ, no "Holy Spirit" involved, and no Christians present. We must be careful not to [read in ]{.class_4257}what God has purposely [left out. ]{.class_4257}["On the evil and on the good...on the just and on the unjust" ]{.class_4258}does teach an important general doctrinal truth---the truth that the [mercies of God ]{.class_4257}are not to be confused with the [salvation of God ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 2:4--6). \[It is a common mistake of self‑righteous, religious people to suppose that because God has fed them and kept them out of hospitals and jails, that this is proof that they are prepared to stand before Him [in judgment. ]{.class_4257}This is a sad and even tragic mistake. God "puts up" with plenty that He does not approve of nor accept (Ecc. 5:8, 8:11).\]

5:46 For if ye love them which love you, what reward have ye? do not even the publicans the same?

47 And if ye salute your brethren only, what do ye more than others? do not even the publicans so?

48 Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven is perfect.

These verses can be applied in [some ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_103}way to the Christian, for they come near to the two "new commandments" given for Christian living after the resurrection (see Rom. 13:8--11; John 14:15, 15:10--12, 15:16; 1 John 3:23). However, some distinctions must be made to keep the account intelligible. All Bible believers should be careful enough and diligent enough to search the Scriptures and find the solutions to apparent discrepancies and differences. The "Liberal," unfortunately, will have neither the zeal, motive, sincerity, or honesty to do this; so he will come into a Satanic and unsolvable mesh when he arrives at Matthew 5:46--48. His reasoning runs as follows:

1\. I am to love everyone.

2\. This is "perfection" in God's sight.

3\. Therefore, if I love everyone, I can get to heaven by my own effort.

4\. I will love everyone and thus save my own soul and be ["like" ]{.class_4257}the Father!

This false logic leads to terrific and eternal consequences:

1\. It makes the Liberal put on a [false appearance of love ]{.class_4257}to people that he [inwardly despises.]{.class_4257}

2\. It forces him to accept other religions and religious convictions [contrary to the Bible.]{.class_4257}

3\. It makes him trust his own righteousness for salvation, and [this ]{.class_4257}forces him to reject the explanation and application of true Biblical salvation, [as God revealed it to Paul ]{.class_4257}(Gal. 1:1--10).

Now, lest we should fall into this trap and make the same disastrous error, let us "search diligently" (Acts 17:11) this portion of the Sermon on the Mount. In verse 46, the question is put to them reasonably (see also Luke 6:32--34). To put the question into colloquial Americana: "If you love just the people that are members of your church or support your radio program, or just those that send money to your program and belong to your group of churches, [don't unsaved drunkards do the same thing?" ]{.class_4257}There is an implied, "Yes, sir, [they do." ]{.class_4257}Verse 47: "If you go along and greet just your own kinfolks and your own relatives and the members of your own lodge and society, doesn't any [unsaved church‑member do the same thing?" ]{.class_4257}"Yes, sir, they [sure do."]{.class_4257} The question is searching. The []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw420.xhtml_page_104}answer is humiliating. It will take some searching to find the meaning of real love (John 5:39).

A. The Christian is to love everyone that is "born of God," in a special way that he does [not love ]{.class_4257}the world (1 John 5:1, 2:15).

B. The Christian is to love God first, above anyone and everyone, and love his neighbor as himself (Rom. 13:10).

C. This [love to your neighbor ]{.class_4257}does not include buying him a new car everytime you buy one, or letting him use your family the way you use them or enjoy them (Eph. 5:28, 33; Titus 2:4). (The modernistic teaching that "loving your neighbor" means "allowing him all the [social privileges ]{.class_4257}you allow yourself" is a communistic, socialistic idea that has its roots in Revelation 13 and Daniel 10. The Christian may ignore it without a second thought.)

D. The Christian is to restrain his love for the unsaved, when it comes to getting [involved ]{.class_4257}with them in things that will hurt his testimony or go against his Scriptural orders (2 Cor. 6:14--18; Titus 1:10--13).

E. The Christian should love the unsaved enough, however, to be willing to honor their conscience and their ideas on Christian conduct, where they do [not ]{.class_4257}contradict the Bible (1 Cor. 10:24--33, 9:16--23).

The passage then is more complex than it would appear on the surface. The light and frothy exposition in Matthew Henry and the [Interpreter's Bible]{.class_4257} is hardly helpful to anyone and does more damage than good. Most commentators tend to [over‑simplify ]{.class_4257}the passages that deal with Christian conduct and [over‑complicate ]{.class_4257}the passages that convict the unsaved of their sins!

["Be ye therefore perfect, even as your Father which is in heaven." ]{.class_4258}Notice how careful Jesus is [not ]{.class_4257}to say: "Our Father" (which would classify Him with His audience).

The ["perfect" ]{.class_4258}needs some Bible interpretation. Deuteronomy 18:13 indicates that the "perfection" has nothing to do with [sinless ]{.class_4257}perfection in the soteriological sense (as was taught by the Pelagians and Semi‑Pelagians and continued in some "holiness" groups). Job 9:20 makes it abundantly clear that if a man were perfect, he wouldn't dare say it or mention it, and John confirms this in 1 John 1:8--10.

To get the exact meaning of the word (without referring to the Greek, ["teleioi"]{.class_4262}),[ ]{.class_4262}the best place to go will be Colossians 1:28 and compare it with Philippians 3:12. In these passages the word ["perfect"]{.class_4258} is used of the spiritual‑minded Christian in his [present ]{.class_4262}state (Phil. 3:15), yet the greatest Christian himself says he has not [attained that state now ]{.class_4262}(Phil. 3:12). In both contexts, the subject is the [completeness ]{.class_4262}of the Christian, in fulfilling the requirements of Christ's sufferings, as a true disciple. "Sinless perfection" is found no where even remotely connected with the word ["perfect."]{.class_4258} The ["perfect," ]{.class_4258}therefore, in Matthew 5, is "perfection" in meeting the requirements of verses 45--47; i.e., treatment of people you don't like. Impartiality in dealing with people, then, is the teaching of this passage (being careful to observe, of course, the limitations of the other New Testament passages).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml}

# Chapter 6 {.heading_84-0}

6:1 Take heed that ye do not your alms before men, to be seen of them: otherwise ye have no reward of your Father which is in heaven.

2 Therefore when thou doest thine alms, do not sound a trumpet before thee, as the hypocrites do in the synagogues and in the streets, that they may have glory of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

3 But when thou doest alms, let not thy left hand know what thy right hand doeth:

4 That thine alms may be in secret: and thy Father which seeth in secret himself shall reward thee openly.

The ["alms,"]{.class_4258} of course, are gifts of charity given to people, as we find in Acts 3:3, 10:2. The whole setting is Jewish, including the synagogue, so we should not wander too far from the text for application. However, there are some noticeable examples of Christian conduct found in the passage. Second Corinthians 6:7--10, for example, is a perfect illustration of the contradictions found in fasting, "while appearing not to fast," and 2 Corinthians 6:6--10 is a fine dissertation on "hilarious" giving which does not keep account of its deficits. The reward a man gets for this giving is already his if he lets the world know about it, but the reward is future if no one knows about it but the man and his Saviour---see Luke 14:12--14. That this passage cannot be jammed down the Christian's throat is apparent; for the "giver of alms" in Matthew chapter 6 is rewarded openly by the Father in this life (Matt. 6:4), while "the giver" of 2 Corinthians chapter 9 is rewarded at the Judgment Seat of Christ (Rom. 14:10--12; 2 Cor. 3:8--14).

Inspirationally speaking, the passage is of peculiar appropriateness to the twentieth century, in view of the tremendous revival of "pledging" services going on in modern churches. In these services, the Christian is called upon to publicly declare how much he will give, and in some cases is even threatened with "breaking a vow" (Eccl. 5:2, 4, 6) if he does not meet the "pledge."
