Such public "sounding of the trumpet" is hardly consistent with the theology of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_107}the people who profess to "live by" the Sermon on the Mount!

6:5 And when thou prayest, thou shalt not be as the hypocrites are: for they love to pray standing in the synagogues and in the corners of the streets, that they may be seen of men. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

6 But thou, when thou prayest, enter into thy closet, and when thou hast shut thy door, pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly.

The word "hypocrite," [hupokrites]{.class_4257}, here (which is used by unsaved people so often to describe born-again Christians), can mean an "actor," or by another derivation "hyper-critical," [huper-krivo.]{.class_4257} Both translations suit the individual Pharisee, who personified hypocrisy (Matt. 23:3--23) and was critical to the extreme. They found fault with everything Jesus did and totally ignored the miracles. In conjunction with this attitude, there is an interesting study which every Christian should make to learn how religious leaders in any age (representing the non-literal school of Biblical theology) handle genuine Biblical religion. It will be found in Mark 2:5, 7, 3:5--6, 15, 22, 5:42--6:2--3, 6:43, 50--7:1--2; 8:9, 11. In every case the hyper-criticism follows a genuine miraculous demonstration of the power of God. These "hypocrites" are easy to follow. In every period of church history they are marked by a desire to correct the word of God where the words are not essential to doctrine, while at the same time they are perverting the words which deal with essential doctrines. The best verse describing them is Matthew 23:24, and if a twentieth-century "hypocrite" were to handle the passage, he would omit part of 23:14, revealing the Roman practice, and change ["at"]{.class_4258} in verse 24 to "out," and then brag about his being a more "accurate" translation.

The public praying (condemned here) is specifically Old Testament, Jewish praying (with an application to the Millennium), for when we come to New Testament praying, we find it is in public (Acts 27:35), in any place, at any time (1 Tim 2:8), without being censured.

The closet (Gk. [tameion]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_108}) is an inner chamber of some kind and, therefore, not entirely out of keeping with the use of the word ["closet,"]{.class_4258} today. The ["thy Father"]{.class_4258} again calls your attention to the fact that Jesus never uses the term ["our Father"]{.class_4258} so as to classify Himself with His audience. Practically speaking, the passage would teach that the "Christian" (and there are no Christians in any of the gospels---the word is a word applied to the New Testament Church after the resurrection, by their enemies---Acts 11:26) is to have times of secret prayer alone with the Lord, away from the hustle and bustle and hurry of social, business, and family life. The motive of his prayer at any time, public or private, should be to reach God, not to be heard. The "read prayers" and "written prayers," used by modern ecclesiastics, are typical of the thing that Jesus is here condemning.

6:7 But when ye pray, use not vain repetitions, as the heathen do: for they think that they shall be heard for their much speaking.

8 Be not ye therefore like unto them: for your Father knoweth what things ye have need of, before ye ask him.

The passage ends forever any dispute about who the ["your Father"]{.class_4258} is. In the Sermon on the Mount, He is certainly not Father of anyone but a Jewish believer in Israel, for the ["heathen"]{.class_4258} (Gk. [ethnikoi]{.class_4257}) is all too clear in indicating who the people are who do not have God for a Father. Again, the fabulous "Fatherhood-of-God" theory is blown to smithereens.

The ["vain repetitions"]{.class_4258} is a phrase that the papists find rather embarrassing---since the "Hail Marys" and the "Our Fathers" run into endless repetition on the rosary, as the benighted "heathen" try to make God hear them through ["much speaking"]{.class_4258}---so the [RSV ]{.class_4257}comes to the aid of the ["heathen"]{.class_4258} by the wild translation: "heap up empty phrases" instead of ["vain repetitions."]{.class_4258} This is a fatuitous rendering of all Greek manuscripts extant, and demonstrates again the feebleness of the scholarship on the [RSV ]{.class_4257}committee.

The verse teaches, inspirationally, that prayer is to be from the heart, in a sincere manner, and that it is not to be a rite or ritual at any time. (The formula of Phil. 4:6 is a much clearer and more specific exposition of the general truth taught here, and of course, as []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_109}such, it is a far better guide to true Christian prayer than the so-called "Lord's prayer" which is about to follow in the pre-resurrection, Jewish Sermon on the Mount.)

6:9 After this manner therefore pray ye: Our Father which art in heaven, Hallowed be thy name.

10 Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done in earth, as it is in heaven.

The reader should mark the expression ["pray ye."]{.class_4258} Jesus is always very careful never to call God "Our" Father, thereby classifying Himself with His listeners. He is telling His listeners to call God ["Our Father,"]{.class_4258} and thus He distinctly shows us that the ["Our Father"]{.class_4258} is not the "Lord's prayer" at all, since the Lord never prayed it, is not praying it now, and never will pray it. It is a "Disciples' Prayer" given to Old Testament, Jewish disciples before the crucifixion, resurrection, and Church Age. This should be kept in mind, as untold damage has been done by the irreverent and reckless expositions of "Liberals" on the famous prayer. Most of the trash published today by Sockman, Oxman, Weatherhead, Allen, Fox, Cady, Peale, Jones, and Co. is built around their hasty, clumsy, and presumptuous application of this prayer.

["Our Father"]{.class_4258} again refers to Israel, as is seen by the passage in which the prayer occurs (vss. 7--8, 6:32). Notice that ["Our Father"]{.class_4258} is never given to a Gentile to pray, nor does Christ anywhere in the Sermon on the Mount even intimate that God is "Father" to anyone but an Israelite! Exactly why Gentiles in the National Council of Churches keep patting themselves on the back with talk about this sermon being for them is a great mystery, for Jesus Himself clearly CONTRASTS a Jew, whose Father is God, with a Gentile who is worrying about bringing in a socialist kingdom (Matt. 6:32).

["Thy kingdom come"]{.class_4258} is interesting, since the Kingdom of God is said to be within, not on the earth (Luke 17:20). The Kingdom of Heaven can come on earth, as announced by John and Jesus (Matt. 3:2, 4:17); but for men to try to bring in a kingdom that God never intended to come on earth, and pretend that this kingdom is spiritual---while at the same time they advocate a totalitarian church-state with forced socialism---is overdrawing the bank account. The []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_110}"kingdom" the modernist is working to "bring in" is nothing but a Kingdom which is already here (Matt. 4:9). It has no connection with the New Birth, which admits a believer into the kingdom of God (John 3:3--8), nor is it connected with the kingdom hailed in Matthew chapter 6, which will be governed by Israel (Matt. 19:28). The "kingdom" of Liberal theology is an amillennial-Roman-Protestant-church-state monstrosity.

6:11 Give us this day our daily bread.

12 And forgive us our debts, as we forgive our debtors.

13 And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil: For thine is the kingdom, and the power, and the glory, for ever. Amen.

At this point, the Sermon on the Mount (and the so-called "Lord's prayer") hits another snag when expositors try to force it into Christian practice. The Christian can follow the prayer to verse 11, but verse 12 offers some difficulties, which the simpler-minded Modernists are liable to overlook. As a matter of fact, no Christian is forgiven on the basis of forgiving others; he is forgiven, and cleansed, when he judges his own sin and confesses it to Jesus (1 Cor. 11:30--33; 1 John 1:9). If his forgiveness is conditional on forgiving others, then the atonement of Christ (not yet accomplished at this point!) is insufficient, for "forgiveness and remission" of sins are clearly said to be part of the finished work of Christ in the believer (Col. 1:14; Acts 13:38--40). Numerous attempts have been made to spiritualize the passage away that says: ["as we forgive our debtors,"]{.class_4258} but the cold, hard facts are there. A Jew under Christ's earthly ministry at this time (and again when He comes) obtains forgiveness on a CONDITIONAL basis. You may as well face it; it's there.

Verse 13 closes the prayer. The prayer, as such, is divided into three petitions Godward and four manward. (This peculiar structure is found in several other places in the Bible; for example, in Matt. 13 the Lord Jesus gives three parables to the disciples and then four to the multitude. "New Jerusalem" lists three gates on each of its four sides. The seven letters to the churches in Revelation are divided into four, addressed in chapter 2, and then three in chapter 3. Where []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_111}seven does occur in the Bible, divided into component parts, the Holy Spirit seems to use the division of three and four, in place of five and two, or six and one.) The actual model for the "Lord's prayer" is found in 1 Kings chapter 8, and the prayer here is much more complete. Again, it is divided into four and three sections, and if the student will match the prayer of Matthew chapter 6 with this prayer of Solomon, he will find the entire prayer reproduced in detail in Solomon's seven petitions to God for Israel.

The ending of verse 13, ["thine is the kingdom,"]{.class_4258} is a disputed passage among dead-orthodox scholars. It is missing in Nestle's text and Cantabregensis (fifth--sixth century) and in Sinaiticus and Vaticanus (fourth) and Jerome's Catholic revision (fifth). However, the testimony for the reading, as it stands in the [Authorized Version]{.class_4257}, is ponderous. The reading is found in every Greek manu- script but ten of them, including Freerianus (fifth), which is older than Cantabregensis, Sigma, and Phi texts (from the sixty century). It is quoted by Chrysostom before Vaticanus or Sinaiticus were completed, and also cited by Isidore of Pelusium (370--440). The critical objections to this evidence take the form of several theories as nebulous and irrelevant as Darwin's "monkey story." The main theory is stated clearly by Prof. Michaelis of Bern (1948), and if the reader would like to waste several hours in worthless investigation, he may follow this author as he attempts to destroy the Received Text of the Bible. The [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 text is undoubtedly the correct reading.

6:14 For if ye forgive men their trespasses, your heavenly Father will also forgive you:

15 But if ye forgive not men their trespasses, neither will your Father forgive your trespasses.

If there was any doubt as to the "salvation" involved in verse 12, it is now dispelled. The passage is teaching salvation from sin (see parallel passage in Luke 11:4) and forgiveness on the basis of works. And now we have the key to the tremendous popularity which the "Lord's prayer" enjoys in Liberal circles! It enjoys the popularity enjoyed by any passage of Scripture which deals with salvation by works without mentioning Blood-Atonement! The passage will []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_112}forever enjoy a perennial "bravo" from the hands and mouths of self-righteous Pharisees who reject such passages as John 3, 8, 10; Revelation 13; Matthew 23; Romans 4, 10; and Galatians 3. Matthew 6:12, 14--15 falls like the sound of a melodious flute on the ear of the Christ-rejecting sinner who refuses to accept the Pauline revelation on the death and atonement of Christ for sinners. Matthew 6 becomes his hiding place; and here, safely entrenched in Scripture (with 1 Cor. 13; Psa. 23; and Exod. 20 for a bulwark), the unregenerate, religious hypocrite defies the grace of God and goes to Hell, preaching a social gospel of humanistic reform. The passage has been wrested (2 Pet. 3:16); unless it is left in its Jewish setting, with a primary doctrinal application to the Second Coming, it will destroy the soul of the man who seeks to justify himself by "following" its example.

6:16 Moreover when ye fast, be not, as the hypocrites, of a sad countenance: for they disfigure their faces, that they may appear unto men to fast. Verily I say unto you, They have their reward.

17 But thou, when thou fastest, anoint thine head, and wash thy face;

18 That thou appear not unto men to fast, but unto thy Father which is in secret: and thy Father, which seeth in secret, shall reward thee openly.

There are no deep or complicated things involved in the passage, as it strongly resembles the discourse on prayer and "alms-giving" in verses 1--6. The ["thy Father,"]{.class_4258} again, is a Jewish God who is a Father to Israel (see Exod. 4:22--23), as we find it throughout the sermon in contradistinction to Gentiles (Matt. 6:7, 32). The Fatherhood-of-God theory (invented by the modernist and forced into the gospels) is not found in the context at hand or remotely. The "actors," found here, let folks KNOW they are suffering the pangs of hunger by even putting "make-up" on the face to augment the fact that they are fasting. The "anointing and washing" of verse 17 is faintly reminiscent of 2 Samuel 12:20, where David appears to be happy while his heart is actually breaking.

The subject of fasting, for the New Testament Christian, is []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_113}thoroughly discussed in type and fact in both Testaments. According to the word of God, fasting is for Christians this side of the cross. This is evident by Christ's prophecy (Matt. 9:15), by the practice in the local church (Acts 13:2--3), and by Paul's life-long example (2 Cor. 11:27; 1 Tim 1:16). This fasting is to be true fasting and not done merely for the purpose of putting on a show (Zech. 7:4--7); it is to be accompanied by "right living" (Isa. 58:3--7). (The Lenten fasting that saves up money for a drunken orgy or a gluttonous banquet when the season is over, or before it begins, is just one more example of God-dishonoring sin among religious people.)

6:19 Lay not up for yourselves treasures upon earth, where moth and rust doth corrupt, and where thieves break through and steal:

20 But lay up for yourselves treasures in heaven, where neither moth nor rust doth corrupt, and where thieves do not break through nor steal:

21 For where your treasure is, there will your heart be also.

"Earthly subtraction is heavenly addition," or as someone has well said: "Treasures in heaven are only laid up as treasures on earth are laid down." That the verses have some application to the Christian must be true, for in the Pauline epistles, a great deal of time is spent convincing the Christian that his true riches are spiritual, not earthly, and that he is to set his affections on things above, not on things on this earth. Even further, he is to live in the light of his heavenly home and the immediate return of his Lord (Col. 3:1--4, Rom. 8:18; 2 Cor. 4:17--18; 1 Thess. 4:13--18).

For the Christian, the "treasures" are listed specifically in 1 Corinthians 3:12 and are defined as: gold---Christ's deity (see the articles of furniture in the tabernacle), silver---redemption (see passages on Judas' price and Christ as a "ransom" for men), precious stones---men and women won to Christ (see Mal. 3; Zech. 9:16; Prov. 17:8). Truly, the treasures are to be put in the "Heavenly Savings Company." \[The only "stocks and bonds" Paul had were on his feet and on his hands (Acts 16:24, 26:29).\]

6:22 The light of the body is the eye: if therefore thine eye be single, thy whole body shall be full of light.

23 But if thine eye be evil, thy whole body shall be full of darkness. If therefore the light that is in thee be darkness, how great is that darkness!

The singleness of eye is apparently aimed at convincing the listener that he should have singleness of purpose. We find this well described in Colossians 3:22--23 in connection with the eye. "EYE-SERVICE" is compared to singleness of heart, which of course implies a fixed motive, a firm resolve, a steadfast resolution to walk in the light.

The passage is of great contemporary importance, as devotees of the Sermon on the Mount have failed to evaluate television, in the light of the passage. The eyes are the source of sin in Genesis 3:6, and no reader of David's or Achan's tragic falls would tend to overlook the power of the eye. Job considered the eye so important that he made an agreement with it not to look at the wrong thing (Job 31:1), and David, who learned his lesson only too well, said finally that he would "set no wicked thing before his eye" (Psa. 101:3). In the "last days" the eye will be the ruin of most of the male population of the earth (2 Pet. 2:14), and God considered the matter of such terrific importance that he specifically warned Israel about "leaving pagan pictures around," after they entered the land (Num. 33:52).

(A side-note: 40 percent, or at least 30 percent, of every film shown on TV or in a theatre is pitch-black darkness, for the shutter on the projector is shutting all light out between frames on the film! The child of God should give this seeming side-note a good deal of consideration, in view of 1 Thess. 5:1--8.)

6:24 No man can serve two masters: for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to the one, and despise the other. Ye cannot serve God and mammon.

The verse is an excellent statement on the age-old problem of getting "on-or-off the fence." (Elijah asks the question of Israel in 1 Kings 18:21 and gets the standard answer---silence. It reminds one of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_115}the colored man who was asked by the judge if he pleaded guilty or innocent. The man replied: "Ah stands mute.") It lies deep within human nature to be so broadminded on a moral decision that a man never commits himself, anywhere, where it will cost him any prestige or income. Men stick fast to an "open-mindedness" which keeps God from "getting to them" about their sins. (Joshua accosts Israel with the same proposition in Josh. 24:15.) As an old colored preacher down South said, when asked about "decisions" in his church: "Dey done concreted on me."

Despite the fact that millions of people spend a lifetime trying to prove they can do service to ["God and mammon"]{.class_4258} (Syriac for "riches"), our Lord tells us plainly here that it cannot be done. Those who try it are spiritual fornicators. They live two lives caught between two fears and two loves, and they succeed at neither. They wind up "holding to one and despising the other," and if it is mammon they are after, they wind up despising the word of God, even when they don't profess to do so. The greedy, "Christian" landlords with extravagant furnishings, elegant abodes, and sumptuous apparel, trying to corner the market on commodities, show their slip sooner or later (note 1 Tim. 6:6--11). Our Lord says: ["Ye cannot serve God and mammon,"]{.class_4258} and that is it. An old-time preacher once said: "You are entitled to everything that God gives you along the line of God's purpose for your life, but you are entitled to nothing you get when you step out of God's purpose for your life to get it." If you get mammon while serving God, that is one thing, but if you try to serve God and get mammon at the same time, that is something else.

6:25 Therefore I say unto you, Take no thought for your life, what ye shall eat, or what ye shall drink; nor yet for your body, what ye shall put on. Is not the life more than meat, and the body than raiment?

26 Behold the fowls of the air: for they sow not, neither do they reap, nor gather into barns; yet your heavenly Father feedeth them. Are ye not much better than they?

In Luke 12:22, we find a similar account, made all the more impressive by the striking illustration of the rich fool who was []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_116}building "bigger barns" when he should have been building a coffin. Verse 32, in Matthew 6, indicates that this constant obsession with material things is a characteristic of Japheth's descendants (Gen. 10:5). The Christian is admonished along the same lines of this passage, in 1 Timothy 6:6--11, so there is definite New Testament application of the warning. Jesus appeals to a simple illustration in nature to drive home His thought, and Luke 12:6, as well as Matthew 10:29, reinforce the thought by showing the comparison between a man and a bird. \[There are thirty species of fowl mentioned in Scripture, and they can all be located with a good concordance. Where they occur, they usually represent "demons" (Matt. 13:32; Rev. 18:2; Mark 4:4, 15).\]

6:27 Which of you by taking thought can add one cubit unto his stature?

Jesus follows the line of his discourse by getting His audience to "analyze their worry problems." In modern terminology, He is "releasing tensions" by making them face the fact that worry doesn't solve a thing; trust is what will accomplish the result. The [Amplified Version ]{.class_4257}of the New Testament, along with the [New Bible Commentary]{.class_4257}, adopts the curious attitude of the [RSV ]{.class_4257}in mishandling the verse. The[ "cubit,"]{.class_4258} here, is likened to a length of time, instead of the height of the stature; the apology for this transposition being the statement in Psalm 39:5. However, here, the Greek (the favorite escape mechanism of the scholars to flee from the conviction of the English text!) comes home with a grinding screech to produce "helikia," which in Luke 19:1--3 is exactly the same word! Scripture-with- Scripture produces the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 reading, not the [RSV ]{.class_4257}reading. We have, here, another example of the arbitrary and fatuitous handling of the text by the "scholars." It is most certainly "height'' here discussed, and not "age. " The "span of life" in the [RSV ]{.class_4257}is a modernistic interpolation, bearing no resemblance to any Greek manuscript in existence: Western, Egyptian, Byzantine, or Alexandrian.

6:28 And why take ye thought for raiment? Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow; they toil not, neither []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_117}do they spin:

29 And yet I say unto you, That even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.

30 Wherefore, if God so clothe the grass of the field, which to day is, and to morrow is cast into the oven, shall he not much more clothe you, O ye of little faith?

The line of reasoning continues in the same vein. What is the use of worrying about materialistic things? Doesn't God bother to take care of flowers? Or even oxen? (1 Cor. 9:9). If God is that careful with his natural and brute creation, will He not provide your need also? Again, we must carefully locate the audience in Matthew 6:28 for proper theological understanding. No such promise is given to any unsaved man (John 3:36), but only to the saved in the New Testament (Phil. 4:19). The ["how they grow"]{.class_4258} indicates sermonic material will be found in the lily; a study of the lily would yield some great truths, for every similitude God gives is for a distinct purpose (Hosea 12:10).

["The grass of the field"]{.class_4258} includes all flora and fauna, as well as the vegetable kingdom, which is evident by the reference in 1 Peter 1:24; note the ["flower"]{.class_4258} in the verse. There is some likeness in the passage to the comparison of saved and lost, for the oven, with the fire, is mentioned in connection with the lost (Matt. 13:42). The inspirational teaching, then, is "if God is careful to preserve even the unregenerate and to supply their physical needs (Matt. 5:45), will He not much more provide all to those that are trusting Him?" The application can not be strained, however, for the remainder of the New Testament makes it plain that the ones who are really "trusting" Him are those who have received Jesus Christ as their own Saviour from sin (John 1:12--13; Gal. 3:26). We are constantly in danger of constructing the universal "brotherhood theory," in the Sermon on the Mount, if we stray from the direct and historical context. The ["O ye of little faith"]{.class_4258} seemed to be a favorite rebuke of Jesus in connection with Israel at the time of his earthly ministry; notice the frequent references (Matt. 14:31, 8:10, 17:17).

6:31 Therefore take no thought, saying, What shall we eat? or, What shall we drink? or, Wherewithal shall we be []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_118}clothed?

32 (For after all these things do the Gentiles seek:) for your heavenly Father knoweth that ye have need of all these things.

Now the Sermon on the Mount "comes to a head," so to speak, for we find that Jesus (with full knowledge of His use of terms) has been speaking to Israel of their God in contradistinction from the God of the pagan Germans, Spaniards, Italians, Swedes, Englishmen, Chinese, and Americans!! This is rough ground, but we may as well believe it. Verse 32 plainly states that the Gentiles are compared unfavorably with "the children of the Heavenly Father." There is no way to avoid the implication unless one subscribes to the butchered-up theology of Rauschenbusch or Schleirmacher, or the more recent ravings of their offsprings. The ["our God"]{.class_4258} of Acts 2:39 is a Jewish God, as is the ["your God"]{.class_4258} of Acts 3:22. The fact that God would be the personal Father of every Gentile who believed on Jesus Christ as Saviour is a new revelation, which does not even appear in the Bible in a formative statement until Acts 10! \[Somewhere along the line our liberal cowbirds have stolen the Pauline concept and Johannine concept of the new birth into God's family and inserted it back into the gospels (where Jesus is speaking of God's Fatherhood to Israelites). Yet at the same time, they have rejected Paul's and John's teachings on HOW to be "born again!" The liberal offers the lopsided and perverted system of following Christ to become a child of God---to a Father who is only Father to those who are born again, before they follow Christ. It is a nice mess.\] The real meaning of the passage is clear. The Sermon on the Mount is an Old Testament dissertation given to Jews, before the crucifixion, to delineate the rules and principles of the coming Millennial Kingdom.

6:33 But seek ye first the kingdom of God, and his righteousness; and all these things shall be added unto you.

34 Take therefore no thought for the morrow: for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself. Sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof.

Verse 33, for the first time in the gospel, introduces a new note---the veiled hint that more than a Jewish Messianic Kingdom is approaching. Here, for the first time, we find the expression ["the kingdom of God,"]{.class_4258} and associated immediately with it is "God's righteousness." The verse is leaning toward a spiritual standard here; here is the suggestion of another kingdom, which may be more than ["meat and drink"]{.class_4258} (Rom. 14:17).

Now, in Mark 1:15, we find that the ["kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} is one of the designations of John the Baptist's message. This similarity has caused a tremendous amount of confusion in the ranks of evangelical Christians, and has led a thousand scholars and preachers in those ranks to throw up their hands and quit "rightly dividing," and just say outright: "The Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Heaven are the same thing." However, this is a mistake in basic, fourth-grade English. God is not Heaven (Gen. 1:1). The Bible does not teach the eternity of the heavens nor the pantheistic-materialism of the nebular hypothesis. A bird can fly up to heaven without flying up to God, and there can be clouds in the heaven without their being in God. However, so much study must be undertaken to discern the difference (as we find the words used in Scripture), that the average scholar throws up his hands and surrenders before he has fired a full clip.

God's righteousness is defined in both Testaments (Micah 6:5; Rom. 10:1--4) as being something totally apart from the deserved merits or works of man. If a man is going to seek this righteousness, he will have to find it outside of his own righteousness! And this is where the self-righteous liberal misses the mark completely and destroys his soul with his misapplication of the Sermon on the Mount, for the only time we find works connected with the commandments, in regard to salvation, is in the passages that deal with "the tribulation" (Rev. 12:17, 14:12, 22:14) and the "kingdom-age" (Matt. 5--7). Since the Liberal has already closed his eyes to the book of Revelation (see remarks on 3:1--2) and has thrown away the key to the Old Testament and the Second Coming, then, of course, there is not the slightest chance that any amount of reasoning will convince him that works are not the means of salvation in this age; not even the crystal clear passages of Acts 15:9--11; Ephesians 2:8--[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_120}10; or Romans 10:9--10.

["The kingdom of God,"]{.class_4258} then, is closely associated with a moral and spiritual righteousness not found necessarily in the ["kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4258} Notice that the ["kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} includes "bad fish," "tares," "lost virgins," "unsaved servants," and people who lose the "word" (Matt. 13:25). The "new birth" is not mentioned as a requisite for the ["kingdom of heaven,"]{.class_4258} but for the ["kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} (see John 3:3--5). Thus we learn that although the two kingdoms are associated, they are not the same. If the reader would like to study the subject exactly, he may begin by observing that the Pre-Adamic conflict in the universe was over a "throne" (Isa. 14:12--16), that Adam was given "dominion" as a king (Gen. 1:26--28), as Noah (Gen. 9:1--10), and that this literal, visible, earthly, heavenly (in the sense of literal stars, galaxies, nebula, and star clusters), Messianic, Davidic kingdom is the primary object of God's dealing with the "earth" and its people: this is the ["kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4258} All the scientists seem to have overlooked the Biblical plan for populating the universe AFTER the Millennium! The Kingdom of Heaven means just what it says---a universal, literal universe kingdom, with the earth as capital (Rev. 21--22; Luke 1:30--33). \[See publication, [Sure Word of Prophecy,]{.class_4257} (formerly, [The Kingdom of Heaven versus the Kingdom of God,]{.class_4257} 1969.\]

["The kingdom of God,"]{.class_4258} on the other hand, is something distinctly spiritual and related to the moral righteousness of God Himself (Rom. 14:17). The two kingdoms meet in:

1\. Adam before the fall.

2\. Jesus Christ at the First Coming.

3\. Jesus Christ at the Second Coming.

\[The classic illustration is the dying thief, availing himself at once of the ["kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} (["remember me!"]{.class_4258}) and the ["kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} (["when thou comest into thy kingdom!"]{.class_4258}). Here, he obtains in one petition God's righteousness and the Messianic, Davidic King! The perversion of the word Kingdom to "kingly power," found in the [RSV ]{.class_4257}at this text (Luke 23:42--43), is just another example of the bias and ignorance which went into the making of the translation.\]

How careful our Lord is to say that if the moral righteousness of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw585.xhtml_page_121}God is obtained first, then the literal, Davidic, Messianic Kingdom will follow-look at verse 33: ["and all these things shall be added unto you."]{.class_4258} The ["you," ]{.class_4258}everywhere you find it in the sermon, is a Jew under the Old Covenant.

Here, then, is the veiled hint that before the Messianic, Davidic Kingdom comes (with its victory for "Israel in the flesh"), there must precede a turning to the Messiah (from sin) and spiritual entrance into the Kingdom of God. This is the first mention in the New Testament (in the order of books) where we find the word. It is extremely significant.

Verse 34 gives a fine moral teaching applicable to any age, time, or person, in any place. "Most of our troubles never come," "Why worry when you can pray?", "Don't put off till tomorrow what you can do today," etc., are all illustrations of the same truth. You have enough trouble living for God one day at a time (1 Cor. 15:31; Eph. 5:16), without "borrowing trouble" from tomorrow. The evil of today (notice Jesus' anti-positive view of life!) is sufficient to give you all you can handle.

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml}

# Chapter[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_122} 7 {.heading_84-0}

7:1 Judge not, that ye be not judged.

2 For with what judgment ye judge, ye shall be judged: and with what measure ye mete, it shall be measured to you again.

The passage has probably suffered more abuse in the hand of the Liberals and church leaders than any other passage in the Bible, outside of the so-called "Lord's prayer." By constant repetition of the passage, the unregenerate church leaders of America have cowed Protestants into shutting their mouths against anything and everybody, and these same rascals have even used the verses, theologically, to prove that you cannot tell whether anyone is saved; therefore, you have no business dealing with them personally about their soul's salvation. Matthew 7:1--2 is one of Satan's favorite passages, along with Acts 2:38; Matthew 7:12; and Hebrews 10:26--30. \[You may as well face it; the devil quoted Scripture in Matthew 4, and there is no reason for anyone to believe that he has ever repented of the practice since (2 Cor. 11:13--15).\]

Now doctrinally speaking, what is in the text?

["Judge not."]{.class_4258} The imperative command is immediately confronted with ["He that is spiritual judgeth all things"]{.class_4258} (1 Cor. 2:15), and ["Why even of yourselves judge ye not what is right?"]{.class_4258} (Luke 12:57), and ["We shall judge angels"]{.class_4258} (1 Cor. 6:3). The Liberal overlooks these texts, or pretends that Paul's authority is secondary to the pre-crucifixion, Jewish discourse in Matthew 7. ["That ye be not judged."]{.class_4258} Notice that there is no mention of Hell, and if the issue is judging a man to be lost, no one has to judge that, for the man is already judged and condemned (1 Cor 5:13; John 3:18).

The rule in Matthew, therefore, is a general rule covering rules of conduct between Jewish saints, with a direct reference to the Kingdom-age and the judgment already mentioned in chapter 5 verse 22.

To amalgamate the Bible information on the subject, we learn that the statement (far from teaching the simple idea of the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_123}Modernist, that you are not to preach against anything or anyone) teaches a very complex system of Christian judgment and discernment.

1\. The Christian is to judge things in his own life and the lives of others (1 Cor 11:13, 31, 5:3, 6:2, 4).

2\. He is not to judge a brother as unsaved if that brother disagrees with him on MINOR doctrinal points of practice (Rom. 14:1--10, 13).

3\. He is not to judge anything hastily or when not required or unmercifully or from an unscriptural standpoint.

However, he is to clearly mark out and avoid preachers and teachers who do not believe the word of God, or who profess to believe it but, practically, deny it by their preaching and teaching (Rom. 16:17--18; Phil. 3:18--19; Matt. 7:15). He had better judge the "fruit" quickly and accurately by the standard of absolute truth (John 17:17; 1 Thess. 5:21). The maudlin and foppish Liberal teaching that we are not to judge whether or not a doctrine is true, according to Divine revelation, is nothing but a Satanic snare for lazy and feebleminded dignitaries, who are too dormant or too wicked to ["search the scriptures."]{.class_4258}

7:3 And why beholdest thou the mote that is in thy brother's eye, but considerest not the beam that is in thine own eye?

4 Or how wilt thou say to thy brother, Let me pull out the mote out of thine eye; and, behold, a beam is in thine own eye?

5 Thou hypocrite, first cast out the beam out of thine own eye; and then shalt thou see clearly to cast out the mote out of thy brother's eye.

The illustration is a good one, and it is driven home very forcibly by the sarcasm, to which our Lord so often resorts. (See the expressions in Matt. 23 and particularly Luke 13:32.) The ["mote"]{.class_4258} is equivalent to a "splinter," while the ["beam"]{.class_4258} is our modern designation for a "2 by 4" plank. Literally, then, in modern American: "What are you looking at that splinter that got in his eye when you got a two-by-four stuck in your own? And how can you get []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_124}his out with a pair of tweezers, when you can't even see it, because you've got a stud stuck between your own eyelids?" (No true "American" translation has ever been written!)

The lesson is obvious. Before we can preach to others, we must preach to ourselves (Rom. 2:21--24), and even after that, in a Christian spirit, considering the fact that there still may be a ["mote"]{.class_4258} in our own eye (Gal. 6:14). The verse not only condemns those who preach against the sins of others (which all Gospel preachers are called to do), but it also has a double edge. It plainly condemns all socialistic and religious humanitarians, who are trying to "do something for mankind" to make the world "a better place to live in," when they themselves are in an unregenerate, depraved state from having rejected the gospel!

7:6 Give not that which is holy unto the dogs, neither cast ye your pearls before swine, lest they trample them under their feet, and turn again and rend you.

This time, the text has suffered more at the hands of the Bible believers than at the hand of the "Sermon-on-the-Mountites." The Christian uses this verse as proof that he should not witness to some people since they despise him or refuse to accept the truth at his hand. However, a Scripture-with-Scripture interpretation of this verse (and this is the only correct method---see 1 Cor. 2:13) yields something more logical and far more in keeping with the body of revelation.

In 2 Peter 2, we find a drawn-out discussion about two classes of apostates---false prophets and false teachers (and the reader may notice that the context of Matt. 7 is this subject, in vss. 15--23). These false prophets and teachers are listed as the very animals we find in Matthew 7:6---that is, ["dogs"]{.class_4258} and ["swine."]{.class_4258} In 2 Peter 2:22 we find both animals used to represent the male prophet and the female teacher. (Notice the ["his"]{.class_4258} and ["her"]{.class_4258} in the text---2 Pet. 2:22.) When applied to the passage in the Sermon on the Mount, a double truth unfolds itself before our eyes. If we can now locate the ["pearls"]{.class_4258} and ["that which is holy,"]{.class_4258} we have the correct interpretation of the text. The ["pearls"]{.class_4258} are not hard to find.

Through the apostle John, the Holy Spirit connects the pearls []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_125}with the names of men (Rev. 21:12), and throughout the Old Testament we notice this peculiar association of ["precious stones"]{.class_4258} with human beings (Prov. 31:10, 11:22; Mal. 3:17; Exod. 28:21). The true Church, composed of human beings, is even called a ["pearl of great price"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 13:46). There can be no doubt about "interpretation," as the Bible is self-interpreting and defines its own terms independently of human tradition, psychological guesswork, and philosophical speculation. Pearls represent people.

Nor do we find any difficulty in locating ["that which is holy,"]{.class_4258} for anything belonging to God is said to be "holy"; notice the numerous references (1 Cor. 7:14; 2 Pet. 1:18; 1 Cor. 3:17, etc.).

Our text now becomes illuminated. No one is telling us, here, that it is wrong to witness to people who despise the gospel message. The Lord Jesus is here telling us that it is wrong to take the people we win to Him (which compose His body, the church) and turn them over to false teachers! It is also wrong to take anything that belongs to God and give it to false prophets! \[Look at 2 Pet. 2:22 in conjunction with Matt. 7:6. Nothing could be clearer. As a matter of fact, Matt. 7 goes right on with the discussion of these "pigs and dogs," who are interested in trampling Christians under their feet and "rending" the men responsible for the Christian's conversion (Phil. 3:2, Matt 7:15--23).\]

Of course, in the context of the Sermon on the Mount, the saying would have primarily a Jewish application, but since the ["pearls"]{.class_4258} are already mentioned in the Old Testament, as people, and since any Jew understood what "holy things" were, there is no straining of application.

7:7 Ask, and it shall be given you; seek, and ye shall find; knock, and it shall be opened unto you:

8 For every one that asketh receiveth; and he that seeketh findeth; and to him that knocketh it shall be opened.

The formula here, given for prayer, is a little more Old Testament than New, for the reader will observe that prayer in the name of Jesus is not given (nor is it given in the so-called "Lord's prayer" of Matt. 6), indicating that this will be instruction for a Jew praying to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_126}the Father. One of the reasons why Liberals and Catholics are so fond of the "Our Father" is that it is an Old Testament form which ignores the future and more complete revelation of correct prayer in John 16:24, "in the name of Jesus." The "Our Father" is more conducive to ecumenical efforts between Buddhists, Atheists, Jehovah's Witnesses, Agnostics, Jews, Protestants, Beatnicks, Catholics, and Existentialists, than an out-and-out "CHRISTIAN" prayer in the name of Jesus Christ (Phil. 2:9--10).

There is spiritual truth, however, found in the instructions of Matthew 7:7--9, for the Pauline epistles make it clear that the Christian prayer should keep on asking, seeking, and knocking (Phil. 4:6; 1 Tim. 2:1--2; 1 Thess. 5:17). He should, however, ask ["in the name"]{.class_4258} of Jesus Christ (Col. 3:17), and not follow the Sermon-on-the-Mount example, which Bible-perverters like to use, since it avoids placing emphasis on the Lordship of Jesus Christ over Mohammed, Buddha, Leibnitz, Kennedy, Spinoza, Descarte, Kierkegaard, Schweitzer, Confucius, Zoroaster, and the popes.

7:9 Or what man is there of you, whom if his son ask bread, will he give him a stone?

10 Or if he ask a fish, will he give him a serpent?

11 If ye then, being evil, know how to give good gifts unto your children, how much more shall your Father which is in heaven give good things to them that ask him?

Jesus now reasons with His Hebrew disciples by calling their attention to the care which a human father exercises over his child when it comes to granting requests. Again, we notice that the ["your Father"]{.class_4258} is limited in scope to Old Testament Israelites in the historical context of the passage. Jesus makes it very plain (in spite of modernistic objections) that the Father of His audience is not the Father of the Gentiles, but stands in opposition (Matt. 6:7--8, 32) to them in this matter. \[Incidentally, God is never spoken of anywhere in either Testament, in any Hebrew or Greek manuscript, as the "Father of Gentiles." He is the Father of Israel as a nation (Exod. 4:22) or the Father of the born-again Bible believer (Rom. 8:14--17), but never "Father of Mankind" or Universal Father or Father of Gentiles or Father of Races, etc. All these concepts are derived from []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_127}the out-moded and archaic philosophies of pagan "scholars."\]

A stone is mentioned in connection with ["bread,"]{.class_4258} reminding us that Christ was tempted to do this in Matthew 4:3. The two passages show that although the Father can give literal bread and not stones, He may sometimes withhold literal bread until a man learns to live by the word of God first (see Matt. 6:33). The fish is compared to a serpent, reminding us that there is some connection. The student can examine it further by the studies of the fish species in connection with the Noahic covenant, and in connection with Satan (as a serpent) being called a "water monster" (Gen. 7:14, 9:10; Job 41:1; Rev. 21:1). (See [Commentary on Genesis,]{.class_4257} Gen. 7.)

The inspirational application is obvious even on the surface. The bread and fish are desirable, the stone and serpent are not. If a human father (whom Jesus calls ["evil"]{.class_4258}!---vs. 11) knows better than to give a serpent for a fish, doesn't God have as much sense and as much concern over His people? The ["evil,"]{.class_4258} in verse 11, is said in such a matter-of-fact way that it is a wonder that the National Council of Churches has never taken note of it. Jesus in Luke 17:20 makes a statement concerning the nature of the kingdom of God, but when it comes to making a statement on the nature of men, He says calmly and flatly: ["If ye then, BEING EVIL\..."]{.class_4258} which, of course, is in line with the entire body of New Testament revelation (Rom. 3:10--23; John 2:23--24; Eph. 2:1--3). The ["good things,"]{.class_4258} here, are said to be ["the Holy Spirit"]{.class_4258} in Luke 11:13, and can be associated doctrinally with the text, as the Holy Spirit is said to be a ["gift"]{.class_4258} in Acts 8:19--20, and ["gifts"]{.class_4258} are mentioned here, in Matthew 7:11. The ["good things,"]{.class_4258} then, can refer to the gifts of the Holy Spirit, if the Christian wishes to make an inspirational application to the Church Age (Eph. 4:8, 1 Cor. 12:1--12).

7:12 Therefore all things whatsoever ye would that men should do to you, do ye even so to them: for this is the law and the prophets.

The parallel is found in Luke 16:16 and is often referred to by ignorant Christians as "The Golden Rule." It is actually the original for Kant's plagiarized, "Categorical Imperative," and as such, it antedates the philosopher's thinking by several hundred years. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_128}Christians, misled by the hyper-Arminian radicals in the Liberal camp, have gotten the curious notion that this "Rule" is some kind of "Plan of Salvation," or that it will in some mysterious way justify a man at the judgment, if it is carried out in this life. This gross misconception can be traced (as most misconceptions) to ignorance of the Scripture (Matt. 22:2), for in plain, black, printed English, the same Lord who quotes the "Golden Rule," defines it as ending with John the Baptist as a "Plan of Salvation" (Matt. 11:13; Luke 16:16). The law and the prophets are UNTIL John. "The Golden Rule," then, is a statement of Old Testament righteousness, as it should practically affect the believer of the Law and Prophets. That is, this is what the Rule is according to the Author of the Rule.

Liberals have done incalculable damage with their perverted and irreverent wresting of this Scripture. By coupling it up with the second commandment, they have brought "Scriptural" pressure to bear on all kinds of issues, from trying to mix races in washrooms to recognizing Red China as a "Christian nation." No real philosopher, with any regard for intellectual honesty, could be long deluded by this liberal hoax, for he would try to apply the rule where the Liberal tells him it applies, and he would quickly find that it is a twentieth-century joke. I might want my neighbor to give me \$50,000 tax-free, but that does not mean that I should give it to him (especially if he is going to hire a lawyer with it to sue me for messing up his lawn!). I might want a man to wake me up at three in the morning, but that doesn't mean I should treat him from that same premise. A man might want a man to give him a "fifth" of whiskey, but that does not mean I would appreciate the same man offering it to me. The "Golden Rule" had better stay where it is the sum total of practical teaching of the Old Testament Law and Prophets---to a Jew (Luke 16:16; Matt. 7:12)---and not move over into church politics, international intrigue, and Christian charity. As a "separatist" once said to a woman on a train, who was fumigating him with her cigarette smoke, "I wished I chewed tobacco, lady, 'cause everytime you blew it in my face, I'd spit it in yourn."

7:13 Enter ye in at the strait gate: for wide is the gate, and broad is the way, that leadeth to destruction, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_129}many there be which go in thereat:

14 Because strait is the gate, and narrow is the way, which leadeth unto life, and few there be that find it.

The teaching, doctrinally, is obscure to the hearers, since the great discourse on Jesus Christ Himself, as the Way (John 14:1--6), has not yet been given. To his hearers, Jesus is elaborating on a familiar doctrine from Proverbs 14:12 and 16:25; He is using, for a practical point of reference, the city gates of some city. (In the Kingdom age, yet to come, the reference may have an application to Zech. 14:16 or Rev. 21:24.) Practically, He is teaching that the majority is wrong, as usual, when it comes to matters of spiritual salvation. Since the majority of mankind has always thought that "if you live up to your religion" and "do the best you can" you'll be all right, it may be stated, dogmatically, from our Lord's assertion, that they are, without a doubt, dead-wrong. The [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators reach a peculiar pinnacle of superstition at this point, and when they approach Matthew 7:13--14, they suddenly deviate sharply from all common sense and Greek manuscript authority, in order to make the verses teach "salvation by works." Their denominational bias is glaringly set forth in the [RSV]{.class_4257}, which reads: "EASY is the way that leadeth to destruction" (vs. 13), and "HARD is the way that leadeth unto life." This peculiar and original monogram appears more un-Christian by the minute when we consider that if Jesus is ["the way"]{.class_4258} (John 14:6), then the [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators have called Him "hard"---which is exactly what the hell-bound sinner called Him in Matthew 25:24! A truly unique situation! The really amusing part of the false rendering in the [RSV ]{.class_4257}is the fact that no Greek manuscripts give the [RSV's]{.class_4257} reading. The Greek manuscripts are all in line with the highly accurate [Authorized Version ]{.class_4257}(1611).

7:15 Beware of false prophets, which come to you in sheep's clothing, but inwardly they are ravening wolves.

["False prophets"]{.class_4258} turn out to be one of Jesus' favorite themes, and He gives warning of them many more times than He gives any positive teaching concerning the Beatitudes. The entire New Testament, as a matter of fact, is greatly concerned with the "false []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_130}prophets" and is equally concerned with the danger of the Christian being deceived by them (2 Pet. 2; 1 Tim. 4; Jude 4--18; Matt. 23; 24:5, 11; Luke 6:26; 1 John 4:3; 2 Cor. 11:12--14).

This seems to be another area of the "Sermon on the Mount" which lies untouched by the Liberal pulpit, for the only "false prophets" ever denounced by the Papists and Liberals are the Bible-believing brand of people who profess that what Jesus said is superior to what the Papists and Liberals say Jesus "taught." The "false prophet" in the Bible turns out to be a man who loves long robes (Mark 12:38), loves to be called ["father"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 23:9), and denies the efficacy of the Blood Atonement (2 Pet. 2:1--2). In keeping with the "broad way," it is said that ["many"]{.class_4258} will follow the false prophets (2 Pet. 2:2; Matt. 24:11, 24), and they will do what they do ["in thy name"]{.class_4258}---the name of the Lord (Matt. 7:22)! They can be spotted immediately in either Testament by their attitude toward the revealed authority of the word of God (Mark 7:1--13; Gen. 3:1), which, of course, is alibied by appeals to "recent findings," "inductive methods," "age of enlightenment," "critical research," and kindred "pitch" lines.

The false prophet here, as elsewhere, is classified with the canine species (2 Pet. 2:22; Rev. 22:15; Phil. 3:2), yet as such, he parades as a sheep. Note that the warning is not against thieves, harlots, drunkards, dopers, or Communists, but against men who appear as sheep! A sheep would appear innocent, gentle, and meek, and this again accurately describes the men we are to carefully "look out" for. They do not appear as wolves. The ideal false prophet would be a shrewd, slick, talented, well-bred, mild-mannered, polished individual who operated primarily through sophistry, subtility, scholastic appeal, and flattery. Inside, he is a wolf (John 10:12).

7:16 Ye shall know them by their fruits. Do men gather grapes of thorns, or figs of thistles?

17 Even so every good tree bringeth forth good fruit; but a corrupt tree bringeth forth evil fruit.

18 A good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit, neither can a corrupt tree bring forth good fruit.

19 Every tree that bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_131}down, and cast into the fire.

20 Wherefore by their fruits ye shall know them.

The passage is a classic for discerning false doctrine in practical living; it may apply to Christ's Jewish audience or the modern believer. Martin Luther said, "If you don't know what kind of fruit the tree bears, shake the tree." A rotten tree will cast off rotten fruit when you shake it and a good one will drop good fruit. The example is so plain it needs little explanation to the reader. The Greek student might be interested in the peculiar twisting of the passage in Nestle's Critical Greek Testament (supposedly the best in print), where the words for ["bring,"]{.class_4258} in verse 18, are changed from the [AV ]{.class_4257}to another Greek word. The change is peculiar, as here, the [AV ]{.class_4257}just happens to agree with Sinaiticus and Vaticanus---the very two manuscripts which delight the Westcott and Hort followers, as they pervert and destroy the [Authorized ]{.class_4257}Bible.

The evil fruit-bearing tree is destined for the fire, which agrees again with 6:30 as a distinct reference to Hell. This corrupt fruit (and its source) is identified in the context as the work of false prophets; they can be easily spotted by anyone versed in Scripture due to the following traits:

1\. They minimize sin.

2\. They humanize God.

3\. They deify man.

4\. They teach self-atonement.

5\. They delay repentance.

6\. They avoid preaching on Hell.

Their fruits are like them, and verse 15, in the same paragraph, indicates what kind of fruit this will be. It will have to come from slick, talented, well-bred, mild-mannered, polished wolves, in sheep's clothing: thistles that look like figs, thorns that look like grapes, corrupt fruit that looks like good fruit, tares that look like wheat, wolves that look like sheep.

7:21 Not every one that saith unto me, Lord, Lord, shall enter into the kingdom of heaven; but he that doeth the will of my Father which is in heaven.

22 Many will say to me in that day, Lord, Lord, have we []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_132}not prophesied in thy name? and in thy name have cast out devils? and in thy name done many wonderful works?

23 And then will I profess unto them, I never knew you: depart from me, ye that work iniquity.

The teaching sums up the discourse on "false prophets." It is a dramatic summation pointing out clearly how close the false thing can be to the real thing. Notice the elements: ["Lord, Lord", "In thy name", "Wonderful works,"]{.class_4258} etc. The prophet could clearly fool 90 percent of the Liberals in America today and perhaps 50 percent of the Christians. His ability to deceive will be fabulous, according to the plain statements of 2 Corinthians 1; 2 Peter 2:2; and Matthew 24:11.

["He that doeth the will of my Father"]{.class_4258} is later defined in the Gospel of John as believing on Jesus Christ as Saviour (see John 6:29, 39--40).

["In that day"]{.class_4258} rings like a clarion in the ears of the one who "has ears to hear," for it is that very familiar expression from the Old Testament, occurring hundreds of times, and always pointing to the Advent---never to the "Last Judgment" (Isa. 3:7, 18, 4:1--2, 5:30, 7:23, 10:20, 27). Whoever these miracle workers are, they are denied entrance into the Kingdom of Heaven (see remarks on Chapter 3), at the end of the Tribulation.

["In thy name have cast out devils"]{.class_4258} shows the extent to which the false prophet is able to deceive, for in the New Testament we have an exact case of this noted (Acts 19:13--15), and Judas himself, who was said to be a devil (John 6:70--71), was given a commission to ["cast out devils"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 10:5--8).

["I never knew you"]{.class_4258} implies an intimate and friendly knowledge, for God knows all and sees all. (In Amos 3:2, we have a similar use of the expression to show that the "knowing" is more than just "acquaintance"; it means practically, "I never knew you as real believers, friends, and followers, but I certainly did know you as hirelings, professors, and reprobates." Hosea 8:4 is another case exactly like it, where God pretends not to know what has gone on.)

The passage has often been used by the "salvation-by-works" crew to prove that a born-again Christian can "lose it." However, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_133}only a slight study of the context would eliminate any such conjecture. Jeremiah 14:14 identifies the prophets of Matthew 7:21--22 as self-called and self-deceived. There are no "Christians" present here. The ["I never knew you"]{.class_4258} banishes forever any thought of "Christians" being represented in the passage, for Christ does know the Christian intimately and could never say that He never did (see John 10:14--27).

7:24 Therefore whosoever heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them, I will liken him unto a wise man, which built his house upon a rock:

25 And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell not: for it was founded upon a rock.

26 And every one that heareth these sayings of mine, and doeth them not, shall be likened unto a foolish man, which built his house upon the sand:

27 And the rain descended, and the floods came, and the winds blew, and beat upon that house; and it fell: and great was the fall of it.

The conclusion of the Sermon on the Mount presents a homey illustration of two men: a "doer" and a "hearer" (see James 1:22). Since this is an excellent place for the self-righteous to try to get saved by works, we are not surprised to find thousands of people trying to get to heaven by doing "The Sermon on the Mount." However, the judgment here is not fire, but wind and rain; and the audience to whom it is addressed are not Christians, and they are not "born again." To try and ram the inspirational teaching of the parable down the throat of the unregenerate, as a "hope" of getting to heaven, is certainly a despicable trick.

The ["house,"]{.class_4258} here, can only mean one thing, where it is found in Matthew. It is the ["house of Israel."]{.class_4258} (The slightest examination of Matt. 12:43--47 and 23:38 brings this fact out.) The believing remnant of Israel is the wise builder, and the Christ-rejecting Israelite is the foolish builder. Further pressing of the passage, doctrinally, will only lead to disaster, as it is strongly flavored with the Old Testament "salvation by works" seasoning.

Inspirationally, one may say that the builders were alike in that---

1\. They both heard.

2\. They both saw the need.

3\. They both made an effort.

4\. They both were confident they would stand the test.

5\. They both were exposed to the testing.

The two builders were different, however, in that---

1\. They responded to the message differently.

2\. The foundation they chose was different.

3\. And the results of the testing naturally differed.

7:28 And it came to pass, when Jesus had ended these sayings, the people were astonished at his doctrine:

29 For he taught them as one having authority, and not as the scribes.

The insinuation is that the scribes were broadminded liberals who spoke dogmatically on nothing. There is no way to avoid this insinuation given to us by the Holy Spirit in Matthew's text. Christ's dogmatic teaching is set in contrast to the "we-may-reasonably-assume" type of instruction dished out by the "scribes." This is important as the word "scribe" is closely akin to "script" and "Scripture," and hence identifies the uncertain "scribe" with the modern day translator and commentator. A few volumes of reading in the [Interpreter's Bible ]{.class_4257}will drive the point home forcibly. If the student will borrow (don't buy!) a set of the [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_4257}or the [Interpreter's Bible]{.class_4257}, he will be astonished to find the first-century scribe still teaching with no authority, for every page is replete with such unauthoritative guesses as: "we may assume," "it is supposed," "in all probability," "it is highly probable," "it is not impossible," "he may have meant," "it would seem under the conditions," "perhaps it should have been," "let us not be dogmatic," "it would be reasonable to suppose," and so on into the night.

Our Lord Jesus Christ, to the contrary, says: "It is THIS WAY." There are no "seems," "supposes," or "probabilities" about it. It is no wonder that his audience was astonished at this didactic and dogmatic form of discourse. They had been feeding 400 years (Malachi to John the Baptist) on the dried-up husks of the Talmud, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw666.xhtml_page_135}Midrashim, Kaballah, Mishna, and Gemara as taught by the "fathers" (Matt. 23:9).

Thus ends the Jewish "Sermon on the Mount" to Old Testament Hebrews under the law. It sums up the law and lays down the principles of the millennial kingdom to come. It contains indirect Christian doctrinal references and numerous inspirational helps. It is nowhere given in the Bible as a plan of salvation for anyone in the Church Age, and nowhere in it can anyone find the great doctrines of salvation stated clearly. Jesus chose a "successor" to do this work (Acts 9:16; Rom. 15:16).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml}

# Chapter 8 {.heading_84-0}

8:1 When he was come down from the mountain, great multitudes followed him.

This phrase, following the Sermon on the Mount, clearly draws a distinction between the similar passages in Luke 6. These two messages (Luke 6 and Matt. 5--7) are often confused in the mind of liberal and Catholic scholars as being part of the same discourse, but the discourse of Luke 6:17--49 is given on a plain, not on a mountain. And furthermore, at this time in Matthew 8, the disciples have not been called; whereas, all twelve are present in Luke 6.

8:2 And, behold, there came a leper and worshipped him, saying, Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.

3 And Jesus put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will; be thou clean. And immediately his leprosy was cleansed.

4 And Jesus saith unto him, See thou tell no man; but go thy way, shew thyself to the priest, and offer the gift that Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them.

The parallel account is in Mark 1:44, and in both cases Jesus cautions the man not to spread the news abroad. The answer to this curious instruction is found in the fact that the events of Luke 4 take place before this time; that is, the temptation and the rejection of His official public ministry in the synagogue at Capernaum (Luke 4:27--29). Christ's Messiahship had already been rejected by the leaders, and strangely enough, the crowning blow which prompted their rejection was the case of Naaman the leper. In Matthew 16:20, we find the Lord still cautioning His disciples not to mention that He is THE "Christ," the Messiah. The leper calls Jesus ["Lord"]{.class_4258} here in verse 2, which is the first time in the New Testament we find Him addressed as such. This is suggestive, for the last man to talk to Him before He died was a criminal, who said: ["Lord, remember me\...."]{.class_4258} Lepers and criminals! No wonder it was written that He was ["numbered with the transgressors."]{.class_4258}

(Numbers 12:12 would indicate that leprosy is a type of sin, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_137}as such, the leper and his contact with Jesus makes an excellent sermon. Leprosy could only be healed by God; it could spread if not checked; it could pollute houses and clothes as well as bodies, and a blood sacrifice was required for the cleansing \[Lev. 13--15\].)

["And touched him, saying\...."]{.class_4258} The words are fraught with drama, for no one could touch a leper in the Old Testament, not even the priest, who could only "look" on the diseased man. When Christ, the Sinless One, touches the leper, He is in a sense violating the law of Moses (Lev. 13:46); yet the Mosaic priest is allowed to touch the leper by means of shed blood (Lev. 14:14). The lesson is obvious. Christ bore our infirmities as well as our sins (Matt. 8:17), and as our great High Priest (Heb. 3:1), He can apply the remedy for the leprosy of Sin.

["The gift that Moses commanded"]{.class_4258} is linked with the requirements for cleansing in Leviticus 14:19--24, and the testimony ["unto them"]{.class_4258} is a shocker. Consider, what would a priest think, who was commanded to cleanse lepers, when a cleansed leper walked in whom someone else had cleansed, and that "Someone" was only a "carpenter's son"! What a blow to the "father" (Matt. 23:9)!

8:5 And when Jesus was entered into Capernaum, there came unto him a centurion, beseeching him,

6 And saying, Lord, my servant lieth at home sick of the palsy, grievously tormented.

7 And Jesus saith unto him, I will come and heal him.

8 The centurion answered and said, Lord, I am not worthy that thou shouldest come under my roof: but speak the word only, and my servant shall be healed.

There are several centurions mentioned in the Bible, and all seem to have the divine stamp of approval on their characters. It is a centurion who bears witness to Christ's righteousness and deity at the crucifixion (Matt. 27:54; Luke 23:47), and it is a centurion who has the privilege of hearing Simon Peter's Gentile, gospel message preached for the first time to a group of Gentiles in the New Testament. It is also a centurion who is responsible for Paul's release at the time of "examination by scourging" (Acts 22:26).

["Sick of the palsy."]{.class_4258} Palsy is here distinguished from a number []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_138}of other diseases in the New Testament, among which we find fever, bloody fluxes, blindness, impotency, halt, lame, withered, maimed, crippled, demon-possession, lunacy, and others. Contrary to Dark Age Catholic theology, not all sickness is due to "unclean spirits," and the charge that the Bible is "unscientific" in treating mental ailments as "demon possession" is itself unsound. Some ailments are and some are not, as witnessed to 1 Thessalonians 5:14---["comfort the feebleminded."]{.class_4258}

8:9 For I am a man under authority, having soldiers under me: and I say to this man, Go, and he goeth; and to another, Come, and he cometh; and to my servant, Do this, and he doeth it.

10 When Jesus heard it, he marvelled, and said to them that followed, Verily I say unto you, I have not found so great faith, no, not in Israel.

11 And I say unto you, That many shall come from the east and west, and shall sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and Jacob, in the kingdom of heaven.

12 But the children of the kingdom shall be cast out into outer darkness: there shall be weeping and gnashing of teeth.

13 And Jesus said unto the centurion, Go thy way; and as thou hast believed, so be it done unto thee. And his servant was healed in the selfsame hour.

The centurion's profession, regarding his authority in military service, gets to the heart of the Saviour. With a homely simplicity found only among the lower class of the "uneducated," he says in effect, "If I have to obey my superiors and the men under me have to obey me, then You ought to be able to control anything under You, because You are the Lord." Jesus calls this reasoning ["faith,"]{.class_4258} and contrasts it sharply with the unbelief of the Jews in the synagogue who thrust Him out to the brow of the hill to murder Him, just a few weeks before (Luke 4).

The ["many"]{.class_4258} ["from the east and west" ]{.class_4258}is a clear reference to the part which the Gentiles will have in the Millennial blessings to come, which of course were promised primarily to Israel (see notes []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_139}on Chapter 3). Matthew shields the hidden mystery revealed by Paul, but the past references to Gentile women in Christ's genealogy (Matt. 1) and the kings from the East (Matt. 2) and this incident in Matthew 8, all betoken something to come which will include more than the physical seed of Abraham. ["The kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} looms up again, reminding the careful reader that Jesus is still heralding an earthly, literal, physical, visible, Davidic Kingdom, prophesied by the Old Testament prophets (see 3:1--2, 4:17). There is no mention of Jew and Gentile being "one in the Body of Christ," the Church, and here is where many of the theologians deceive themselves. The subject here is not the church, but ["the kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4258}

["The children of the kingdom,"]{.class_4258} in the next verse (vs. 12), should straighten out all difficulties for the gullible, who have mistakenly identified the ["kingdom"]{.class_4258} with the "church"; for if the two are the same, then here is a plain statement that "children of God," in the Body of Christ, will go to Hell! Another truly unique situation. No, there can be no doubt about interpretation. The subject refers to the future, visible return of Christ to an earthly kingdom (Rev. 20:1--6, 5:10); any spiritualizing of the passage only wreaks havoc with the other 31,000-plus verses in the Testaments.

["The children of the kingdom"]{.class_4258} cast into outer darkness must have been quite a blow for Christ's audience. The statement is enigmatical, fitting in with another statement in Matthew 13:11 that the Kingdom of Heaven has some "MYSTERIES" connected with it that the postmillennial and amillennial scholars are not going to be able to solve. You cannot explain how "the covenant children of the kingdom" go to hell, if the "kingdom" is the "church"! Here, the amillennial and postmillennial Catholic systems of interpretation break down completely and never recover.

["As thou hast believed."]{.class_4258} In this statement the Lord is honoring genuine faith, which must not be confused with "presumption." The twentieth century has seen a revival of "presumption" in the name of "faith," attended by a vast cortege of "faith miracles," "faith healing," "faith revivals," etc. There is a current feeling (which is capitalized on by "Unity" and "Christian Science") that if you "only believe," something will happen. This, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_140}however, is a tragic error leading eventually to loss of real God-given faith. The classic example, of course, is the Roman Catholic mass, where the communicant is told to have faith and believe something God DID NOT tell him to believe: i.e., that the "elements" are the literal body and blood of the human Christ, who had a literal body and blood. The results of such "faith" are disastrous, for the communicant is asked to not only violate common sense in the name of religion (which sometimes is genuine in the case of "Bible miracles"), but he is asked to have religious "Bible" faith in a hoax which is contrary to Scripture---and then he must expect God to bless it! The eating of literal blood is strictly forbidden before the Old Testament (Gen. 9:1--6), during the Old Testament (Lev. 17), and under the New Testament (Acts 15). Real "faith" is believing what God revealed, the way He revealed it, under His conditions of revelation. The centurion in our text shows this kind of faith.

8:14 And when Jesus was come into Peter's house, he saw his wife's mother laid, and sick of a fever.

15 And he touched her hand, and the fever left her: and she arose, and ministered unto them.

The appearance of Peter's mother-in-law poses a dilemma for the papists, for the pope is unmarried and forbids his priests to marry. Peter, the first successor to the popes (according to Roman superstition), is married and travels with his wife (1 Cor. 9:5)! This is not the least or the greatest of the embarrassing texts found in the Bible, in regard to the papal system, but it is a good sample. No pope ever followed Peter's example. How ridiculous it would be for a man to claim Matthew 16:16--18 as "his church," founded on Peter, when not a single successor of Peter even followed Peter's Bible example!

The healing of the fever brings us through three demonstrations of Christ's power to heal, all immediately following the Sermon on the Mount. They are affected on leprosy (vss. 1--4), palsy (vss. 5--13), and fever. One more major healing occurs in the chapter---the healing of the demoniac of Gadara. Occurring where they do, the healings are significant, for they fulfill in type the coming Millennium and answer to the prophecies of the "Day of the Lord," in the Old Testament (see Ezek. 34:16; Isa. 33:24, 35:6, etc.).

["And he touched her hand."]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_141} This passage is responsible in a large measure for a resurgence of teaching on the "point of contact," in the Holiness movements. Briefly, the teaching is that since Jesus is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Heb. 13:8 taken out of context), then all one needs is a physical point of contact with Him or His Spirit to be healed (Acts 19:12 is cited to prove this). The teaching, however, runs badly afoul of the New Testament in a dozen places.

In the first place, Christ is not physically on the earth today. In the second place, He is not the same yesterday, today, and forever in every matter, for He was a baby! He was on a cross! He was thirsty at one time. Let us not over exercise our Biblical imagination. In the third place, the reader was warned in the introduction of getting balled-up in Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews. In the fourth place, the "point of contact" was only used during apostolic times, and then not always---for example, right in our text (Matt. 8:13), a man was healed "long distance" with no point of contact. In the fifth place, no one in the Bible is sick (nor is sickness mentioned) until the Jews go into Egypt. No miracles of healing take place till Exodus 2--4 where Moses is commissioned to bring them OUT of Egypt. Healing is primarily connected with Israel, as a nation. We are not saying "exclusively." We are saying: "You should watch your step and watch your Scriptures before you go presuming and calling something 'faith'."

8:16 When the even was come, they brought unto him many that were possessed with devils: and he cast out the spirits with his word, and healed all that were sick:

17 That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet, saying, Himself took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses.

These two verses are the great battleground of "whipping-post" theology and the "no-man's" land between the Healing-Holiness groups and the Calvinistic groups, which confine healing to occasional "in the will of God" situations. The verses would bear some careful attention.

["All that were sick."]{.class_4258} The first thing that strikes us about Jesus' []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_142}healing campaigns is that there were no failures. Like Simon Peter, in Acts 5:16, there were no cases of anyone "not having faith to be healed." As a matter of fact, there are no cases like this in the Bible. On the grounds of one positive statement in Acts 14:9 (look out for Acts!), it is assumed that if you are not healed it is because you do not "have the faith"; however, this is a wild assumption. In Mark 2:5, a sick man is healed, who has no faith at all and is healed on someone else's faith. Furthermore, healing continued where no one even believed on Christ (see Mark 6:5). In James, we are told that the fault in a cure lies not with the sick man, but with the man who is praying for him (James 5:15--16). This is hardly the tent-healer's "line of goods."

["That it might be fulfilled."]{.class_4258} The reader is quick to notice the change of quotation. Where Isaiah reads: ["borne our griefs, and carried our sorrows,"]{.class_4258} Matthew quotes: ["took our infirmities, and bare our sicknesses."]{.class_4258} The change has caused radical and critical scholars to assume Jesus is quoting the mythological Septuagint (LXX in Greek), instead of the Hebrew, or from some "second Isaiah," etc. But these attempts to beat the word of God out of its authority overlook a much simpler explanation. It is a common, established, literary principle that any author may take the liberty of freely quoting his own work. It is assumed the author knew the original intention of what he wrote, and if he wishes to give a "loose" quotation, then he is entitled to it. The Holy Spirit, in Matthew, is giving you a second meaning of Isaiah's words; there is no contradiction. (We find the same situation in 2:18 and 15:4.) As Matthew quotes the text, it is apparent that healing IS IN the atonement. To avoid this deduction is cowardly. The passage in Isaiah is on the atonement, even if the passage in Matthew is referring to Christ's "active obedience" in the flesh before the Atonement.

The Holiness people carry the reasoning a little farther; if healing is in the atonement (as well as salvation from sin), why cannot anyone by faith, avail themselves of it? After all, do we not avail ourselves of salvation by faith? The argument is logical. It is so plausible that it is accepted blindfolded by millions and reinforced with such texts as 1 Corinthians 12:9 and 3 John 2. However, let us []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_143}not stop here. Let us really examine the atonement. Are healing and salvation the only things Christ took care of when He died? Not quite. When we examine the atonement carefully, we suddenly discover:

1\. He died for the curse God placed on nature. Thorns came from this curse, and thorns were jammed on His head.

2\. He died for the curse placed on animal life, for animals were vegetarians before the fall, and they return to this state at the Advent (Isa. 11), when the work of the Atonement is complete.

3\. He died for the curse placed on the world itself, for He is said to have bought this world (Matt. 13:38, 44) by His blood, as well as the purchase of the redeemed sinner.
