4\. In short, He died not only for sin, but for sickness, poverty, cruelty, damnation, destruction, and even physical disturbances, which came as a consequence of sin (Rom. 8:20--23).

Now we can find the correct meaning of "healing in the atonement." Healing is IN THE ATONEMENT, but you cannot get all the benefits of the atonement now. Many of the benefits await the Second Coming (Phil. 3:20--21). You cannot claim a lion (by faith) to start eating straw, but this is in the atonement; you cannot claim (by faith) for your body not to rot in the ground, but a new body is in the atonement (1 Cor. 15); you cannot claim (by faith) that all the earthquakes and tidal waves will stop, but this is in the atonement. In short, God can heal you and may heal you, and praise the Lord if He does. He may do it by medicine (1 Tim. 5:23; Isa. 38:21) or without medicine (Acts 3:7), or He may leave you sick (2 Tim. 4:20; 2 Cor. 12:5--8); but healing is still in the atonement and God is still the author of any healing that is done.

8:18 Now when Jesus saw great multitudes about him, he gave commandment to depart unto the other side.

19 And a certain scribe came, and said unto him, Master, I will follow thee withersoever thou goest.

20 And Jesus saith unto him, The foxes have holes, and the birds of the air have nests; but the Son of man hath not where to lay his head.

21 And another of his disciples said unto him, Lord, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_144}suffer me first to go and bury my father.

22 But Jesus said unto him, Follow me; and let the dead bury their dead.

The Lord Jesus, during His earthly ministry, seemed to have a peculiar aversion to large crowds. Time after time we find Him fleeing from them either for prayer or for rest or for purposes known only to Him (see Matt. 14:13; Mark 1:45, 3:9; Luke 6:12). Though He had compassion on them and healed them, He seems to have felt the need for being away from them at seasons (John 6:15).

The scribe who comes to Jesus is undoubtedly sincere, but like many a scribe since his time, he had not "counted the cost." ["Master, I will follow thee"]{.class_4258} is one thing to say---Simon Peter said it (Luke 22:33)---but to put this into execution is something else (Luke 22:46). To "follow Christ" in this text (Matt. 8:18), the disciple must be able to sleep out-doors, on the ground. That this is the intended instruction is evident by the passage in John 7:53 and 8:1, where we discover that on occasions Jesus had no house to sleep in.

["The foxes\...and the birds"]{.class_4258} are peculiar illustrations, as they stand in Scripture for demons (see Song of Sol. 2:15; Luke 13:32; Rev. 18:2; Matt. 13:4). Therefore, there is a biting irony and sarcasm in the Lord's rebuke of the scribe which is not apparent to the present-day "Liberal." The gist of the statement is: "The devil and his crowd are better taken care of on this earth than God's Son, as far as the physical comforts go." And the statement is not an exaggeration (see 2 Cor. 6:3--10).

["Suffer me first to go and bury my father."]{.class_4258} Here is certainly a sincere and innocent request, but the brutality with which the Lord answers the "seeker" would certainly empty a twentieth-century church of pews in fifteen minutes. Imagine telling a man that his relatives are just as dead as the corpse, and not even to go home for the funeral! What a thing to say. To the Bible believer, such passages as these are amusing, for he knows the Liberal has tried to sugarcoat them into nothing but spiritual evaporations. The Liberal is not even honest enough to confess that if a modern follower of Christ imitated "Christ's example" (and rebuked someone in this fashion), he would be immediately branded as "dogmatic," []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_145}"intolerant," "race-hater," "schismatic," "separatist," "trouble-maker," "rude," or some other choice epithet salvaged from the junkpile of the National Council of Churches. As in Luke 9:60, the "teachings of Jesus" are clear on this point. The call to preach during His earthly days was more important than "burying a relative." The fact that He refers to "live" people as "dead" (Matt. 8:22) should also give the Liberal some cause for alarm, for this is the doctrinal teaching of Paul in the Pauline Epistles (Eph. 2:1--3; 1 Tim. 5:6). It concerns the necessity of a "new birth" (Eph. 5:14).

8:23 And when he was entered into a ship, his disciples followed him.

24 And, behold, there arose a great tempest in the sea, insomuch that the ship was covered with the waves: but he was asleep.

25 And his disciples came to him, and awoke him, saying, Lord, save us: we perish.

26 And he saith unto them, Why are ye fearful, O ye of little faith? Then he arose, and rebuked the winds and the sea; and there was a great calm.

27 But the men marvelled, saying, What manner of man is this, that even the winds and the sea obey him!

We now come to the first obvious miracle in the New Testament that could be called a miracle. The healings were miraculous and there were some miraculous occurrences in connection with the visit of the Magi. The Virgin Birth itself, of course, is a genuine miracle. But now we find the first real miracle in the New Testament (according to the order of books) that Jesus Himself actually performs to manifest His Deity (John 2:11 actually being the first miracle chronologically).

Liberal commentators have gone to great lengths to explain the miracle, and their irreverent foolishness is perhaps best summed up in the opinions of Harry Emerson Fosdick, who said in effect, that if they had known anything about inductive science, the Christians would not have spoken of "miracles." This bit of conceit is the private property of unscientific men, who think they know more than the Holy Spirit. The believer can rest assured that the Saviour []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_146}who saved Him and granted him justification and righteousness, unmerited, is well able to put the lid on a gale and rock it to sleep.

["What manner of man is this?"]{.class_4258} is a very appropriate question. What manner of man is it who curses a fig tree and it dies in twenty-four hours, who rides a wild ass that has never been tamed, who tells a rooster when to sound off (Mark 14:30), and who tells a tempest to "take ten"? What manner of man is this? Nature obeys Him. He must be the God of all nature (Psa. 104).

Inspirationally, the Christian learns that though the "billows roll," Jesus is the God of the billows, and when He arises and works, the peace of God ["which passeth all understanding"]{.class_4258} is sure to follow.

8:28 And when he was come to the other side into the country of the Gergesenes, there met him two possessed with devils, coming out of the tombs, exceeding fierce, so that no man might pass by that way.

29 And, behold, they cried out, saying, What have we to do with thee, Jesus, thou Son of God? art thou come hither to torment us before the time?

30 And there was a good way off from them an herd of many swine feeding.

31 So the devils besought him, saying, If thou cast us out, suffer us to go away into the herd of swine.

["Gergesenes"]{.class_4258} identifies the locality as the same with Mark 5:1, and here the scholars have several difficulties. In Mark 5:1 we read ["Gadarenes"]{.class_4258}; in keeping with this, the scholars have changed Matthew 8:28 to read "Gadarene"---this being done to reinforce the Liberal theory that Matthew copied from Mark (see notes in introduction). A liberal will change the Greek text to back up a preconceived theory, which is contrary to Scriptural fidelity. The results are amusing. Nestle's critical text changes ["Gadarenes"]{.class_4258} in Mark 5:1 to "Gerasenon," and then changes ["Gergesenes"]{.class_4258} in Matthew to "Gadarenon," maintaining the apparent contradiction, and destroying the Received Text to do it. The majority of manuscripts have the [Authorized Version ]{.class_4257}reading, and the Bible believer will do well to stick to it. The change in names should not be in the least confusing, for the sea of Galilee is called[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw759.xhtml_page_147}[ "the sea of Chinneroth" ]{.class_4258}(Josh. 12:3) and the Sea of Gennesaret (see maps) and ["the sea of Tiberias"]{.class_4258} (John 6:1). Exactly why Nestle, Westcott and Hort, Tischendorf, Tregelles, and Alford should sweat out ["Gergesenes"]{.class_4258} and ["Gadarenes"]{.class_4258} is rather puzzling.

["Two possessed with devils."]{.class_4258} Mark lists only one, which again has caused lazy or insincere scholarship to say that they "contradict." However, upon examining Mark and Matthew a little more carefully, we find that it is the customary style of Mark to go into detail on one case, while Matthew lists the number involved in the case. For example, Mark 10:46 tells how one beggar was healed, but Matthew 20:30 gives the number of beggars involved---two. Simply because Mark has picked out one to talk about, in detail, does not put him at variance with Matthew. (Unregenerate scholarship comes to the Bible with the tools of ancient Greek criticism, used by the Greeks on secular writings, and forgets that we are here dealing with God-breathed books. The tools are too blunt to handle the material. Mark and Matthew supplement; they do not contradict.)

Mark, filling in details omitted by Matthew, gives us a splendid insight into correct Biblical demonology.

1\. Demons have an affinity for wet places (Mark 5:13) and, in particular, hot wet places (Matt. 17:15); hence, the explanation for the moral condition of sea ports and "river towns": L.A., Frisco, New Orleans, Shanghai, Paris, Cairo, Suez, Natchez, Paducah, Chicago, London, Baltimore, New York, Honolulu, etc.

2\. Demons have an affinity for heights (Mark 5:5; Gen. 11:3--4; 2 Kings 15:4; Isa. 14:12--14).

3\. Demon-possession is marked by sadism or masochism (Mark 5:5; 1 Kings 18:28).

4\. Demons have an affinity for dead bodies (Mark 5:5, 13).

5\. Demon-possession is marked by excessive crying and nakedness (Mark 5:5; Luke 8:27--28).

6\. It is always religious (Mark 5:7).

7\. And it is genuine, spiritual schizophrenia (Mark 5:9).

8\. It can be attended by unusual physical strength (Mark 5:4) and does not yield to love or persuasion (Mark 5:4).

8:32 And he said unto them, Go. And when they were come out, they went into the herd of swine: and, behold, the whole herd of swine ran violently down a steep place into the sea, and perished in the waters.

33 And they that kept them fled, and went their ways into the city, and told every thing, and what was befallen to the possessed of the devils.

34 And, behold, the whole city came out to meet Jesus: and when they saw him, they besought him that he would depart out of their coasts.

Someone has aptly said: "If the devil cannot possess you, his next choice would be a hog" (2 Pet. 2:22). Unclean spirits gravitate, evidently, to the "unclean meat" of Leviticus 11. It is also quite apparent from the passage that unclean spirits want to go to water (see vs. 32 and note 1) and do not care whether their "temple" is dead or alive. (An old preacher once said: "The pigs done ran down de hill and committed hogicide.")

Mark 5 adds some details missing in Matthew 8:33--34. Among them is the fact that not only did Jesus' power frighten them, but that the demoniac's conversion frightened them. Whereas he was naked, wild, and insane, he was now ["sitting, and clothed, and in his right mind"]{.class_4258} (Mark 5:15). The miraculous transformation in a human life, wrought by the New Birth, can often produce the same affect with the same startling response from the unbelievers. The believer is ["sitting"]{.class_4258} (Luke 10:39), ["clothed"]{.class_4258} (Isa. 61:10), ["and is in his right mind"]{.class_4258} (2 Tim. 1:7).

Another element commonly overlooked in the story is the prominent part played by ["filthy lucre"]{.class_4258} (1 Tim. 6:10). The people did not really get upset till they realized they had lost 2,000 head of hog at seventy cents a pound! That is what makes it expensive for Jesus to live "amongst us"! (Mark 5:16). Mark also adds the touching conversation which took place after the demoniac was healed. He wanted to be with Jesus (Matt. 8:19), but instead, Jesus left him to be a witness (see John 17:15; Acts 1:8).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml}

# Chapter 9 {.heading_84-0}

9:1 And he entered into a ship, and passed over, and came into his own city.

2 And, behold, they brought to him a man sick of the palsy, lying on a bed: and Jesus seeing their faith said unto the sick of the palsy; Son, be of good cheer; thy sins be forgiven thee.

["His own city"]{.class_4258} would indicate Capernaum ("City of Nahum").

["A man sick of the palsy."]{.class_4258} Again, we find Mark adding details not mentioned by Matthew. The man is lowered through the roof of a dwelling into Jesus' presence (Mark 2:4), and the man is healed on the grounds of the faith of those who brought him, not his own faith (Mark 2:5).

In verse 2, Jesus connects sickness with sin and puts the forgiveness of sin ahead of the healing. This is confirmed by James 5:15, and the Christian should be careful to observe that wherever healing occurs, even in the Jewish economy before the crucifixion, it is preceded by the preached word of God, in connection with spiritual issues (see Matt. 4:23, 10:7--8; Luke 9:6, etc.).

["Son, be of good cheer."]{.class_4258} The words hint at Christ's authority. It is quite a thing for a thirty to thirty-one year old man to address these words to a sick man on a bed, unless the speaker is more than human. Catholic priests often plagiarize this authority, borrowing from Paul's authority in 1 Corinthians 4:14--15 and 1 Thessalonians 2:11; but Paul never recognized any priesthood in black robes, and God never commissioned any priest in black robes to write any Scripture. The addressing of a sinner as[ "son"]{.class_4258} should certainly be used with discretion. This "son" is the form of address Abraham uses to the rich man in hell (Luke 16:22--25) and is identical with the calling of Israel corporately as God's "son" (see Matt. 2:14--15).

9:3 And, behold, certain of the scribes said within themselves, This man blasphemeth.

4 And Jesus knowing their thoughts said, Wherefore think ye evil in your hearts?

5 For whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Arise, and walk?

6 But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins, (then saith he to the sick of the palsy,) Arise, take up thy bed, and go unto thine house.

7 And he arose, and departed to his house.

8 But when the multitudes saw it, they marvelled, and glorified God, which had given such power unto men.

The charge of blasphemy seems to be one of the favorite charges brought against Jesus by his enemies. Religious persecution is always the worst sort as it involves "convictions," and where people feel deeply they are liable to act violently. Especially is this true of the Catholic religion, where it is very common thing for papists to praise each other or bless each other for the mass murder of Protestants (see John 16:2). Nor is the application stretched, for in the context of our passage under study, we find the issue of who forgives sins on earth being brought up.

Blasphemy is the charge brought against Christ at His final "hearing" (Matt. 26:65; John 19:7) and before Pilate. As usual, those bringing the charge were guilty of their own accusation (Matt. 12:31--32).

Christ, here, literally "slaps" His opponents in the face. Here, they were talking about God only being able to do this and that, because they themselves were not able to heal the man (see vs. 5). Up shows a man who professes to be able to do what only God can do, by their own standards, and then does it! The proof is in the pudding. They knew they couldn't heal the man, and they knew they couldn't forgive Him. So Christ does both, proving the contention from their own side of the argument. If it took God to do either, then Jesus is God (2 Cor. 5:19). But the scribes (as our present-day Liberals) are not to be convinced so easily. When the point is proven to their face, their reaction is not, "Then he must be God"; to the contrary, it is, "All right, let's kill him" (Mark 3:6). The moral of the story is, you cannot convince a lazy or wicked man with absolute, reasonable, scientific evidence; his trouble is "heart" trouble, not "head trouble."

["Power on earth"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_151} is to remind us that no visible representative of Jesus Christ needs to be on this earth other than His body of born-again believers. At His ascension, or shortly before, He reaffirms that the ["power on earth"]{.class_4258} is still His and is not delegated to anybody (Matt. 28:18). The only power that the visible representatives of Christ have (2 Cor. 5:19--21) is the spiritual power of the Holy Spirit Himself. (The power to "remit or retain" sins was given to Paul and a dozen other disciples, as well as the original 12---see John 20:19 with Luke 24:33 and 2 Cor. 2:10.) The expression "Son of Man" is used deliberately, which is worthy of note, as it occurs nowhere in the Pauline epistles and is specifically the earthly, Jewish title connected with Christ's earthly ministry to Israel (see Ezek. 1--6).

There is a case of "mental telepathy" found in the passage in verse 4 (if science wants to call "omniscience" by that term). However, Jesus' knowledge was limited on earth in the flesh, which is evident by a certain lapse of time between the occurrence of another's thoughts and Jesus' perception. Notice this "time element" in thought-reading in Matthew 16:8; Mark 2:8; and Mark 8:17. Having limited Himself to a fleshy tabernacle, the God of Glory takes on limitations, including hunger, thirst, sadness, and pain (Heb. 4:15).

9:9 And as Jesus passed forth from thence, he saw a man, named Matthew, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he saith unto him, Follow me. And he arose, and followed him.

10 And it came to pass, as Jesus sat at meat in the house, behold, many publicans and sinners came and sat down with him and his disciples.

This ["Matthew"]{.class_4258} is the author of the gospel and is called ["Levi"]{.class_4258} in Mark 2:14 and Luke 5:29. Luke supplies the fact that Matthew himself was one of the despised "publicans," present at the feast in Matthew 9:11. The "custom money," of course, is the tax money levied on the Jews by Rome. Christ never failed to pay it (17:27), although he was accused of not paying it (Luke 23:2); this was the bone of contention between Him and the Pharisees in Matthew []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_152}22:15--18.

Matthew's call to service was of GRACE. It was a ROYAL call, and it was a PERSONAL CALL. His response was a DARING one; it was by FAITH, it was SACRIFICIAL, and above all, it was IMMEDIATE. He did not say: "Lord, suffer me first...."

9:11 And when the Pharisees saw it, they said unto his disciples, Why eateth your Master with publicans and sinners?

12 But when Jesus heard that, he said unto them, They that be whole need not a physician, but they that are sick.

13 But go ye and learn what that meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice: for I am not come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.

The passage is crucial in the study of New Testament doctrines. ["Publicans and sinners"]{.class_4258} indicates that the contemporary, Jewish, ecclesiastical powers included only the lowest class of people as sinners: for example, the fallen woman (Luke 7:39) and the blind man (John 9:31, 34). This reasoning is still found among the twentieth-century Pharisees, who range from doctors, bankers, and lawyers, to professors, ministers, and philosophers. This "reasoning" is really false logic, corrected emphatically in Romans 3:22--23 by the statement: ["there is NO DIFFERENCE: For all have sinned." "Sinners,"]{.class_4258} in the religious vocabulary of the self-righteous, is a reference to whoremongers, adulterers, drunkards, dopers, cussers, extortioners, bank-robbers, kidnappers, and wife-beaters. ["Sinners,"]{.class_4258} in Bible language (and this accounts for the tremendous antipathy found among cultured people to the Bible), means the envious, the lazy, ex­aggerators, gluttons, the self-righteous, the proud, the deceitful, the stubborn, and those who refuse to recognize Truth. In our text, Christ had truly "gone to eat chicken dinner with a sinner," but who is not a "sinner," but Christ only?

The proverb on the physician is significant for two reasons. In the first place, it debunks the idea that physicians are of the devil (an idea found in some religious groups), and in the second place, it becomes applicable to Christ as the Great Physician (Luke 4:23). []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_153}That God approves of physicians is apparent by the fact that the Holy Spirit Himself calls Luke ["the beloved physician"]{.class_4258} (Col. 4:14). To refuse a medicine which would affect a cure would go down in the books in some states as "suicide." "Medicine" is recommended in both Testaments (see Prov. 17:22; cf. Matt. 8:17).

The quotation from the Old Testament, ["go ye and learn what that meaneth,"]{.class_4258} is a reference to Hosea 6:6, where God is rebuking the sins of Ephraim and Judah.

The word "sacrifice" in the text is ["thusian,"]{.class_4262} the actual flesh of the victim sacrificed, as in 12:7. The fact that Jesus quotes the passage twice should give the reader considerable cause to study its importance. To begin with, there is an apparent contradiction present here, which has bothered Liberals and infidels for years. In Jeremiah 7:21--23 we find: ["Thus saith the ]{.class_4258}[Lord ]{.class_4267}[of hosts, the God of Israel; Put your burnt offerings unto your sacrifices, and eat flesh. For I spake not unto your fathers, nor commanded them in the day that I brought them out of the land of Egypt, concerning burnt-offerings or sacrifices: But this thing commanded I them, saying, Obey my voice...."]{.class_4258}

On the basis of this apparent contradiction, Astruc, the Graf-Wellhausen, and the Tubingen schools of theology erected a whole theory of "re-interpreting the Bible," which led to the denial of Mosaic authorship of the Pentateuch, inserted four writers in Genesis who weren't even there, stepped-up the dates of Genesis--Chronicles to as late as 400 B.C., and practically denied every miracle in the Old Testament (see Commentary on Genesis, Gen. 17:17--21). Now let us learn from this example of our deluded, liberal "brethren" (and I use the word loosely), and let us honestly and scientifically examine the evidence.

1\. In the day God brought them out (Exod. 15), He most certainly said nothing about sacrifices. (What He said is in Exod. 15:26.)

2\. When He did say something about sacrifice (after they came out of Egypt), it was not commanded (Lev. 1:2--3); it was offered on a voluntary, free-will basis.

3\. The passover lamb was not offered as a "sacrifice" or "burnt offering" before they came out of Egypt, but as a[ "feast to the ]{.class_4258}[Lord]{.class_4267}["]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_154} to commemorate mercy and grace (see Exod. 12).

4\. Christ's ["go ye and learn what that meaneth,"]{.class_4258} then, is a rebuke to the Liberal himself, for not "going and learning what it meaneth." The Liberal is still trying to get saved by sacrificial works of self-righteousness, and he doesn't know "what it meaneth." He is so far back in the theological swamp, he not only "doesn't know anything"---he doesn't even "suspect anything"!

Romans 11:32 states the exact meaning of Christ's ["go ye and learn,"]{.class_4258} and if a man is capable of learning, he can certainly find out in short order. Our Lord is rebuking the Pharisees by saying in effect: "If God thought you could earn your way to heaven by burning animals at an altar, I would be coming to eat with you to find out how to do it. But since God knows you can't, and He knows you are ALL sinners and need Divine mercy and forgiveness (by a righteousness which only God can give), I'm going to eat this meal with the folks who believe that." If there is any doubt about this free transliteration, read Matthew 21:31--32.

9:14 Then came to him the disciples of John, saying, Why do we and the Pharisees fast oft, but thy disciples fast not?

15 And Jesus said unto them, Can the children of the bridechamber mourn, as long as the bridegroom is with them? but the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken from them, and then shall they fast.

From here on, the ministry of our Lord is marked by constant questions of religious controversy. They never let Him rest a minute; from here, till the great debates and questions of the "Passion Week," Jesus is constantly being "put on the spot." This question is about fasting. (Fasting is not found as a Divine commandment anywhere in the Old Testament; it seems to have sprung up as a voluntary practice among people who were going through deep times of testing.) We find it referred to in Isaiah 58:3--7 and Zechariah 7:5--6, but here the malpractices connected with it are rebuked. David, Daniel, Ahab, Moses, Elijah, Ezra, and Mordecai all fasted; and God does not rebuke the practice, in itself, as being sinful. ["Children of the bride­chamber"]{.class_4258} is a reference to Israel in []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_155}one of two positions; either as Jehovah's bride, with God as Bridegroom (see Hosea 1--3; Isa. 62:4--5), or to Israel as attendants on Christ as Bridegroom with His bride, the church (see Eph. 5:23--33; John 3:29). Both meanings are veiled in the illustrations. Since there is no revelation of the church to this point, the saying is enigmatical to the hearers if He is not referring to Jehovah and Israel. Both meanings are manifest, however, later as Christ died, was buried, and rose, and "when He was taken from them" (John 14:28), then they did begin to fast (Acts 13:1--3; 14:23; 2 Cor. 11:27).

The prophetical import of the statement should also be studied. Israel is fasting, weeping, and wailing in the Tribulation, after Christ comes and catches His bride out; the "virgins" of the Great Tribulation will attend the Bridegroom when He returns (see Psa. 45; Song of Sol. 6:8--10; Matt. 25:1--8).

9:16 No man putteth a piece of new cloth unto an old garment, for that which is put in to fill it up taketh from the garment, and the rent is made worse.

17 Neither do men put new wine into old bottles: else the bottles break, and the wine runneth out, and the bottles perish: but they put new wine into new bottles, and both are preserved.

Both illustrations must have a connection with Christ's ministry on earth compared to His post-resurrection ministry. The ["garment"]{.class_4258} and the ["cloth,"]{.class_4258} where they occur in Scripture, usually are a reference to the human body (see Luke 12:23) or some kind of righteousness which covers the body (Luke 15:22; Isa. 61:10, 59:17). (In Psa. 104:6, God's Light is said to be a garment, and the\
heavens are likened to clothes which can be folded up and stored away.) The application of the illustration is difficult. It is obvious that a strong piece of new cloth sewed onto a worn-out, thread-bare suit will not hold long, but what the new cloth is is difficult to discern.

By comparing verse 16 with 17, the ["new wine"]{.class_4258} must answer to the ["new cloth,"]{.class_4258} and the ["old garment"]{.class_4258} must answer to the ["old bottles."]{.class_4258} The parable is a little clearer, for new wine is a type of the Holy Spirit (Acts 2:13; Matt. 26:29; Eph. 5:18)! The new wine is not found in the vat or the still or the bottle, but ["in the cluster"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_156} (Isa. 65:8), and it is the only wine the Jew was allowed to drink (Deut. 29:6, 32:14) in the wilderness journey. It is the "grape juice" of Genesis chapter 40 and is said to be that "wine" at the Lord's supper (Matt. 26:29); Paul recommends it for Timothy's stomach (1 Tim. 5:23).

It looks as though our Lord is giving a hidden illustration regarding the coming dispensation of the indwelling Holy Spirit. In the Old Testament, He enters and leaves the body (Isa. 63:11; Exod. 31:3; Num. 27:18; Neh. 9:30). (The Scofield note which says He only "came on them" is quite incorrect.) But in the New Testament, the Spirit of God indwells till the day of redemption (John 14:16; Eph. 4:30). If we are to gather anything of this nature from the Lord's remark, it must be that it will take a "new people" to receive the indwelling Holy Spirit, and that Israel (as a nation), represented by the old garment and old bottles, cannot fill the bill. This interpretation is stretched, however, and has its problems. In the first place, no man is a new bottle or new garment until he has been "born again," and the Holy Spirit Himself is the author of this work. In the second place, it is doubtful if the Lord Jesus is giving to a Jewish audience a reference on the coming "body of Christ," as it certainly would be a dark secret to 100 percent of His audience. The best we can do with the illustration is say that it in someway explains the difference between pre- and-post-resurrection ministry. John the Baptist is the author of the question, or at least his disciples (Matt. 9:14). This is highly significant, for John is the "end of the law and the prophets," and is not part of the bride of Christ (Luke 16:16; John 3:29).

9:18 While he spake these things unto them, behold, there came a certain ruler, and worshipped him, saying, My daughter is even now dead: but come and lay thy hand upon her, and she shall live.

19 And Jesus arose, and followed him, and so did his disciples.

Not since the visit of the Magi have we read of anyone "worshipping" Christ, but here as in other places, someone does, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_157}Christ never refuses their homage. Christ always accepts worship as the natural and right thing (Matt. 14:33, 28:17; John 9:38, 20:28). This is quite a thought. It would be interesting to read a book by Dahlberg, Weigle, Weatherhead, Allen, or Peale, sometime, on why Jesus always accepted worship, which was allotted only to the Supreme Deity. We hear a lot of nonsense about God using the Hebrews to teach the world "Monotheism" (there were no polytheists before the flood!), and yet when Jesus Christ shows up in the line of David, He accepts worship reserved only for God (John 10:33). This was the charge brought against Him, and it was a more honest charge than the charge of the [RSV ]{.class_4257}that Christ was the "son of Joseph" (Luke 2:33, [RSV]{.class_4257}). All knew what He professed to be---He professed to be incarnate Deity (John 14:9).

The man who comes has the utmost faith in Christ's power to heal, but of course he has good reason to believe from the results already "blazed" abroad by converts of Matthew 8:4, 13, 16, 32.

9:20 And, behold, a woman, which was diseased with an issue of blood twelve years, came behind him, and touched the hem of his garment:

21 For she said within herself, If I may but touch his garment, I shall be whole.

22 But Jesus turned him about, and when he saw her, he said, Daughter, be of good comfort; thy faith hath made thee whole. And the woman was made whole from that hour.

Again, according to his customary style of writing, Mark puts in details omitted by Matthew (see Mark 5:25--31). We learn the time of her infirmity---twelve years---and her attempts to be healed at the hands of physicians and of Christ's reaction to her when she ["touched the hem"]{.class_4258} of his garment.

The age of infirmity is peculiar as in the same chapter (Mark 5) we find the damsel of twelve being healed. There is undoubtedly a prophetical application to the future resurrection and healing of Israel, as Israel's number is twelve. The ["hem of his garment"]{.class_4258} again produces the modern "healing" movement's "point of contact." As Jesus remarks on something going out from Him to the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_158}woman, at the time of contact, it is not surprising to find radio preachers talking about "feeling power go through my arms," or "I feel the power of God in my right hand," etc. Someone fancies that he is something like God incarnate, but for the Holy Spirit to indwell a ruined, grave-bound bag of flesh is something different than Divine flesh which would not even corrupt after lying three days in the grave (Acts 2:27).

"THE ROBE," in song and story, is a peculiar perversion of this magical, Catholic idea that virtue or power can reside in "HOLY THINGS." And so to this day, twentieth-century ambassadors, premiers, and presidents have faith in "holy water," "holy Mary," "holy rosaries," "holy festivals," "holy days," "holy elements," and other relics of black magic.

It will suffice to say that this was a case where the physicians couldn't make it and God could. The woman was healed, and she was healed by faith in the visible, earthly, Messianic ministry of the Jewish Messiah.

9:23 And when Jesus came into the ruler's house, and saw the minstrels and the people making a noise,

24 He said unto them, Give place: for the maid is not dead, but sleepeth. And they laughed him to scorn.

25 But when the people were put forth, he went in, and took her by the hand, and the maid arose.

26 And the fame hereof went abroad into all that land.

Again, Mark supplies numerous details for which Matthew does not take time. In Mark chapter 5, we find out who entered the house with Him, the conversations which took place there, the exact manner of the resurrection, and the things that took place afterwards. Several Old English expressions occur in the passage in Matthew: ["minstrels," "give place,"]{.class_4258} and ["laughed him to scorn."]{.class_4258} These expressions are easily understood by the average reader, but scholars seem to be put in a great deal of confusion over them and often change them in the "new" translations.

The word with which Jesus raises her is: ["Talitha cumi,"]{.class_4258} which is an Aramaic expression (Mark 5:41). These insertions of Aramaic (Matt. 27:46; John 19:13, 17, etc.) are one of the surest proofs that []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_159}Papias' theory on a "Q" document (from which Matthew and Mark were supposed to have copied) is just more high-sounding nonsense. There is no need for inserting an Aramaism into the Greek, and then interpreting it, if the manuscript you are "copying" from is all in Aramaic.

Mark 5:37 indicates that the "inner circle" of Peter, James, and John were the only ones allowed to enter with Him. These three seem to have a peculiar relationship to Christ in connection with the Second Advent; they only are on the Mount of Transfiguration and in the Garden. On one rare occasion, Andrew is present with the three, when Jesus discusses the details of the Second Coming. All three are martyred.

The ["ruler"]{.class_4258} is a ruler of a synagogue (Mark 5:38). The minstrels are the professional mourners and musicians attending the funeral. The damsel herself is raised by the spoken word, exactly as the widow of Nain's son (Luke 7:14--15) and Lazarus (John 11:43--44). In the order of books, this is the first case of "raising the dead," and the news of it spreads rapidly (Matt 9:26), with attendant persecution (Matt 9:34).

9:27 And when Jesus departed thence, two blind men followed him, crying, and saying, Thou Son of David, have mercy on us.

28 And when he was come into the house, the blind men came to him: and Jesus saith unto them, Believe ye that I am able to do this? They said unto him, Yea, Lord.

29 Then touched he their eyes, saying, According to your faith be it unto you.

30 And their eyes were opened; and Jesus straitly charged them, saying, See that no man know it.

["Thou Son of David"]{.class_4258} now appears in the text, giving us another title which shows that the common people of Christ's day were beginning to recognize Him as the long-promised Messiah. The full revelation that He was not only the ["Son of David,"]{.class_4258} but God's Son, does not come until Matthew 16:16; but here, already, we have people calling Him ["Lord"]{.class_4258} and worshipping Him. The devils (being a little ahead of the scribes, Pharisees, and disciples) have already called Him ["the Son of God"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_160} (Matt. 8:29).

Verse 29 is often quoted by Christians as a prayer promise; of course, there is an element of truth in it, but as stated elsewhere (see Matt. 8:13), one must not confuse genuine, God-given faith with an attempt to "try real hard to believe" something you want to believe. This is presumption, not faith. There are certain conditions attending real answers to prayer, which qualify the statement: ["According to your faith\..."]{.class_4258} Some of these conditions are found in James 4:2--4; 1 John 3:22; Matthew 6:14.

Again, the Lord cautions His converts to be quiet about their healing, and again it is useless ("a city on a hill cannot be hid!"). They go far and wide spreading the news, and the crowds increase. Subsequently the opposition increases also (see vs. 34).

9:32 As they went out, behold, they brought to him a dumb man possessed with a devil.

33 And when the devil was cast out, the dumb spake: and the multitudes marvelled, saying, It was never so seen in Israel.

34 But the Pharisees said, He casteth out devils through the prince of the devils.

35 And Jesus went about all the cities and villages, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing every sickness and every disease among the people.

["A dumb man"]{.class_4258} is qualified by the additional statement, ["possessed with a devil,"]{.class_4258} indicating that some "dumbness" is caused by spiritual forces and some is not. The ["dumb"]{.class_4258} here, of course, is the word for "muteness" or inability to speak and hear---not "stupidity."

["It was never so seen in Israel"]{.class_4258} is a true statement (11:5), and again bears testimony to Christ's claim as the Messiah (John 5:33, 36).

The Pharisees' charge indicates a sly and wicked reasoning which will put Jesus in the worst light. They could not cast out devils themselves, they could not heal, they could not forgive sins, and they had seen Jesus do all of these things. A quick "scientific" []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw846.xhtml_page_161}explanation was necessary in order to hurt His influence with the people. "The devil is giving him power to do it" is the logical accusation in Matthew 12:27 .

["Preaching the gospel of the kingdom."]{.class_4258} Again, here is a clear passage which is bound to give the correct key to Bible interpretation if it is believed. This ["gospel of the kingdom"]{.class_4258} cannot by any wild stretch of the imagination refer to the gospel of 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. (Of which Paul said, ["If any man preach any other gospel\...let him be accursed"]{.class_4258}---Gal. 1:9.) One can go even further, for the gospel of the kingdom of God, preached by Paul in Acts 20:25, contains elements not found here in Matthew 9:35. Anyone undertaking to synthesize the "Kingdom of God" and the "Kingdom of Heaven" should spend at least three years studying the differences between them before attempting it. The "Kingdom of God" itself changes from a heralding of a coming spiritual kingdom to a revelation of a spiritual body in that kingdom, and this change takes place during the time of the events found in the Acts. (The reader should once more take heed to the introductory remarks in regard to Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews.) Matthew 3:1--2 is the first mention of the Kingdom about which Jesus is preaching here in 9:35, and the comments on that passage will be found helpful if they are carefully checked with an open Bible. ["The gospel of the kingdom"]{.class_4258} is a tribulation message preceding the Second Advent (Matt. 24:14), and it would do the student of Scripture considerable "good" if he would review the material on Matthew 1--7, being primarily the preparation of Israel to receive the Messiah, as prophesied in the Old Testament, with regard to His Second Coming in glory. (See [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_4257} \[formerly titled [The Kingdom of God versus The Kingdom of Heaven,]{.class_4257} 1969\].)

9:36 But when he saw the multitudes, he was moved with compassion on them, because they fainted, and were scattered abroad, as sheep having no shepherd.

37 Then saith he unto his disciples, The harvest truly is plenteous, but the labourers are few;

38 Pray ye therefore the Lord of the harvest, that he will send forth labourers into his harvest."

The shepherd-and-sheep reference undoubtedly is a fulfillment of the passage in Ezekiel chapter 34 (see especially Ezek. 34:5), and is primarily concerned with Israel's condition at the time of both Advents.

The[ "harvest"]{.class_4258} in verse 37 may be applied spiritually to the Christian activity of winning souls to Christ in this age (1 Cor. 9:7--12; John 15:1--3), but the observing student will not fail to notice that there is a literal harvest at the end of this age (Matt. 13:20; Rev 14:15) which speaks again of Israel's conversion to the Messiah at the Second Coming, not the first. (Note the remarks already made in this commentary in regard to Matt. 3:1--2.)

Verse 38 is self-explanatory. John 4:35--38 will add some illuminating and inspiring material on the passage, as it is connected with "soul-winning" and the ministry of reaching the lost for Christ.

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml}

# Chapter 10 {.heading_84-0}

10:1 And when he had called unto him his twelve disciples, he gave them power against unclean spirits, to cast them out, and to heal all manner of sickness and all manner of disease.

The choosing of ["twelve"]{.class_4258} again reinforces the idea that this is a Jewish Messiah sent to rescue Israel. The twelve are a substitute for the twelve "fathers" of Genesis 29--30. The signs and wonders which He authorized them to do (Heb. 2:3--4), again, are Israelish in character, for the first "sign" performed by a man on this earth was by Moses (Exod. 4:1--6), as he was commissioned to bring the children of Israel out of the land of Egypt. The Jew seeks for a ["sign"]{.class_4258} (1 Cor. 1:22), and rightly so, as it is a nation that began with ["signs"]{.class_4258} and ["wonders"]{.class_4258} (Deut. 26:8).

[The first healing of any kind is in connection with Israel's liberation from a foreign power]{.class_4257} (Exod. 15:26), so now, when Israel is in bondage to Rome, the successor of Moses (Heb. 3:1--6) shows up with all the signs and wonders necessary to prove His Divine Calling.

10:2 Now the names of the twelve apostles are these; The first, Simon, who is called Peter, and Andrew his brother; James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother;

3 Philip, and Bartholomew; Thomas, and Matthew the publican; James the son of Alphaeus, and Lebbaeus, whose surname was Thaddaeus;

4 Simon the Canaanite, and Judas Iscariot, who also betrayed him.

The list is found again in Mark 3:16--19 and Luke 6:14--16. A comparison of the names with other passages in the Bible reveals that there are only twelve men, in spite of the apparent discrepancies and these twelve are ["apostolos,"]{.class_4257} literally---"sent ones." ["Lebbaeus, whose surname was Thad­daeus,"]{.class_4258} is undoubtedly the ["Judas"]{.class_4258} of Luke 6:16 (called ["Judas\...not Iscariot"]{.class_4258} in John 14:22). ["Simon called Zelotes,"]{.class_4258} of Luke 6:15, would be the Canaanite in this passage in Matthew. ["Peter"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_164} occurs first in all three listings, and James and John follow him immediately in the listings of Mark and Luke. From Luke 6:12 and Mark 3:13, it would appear that Jesus prayed all night before making the selection, and consequently, His selection of Judas (John 6:70--71) is deliberate and intentional and in keeping with the foreknowledge of the Father's divine will.

In Acts 1:13, two or perhaps even three ["James"]{.class_4258} appear. The Scofield note would indicate that there are only two ["James"]{.class_4258} in Scripture; James Zebedee and ["James the less,"]{.class_4258} a "cousin" of the Lord. This interpretation is in keeping with the best Roman Catholic tradition and is far removed from correct English reading of the texts involved. To begin with, the legendary "cousins," which preserve the fable of Mary's Perpetual Virginity, are not "cousins" at all, as has already been clearly pointed out (see Matt. 1:25). It is greatly in error then to suppose that the Lord did not have a literal brother, ["James,"]{.class_4258} as the Scripture states in Galatians 1:19. That this James is someone special, and not merely a "cousin," is evident by the fact that he is blessed with [a special post-resurrection appearance of the risen Saviour]{.class_4257} (1 Cor. 15:5--7).

["James of Alphaeus,"]{.class_4258} therefore, listed in Acts 1:13, is a third James and most certainly [not ]{.class_4257}the ["Lord's brother,"]{.class_4258} although this one could possibly be a cousin, and Judas, his brother, also a cousin. Since eleven names are listed in Acts 1:13, these must be the eleven apostles that are left after the apostasy and defection of Judas Iscariot. Consequently, it follows that they must match the list in Matthew 10. Lebbaeus whose surname is Thaddaeus is the only one who can fill the bill; therefore, the ["Judas"]{.class_4258} (["not Iscariot"]{.class_4258}) must be this man. This leaves us with [three ]{.class_4257}James'.

1\. James Zebedee, John's brother.

2\. James of Alphaeus, Christ's cousin.

3\. James the less, the brother of the Lord, by Mary.

10:5 These twelve Jesus sent forth, and commanded them, saying, Go not into the way of the Gentiles, and into any city of the Samaritans enter ye not:

6 But go rather to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.

7 And as ye go, preach, saying, The kingdom of heaven is at hand.

Again, we come face-to-face with the historical, sensible, logical, and unavoidable fact that "The Gospel of the Kingdom of Heaven" has nothing to do with New Testament salvation, as it is revealed in the Gospel of 1 Corinthians 15:1--4. Failure to recognize this fact and to apply it will remain a constant stumbling block to all students and scholars of the word until they "face-up" to common sense. Notice:

1\. No Gentiles are to be preached to (cf. Rom. 10:9--16).

2\. The message is to the House of Israel (cf. Acts 2:36--38).

3\. The message is [not ]{.class_4257}the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ; it is the kingdom (see 1 Cor. 15:1--4; Gal. 1:6--10).

Attempts to make the gospel of Matthew 10:7, 4:17, 23 equal to the gospel of Galatians 1:6--12 and Ephesians 3:1--13, only end in hopeless confusion, garbling of texts, and mangling of passages. The liberal, who refuses to recognize [Paul's conversion and subsequent authority]{.class_4257} (Rom. 15:16--20), cuts his own theological throat before he even picks up the Bible to expound. Paul's "gospel" is not Christ's earthly "gospel" to Israel. Picking the "gospel" or the "words of Jesus" or "the teachings of the Master," as the final authority, is a tragic and stupid blunder. The context of Matthew 10 [is to Israel, and Israel only, and all attempts to force it on the Church, or the unconverted Gentile in this age, ends in farce.]{.class_4257}

10:8 Heal the sick, cleanse the lepers, raise the dead, cast out devils: freely ye have received, freely give.

9 Provide neither gold, nor silver, nor brass in your purses,

10 Nor scrip for your journey, neither two coats, neither shoes, nor yet staves: for the workman is worthy of his meat.

The farce of modern interpretative methods is at once apparent by what follows the preaching of the[ "kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4258} ["Cleanse the lepers."]{.class_4258} Not one out of ten "healers" in America has ever attempted to heal a leper. ["Raise the dead."]{.class_4258} All the healers part company and head for the bushes, for the "raising" implies a raising from a[ TOMB---not a sick bed.]{.class_4257} []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_166}["Freely ye have received, freely give:"]{.class_4258} another exodus to the swamp, with the "healers" in the lead. All healers take up collections, emphasize collections, and think up gimmicks to get collections; there is nothing "free" about it. ["Provide neither gold, nor silver,"]{.class_4258} but alas, all the "healers" do! ["Neither two coats."]{.class_4258} Why waste time with further exposition? No one in the churchage obeys or practices this commission, and no one is supposed to. And if they profess to, they are lying!

Unfortunately, some of God's people are so anxious and zealous to accept the "whole word of God," for ALL Christians, that they steal passages from Jews to appropriate them for themselves. This is a socially acceptable way to rob God, while professing to uphold the integrity of the Scriptures.

The healing "signs and wonders," which accompany Christ's appearance, herald His ministry as the fulfillment of the "second Moses" (Israel deliverer)---see Luke 4:25--28; Deuteronomy 18:15--19. As such, He must appear with the "signs" (Matt. 11:4--6), for the Jews ["seek a sign"]{.class_4258} (Exod. 4:8--30). [They get these signs,]{.class_4257} and the Lord imparts these "signs" to twelve apostles (2 Cor. 12:12; Mark 16:16--19), and here in Matthew 10 we find the signs in operation. To take such a doctrine and thrust it on the Church, or the saved Gentile in the Church Age, is a ridiculous application. Sickness is not mentioned in the Bible until Exodus 4, when Israel is down in Egypt.

10:11 And into whatsoever city or town ye shall enter, inquire who in it is worthy; and there abide till ye go thence.

12 And when ye come into an house, salute it.

13 And if the house be worthy, let your peace come upon it: but if it be not worthy, let your peace return to you.

["Who in it is worthy"]{.class_4258} implies "who in it is worthy to hear the message you are going to deliver." The word ["unworthily"]{.class_4258} is used in the same sense in 1 Corinthians 11:29, and does not teach that a man is too good or too bad to do a thing, but only that he has made the proper preparation [before ]{.class_4257}doing the thing. ["Axios" ]{.class_4257}is the Greek and signifies "fitting" or "suitable." However, Hebrews 11:31 and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_167}Luke 10:5--6 throw more light on the passage than the "original Greek." (This is usually the case. The Book is written so that the Holy Spirit compares spiritual things with things spiritual to bring out the correct interpretation.)

If the occupant of the house says ["shalom elechim"]{.class_4257} ("peace be unto you") and receives the message of the kingdom in a peaceable manner without anger or rejection, then that is the right place to stay. The ["peace"]{.class_4258} referred to in the chapter can be a reference to the famous ["Shiloh"]{.class_4258} prophecy of Jacob in Genesis 49:10, and with the birth of the "peaceful one" (peace personified), the angels herald "peace on earth, good will to men!" Those who exercise "good will and hospitality" toward the Prince of Peace, and the message of His kingdom, are "worthy." (Rahab receives the spies in Heb. 11:31 exactly in the manner that the recipients of Matt. 10 are to receive the message of the kingdom of heaven: i.e., favorably.) ["Let your peace return to you"]{.class_4258} would refer to the peace of heart and mind that Christ Himself is supposed to impart to those who believed the message (see John 14:27).

10:14 "And whosoever shall not receive you, nor hear your words, when ye depart out of that house or city, shake off the dust of your feet.

15 Verily I say unto you, It shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom and Gomorrha in the day of judgment, than for that city."

The shaking of dust off the feet would be the act of either kicking the heels of the sandals together, to bang the dust out of them, or better still, for the rejected messenger to walk to the front door (or city gate), remove his sandals, bang the soles of them together till the dust came out like a beaten rug, and then put them back on and walk off. Lorenzo Dow, the itinerant Methodist evangelist, is credited with having done this at a town in Georgia, which seemed to bear a perpetual curse on it thereafter.

["More tolerable for the land\..."]{.class_4258} is a strong and clear implication that there are degrees of punishment at the Last Judgment even to those who spend eternity in the Lake of Fire. Degrees of damnation is a clear teaching of both Testaments (see Deut. 32:22; Psa. 86:13; Matt. 23:14; Rev. 20:13--15). []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_168}["More tolerable"]{.class_4258} says plainly that it will be easier to "tolerate " or "put up with" the judgment, for some people than for others. Christ's comparisons (always picked carefully and for a purpose) show that the New Testament sin of Israel in rejecting Christ as the King-Messiah was a greater sin than the sex-perversion of the Sodomites.

10:16 Behold, I send you forth as sheep in the midst of wolves: be ye therefore wise as serpents, and harmless as doves.

17 But beware of men: for they will deliver you up to the councils, and they will scourge you in their synagogues ;

The four animals selected in verse 16 are remarkable similies for the warfare engaged in by both Old and New Testament "saints." The sheep, until here, have always referred to the believing remnant within the nation of Israel, often referred to as "the Lord's flock" (Ezek. 34; Isa. 63:11, etc.). From here on, the information is given that some Gentiles will be accounted the sheep of this flock (see John 10:11--16). As such, the believer is likened unto the Shepherd, who Himself is a LAMB (Isa. 53; Exod. 12:1--6, etc.). This "sheep," then, is in the midst of WOLVES, and if that were not bad enough, these wolves [dress like sheep to fool the real sheep]{.class_4257} (Matt. 7:15), and they are [in the flock]{.class_4257} (Acts 20:29). In view of this tremendous deception, the real sheep is admonished not to behave or think as a sheep, but to be ["wise as serpents, and harmless as doves."]{.class_4258} Thus, the fold of the church has become a zoo instead of a pasture. The modern "Christianity," far from being "the flock of the Lord" led by Christ, is a menagerie of sheep, pigs, dogs (2 Pet. 2:22), wolves, birds (Matt. 13:32), and serpents (Matt. 23:33). Although "harmless," in the sense of refusing to kill and harass others, the saint is nevertheless to use strategy, common sense, tact, diplomacy, and shrewdness in dealing with the wolves (2 Cor 12:16).

The occurrence of the word ["councils"]{.class_4258} here begins a long series of references to the organizations raised up by religious leaders to stamp out the word of God and to persecute the Bible-believing segment of any population, in any given age. The Bible is instructive in its use of words, and its consistent use of the word ["council"]{.class_4258} in a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_169}bad sense is educative. Notice that a council is always, in the New Testament, [without a single exception, the word used to describe a professing body of religious leaders who hate the Scriptures.]{.class_4257} (See Acts 4:15, 5:40--41, 24:20, 25:12; Matt. 12:14, 26:59, et al.) Before these councils, the sheep are admonished to be ["wise as serpents, and harmless as doves,"]{.class_4258} and "about four serpents to one dove," as an old, colored preacher exhorted.

10:18 And ye shall be brought before governors and kings for my sake, for a testimony against them and the Gentiles.

19 But when they deliver you up, take no thought how or what ye shall speak: for it shall be given you in that same hour what ye shall speak.

20 For it is not ye that speak, but the Spirit of your Father which speaketh in you.

The governors and kings will begin with Herod and Pilate, but since the subject of the discourse is ["YE" ]{.class_4258}(and not "Christ Himself"), we must look to passages in the book of the Acts for immediate fulfillment (Acts 12:1--2, 22:26). A difficulty is encountered when one considers that Paul is the main one to bear the brunt of this cross-examination by governors and kings, yet Paul is not present in Matthew 10 at the commission to preach the kingdom of heaven. Here, then, a cautious and reverent reader will take stock in what has preceded and will carefully note again the ominous warning, foreseeing a yet [future commission to Jewish evangelists that will take place later, preceding Christ's Second Coming]{.class_4257}. If this interpretation seems fanciful, let the student look quickly at verse 23 in the context of Matthew 10 and the accompanying material in Matthew 24:9 and Mark 13:9--11. We must be careful. Whereas Jesus means what He says and is not lying in His remarks in Matthew 10:17--20, we must face the fact that at the giving of this commission in the immediate historical setting, [the events did not take place with the twelve]{.class_4257}! (A constant review of the remarks regarding Matt. 3:1--2 should be made if any sense is to be made of Matt. 10.)

["The Spirit of your Father."]{.class_4258} Again, notice the selective use of the ["your Father"]{.class_4258} in contrast to the [Gentiles]{.class_4257}. Up till here Christ []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_170}never intimates, directly or indirectly, that God is the Father of anyone but an Israelite. (See Matt. 5:16, 6:32, 6:7--8.) The doctrine of the "Fatherhood of God," of course, is the blasphemous innovation of the "one worlders," who have predetermined to reject the revelation of the very God who made the world and owns it (John 1:1--10). The unregenerate mind (especially the educated mind) hates the selectivity of the "new birth" into God's family, by faith in a crucified and risen Saviour. It hates this as a Communist hates an Independent Baptist Church.

10:21 And the brother shall deliver up the brother to death, and the father the child: and the children shall rise up against their parents, and cause them to be put to death.

22 And ye shall be hated of all men for my name's sake: but he that endureth to the end shall be saved.

23 But when they persecute you in this city, flee ye into another: for verily I say unto you, Ye shall not have gone over the cities of Israel, till the Son of man be come.

Abruptly in the discourse the Lord Jesus refers to passages that will be literally fulfilled at the Second Coming. You may safely ignore every liberal writer and every amillennial or postmillennial writer on the passage, without giving any of their material on the passage any consideration. Indeed it would not be out of order to say that a postmillennial, Catholic, amillennial, or Liberal mind, approaching the passage, would be so handicapped from intellectual inability that we would not even have to consider a sketchy "draft" of their thoughts on the subject. Note the mystifying elements that only a premillennial interpretation can sort-out.

1\. The disciples were not hated of all men at this time (Acts 2:43, 4:4, 23).

2\. ["The end"]{.class_4258} spoken of has no reference to the end of their lives, and it is aimed at ["them,"]{.class_4258} referring to the people to whom they are getting ready to witness!

3\. ["The end"]{.class_4258} (in every context a Jew could understand), in either Testament, refers to a period of time immediately preceding the coming of a literal King, from heaven, in glory, and cataclysmic destruction of the Gentile powers (Dan. 9:26--27, 8:17--19, 7:26, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_171}11:35--40, 12:8--13; Matt. 24:13--14).

4.[ "The end"]{.class_4258} in no way refers to the destruction of Jerusalem in A.D. 70, for many who endured to this end were not saved. Furthermore, many who were saved spiritually were killed physically before, during, and after "the end." History destroys the sense of the words [physically or spiritually ]{.class_4257}if the reference is to the siege of Titus in A.D. 70. To make matters worse, many of the "saved" did not endure to the end of A.D. 70 and died before A.D. 50 (see 1 Cor. 15:5--10).

5\. The crowning insult to the Catholic, Liberal, Amillennial pretense is that the Son of Man [did not come]{.class_4257} (and if He did come in 70 A.D. spiritually, He came [late]{.class_4257}, for He says here that [they will not have finished going over the cities of Israel]{.class_4257} before the Son of Man be come. They finished their testimony to Israel before Acts 28, and He did NOT COME!).

Any interpretation but a premillennial interpretation (which fixes the passage in the future, to be literally fulfilled by Jewish missionaries in the tribulation) is not only a stupid attempt to exposit, but it makes a liar out of Christ or history. It may be repeated, and repeated pointedly, that the amillennial and postmillennial systems, [when applied to interpreting the Bible,]{.class_4257} fail utterly at dozens of points and are hopelessly confused in their aim, purpose, intent, and results. ["If any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant"]{.class_4258} (degrees and all!).

The correct interpretation of the Holy Spirit (comparing Scripture with Scripture, and honoring the dictates of common sense and normal observation) is that the passage is a foreshadowing of something literally true in the future, which could have been literally true if Israel had accepted the risen Saviour in Acts 2--7. This interpretation is a little too complicated and dense for the dead orthodox or liberal mind, and since the "mysteries of the kingdom" are purposely hidden from their understanding, the Bible believer may go happily on, confident that the passage in Matthew 10:21--23 is prophetic and over-leaps the Church Age and forecasts the time of "Jacob's troubles" (Jer. 30:7; Dan. 12:1--3).

Just to bamboozle the scholars, who are always wasting time in the unsearchable riches of the "Greek," the Holy Spirit has written[ "an,"]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_172} and as any beginner in Greek knows, this signifies the second class conditional sentence: "the condition determined as [unfulfilled]{.class_4257}." The ["heos"]{.class_4257} for ["TILL the Son of man be come"]{.class_4258} drives home the premillennial system and demolishes the superficial exegesis of other systems. The ["till"]{.class_4258} is [conditional]{.class_4257}. That condition is not fixed until the closing verses of Acts 7.

10:24 The disciple is not above his master, nor the servant above his lord.

25 It is enough for the disciple that he be as his master, and the servant as his lord. If they have called the master of the house Beelzebub, how much more shall they call them of his household?

Verse 24 is self-evident and is magnified in the New Testament by such additional teachings as we find in John 15:20 and John 13:15--16. ["It is enough"]{.class_4258} states that if the servant and disciple are as good as their Master or Lord, they are accomplishing a superhuman feat; in plainer words, they are certainly not to expect [better treatment ]{.class_4257}than their Master or Lord, when their treatment would normally be worse; or at the best, only [equal]{.class_4257}. ["Beelzebub"]{.class_4258} is identified as the "prince of demons" in Matthew 12:24, and is equated with Satan himself in the same passage---verse 26. (There may be some connection with the god of 2 Kings 1:1--2---the legendary "god of flies" or "god of filth.") Jesus says that if they have called the master (indicating that He had already been called Beelzebub before), they will also call the disciple. And surely enough, in Matthew 9:34, ["the prince of devils"]{.class_4258} appears in the Pharisees' accusation.

10:26 Fear them not therefore: for there is nothing covered, that shall not be revealed; and hid, that shall not be known.

27 What I tell you in darkness, that speak ye in light: and what ye hear in the ear, that preach ye upon the housetops.

28 And fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_173}destroy both soul and body in hell.

The entire passage exhorts the Jewish witness to fear God and not fear men. Numerous examples of the teaching can be found in the Old Testament (too many to number in fact). "The fear of the Lord," far from being a "reverential trust," is in both testaments an awe and a dread and a trembling before the Holy Sovereign of the universe (note Heb. 12:28; Phil. 2:12; Exod. 20:20; Psa. 15:4, 111:10; Prov. 8:13, 9:10, 15:33; 23:17; Job 28:28).

Seduced by his modernistic and liberal church-travellers, the Fundamentalist has been led astray into thinking that[ "perfect love casteth out fear"]{.class_4258} (1 John 4:18) without reading the context, verse 17. (The context is talking about [fear of going to hell at the White Throne Judgment]{.class_4257} (1 John 4:17; Rev. 20:13--15). The Day of Judgment mentioned in the passage always refers to the [Last Judgment of the unsaved dead]{.class_4257} (2 Pet. 2:9, 3:7).

Our deluded liberal friends go right on preaching "don't fear," "we've nothing to fear but fear," and ignore the plain commandment to be hell-scared instead of hell-scorched. The "Master," so reverenced by lip and pen in our liberal pulpits, is told politely to shut up when it comes to such subjects as [fearing hell,]{.class_4257} yet the "Master" goes right on talking about it constantly (see Luke 12:5). And adding insult to injury, the Master quotes Isaiah 66:24 more times in the "gospels" than any passage in the Old Testament, [yet this passage deals with hell-fire!]{.class_4257} Matthew 10:28 is a clear, plain, flat, cold warning on going to Hell. The folks who ignore the warning the most are the ones who are afraid of law suits, foreclosures, bankruptcies, diseases, and the tongues of slander. Still, the warning stands as a Gospel Commandment.

The reference to "hidden things" in the passage serves a two-fold purpose. Practically, it instructs the disciple in the fact that God will judge a man for what goes on in the dark which no one knows about; therefore, Hell is the sure and final end for a lot of folks who look fine in the daytime. Secondly, it notifies the disciples that His message is certain and the prophecies He speaks of will come to pass literally if they are from the Lord's lips. Notice the ["What I tell you in darkness\...."]{.class_4258} There is a peculiar coincidence in the verse in regard to the Church Age, in that from 1920 and on, the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_174}[housetop preaching comes to pass literally through the means of radio aerials and TV antennas on the housetops!]{.class_4257}

["Soul and body in hell"]{.class_4258} introduces some doctrinal truths that must be recognized and handled correctly.

1\. The body is not the soul (1 Thess. 5:23; Job 12:10, 14:22, 33:18).

2\. The soul has a bodily shape (2 Cor. 12:1--4; Rev. 6:9; Luke 16:19--24).

3\. In the Old Testament, the soul is stuck to the body, so often they are spoken of as identical (Ezek. 18:4; Gen. 19:20; Josh. 10:35).

4\. The New Birth in the New Testament is a spiritual operation, cutting this soul loose from the body inside (Col. 2:11--13).

5\. In either Testament, the man's soul is his "ego" or "I am." And as such, it is distinctively his property in distinction from the "spirit of man," which all men have in common (1 Cor. 2:10--14). This "soul" is the "astral body" of Rosicrucianism, and the "Atman" of Brahmism. A man can lose it. It can be destroyed in hell, and this is the plain teaching of the greatest authority on spiritual matters ever to put foot on the face of the earth. ["Fear him."]{.class_4258}

10:29 Are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall on the ground without your Father.

30 But the very hairs of your head are all numbered.

31 Fear ye not therefore, ye are of more value than many sparrows.

32 Whosoever therefore shall confess me before men, him will I confess also before my Father which is in heaven.

33 But whosoever shall deny me before men, him will I also deny before my Father which is in heaven.

Verse 29, with Luke 12:6, was one of the classic "contradictions" of the Bible. I say was, since the Bible has a way of making fools out of unregenerate scholarship with the passage of time, and the original 1,500 "contradictions" (published by students of the Harvard Five-Foot Shelf of cut-baloney) have now dwindled to less than 100, and 90 of these can be solved with common sense, without []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_175}the aid of a Hebrew or Greek lexicon. In Luke 12:6 it is "five-for-two," and here it is "two-for-one." The catch being that the language used is straight, American street-language (Greek Koine in the first century), that any simpleton would understand at a sale. Two-for-one farthing, and five-for-two farthings. What could be simpler? One hershey bar for a nickel, three for a dime. No contradiction; just simple bargains.

["Without your Father"]{.class_4258} states that the falling sparrow does not fall unless God has an actual interest in the falling, and is conscious and aware of the incident. This all-seeing God,, who observes sparrows falling and [hair falling ]{.class_4257}(see vs. 30), is hardly the kind of a God with which a Liberal can keep company. The Liberal God is an overseeing Father that deals only in [generalizations;]{.class_4257} nothing specific, like a New Birth or accepting a blood atonement by an incarnate Saviour. The Liberal has to have a sort of Sears-Roebuck God, who accepts en masse the general homage of every race who recognizes that some God they think is there, probably is. (If that last statement isn't clear, read it over again.)

Notice the ["fear ye not"]{.class_4258} of verse 31 follows the commandment to [fear]{.class_4257}, exactly as it is stated in Exodus 20:20. Love and fear are together (see Heb. 12:4--10).

The confusion and "denial" of verses 32--33 has some application to a stated, formal confession, in the presence of hostile witnesses, as is seen by the context (vss. 23--28). Although the confession can be applied to salvation (as in Rom. 10:9--10), the context here leans to another type of confession. The "denial," although here referring to the salvation of the [soul ]{.class_4257}(see vs. 28), in other places refers to the Lord denying an inheritance to a sinning Christian who is saved---but not a cross-bearer. These verses need to be studied with great care. They are found in 2 Timothy 2:11--13; Romans 8:17; Colossians 3:24; Genesis 15:8, 13; Hebrews 12:17; Ephesians 5:5; and 1 Corinthians 6:9--11.

10:34 Think not that I am come to send peace on earth: I came not to send peace, but a sword.

35 For I am come to set a man at variance against his father, and the daughter against her mother, and the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_176}daughter in law against her mother in law.

36 And a man's foes shall be they of his own household.

37 He that loveth father or mother more than me is not worthy of me: and he that loveth son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me.

38 And he that taketh not his cross, and followeth after me, is not worthy of me.

39 He that findeth his life shall lose it: and he that loseth his life for my sake shall find it.

Again, the liberal mind runs into snares. In spite of the "Peace on earth, good will to men,"[ ]{.class_4258}heralded by the angels (Luke 2), Christ's statement is that [He has not come for that reason]{.class_4257}. The sudden appearance of a ["sword"]{.class_4258} (which He also recommends buying in Luke 22:36) puts the "blessed-are-the-peacemaker-prophets" in a shaky situation. The sword not only refers to a literal sword for literal self-defense (as in Luke), but also refers to Christ Himself [tearing up homes with doctrinal arguments over His own person.]{.class_4257} The liberal mind fails to grasp this tremendous truth.

Within the swamp background of its assorted neuroses and prejudices, the Liberal commentator on the gospels can always be counted on to come through with a "peace loving," "fatherly," "blessed-are-the-little-children" kind of "one-world-togetherness"; but alas, the author of this same theology, where we find Him in Scriptures, has something more to say (see John 9; Matt. 23; and the text, Matt. 10:24--36). "Peace" is not the all-inclusive term of Christ's ministry. "Peace" is the stock and trade-mark [of the Antichrist's religious teaching,]{.class_4257} for observe Daniel 9:25, 11:24. ["Blessed are the peacemakers,"]{.class_4258} applied to international politics or even family disputes, is a misplaced text. Where the peace is not a [spiritual peace ]{.class_4257}(James 3:17) upon believing what God has said in the Bible about the Prince of Peace (Eph. 2:14, 6:15)---instilled into the heart and mind of the individual (Phil 4:6--7)---all talk about "peace" is preparation for [war]{.class_4257}. Politicians understand this concept much better than seminary professors, for they constantly state that "by preparedness [we may maintain 'the peace'!"]{.class_4257} (Which is a nice way of saying: "Be ready to blow his head off if he makes a false move in any []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_177}direction, and he'll think twice before he moves!")

Simeon acknowledges this piercing ["sword,"]{.class_4258} which is going to tear up families (Luke 2:35). The writer of Hebrews 4:12 identifies it with the word of God, and John calls it the Incarnate Christ (John 1:1--2). All the attempts to replace Jesus Christ with Buddha and Mohammed, and all attempts to rob Him of His authority in religious matters by subjective opinions, and all broadminded attempts to cross His dogmatic statements as to His own authority end where they should---in out-and-out war and disaster. This is the one lesson the educated American will never learn. He will never learn that [God is selective.]{.class_4257}

Americans, without the new birth, from high-school freshmen to Ph.D.'s, insist that general good in all religions points to general truths which make men generally acceptable to a general God, who deals in generalities. The God of the Bible has [one Book, one Saviour, one Faith, one Lord, one Body, one baptism, one race, and one way to heaven.]{.class_4257} As such, He can hardly be expected to be found "fit company" for one-world integrationists, who have a dozen ways to get to a man-made heaven. (As a minister once said to a man who was beginning to believe the Bible and preach its passages against man-made religions: "Trim your message, and you'll go all the way to the top." To which he replied: ["But live fish are on the bottom,]{.class_4257} and dead fish float belly-up!")

Verse 36 applies to a quotation from Micah 7:5--6, expressed again in Mark 8:34--38. The import is that when it comes to the essential vital issue of the Deity of Christ as over against Mary, Joseph, Buddha, Confucius, Lao Tse, Zoroaster, Tammuz, Pope Johnny, and Abraham Lincoln, an argument will arise in many homes that will tear up the home; and [Jesus Christ is the Author]{.class_4257} of this torn-up home. (Read Matt. 10:34--35 if this seems incredible.) All unsaved people think alike. They all think that if you live up to the light you have, and sincerely try to do right, you will not go to hell; of course, any reader of the Bible can see that this thinking assumes two propositions, which are not true, and arrives at a conclusion that does not match the propositions.

Verse 37 is worded even stronger by our Lord in Luke 14:26. This "hating" is defined in Genesis 29:30--31 and Deuteronomy 13:6, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_178}33:9, and it brings up an interesting moral and ethical speculation that the sophists of our universities have never considered: namely, ["What is WRONG with loving your MOTHER?"]{.class_4257} The answer is: "Plenty, if you believe what Jesus said about His Deity and power to save and your mother doesn't." But this is too strong a discipline for the philosophical mind, and it turns to easier subjects such as Hegel's dialectical materialism.

Kant's categorical imperative, Kierkegaard's existentialism, and Fletcher's "situation ethics": none of these have any direct relation to the sin of rejecting the Bible statements. Catholic dogma recognizes the issues involved, but perverts the application to where the torn-up home is "torn-up" through someone [obeying the CHURCH DOGMA of the Roman hierarchy]{.class_4257}, instead of someone [obeying the Lord Jesus Christ.]{.class_4257} This misplaced loyalty tears up as many homes as the correct loyalty speaks of, but since it is a ["way which seemeth right unto a man,"]{.class_4258} ["the end thereof"]{.class_4258} (of course) is the "way of death" (Prov. 14:12).

Matthew 19:29 puts additional light on the text, and again it is emphasized that the placing of Jesus Christ Himself (not His church or His sacraments) first, often involves the loss of kinfolk and loved ones. Whereas church officials point the way to teaching, dogma, creed, example, sacraments, and officials, Christ lays the emphasis [on Himself.]{.class_4257}

This continual "self-emphasis," which is found through all four gospels and 85 percent of Jesus Christ's "teachings," clearly marks Him as an egotist, if He is only "ONE way of reaching God out of MANY." But again the implications are too clear and plain for the philosophical mind. (Let us return to something more elusive like "the Golden Mean" or "the Golden Rule.") If the Liberal ever faces, as a redblooded man, the real implications of teaching that Jesus Christ was one of a series of teachers indicating the way to God, he will have to quit praying to Christ or in Christ's name, and he will have to cease to give him an equal place with George Washington; for lo and behold, George was born of married parents in a legitimate state, whereas, if Jesus were not virgin-born, He was the illegitimate product of a harlot and an adulterer! George never emphasized himself as someone great, but Christ constantly claims to be someone special above everyone else, and to prove it, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_179}[He lets people bow down and worship Him]{.class_4257} (Matt. 14:33).

Such "facing-up to the facts" is highly distasteful to the Liberal mind, which prefers to remain "hidden behind relatives." You can't flush a covey of Liberals unless you sick the dogs on them.

Verse 38 goes even further in the emphasis, and implies that unless a man is willing to classify himself as a criminal (capital punishment for a criminal in this age being crucifixion) in the eyes of his contemporaries, he is not a worthy disciple of the "Master." Again the religious liberal is found wanting, for the world and society in which he lives looks upon him as a humanitarian and a benefactor, not a criminal.
