So there would be no mistake in our understanding this passage, the Holy Spirit has added: ["And he was numbered with the transgressors"]{.class_4258} (Mark 15:28---which the [RSV ]{.class_4257}and Moffatt nicely cut out, using the pen-knife of the Westcott and Hort text with which to do it). Christ Himself, to emphasize the truth, describes the instrument of execution that a disciple will have to bring with him, and sure enough, it is the instrument [that executes a transgressor!]{.class_4257}

Verse 39 states one of the many "paradoxical" truths of revelation. Luke 17:33 states it again, and briefly it may be summarized thus: God does not think and act the way men think and act (Isa. 55:5--9); therefore, what would normally be a success to a man is a failure to God, and vice versa. To put it in G.I. talk: "Things are nearly always just the opposite from what they seem to be." The Bible is loaded with examples. Perhaps the outstanding case is Noah, who gives up the "old world," choosing to appear as a pessimistic crank and a fanatical "prophet of gloom"; upon abandoning the whole kosmos, he steps into a wooden boat. When he walks out, he is supreme and sole ruler of seven continents and everything on them! Practically, this paradox can be illustrated a dozen ways: the way to make money is to spend it, the way to create is to suffer death of self, the way to build a body is wear it out with exercise, and the way to avoid self-pity is to give self to others. Biblically, the way up is the way DOWN (Isa. 14:12--16), and the way down is the way UP (James 4:10).

10:40 He that receiveth you receiveth me, and he that []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_180}receiveth me receiveth him that sent me.

41 He that receiveth a prophet in the name of a prophet shall receive a prophet's reward; and he that receiveth a righteous man in the name of a righteous man shall receive a righteous man's reward.

42 And whosoever shall give to drink unto one of these little ones a cup of cold water only in the name of a disciple, verily I say unto you, he shall in no wise lose his reward.

A quick check-up on 11:1 will remind the reader that the immediate context is the sending-out of the twelve, and although numerous practical applications can be made (and have been made) to modern problems of consecration and cross-bearing, the essential truths deal with Jewish disciples during Christ's earthly ministry, with a doctrinal bearing on the coming "Great Tribulation." This is necessary to remember for the reasons that follow in verses 40--42.

Christ's identification with His disciples here is [figurative]{.class_4257}, as in Matthew 25:43. That it cannot be literal is evident, for Christ is standing beside them as a [man]{.class_4257}, in the flesh. When they go, He is still literally standing where they left Him (11:1). In the Church Age, however, the identification is much closer in the Holy Spirit (being Christ in spiritual form---see Col. 1:27, 2:6, 9--10; Eph. 3:17, 4:30). The believer is [in Christ ]{.class_4257}and Christ is [in him]{.class_4257}, whereas the disciple of Matthew 10:40 (as in the tribulation) merely [represents Christ ]{.class_4257}and therefore stands in His stead.

Observe how closely the defense of His ministry follows the passage of Isaiah 61:1--3, which He began to quote in the synagogue of Capernaum (Luke 4). Again, the "gospel" (good news) is not in anyway remotely connected to the death, burial, and resurrection of an individual Saviour for individual Jew-Gentile sinners. The good news here is that God has sent to earth the King-Messiah, who shall deliver Israel and save them from their sins (Matt. 1:21) and their enemies (Luke 1:68--71) and from their sicknesses (Matt. 8:17). To rush for a Christian application of the passage to the local church and quoting "Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and today, and forever," is to flirt with confusion. Don't apply it to healing before you know what it is. [You might need a cold-pack instead of a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw935.xhtml_page_181}mustard-plaster.]{.class_4257}

["In the name of a righteous man"]{.class_4258} is a little obscure, but the probable meaning is that you receive the man at his word and take him to be what he professes to be. The ["receiveth,"]{.class_4258} throughout the context, refers to the hospitable welcome of the disciple by the people to whom he witnesses and their treatment of him in his own need (Rom. 15:27). One cannot help being struck by the tremendous emphasis the Holy Spirit places on this ministration, in regard to treatment of Jews [in the Tribulation ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 25; James 3; 1 John 3:15--18; Heb. 10:32--39). Since the ["prophet's reward"]{.class_4258} is equated with the ["prophet,"]{.class_4258} it follows that those who help out the true prophet will share in the reward that he gets for proclaiming the truth, even though the "helpers" do not proclaim it along with him (2 Kings 4:9--13).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml}

# Chapter 11 {.heading_84-0}

11:1 And it came to pass, when Jesus had made an end of commanding his twelve disciples, he departed thence to teach and to preach in their cities.

The ["their cities"]{.class_4258} implies the cities of the disciples, which of course, would be the region round about the Sea of Galilee. (This is usually called Christ's third tour through Galilee, and it occurs in the middle of His ministry: the first tour being described in Matthew 4, Luke 4, and Mark 1; the second tour being found in Luke 8, Matthew 8--9, and Mark 3,4. John has filled in a number of incidents between Christ's baptism and Matthew 11:1, and these incidents occur during the first Galilean tour in John 5.) All the material in John 2--4 belongs to the first year of our Lord's ministry which, according to some, occurs [between ]{.class_4257}Christ's baptism and Matthew 4:17. At any rate, this tour goes for the third time into the region of Christ's "upbringing."

11:2 Now when John had heard in the prison the works of Christ, he sent two of his disciples,

3 And said unto him, Art thou he that should come, or do we look for another?

A great deal has been written concerning John's apparent skepticism in this portion of Scripture (Luke 7:19--22). That John should be skeptical is peculiar, in view of the fact that many months before he had clearly identified Jesus Christ as the chosen one of Israel and "the Lamb of God" that bears away the sin of the world (see John 1). Neither can it be said that John was "wavering" in faith, for the testimony which Jesus bears of him would deny this (notice vs. 7). There is obviously a truth hidden here that needs to be examined.

The truth of John's motive in asking is not apparent until we read the closing statement of Jesus to the people in verse 6. ["Offended"]{.class_4258} is the word that describes John's condition. He is in jail for preaching the truth. He is in there after heralding the approach and coming of a King-Deliverer. All right, put yourself in John's sandals. "If this is a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_183}'King-Deliverer' and I am His divinely appointed forerunner, what on earth am I doing in the stockade?" You see, when you put yourself in the other man's place, a lot of things get clear! It is almost certain that until the moment John's head fell on the chopping block, he doubted if he had really been in the whole will of God, but he was, from start to finish. The student must once again remind himself of the setting and the Old Testament background. John had a right to look for the Second Advent when Christ showed up the first time. All conditions are fulfilled, [no one knew of the Church Age,]{.class_4257} and even the Church Age is held in abeyance till Acts 7. Get your bearings. Don't jump on John too quickly. A lot of folks that jumped on him never did get Acts 2 straightened out, [even after it happened!]{.class_4257}

11:4 Jesus answered and said unto them, Go and shew John again those things which ye do hear and see:

5 The blind receive their sight, and the lame walk, the lepers are cleansed, and the deaf hear, the dead are raised up, and the poor have the gospel preached to them.

6 And blessed is he, whosoever shall not be offended in me.

This is an important passage, for it shows that when the Lord Jesus wanted to confirm His Messiahship, as the rightful "One who should come" (Gen. 49:10), He points to the "signs and wonders" of healing that accompanied the ministry of Moses (Exod. 4).

These are the credentials of the Messiah, and they identify Him as the Prophet "like unto Moses" (Deut. 18:18).

["Those things"]{.class_4258} must refer (in the context) to the works and mighty wonders done by the Lord Jesus in the vicinity, thus identifying Him as the God-man, Messiah; for in verse 27 follows a statement of His deity, which is one of the clearest in the New Testament. \[The synoptic gospels (four minus John) are the favorite part of the Bible to the unregenerate Liberal, as they contain the salvation-by-works-plan of the "teachings of the Master." But right in the middle of this perverted way of reading the Bible (ignoring the fact that it is a Jewish Messiah with an earthly ministry to Israel, prophesied in the Old Testament), we suddenly run across as clear a statement on Christ's deity as any in John's gospel.\]

11:7 And as they departed, Jesus began to say unto the multitudes concerning John, What went ye out into the wilderness to see? A reed shaken with the wind?

8 But what went ye out for to see? A man clothed in soft raiment? behold, they that wear soft clothing are in kings' houses.

9 But what went ye out for to see? A prophet? yea, I say unto you, and more than a prophet.

["For to see"]{.class_4258} is the Old English, stating in a brief form: "For what purpose did you come out here looking around?" The Old English statement is shorter and much more satisfactory than saying this, or going to the length of saying: "Why did you come out here looking around for something?"

As the passage is constructed, it appears that upon each question His audience regarded Him with a fixed stare or at least thoughtful silence. Since none could answer Him (or did not choose to do so), He continues till finally He answers His own questions.

It is obvious that John was [not ]{.class_4257}["a reed shaken with the wind."]{.class_4258} Any man who would "tell a king off" and make him try to live right (Mark 6:20) is not ["a reed shaken with the wind."]{.class_4258} ["A man clothed in soft raiment"]{.class_4258} can hardly be it, for look at the raiment in Mark 1:6. The ["soft"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}here is ["malaxois,"]{.class_4257} the neuter word used in 1 Corinthians 6:9 to represent an effeminate courtier. This is hardly John. A man who tells Roman legionnaires where to head-in (Luke 3:14) will hardly be found playing an organ in a cocktail lounge. ["A prophet?"]{.class_4258} Christ affirms this, and then adds that John is a prophet plus. What follows in verses 10--13 explains the statement.

11:10 For this is he, of whom it is written, Behold, I send my messenger before thy face, which shall prepare thy way before thee.

11 Verily I say unto you, Among them that are born of women there hath not risen a greater than John the Baptist: notwithstanding he that is least in the kingdom of heaven is greater than he.

12 And from the days of John the Baptist until now the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_185}it by force.

13 For all the prophets and the law prophesied until John.

["Of whom it is written."]{.class_4258} The statement is a double question, as it is found in Isaiah 40 and Malachi 3. Mark 1:2 refers to this also and refers to it correctly, citing ["prophets"]{.class_4258} in the plural, not "prophet," as Moffatt reads. The [ASV, RSV]{.class_4257}, and other corruptions of text quote Mark 1:2 as ["Isaiah the prophet,"]{.class_4257} as it is also found in the Westcott and Hort pro-Catholic text. The Greek Textus Receptus, however, being far more accurate than Westcott and Hort and Company, correctly says ["prophatais"]{.class_4257} the prophets, since it is a plain, English, historical, grammatical fact that more than one prophet cites the passage (see Isa. 40:3 and Mal. 3:1). The Greek, English, and Hebrew bear witness, with common sense and ordinary observation, that the passage in Mark 1:2--3 is a quotation from both prophets---[not Isaiah only]{.class_4257}. The [AV ]{.class_4257}text, therefore, is true to the Greek, Hebrew, and English common sense; Westcott and Hort, as usual, are true to illogical blundering. To imply that credit for Mark 1:2 should be given to Isaiah only is fantastic, for Mark 1:3 [is Isaiah's quotation,]{.class_4257} and Mark 1:2 [is not even found in sixty-six chapters of Isaiah---in any text!]{.class_4257}

["Shall prepare thy way before thee"]{.class_4258} introduces a curious change of "person," for when we read the text in Malachi (not Isaiah), we find ["me"]{.class_4258} for thee, and ["my"]{.class_4258} for thy. This is tremendously important for the "me-my" of Malachi is Jehovah of the Old Testament. Jesus' quotation of the verse then clearly identifies Him as Jehovah manifest in the flesh. This is what He professed (John 20:28), and it is what His enemies [understood Him to profess ]{.class_4257}(John 10:33, 8:58).

["Among them that are born of women."]{.class_4258} This statement is worthy of careful study as it implies that the ["least in the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} are not born of women, or if they are, they are in a dispensation or setup that has opportunities unknown to John. Considering the first proposition, the Bible teaches that the new birth is a birth from God (John 1:13), not from a woman (1 Pet. 1:23). By such a standard, people "born again" in the Church Age []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_186}represent a different group than the Old Testament "law and prophets," who are all "born of women." (John 3:29 and 2 Corinthians 11:1--3 bear this truth out.)

However, we must be careful in reading Church Age "new births" into the passage, for the ["kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} has nowhere been equated with the Church Age, and the church is certainly not equated with the kingdom (see Matt. 3:1--2). To get an exact application of truth we are forced again to look beyond the Church Age to that period of time spoken of in the prophets (Isa. 11; Zech. 13), [which could have come to pass at the First Coming of Christ ]{.class_4257}if the nation of Israel had repented, and accepted Him as their Messiah. The ["least"]{.class_4258} in this kingdom would have better opportunities than John, they would not need ["a rough garment to deceive"]{.class_4258} (Zech. 13:3--6), they would have a risen-and-glorified-Christ in their presence, and they would have a world regenerated by His presence (Matt. 19:28).

["The kingdom of heaven suffereth violence."]{.class_4258} There are several volumes of notes by various commentators seeking to find an explanation. The two main ones which do any amount of justice to the text are: (1) That it takes a violent pressing or determination to accept the message of John the Baptist, and the publicans, harlots, and soldiers represent this "taking by force." (See Luke 16:16, 3:12--14; and Matthew 21:32.) \[John 6:15 may explain the attempts of the "violent" to take the kingdom.\] The kingdom of heaven, as such, it must be remembered, always refers to the [literal, visible, heavenly reign of God over the literal, visible heavens, which will begin again with a literal, physical, visible, Messianic, Davidic King at Jerusalem]{.class_4257} (Matt. 6:33). Attempts to force the doctrine into any other setting ends where all amillennial and postmillennial reasoning ends---on the ash heap. (2) The only other rendering of the passage is that the "violent" are the unlawful usurpers of the Kingdom: i.e., the priests and Pharisees (Matt. 21:43--46). This may be the case, as verses 12--13 clearly divide the kingdom into three dispensations:

A. The Old Testament of the Law and the Prophets, ending at John.

B. An interim period of John, until the crucifixion of Christ.

C. A future period (Matt. 8:11), at the Second Coming.

This further checks with verse 14, a verse always aiming at the Second Advent (Mal. 4:5). John then is the "heading up" of the Old Testament. He is not the first Baptist in the Baptist church; he is the end of the Old Testament Jewish prophets. In connection with this, it may be observed that the "golden rule" goes out [with John ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 7:12)---a bitter pill for a man to swallow, who is counting on it for self-justification before God!

11:14 And if ye will receive it, this is Elias, which was for to come.

15 He that hath ears to hear, let him hear.

["This is Elias"]{.class_4258} poses a flat contradiction to John's emphatic statement that he is [not ]{.class_4257}Elias (John 1:21). The issue becomes more cloudy when we find that Jesus said that Elias "was going to come first," and then followed it by saying that he "already came" (Matt. 17:10--13). No wonder He cautions with ["He that hath ears to hear, let him hear!"]{.class_4258} This will take some listening!

To unravel the mystery it should be noted, first of all, that Moses and Elijah are both associated with the coming of the Lord in glory, to reign (Mal. 4:1--5). In Revelation 11:8, they both appear to herald His coming and both are [beheaded ]{.class_4257}(Rev. 20:3--6),[ as John was beheaded.]{.class_4257} This leaves us where we came in. John the Baptist is a type of Elijah (Luke 1:15--17), who will appear later; but he already has appeared in the sense that [if Israel had accepted Christ,]{.class_4257} John would suffice for the prophecy of Malachi 4:4--5 and 3:1--2. You cannot escape the rock-solid fact that until Matthew 13, all is in correct preparation for the fulfilling of the "Day of the Lord,"[ without a "Church Age."]{.class_4257}

Gradually the "postponement theory," which worries Catholics and Liberals so much, becomes established as a Bible fact, rooted and grounded in every passage in both Testaments. Without recognizing the "theory," the theorem is impossible to learn. John is a type of Elijah, and if the Tribulation had begun with a rapture in Acts 7, all prophecies in the Old Testament would have been fulfilled. The conversion of the Gentiles could have taken place through Israel during [a millennium with Christ on earth, instead of a Church Age with Christ in heaven!]{.class_4257}

11:16 But whereunto shall I liken this generation? It is like unto children sitting in the markets, and calling unto their fellows,

17 And saying, We have piped unto you, and ye have not danced; we have mourned unto you, and ye have not lamented.

18 For John came neither eating nor drinking, and they say, He hath a devil.

19 The Son of man came eating and drinking, and they say, Behold a man gluttonous, and a winebibber, a friend of publicans and sinners. But wisdom is justified of her children.

The verses illustrate graphically the Divine "sense of humor," and they show the ironic bite which Christ could give an audience if He cared to. The entire passage is sarcastic, and it is making fun of John's critics (and His own).

They are in the ["markets"]{.class_4258}; that is where the money is (Matt. 22:5, 23:7), and they are sitting on the curb accusing each other with childish shouts. ["Piped unto you\...."]{.class_4258} The perfect case is the wild dance "to pay the piper" in Matthew 14:6. [John refuses to dance.]{.class_4257} ["We have mourned unto you\...."]{.class_4258} The perfect case is in Matthew 9:23 where [Jesus refuses to mourn. ]{.class_4257}John comes as a sort of ascetic "holiness" preacher, with rules of separation and conduct. Instead of commending him for it, they say: "He's full of the devil." So God sends them a popular evangelist, who eats with "publicans and sinners," so they can't accuse Him of being demon-possessed. They not only do accuse Him, but also accuse Him of breaking the Old Testament Law by gluttony (Deut. 21:20) and wine-drinking---neither charge being true, as [there are no authoritative Greek manuscripts which ever mention that He drank wine in any of the four Gospels.]{.class_4257} This is a Catholic-Episcopal, cocktail opinion.

["But wisdom\..."]{.class_4258} seems to say that if a man is truly a child of the personified wisdom (see Prov. 8), he will recognize wisdom when he sees it and will justify or commend it. The outstanding statement on this is in Luke 7:29, which strangely enough is added by the beloved physician in his gospel, in the very context of Matthew 11:10--16. The ["of,"]{.class_4258} understood as ["justified BY her children,"]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_189} renders the best sense, comparing Scripture with Scripture: i.e., wisdom's children will justify her, for they recognize her when she shows up.

Something further needs to be said in regard to a current fable circulating among the National Council of Churches at this time. This fable is to the effect that Jesus (as John) was an "Essene"---a sort of pilgrims-union for ascetic Separatists. These "Essenes" were discovered to be some religious-political sect existing in Palestine in the first century, and the Qumran tablets (with the Isaiah scroll) are supposed to shed some light on "Christianity." This bamboozle, along with a thousand others (invented to reduce Jesus Christ to a humanitarian, political leader), is typical of the Biblical stupidity exhibited constantly in America by the Liberal leadership from Burrows to Niebhur. "Essenes" forbad the use of the sword, but Jesus recommended it (Luke 22:36). Essenes were strict Sabbatarians observing every tittle of the Sabbath, which Jesus [did not]{.class_4257} (Mark 2:24, 3:2). Essenes shunned all women (see John 4 and Luke 7--8) and would not eat anything cooked by anyone but their own crowd (see Matt. 9:10). \[The information is found in the [Schaff-Herzog Encyclopedia of Religious Knowledge]{.class_4257}, cited by Farrar, p. 147 from [Life of Lives]{.class_4257}, published in 1900.\] Exactly why our Liberals insist on Christ being an "Essene" is another mystery, since He never qualified in any sense of the word. [Not even His enemies identified Him with that group.]{.class_4257}

11:20 Then began he to upbraid the cities wherein most of his mighty works were done, because they repented not:

21 Woe unto thee, Chorazin! woe unto thee, Bethsaida! for if the mighty works, which were done in you, had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes.

22 But I say unto you, It shall be more tolerable for Tyre and Sidon at the day of judgment, than for you.

["Upbraid"]{.class_4258} implies a "bawling out" or a "telling where to get off." One can see in the English construction that it is a braided whip, which is used as a verbal "lash" to attack Chorazin and Bethsaida. Chorazin and Bethsaida turn out to be small towns on the north []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_190}Galilean shore, fairly far inland from the sea itself. Bethsaida is closer to the water than Chorazin, and Chorazin is a little northwest of Bethsaida. Tyre and Sidon are appropriate comparisons, for both the Galilean cities are in North Palestine, and not one hundred miles away were the great Baal-worshipping Tyre and Sidon, from whence we derive our alphabet and black-robed priests (Judg. 18:18--19, 28--31; Jer. 10:1--5; 1 Kings 16:29--33).

["Repented long ago in sackcloth and ashes"]{.class_4258} is a true statement, as we have an exact historical case in Nineveh, which repented in this fashion under the preaching of Jonah (see Jonah 3--4). Christ vouches for the historicity of Jonah, his whale, his commission, and his preaching (Matt. 12:40--41); so we may safely overlook the critical views of liberalism in regard to the book of Jonah.

["At the day of judgment."]{.class_4258} As has been noted before, where the expression occurs it is always referring to the final hours of the "day of the Lord" and looks over the millennial reign of Christ to the last judgment of the unsaved dead, for verification of this, examine closely Revelation 20:13--15; 2 Peter 2:9, 3:7; Daniel 7:9--13. The "more tolerable" has already been analyzed in the comments on Matthew 11:14--15.

11:23 And thou, Capernaum, which art exalted unto heaven, shalt be brought down to hell: for if the mighty works, which have been done in thee, had been done in Sodom, it would have remained until this day.

24 But I say unto you, That it shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment, than for thee.

The verse is self-explanatory and is practically a repeat of the material found in verses 20--22. The comparison of Sodom, however, is interesting for it clearly defeats the standard, liberal interpretation that the ["hell"]{.class_4258} referred to here is the [ground]{.class_4257}: i.e., that the cities which rejected Jesus' ministry will simply be destroyed and "levelled to the ground." This mealy-mouthed interpretation fits the liberal thinking, which recently changed the Apostles' Creed to omit "He descended into hell," [although]{.class_4257} [2,000,000 church members go right on citing it as though they []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_191}thought it had something to do with the apostles!]{.class_4257}

Notice that ["hell"]{.class_4258} is contrasted with ["heaven"]{.class_4258} in the first sentence. Further, the comparison with Sodom is tremendous, for Jude 7 finds the inhabitants of Sodom ["suffering the vengeance of eternal fire"]{.class_4258}! And if that were not enough, the God of revelation heaps double indignity on the "no-heller" by referring the whole context to the last judgment, when there are no more armies around to bring any Palestinian cities "even with the ground." ["Brought down to hell,"]{.class_4258} therefore, means what it says---["brought down to hell."]{.class_4258} The liberal's interpretation is illogical, childish, unrealistic, and inconsistent with common sense and good taste.

11:25 At that time Jesus answered and said, I thank thee, O Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because thou hast hid these things from the wise and prudent, and hast revealed them unto babes.

26 Even so, Father: for so it seemed good in thy sight.

27 All things are delivered unto me of my Father: and no man knoweth the Son, but the Father; neither knoweth any man the Father, save the Son, and he to whomsoever the Son will reveal him.

This comes immediately after His statements on Hell, and the Lord Jesus wisely adds some statements as to His Deity and authority to justify making those statements. In Luke, it is added further that upon making this statement, ["Jesus rejoiced"]{.class_4258} (Luke 10:21). This is significant, for it is the only time in the gospels that it is remarked that Jesus rejoiced. Surely a one-time chance "rejoicing" should bare some careful study. And upon studying the occasion, we find that the "rejoicing" is over the fact that [the wise and prudent can't understand what is going on, and a bunch of fishermen can]{.class_4257} (Luke 10:17; Matt. 21:16; 1 Pet. 2:1--3).

Notice that unless our Lord is [misrepresenting His person ]{.class_4257}(lying), He is claiming that [God is not everyone's Father ]{.class_4257}and that no one can know the Father unless He Himself (Christ) reveals the Father to them. The Father has not picked out a series of heathen, religious leaders to show "the way to God," [but has delivered all things to the Lord Jesus Christ ]{.class_4257}(John 14:6). This is one of the most []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_192}bigoted, unkind, dogmatic, intolerant, inhumanitarian things a religious leader ever said; but it automatically disqualifies every professor, teacher, scholar, bishop, priest, pope, and doctor on the face of the earth who gets so upset about the "poor heathen being sincere," that he slanders the revelation of God in Jesus Christ and sets up fifty saviors of equal merit.

What follows is absolute proof that this "liberal attitude" is unreasonable, for in the next statement, our Lord says something that [no religious leader would dare say, never has said, never will say, and if he said it, he couldn't get ANYONE to believe it!]{.class_4257}

11:28 Come unto me, all ye that labour and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest.

29 Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly in heart: and ye shall find rest unto your souls.

30 For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light.

["Come unto me"]{.class_4258} puts an end, historically, to "other religions," for no religious leader of any other religion dared say it. If he did (and the record is produced), there is an absolute certainty that he couldn't get anyone to believe it. Aside from hypnotic mesmerism of a handful of disciples, no religious huckster can [himself ]{.class_4257}"give rest to the weary and heavy-laden." He can give treatments, pills, shocks, tranquilizers, massages, ultra-violet rays, and psychotherapy, but he [himself ]{.class_4257}cannot give rest.

Notice the inclusive ["all ye that labour."]{.class_4258} Notice further, this is rest to your soul, [not your bodies]{.class_4257}. Buddha's "soul-rest" (through the attainment of Nirvana or Samadhi) is the result of following the Buddha's [teaching]{.class_4257}, not the result of [coming to Gautama Buddha]{.class_4257}. Jesus only can qualify as a dead, buried, and risen Saviour; this invitation, as the promise of Matthew 18:20, can only be genuine if the "teacher" giving it is all-mighty and ever-present. To those "laden" with sins, worry, guilt, disappointments, and wounded affections, the Lord says that His yoke is easier than the yoke of Satan or the yoke of this world. A ["yoke"]{.class_4258} is in the shape of a cross, so He is actually repeating the teaching of Matthew 10:38. Whereas the Greek says: "Man, know thyself," and the Roman says: "Man, rule []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1047.xhtml_page_193}thyself," and the Catholic says: "Join thyself," and the Protestant says: "Find thyself," and the Moslem says: "Submit thyself," and the Chinese says: "Improve thyself," and the Brahman says: "Merge thyself"---Jesus says: "Come unto me and deny thyself." (This explains why all religions teach a life of works to get to heaven, while the born-again Christian rests in the finished work of [Someone else]{.class_4257}.)

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml}

# Chapter 12 {.heading_84-0}

12:1 At that time Jesus went on the sabbath day through the corn; and his disciples were an hungred, and began to pluck the ears of corn, and to eat.

2 But when the Pharisees saw it, they said unto him, Behold, thy disciples do that which is not lawful to do upon the sabbath day.

3 But he said unto them, Have ye not read what David did, when he was an hungered, and they that were with him;

4 How he entered into the house of God, and did eat the shewbread, which was not lawful for him to eat, neither for them which were with him, but only for the priests?

In the actual sequence of events, we are now well along into the gospel of Matthew, and although there may not be any distinct divisions of time which mark out the history of the Kingdom of Heaven in the book, we are certainly coming to a crisis in the order of events as they are recorded in the chapters.

From what has gone before, it may be safe to say that Matthew chapter 1 records the genealogy of the King, chapter 2 records the birth of the King, chapter 3 records the herald of the King, chapter 4 records the preparation of the King, chapters 5--7 show the constitution of His kingdom, chapters 8--9 present His Old Testament credentials as King, chapter 10 shows the message of the kingdom being preached, and chapter 11 has brought us stumbling into [the rejection of the King and the kingdom ]{.class_4257}(note vss. 12, 19, 21, 23, 25). And notice especially how the last three verses in the chapter suddenly digress into a [spiritual note ]{.class_4257}not found in chapter 10 when the kingdom is being preached. There is an ominous undertone in Matthew 11:28, which warns the disciple then listening that there is more that is going to take place than the salvation of a nation. There is in it a veiled hint that salvation is an [individual matter]{.class_4257}.

With this in mind, we may understand the events which take place in Chapter 12. ["The ears of corn"]{.class_4258} has long been a disputed subject. The standard interpretation is that this is not [real ]{.class_4257}corn since []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_195}real corn was unknown in Egypt or Palestine, and therefore must refer to general grain, such as [wheat ]{.class_4257}or [barley]{.class_4257}. This (as nearly all perversions of the 1611 text) is a common error. The ["corn"]{.class_4258} [may ]{.class_4257}refer to other grain, since John 12:24 defines the word ["corn"]{.class_4258} as a "kernel" or "grain" of any crop (more unsearchable riches of the 1611!). However, ["ears of corn"]{.class_4258} are found in Genesis 41:5--7 and ["corn in the ear"]{.class_4258} is found in Mark 4:26--29. To make matters worse for the people that keep insisting on perverting the plain English, we find that in Rawlinson's [History of Ancient Egypt]{.class_4257} (Vol. 1, p. 162), [that real corn was reaped in Egypt by cutting the stalk off below the ear; that is exactly how it was reaped. A reference is found in Deuteronomy 23:25. ]{.class_4257}Again, the wise man will "slow down" and take heed to how he messes up the King's English while appealing to the "original Greek."

["Have ye not read."]{.class_4258} This is one of Jesus' favorite answers to religious leaders who accuse Him on various grounds; the one here being that He is violating the Sabbath (Matt. 21:16). The Scripture Jesus quotes (and He nearly always uses the prooftext method of settling an argument) is enlightening, for He appeals to what David the king did when he was being persecuted as an [exile king]{.class_4257}. \[This is exactly the position the Lord is about to take: a King rejected by His people, with a temporary king on the throne (Satan), whom the people will accept (Zech. 11:16; John 5:43).\]

Jesus appeals to two Scriptural facts to answer the charges of Sabbath breaking.

1\. David broke it.

2\. The priests themselves break it.

Both times He cites [Scripture to contradict the popular thought of His day and time]{.class_4257}. He uses the prooftext method throughout (see Matt. 4; Luke 4), and to this day all false teachers and heretics who want to "put something over," appeal to the Scriptures to do it.

This is such a common practice that the unregenerate educated man in America has become sick of Scripture and will often object to it being [quoted ]{.class_4257}or [read ]{.class_4257}in a discussion, since it has so often been perverted. Nevertheless, Jesus always uses this method. The reference to David is found in 1 Samuel 21:1--6, and the priests' situation is found in Numbers 29:32. (Everyone offers; on the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_196}seventh-day the priests eat it.) David's case affords scholarship a chance to point out an apparent contradiction, and it is found in the synonymous account of David, in Mark 2:26. Here [Abiathar ]{.class_4257}is said to be the high priest, yet [Ahimelech ]{.class_4257}is found in 1 Samuel 21:1--2. Again, the unsearchable riches of the [Authorized Version]{.class_4257} can help us where the Greek and Hebrew fail. In Jeremiah 52:24, we learn that there can be a ["chief priest"]{.class_4258} [and a ]{.class_4257}["second priest,"]{.class_4258} and it is interesting to note that Mark said ["the high priest,"]{.class_4258} yet 1 Samuel 21:1 does [not ]{.class_4257}say [high priest ]{.class_4257}but only ["priest."]{.class_4258} Furthermore, Luke 3:2 shows there can be [two ]{.class_4257}["high priests."]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}Mark, therefore, says: ["in the days,"]{.class_4258} indicating the general time, and therefore he is correct. There is no contradiction but in the mind of ignorant men.

Doctrinally (since David is a type of Christ and since the priest also was a type of Christ), there was nothing wrong with David borrowing some hallowed ["shewbread"]{.class_4258} (set out for "show" or display) from the priest. This truth, however, is a little deep for the Pharisees to grasp, as was the truth of Matthew 22:43--44, and all the Pharisees didn't die out in A.D. 100 either!

12:5 Or have ye not read in the law, how that on the sabbath days the priests in the temple profane the sabbath, and are blameless?

6 But I say unto you, That in this place is one greater than the temple.

7 But if ye had known what this meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice, ye would not have condemned the guiltless.

8 For the Son of man is Lord even of the sabbath day.

9 And when he was departed thence, he went into their synagogue:

Numbers 29:32 is an illustration of sabbath-breaking by the priesthood, for the daily sacrifice continues seven days a week and the priests eat the shewbread seven days a week. ["One greater than the temple"]{.class_4258} again emphasizes the fact that Jesus counts Himself to be somebody unusual and out of the ordinary. (No wonder some modern "psychiatrists" have written that He was "neurotic" or "egotistical," for if He were only "A" son of God, in the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_197}sense that every Liberal thinks himself to be, He had no business making such a statement as follows: ["I will have mercy."]{.class_4258})

["What this meaneth"]{.class_4258} (see the comments on Matt. 9:13). This is the second time the Lord Jesus has quoted the passage from Hosea 6:6. ["The guiltless,"]{.class_4258} of course, refers to Himself, as testified to by Judas Iscariot (Matt. 27:4). Pilate also bears witness to His lack of guilt three times (John 18:38, 19:4--6), [and then goes ahead and has him executed!]{.class_4257}

It is interesting to notice that Jesus never calls Himself "A" son of man, as every "son of man" would be. He is always ["the"]{.class_4258} Son of Man, and so He appears throughout as ["the"]{.class_4258} Son of God, not "A," as the [RSV ]{.class_4257}has Him named in Matthew 27:54. The erroneous and pagan theory that Christ was "A" Son of God is in keeping with the best black-magic and medieval superstitution, which we find occasionally surviving on our university campuses. ["The Son of man"]{.class_4258} emphasized His unusual birth by a virgin. "A" son of man would make Him born exactly as anyone else.

12:10 "And, behold, there was a man which had his hand withered. And they asked him, saying, Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath days? that they might accuse him.

11 And he said unto them, What man shall there be among you, that shall have one sheep, and if it fall into a pit on the sabbath day, will he not lay hold on it, and lift it out?

12 How much then is a man better than a sheep? Wherefore it is lawful to do well on the sabbath days.

13 Then saith he to the man, Stretch forth thine hand. And he stretched it forth; and it was restored whole, like as the other.

The first verse is self-explanatory and reemphasizes the constant battle about Christ's fidelity to the Old Testament Ten Commandments. The Sabbath seems to have been the special battleground chosen, as it comprises a [ceremonial observance ]{.class_4257}right in the middle of a group of moral commands. \[Careful students of the word know that the Sabbath was a Jewish sign given to Israel, by God, to indicate the seventh-day millennium coming, and still future (Ezek. 20:20, Neh. 9:14). No Gentile was ever commanded to "keep []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_198}the Sabbath," and, of course, no one payed any attention to it until the time of Moses. No one in Genesis ever observes it. And according to New Testament revelation (Rom. 13:8--11), Christians are not to keep it.\]

Luke 13:11--17 is a good corollary to Matthew 12, although in that case of healing it is a woman, and the Lord refers to an ox or an ass that needs "watering," instead of a sheep in a pit. In both cases the moral is the same: ["You break the Sabbath yourself under exceptional need, and God allows it, which shows God is not so much a stickler on ceremonial observance as YOU ARE."]{.class_4257} That is the sum of the matter.

Matthew 12:12 is the summation of teaching about the Sabbath, and further controversy is meaningless. The man is healed, in verse 13, as Jeroboam was healed in 1 Kings 13:6.

12:14 Then the Pharisees went out, and held a council against him, how they might destroy him.

15 But when Jesus knew it, he withdrew himself from thence: and great multitudes followed him, and he healed them all;

16 And charged them that they should not make him known:

The peculiar and irrational conduct of the Pharisees can only be explained by the fact that they made their living [by drawing attention to ceremonial observances]{.class_4257}. That this is the case is obvious from the strange thing that happened the Passover week. Notice that while they were [murdering God's Son with a clear conscience ]{.class_4257}(Acts 2:23, 3:15), they were worried to death about walking in the wrong building the same day, lest they should get some "Gentile defilement" (John 18:28).

The Lord [withdrew]{.class_4257}---not because they could have killed Him against His will (John 10:18)---but because from the start He knew the conditions and time of His death, and He avoided any temptations to call down God's power for help until the time (note Luke 4:30; John 8:59). When opportunities come for "dying," [before the Scriptural conditions are ripe]{.class_4257}, the Lord avoids them and walks off.

The explanation for verse 16 will be found in the comments on 9:30 and 8:4.

12:17 That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet, saying,

18 Behold my servant, whom I have chosen; my beloved, in whom my soul is well pleased: I will put my spirit upon him, and he shall shew judgment to the Gentiles.

19 He shall not strive, nor cry; neither shall any man hear his voice in the streets.

20 A bruised reed shall he not break, and smoking flax shall he not quench, till he send forth judgment unto victory.

21 And in his name shall the Gentiles trust.

This tremendous passage, occurring where it does, signals the end of Christ's attempts to put His Messiahship on Israel. The unpardonable sin, which follows, and the mysterious "parables of the kingdom" warn the reader that for the first time since the gospel began, there is a [clear statement ]{.class_4257}that God will deal with the Gentiles during some intermediate period before the kingdom comes.

["That it might be fulfilled"]{.class_4258} must be linked to the warning to His disciples not to make Him known, and of course this checks with verse 19. The quotation turns out to be a description of a silent First Coming, as a suffering servant, who will save not only Jews but Gentiles; but where the quotation occurs (Isa. 42:1--4), there is no way to tell whether it is [an individual ]{.class_4257}(as in Isa. 41:8) [representing Israel ]{.class_4257}(Isa. 45:4), or whether it is the [individual Messiah ]{.class_4257}(Isa. 49:6, 41:25) [representing Jehovah ]{.class_4257}(Isa. 53). Again, we see the subtility and shrewdness of the Divine mind. The prophecies are so placed that they can apply to Israel or to Jesus Christ, depending upon [what Israel does with Jesus Christ ]{.class_4257}when He shows up. This is the mystery that baffled the prophets (1 Pet. 1:10--11). The modern-day Gentile expositor misses the doctrine of the Second Coming now, exactly as the Pharisee missed the First Coming then; he refuses to recognize the divisions which set prophecy into dispensations.

Verse 20 is the most difficult verse in the chapter, and little can []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_200}be done with it except to spiritualize it so that it has some application to God's treatment of weak saints. The reed (the flags of Exod. 20:22) is a ["cane"]{.class_4258} in Isaiah 42:24 and a ["stalk"]{.class_4258} in Genesis 41:5. The many jointed cane in the valley of Lebanon fills the bill. It is very fragrant when bruised (Isa. 43:24).

["A bruised reed"]{.class_4258} usually refers to a saint who has been "bruised" in service, and God does not break him or throw him away, but heals him (Heb 12:13). The ["smoking flax"]{.class_4258} is found to be a type of the Holy Spirit in the saint (1 Thess. 5:19), and the promise is that God will sustain the saint till the day of redemption. Both these interpretations, however, are far-fetched and [strain ]{.class_4257}at the context, for the ["till he send forth judgment unto victory"]{.class_4258} is a reference to the Second Advent, not the victorious end of a saint's life. The passage still awaits spiritual evaluation, insight, and proper exposition. Matthew 15:24--28 is a good practical example of the statement ["in his name shall the Gentiles trust."]{.class_4258} Doctrinally, the statement is well- explained in Paul's Epistle to the Romans, especially chapters 9--11.

12:22 Then was brought unto him one possessed with a devil, blind, and dumb: and he healed him, insomuch that the blind and dumb both spake and saw.

23 And all the people were amazed, and said, Is not this the son of David?

24 But when the Pharisees heard it, they said, This fellow doth not cast out devils, but by Beelzebub the prince of the devils.

Prophetically speaking, the case of the healing (occurring here where it does) shows a remarkable order in Scripture. Notice that in verses 1--13 you have a picture of the kingdom "signs and wonders" offered to Israel, by the Messiah (John 1:11), but upon rejection He turns to the Gentiles (John 1:12), ["until the fullness of the Gentiles be come in"]{.class_4258}---while Israel is [blinded ]{.class_4257}(Acts 28:25--28); where- upon, [He restores Israel as He restores the "blind-and-dumb" man.]{.class_4257} The context of the stories and illustrations in Matthew chapter 12 (Beelzebub, Jonah, and the Queen of Sheba) goes immediately into the Great Tribulation, where the "Prince of Devils" reigns, and where [Gentiles ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_201}are converted by Jewish missionaries (Rev. 7:4--10).

Scripturally speaking, the man healed is not only blind and dumb but demon-possessed. The apostolic signs from Christ, as Israel's signs from Moses, include casting out devils, as well as physical healing (Mark 16:17).

["Is not this the son of David?"]{.class_4258} The answer is that it is. Matthew 1:1 lists Him as the Son of David, and He Himself poses a theological question which carries such implications with it that no one can answer it without admitting that He is the ["son of David"]{.class_4258} and the ["son of God"]{.class_4258} at the same time (see Matt. 22:41--46). He is the ["son of David"]{.class_4258} through Mary (Luke 3) and, as such, is heir-apparent to the throne of David (Luke 1:30--34). He is God's Son also (John 9:37) and, as such, is heir-apparent to the throne of the universe. He is a double King. [He is King over the kingdom of Heaven and King over the kingdom of God. ]{.class_4257}["Is not this the son of David?"]{.class_4258} shows again that the common populace of 2,000 years ago was much better versed in Bible prophecy than the seminary professors of the twentieth century. All the people know for [whom ]{.class_4257}they are looking (John 1:19--23), [why ]{.class_4257}they are looking for Him (Matt. 2:6), from [where ]{.class_4257}He is to come (John 7:42), [what ]{.class_4257}precedes His coming (Matt. 24:37), and [from whom ]{.class_4257}He comes (Matt. 12:23). This knowledge is a good bit ahead of our dumb dogs (D.D.'s), who think Christ's coming is an ethereal effusion of some kind of vaporous spirituality appearing at death or in positive meditations.

["This fellow\...."]{.class_4258} The vehement objections of the Pharisees can only be traced to one cause: they cannot understand how the miracles could be anything but of demon-origin, since the man that does them [will not keep the ceremonial sabbath]{.class_4257}. There is no way to figure out the Pharisees' mental pattern unless one takes this approach. Perhaps Jesus' informal meals with "sinners" had something to do with it (Matt. 9:11), but this was not going to low-rate the Pharisee or hurt his profession. What would hurt it would be people getting the idea that the ceremonies weren't important, because as all ritualistic-sacramentarians since, the Pharisees were exact and particular in the ["gnat-straining" part of religion]{.class_4257} (Matt. 23:23, 15:2, 9).

12:25 And Jesus knew their thoughts, and said unto them, Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation; and every city or house divided against itself shall not stand:

26 And if Satan cast out Satan, he is divided against himself; how shall then his kingdom stand?

27 And if I by Beelzebub cast out devils, by whom do your children cast them out? therefore they shall be your judges.

28 But if I cast out devils by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God is come unto you.

["And Jesus knew their thoughts."]{.class_4258} This insight is not always immediate and sometimes we read ["when"]{.class_4258} Jesus knew or perceived (Mark 8:17, 11:13; Luke 5:22, 9:47, 20:23). It is another demonstration of Christ's humanity as well as His deity. As omniscient Deity, He "knew it all," but confined to a body of flesh and bones (Phil. 2), He often was limited in energy, thought, and perception by His humanity---see John 4:6.

The kingdoms, cities, and houses that stand divided---fall. This is a truth apparent to everyone. We either "hang together" or we "hang" separately. Berlin was a divided city. Katanga (the Democratic Republic of the Congo, formerly Zaire) is a divided kingdom, and the divorce courts in America bear eloquent testimony to the truth of Matthew 12:25--26.

["If Satan cast out Satan."]{.class_4258} Doctrinally, this occurs in Revelation 12:4--8, and with it Satan's dominion ends. ["By whom do your children cast them out?"]{.class_4258} The argument is "ex-concess" in that it notes the contradiction in the Pharisees' thinking and brings it out; the argument is also "aposteriori," in that it shows that what you see proves what preceded it. Reduced to colloquial Americana the thought is: ["Your sons profess to have the power to cast out devils, and everytime they do it, you go around bragging about how that proves that God is 'with you,' and you have the 'power of God.' All right, if it works for them, it works for Me. Why don't you brag about Me? I'm doing what your sons claim they do!"]{.class_4257}

["Therefore they shall be your judges."]{.class_4258} A similar []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_203}construction is found in verses 39--42, where some judges arise from unexpected places to judge not only the generation which rejected Christ's credentials (as the Son of David), but also the Pharisees. The thought is: ["When your sons return after a 'casting out' and talk with you about it and you tell them what I have been doing, they will decide for themselves whether it is God or Satan doing the casting out, and they'll 'eye you' close if you imply that it is Satan that is doing it; that will reflect on them."]{.class_4257}

["The kingdom of God is come unto you."]{.class_4258} This marks the second place that the expression has popped up in Matthew's gospel. If you will study the remarks on Matthew 6:33, you will find that the ["kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} is a separate concept from the kingdom of Heaven, and that it is only [hinted ]{.class_4257}at in the Sermon on the Mount, which deals mainly with the gospel of the Kingdom of Heaven (see 4:17, 23). But since Mark mentions the gospel of the Kingdom of God simultaneously (Mark 1:14), we must ever bear in mind that Christ is a [double-King]{.class_4257}. As a human-being from Mary, according to the flesh, He is the heir to the literal, physical, visible throne of David, which will make the earth the capital of a literal, physical kingdom spreading eternally out through the literal, physical, visible universe (see Isa. 9, 66; Rev. 21--22). As a spiritual-being, born of God, bearing the image of God, He is King over all created intelligences, angels, principalities, powers, and spiritual children under God. [He is King of both kingdoms ]{.class_4257}(see Heb. 7:2).

Showing up where it does (Matt. 12:28), one finds again that ominous undertone which should warn the contemporary Jew of Christ's day that God is going to require something more than Sabbath-keeping for entrance into "the Kingdom." There is going to be the personal matter of the heart being right with God, plus a sincere desire to turn from sin in every form (Matt. 21:21--32), before the kingdom of heaven shows up. Modernists and postmillennialists ball it up both ways; they have the Kingdom of Heaven conditional on the hearts getting right in [this age]{.class_4257}, when God is dealing with the Gentile, [not the Jew]{.class_4257}. Then they [throw out the real kingdom of heaven that is coming, ]{.class_4257}which was offered to the Jew, [not the Gentile]{.class_4257}, in the gospels! A typical piece of National Council bungling.

12:29 Or else how can one enter into a strong man's house, and spoil his goods, except he first bind the strong man? and then he will spoil his house.

30 He that is not with me is against me; and he that gathereth not with me scattereth abroad.

This parable is so deep and complicated in its doctrinal meaning that no one even answers it, nor is it probable that any of the audience could have answered it. As a simple illustration it is self-apparent, but when we note the elements of speech by comparison, it becomes prophetic and doctrinal. By comparison, the ["strong man"]{.class_4258} is Satan. ["His goods"]{.class_4258} must be people he controls (see 12:22). ["His house"]{.class_4258} can refer to Israel as a nation (see 12:42--45), or it can be the body or a man as the temple of the "unclean spirit" (Luke 11:24).

Reading from this standpoint, the thought is that Satan must be [stopped ]{.class_4257}before either a body or a house or a nation or a kingdom can be liberated. Christ is going to do the "stopping" (John 12:31, 16:11). The final [spoiling ]{.class_4257}of the house will not take place until Revelation 11:15, and then the spoiling is cataclysmic, not gradual. \[Once again, we can count on our bungling brethren to botch up the application. [They are trying to bring in a kingdom with the "strong man" still loose and in control, and the man who is going to do the "spoiling" is absent, with no intention of returning until the strong man is bound. ]{.class_4257}Notice also, that the actual "binding" does not occur until the Lord has returned (Rev. 20:1--3).\]

["He that is not with me\..."]{.class_4258} is self-explanatory. It should be read in conjunction with Mark 9:40. Directly, the remark is aimed at the Pharisees, who certainly are not [with Him ]{.class_4257}and are certainly "scattering abroad." (See Matt. 9:36 as historical witness of the Pharisees' lack of ability to lead the flock anywhere; also 15:14.)

12:31 Wherefore I say unto you, All manner of sin and blasphemy shall be forgiven unto men: but the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost shall not be forgiven unto men.

32 And whosoever speaketh a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but whosoever speaketh against the Holy Ghost, it shall not be forgiven him, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_205}neither in this world, neither in the world to come.

The passage stands as an enduring monument to man's inability to read English correctly, where it deals with [his sinfulness]{.class_4257}. A number of interpretations present themselves, none of which have anything to do with the passage (at least if we can go by the grammatical rules of sixth-grade English).

First, the papist comes up with the fanciful idea that the passage proves that [some sins can be forgiven after death, ]{.class_4257}since ["the world to come"]{.class_4258} must have some reference to some future state, [beyond the grave]{.class_4257}, opposite to ["this world."]{.class_4258} This slap-stick theory is part of the numerous "Ex-Cathedra" mutterings of the "infallible" pope, who assents that such a dogma is [true]{.class_4257}. We may dismiss it immediately as unworthy of anyone professing Bible Christianity. The ["world to come"]{.class_4258} is described throughout both Testaments as the coming Millennial reign of Christ on the earth (see Matt. 13:39, 49, 24:13; Mark 10:30). The ["world to come"]{.class_4258} has literal cities (Luke 19), with literal rulers (Matt. 19), and literal transformations in nature (Isa. 11; Rom. 8). To equate it then with ["life in the grave, after death,"]{.class_4257} is merely to demonstrate the [private interpretation ]{.class_4257}for which the Roman Church is famous: i.e., interpreting for her own benefit, without any regard to what God has to say about it anywhere.

Secondly, the sin is defined as [speaking]{.class_4257}; this knocks out the hit-or-miss expositions which equate it with [suicide ]{.class_4257}or [murder ]{.class_4257}or [divorce]{.class_4257}. Furthermore, the interpretation that it is "ascribing to Satan the works of Jesus Christ," or the "Holy Spirit," is also erroneous. Many a man since this time, through lack of ability to "discern the spirits," has thought a work to be of the Lord when it was of the Devil or to be of the Devil when it was of the Lord; this is a common failing among all born-again members of the Body of Christ, at least on occasions. Such failures never consigned anyone (in the New Testament) to Hell.

The evangelistic equation that this sin represents the final rejection of the Holy Spirit in His convicting work is again a deviation from truth. For many reject this work [without speaking anything]{.class_4257}, and when they do speak [they do not speak against the Holy Spirit, ]{.class_4257}and when they do, some have been saved later! We must []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_206}go to someplace besides expositors if we are ever to arrive at the meaning of the text. The best place to go is Mark 3:30, where the Holy Spirit Himself defines what the unpardonable sin is.

Thirdly, in Mark 3:30, it is [saying ]{.class_4257}that Jesus Christ [has ]{.class_4257}an unclean spirit. As to how this can be applied to the Church Age, "this side" of the resurrection, is a problem. No one would be tempted to say it; if they have, it has been such a rare occurrence that it is not mentioned [in any church history, any gospel tract, or any set of sermons published by anyone, in the last 400 years. ]{.class_4257}Job 7:16--19 is abundant proof that telling God to "get away" and leave you alone and quit dealing with you is not the "unpardonable sin." As it stands, "the unpardonable sin" occurs at the crisis of the kingdom-question when Christ was on earth showing His credentials. It is recorded that the sin is committed (or was nearly committed---see Mark 3:29) by the Pharisees; certainly a number of them rejected Christ fully and finally here and never repented, even after His resurrection (see Acts 4--7).

12:33 Either make the tree good, and his fruit good; or else make the tree corrupt, and his fruit corrupt: for the tree is known by his fruit.

34 O generation of vipers, how can ye, being evil, speak good things? for out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh.

35 A good man out of the good treasure of the heart bringeth forth good things: and an evil man out of the evil treasure bringeth forth evil things.

The verses refer immediately back to the Sermon on the Mount (7:16--19). They are a fair warning to the reader that he is in the middle of "an-offer-of-the-kingdom" situation, and that it is about to be turned down. ["O generation of vipers"]{.class_4258} has direct reference to the Pharisees of verse 24, for Christ calls them this again in 23:33. When we study this scalding denunciation we should consider its relation to the twentieth century thoughtfully. If the modernist is true in his little girl-scout, love everybody-help everybody-tolerate everybody act, [whom ]{.class_4257}is he following? [Certainly not Jesus Christ]{.class_4257}. Jesus Christ never hesitates to blast to shreds any religious leader []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_207}who rejects the authority of Scripture, or who calls himself "rabbi" or "father" (Matt. 23:8--9). Something has gone wrong in America with our values and our ability to think, when we profess to follow a "lowly Galilean" and adopt a pacifist "turn-the-other-cheek-policy," when the only time Jesus "turns" the cheek [is to fulfill Scripture. ]{.class_4257}At all other times, He lashes back with a verbal barrage that would take the bark off an oak tree: ["O generation of vipers."]{.class_4258} Nice language, isn't it? Especially when one considers that it is aimed at [the religious leaders of a council ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 12:14)! The ["evil things," ]{.class_4258}out of the mouth, must be rebukes and exposés of false prophets, which is what our Liberal stable-mates blame the "Fundamentalists" for. If open criticism and violent rebuke is an evil thing, our Lord Himself is guilty! (Compare vss. 34--35 and think about it a while. If you do not recognize Jesus Christ as God in the flesh yet, sit yourself down and consider this passage. As a religious leader, Christ was violating His own principles in verses 34--35, [unless He was God]{.class_4257}.)

The ["good treasure\...evil treasure"]{.class_4258} may be likened to :

1\. The word of God in the heart of the "good man" (see 13:52, Lev. 26:10).

2\. The [hardness ]{.class_4257}and [stubbornness ]{.class_4257}of the bad man to bow to the authority of this word (see Rom. 2:5).

12:36 But I say unto you, That every idle word that men shall speak, they shall give account thereof in the day of judgment.

37 For by thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned.

["Every idle word"]{.class_4258} is evidently very important, for every idle word shows up at the Last Judgment (see comments on 11:20--22). Furthermore, there is evidence that the Bible shows up at the Last Judgment to condemn its critics (John 5:44--47, 12:48; Luke 21:33; Psa. 119:89). ["By thy words\..."]{.class_4258} must be true, for before this we have the doctrinal teaching that ["out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh"]{.class_4258} (vs. 34). This is psychologically sound. What is inside will come out. It will [force ]{.class_4257}its way out (Jer. 20: 9; Psa. 109:17--20).

It is interesting to note that of the six questions that are asked by Job, when he is under trial, [four of them deal with what is spoken]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_208}. In Job 26:2--4, we find all six questions, and only the first two can be said to be acts, exclusively. The last four are things which [the person has said]{.class_4257}. The last question in verse 4 is also a verbal examination, for the "spirit" can be the "spirit of man" coming from the man (1 Cor. 2) or the "spirit of the Lord" (1 Cor. 2) or the "spirit of Satan" (Eph. 2:1--4) coming from the man's mouth when he speaks. For a perfect illustration of this see 1 Kings 22:15--28.

12:38 Then certain of the scribes and of the Pharisees answered, saying, Master, we would see a sign from thee.

39 But he answered and said unto them, An evil and adulterous generation seeketh after a sign; and there shall no sign be given to it, but the sign of the prophet Jonas:

40 For as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale's belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.

41 The men of Nineveh shall rise in judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: because they repented at the preaching of Jonas; and, behold, a greater than Jonas is here.

42 The queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it: for she came from the uttermost parts of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and, behold, a greater than Solomon is here.

["We would see a sign\...."]{.class_4258} The question is asked with a pretence of humility. Considering Israel's background (1 Cor. 1:22), they have a right to ask for a sign (Exod. 4:9--30). There are several difficulties, however, the main one being that they had already been presented with [a thousand signs]{.class_4257} and had rejected [all of them]{.class_4257} (see Matt. 8:4, 14, 16, 34, 9:6, 22, 26, 30, 10:8, 11:5--6, 23, 12:13). The question, therefore, is the question of a bigoted hypocrite, with his mind already made up. It is like the saying: "I've got my mind made up; don't confuse me with a lot of facts." That isn't all. Verse 38 shows that the response to verses 35--37 [was immediate]{.class_4257}. The question, therefore, is the reaction of a guilty conscience as it is caught attempting to change the subject of verses 35--37.

Christ's answer, strangely enough, omits the miracles of healing which He has just performed. In view of this, His answer must be analyzed carefully. Notice that it is said to be a sign to a [whole generation ]{.class_4257}and not merely to the people who may or may not have witnessed His earthly miracles. ["The sign"]{.class_4258} He gives merits our deepest considerations, for it turns out to be the [sign most disbelieved by the National Council of "Christian" Churches ]{.class_4257}in the twentieth century. It is the "Sign of Jonas" being ["in the whale's belly."]{.class_4258} How ludicrous it is to see these modern wolves in sheep's clothing in our midst, constantly bleating about "following the master" when they won't even take Him to be an honest man who meant what He said! That this [sign ]{.class_4257}would ["be spoken against,"]{.class_4258} is a plain prophecy of Scripture (see Luke 2:34), and the context of the sign is the piercing, two-edged sword of the word of God (see Heb. 4:12--13).

["Jonas,"]{.class_4258} in verse 40, brings on the controversy. Was it a whale? Was it not a ["great fish"]{.class_4258}? How did he stay alive? Is it not a "myth to teach a truth"? Are the three days and nights literal? or "Jewish time"? Isn't it tragic that the only sign our Lord picked was one which no one would believe, [including a thousand fundamentalists?!]{.class_4257}

Hardt, Less, Palmer, and Krahmer say: ["It is an allegory."]{.class_4257}

Eichorn, Muller, and Augusti say: ["It is a legend."]{.class_4257}

Ewald, Knobel, DeWette, Paulus, and Gesenius say: ["It is a fable."]{.class_4257}

Bauer, Friedrichs, Gorgiger, and Weigle say: ["It is a myth."]{.class_4257}

Koster and Hitzig say: ["It is fiction."]{.class_4257}

The [Interpreter's Bible ]{.class_4257}says: ["It is a magnificent document, full of spiritual insight and beauty...it is a dream!"]{.class_4257}

Jesus said it was the only sign given to a generation of Christ-rejectors.

Isn't it a pity that modernistic and orthodox scholarship both look at it the same way that the ["generation of vipers"]{.class_4258} looked at it?

Now, let us approach the word of God reverently and sensibly and comment on what we can accept as absolute, infallible truth, without asking anyone's opinion.

1\. [It does not say Jonah stayed alive.]{.class_4257} The ["sign"]{.class_4258} turns out to be the sign of the resurrection! [Jonah was not alive three days and nights. He was dead ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_210}(see Jon 2:2, 6, with Acts 2:27--34). The unbelief, then, is the unbelief in a literal, bodily resurrection. The scholars at Athens fall in this category (Acts 17:18--32).

2\. There are whales that have swallowed men and disgorged them long after three days, and there are whales big enough to keep a man alive [three days]{.class_4257}. (For this information, pay no attention to the amateurish writers of the [Interpreter's Bible]{.class_4257}; go to Charles Bell Emerson, the world's foremost authority on whales.)

3\. Since Christ turns out to be in the earth an exact even three days and an exact even three nights, there is no need to compromise with the "good Friday" Catholic fiction, by reducing the days to one and one-half. A. T. Robertson (Friday) is most certainly in error with the pope. Allen (Thursday) has done a bad job of trying to reconcile the passage by meeting the pope half-way. The [Scriptural ]{.class_4257}days listed from Palm-Sunday to Christ's burial are [Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday---6 p. m.]{.class_4257}

1\. He came in Sunday morning (Mark 11:11).

2\. He slept at Bethany Sunday night (Mark 11:12).

3\. He cursed the fig tree Monday morning and saw it withered Tuesday (Mark 11:20).

4\. Tuesday evening at 5 was [two days before the Passover ]{.class_4257}(Mark 14:1).

5\. Passover was eaten after 6 Thursday (Matt. 26:1). Disciples went out to find a room on Tuesday (Luke 22:7).

6\. Tuesday evening at the end of the third day of the week the passover is killed---[not eaten ]{.class_4257}(Matt 26:1).

Let's face up to it. The Bible lists [no extra day ]{.class_4257}between Sunday and Wednesday morning at 9 o'clock, when He is crucified. Allen, in his defense, makes up one day which is found nowhere and for which he produces no Scripture, and Dr. A. T. Robertson (with all due respect for his ability in Greek syntax) [invents two days ]{.class_4257}found nowhere in Scripture. The plain Scripture is that Christ is crucified Wednesday and is in the tomb at 6 p.m., making exactly three nights of twelve hours each (Wed., Thurs., Fri.) and three days of twelve hours each (Thurs., Fri., and Sat.) in the tomb. He comes out the first day of the week---Saturday night at 6 p.m. (see Gen. 1:5, 8, 13, etc.).

The angel descends at midnight Saturday night and the women come at 5:30 a.m., Sunday morning. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_211}[This checks with all passages.]{.class_4257}

Matthew 12:40, then (as most Scriptures), means exactly what it says, the way it says it, and there is certainly no flaw in it. You can rest assured it is exactly right and intended to be precisely as you find it, without error or admixture of error. We may always say confidently and dogmatically, "The Scriptures are always right---and your opinion of them is always wrong, where it runs afoul of them." Paul put it this way: ["Let God be true, but every man a liar."]{.class_4258} That includes the popes and the bishops, as well as the Greek professors and Conservative scholars.

["The men of Nineveh\...."]{.class_4258} The statement seems to imply that at the last judgment (and "the judgment" is always used in this sense---see Matt. 11:22, 24), there will be heathen people, who lived by conscience only, condemning those who had the greater light of the Law and the Prophets. Romans 2:26--27 bears this out abundantly. ["A greater than Jonas"]{.class_4258} is true, for Jonas did not have compassion on his audience, whereas Christ did; Jonas could not forgive sin, whereas Christ did; Jonas was a bad example to his converts, whereas Christ was a perfect example; and Jonas suffered little, while Christ suffered the full judgment of God against sin (2 Cor. 5:19--21).

["The queen of the south"]{.class_4258} is a reference to the Queen of Sheba and would indicate that Sheba is possibly identified with the southern-most part of the Arabian peninsula. Notice: ["uttermost parts of the earth"]{.class_4258} (the earth referring to "land surface before arriving at the water's edge"). It is obvious that Jesus is a greater than Solomon, but what is not so obvious is the reason why the dark-skinned heathen queen will have [a right to JUDGE]{.class_4257}. Upon examination, it is noticed that [she was uninvited]{.class_4257}, whereas Israel had an invitation (Matt. 22:3; 11:28--30); [she came on a mere report]{.class_4257}, where Israel had evidence; [she came from afar]{.class_4257}, while the miracles to Israel were performed in their "hometowns"; [she spent thousands to make the trip]{.class_4257}, while these spent nothing; and she came to a mere [mortal man]{.class_4257}, while these had an opportunity to come to a Man who never had to ask anyone to forgive Him of anything and never confessed a sin a day in His life. The comparisons remind one of the news­paper clipping from 1959--60, where a man in India walked 114 miles to hear Billy Graham and gave him an invitation to come []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_212}preach at his village.

12:43 When the unclean spirit is gone out of a man, he walketh through dry places, seeking rest, and findeth none.

44 Then he saith, I will return into my house from whence I came out; and when he is come, he findeth it empty, swept, and garnished.

45 Then goeth he, and taketh with himself seven other spirits more wicked than himself, and they enter in and dwell there: and the last state of that man is worse than the first. Even so shall it be also unto this wicked generation.

The unidentified man in the parable is cited as a perfect case for Israel. However, the case is not a borrowing from contemporary fable, for in Luke 11:24 the case is applied directly to [any man]{.class_4257}, as an individual. Again, one cannot help wondering how such passages and stories set with the "intelligentsia" who betake themselves to psychiatry for a [natural explanation ]{.class_4257}of erratic, abnormal, or immoral behaviour. There is evidently something here of which Freud, Menninger, Jung, and Pavlov are woefully ignorant. Fosdick and other blatant Liberals avoid the problem by simply stating that Jesus had no access to the "inductive scientific method," or that He resorted to the "popular language" of His time in analyzing such phenomena. But these childish methods adopted to side-step the deity of Christ are hardly in keeping with the plain record and Christ's integrity. Christ refers to demons constantly, and recognizes and treats them as such (Matt 17:18; Mark 5:1--13).

Where are some modern textbooks debunking this? Where is the proof? Has someone [disproved the existence of demons?]{.class_4257} Where is he? Where is the proof? It is certainly not found in the Harvard Five-foot Shelf of Classics, and no nuclear physicist in Europe or America has ever conclusively proved that there are not spiritual beings at work in the universe. What is it that the unregenerate population of "educated America" is afraid to face? Why is it always schizophrenia, phobophobia, megalomania, paranoia, schizoid, and homicidal mania and never diagnosed ["demon-possession"]{.class_4257}? Is someone worried about a [religious ]{.class_4257}controversy? [Could it be that "unclean" and "seducing spirits" have a religion]{.class_4257} (1 Tim. 4:1--5)? Could it be []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_213}that demons are smart enough to prevent detection by convincing scientists that only "Bible religion" can drive people "cuckoo"? Let us study our Lord's statement and learn the correct facts of demonology, overlooked by "modern science."

1\. Demons have an affinity for [wet ]{.class_4257}places. This is brought out in the text (Matt. 12:43) and is confirmed by three facts:

\(a\) Demons seek a body, and the human body is 80 percent water (Mark 5:12).

\(b\) The bodies thus inhabited tend to go to water (Matt. 17:15; Mark 5:13).

\(c\) The final destination of demons is a [lake ]{.class_4257}of fire (Rev. 20). It may be added also that the fallen angels of pre-deluge days are drowned in a flood of water (2 Pet. 2).

2\. A further insight is gathered in that the demon-possessed boy of Matthew 17 heads for fire. The recent case of the oriental potentate who soaked himself in gasoline and burnt himself, leaving a suicide note, is a case in point. [Warm, wet places ]{.class_4257}are the moving point for demons as they are described in the Bible. This finds tremendous application. Shanghai, Honolulu, Chicago, New Orleans, Cairo, Suez, Marseilles, Paris, London, New York, Miami, Los Angeles, St. Louis, Cincinnati, and Natchez are all brick and stone testimony to the fact that liquor, immorality, murder, and false doctrine [follow the waterfront]{.class_4257}. It may be argued: "civilizations tend to waterfronts for transportation and communication." To which it may be answered, ["the whole world lieth in wickedness"]{.class_4258} (1 John 5:19), and the only sinless man who ever lived makes it [an express point not to pray for the world, as such]{.class_4257} (John 17:9) .

3\. The demon or unclean spirit refers to a man's body as "his house" (Eph. 2:1--3), indicating that as the Christian's body is the Temple of God, the unsaved man's body is the house of the "Old Man," the unclean spirit. ["Empty, swept, and garnished,"]{.class_4258} where it occurs in the text, indicates that the body is [empty ]{.class_4257}(the Holy Spirit is not in it!). It is [swept ]{.class_4257}(the man has "cleaned up his life, morally") and [garnished ]{.class_4257}(he has religious junk hanging all over it to look good and make him feel better), but alas! he is lost, none-the-less. For a classic illustration see the case in 2 Peter 2:19--22, where the man has cleaned up and has done it with knowledge about Christ [and commandments]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_214}! (Saul and Balaam are outstanding examples in the Old Testament.)

Further information on demonology may be found in the compendium of notes on Matthew 8:28 in the commentary. The Bible gives accurate detailed information on their habits, natures, manners, desires, locations, methods, expressions, and fears.

["Even so shall it be\...."]{.class_4258} In the application, Jesus applies the case of a demon-possessed man to the nation of Israel; in particular, the generation that rejected Him as their Lord and Saviour. Peter brings this thought out in Acts 2:40, and the ["this generation"]{.class_4258} is a constant reference in Matthew's gospel (see 11:16, 23:36, 24:34).

12:46 While he yet talked to the people, behold, his mother and his brethren stood without, desiring to speak with him.

47 Then one said unto him, Behold, thy mother and thy brethren stand without, desiring to speak with thee.

48 But he answered and said unto him that told him, Who is my mother? and who are my brethren?

49 And he stretched forth his hand toward his disciples, and said, Behold my mother and my brethren!

50 For whosoever shall do the will of my Father which is in heaven, the same is my brother, and sister, and mother.

"His mother and his brethren" are Christ's [literal, physical brothers and sisters]{.class_4257}. The ancient superstition about the "perpetual virginity" of Mary, like all other superstitions, is exploded in the light of this Scripture. See the references and notes under Matthew 1:25, where there is abundant evidence from both Testaments, in all manuscripts, that Mary had children by Joseph, after Jesus was born. The context we are studying also amply bears it out, for when Christ answers, He answers: ["the same is my brother, and sister."]{.class_4258} He is careful [not ]{.class_4257}to say "cousin," so the papists will get the doctrine right. (But they never could get it right anyway!)
