The word "cousin" occurs twice in Luke's Gospel (1:36, 58) and never refers to ["brothers and sisters."]{.class_4257} The word ["brethren"]{.class_4258} could include either, but where the words ["brother, and sister"]{.class_4258} occur, do you have any idea what they mean? (You'd never guess!) They mean: []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1118.xhtml_page_215}["brother, and sister."]{.class_4258} Remarkable, isn't it?

["Who is my mother?"]{.class_4258} This apparent rudeness to Mary is found in every passage where Jesus deals with her. Notice the: ["Woman, what have I to do with thee?"]{.class_4258} in John 2, and the ["Woman, behold thy son!"]{.class_4258} in John 19. Jesus is explicit in His choice of words and use of terms, never calling Mary His ["mother."]{.class_4258} He never reverences her more than any other woman, and He is careful to avoid leaving the impression with the apostles that she should even be [mentioned in prayer]{.class_4257}. He always calls her by the indefinite, feminine, collective noun, ["woman."]{.class_4257} Consequently, in the book of Acts, we find no one praying to her, no one "honoring" her, no one "reverencing" her, and certainly no one at any time mentioning her to anyone in connection with the Church, Salvation, the Trinity, God, Christ, the New Birth, Right-Living, Prophecy, Good-Works, or Meditation. Mary, as Ashtaroth, Venus, Isis, Ishtar, Diana, and Semiramis, gets her position as "queen of heaven" only in the imaginations of heathen people who have a sex-god set-up to honor fertility and reproduction. The modern papal version of Mary is found nowhere in the book of Acts or the Pauline epistles written to the one, true church. Paul, Peter, James, and John are very plain in purposely omitting her from any discussion of Christian doctrine, Christian living, Christian prayer, or Christian truths.

["Behold my mother and my brethren!"]{.class_4258} Here, where the Lord finally calls someone ["His mother,"]{.class_4257} He purposely points at someone [other than Mary]{.class_4257}! How confusing to Mariolatry! What is worse is that in the modernistic conception of the "lowly Galilean," we run into a confusion of ethics at this verse. How is Christ any "example" to follow (in the sense of our text here and the warning of Luke 9:60)? No Modernist or Liberal, living or dead, ever followed this example, and if they caught anyone [who did]{.class_4257}, they would not call him a "lowly Galilean," they would call him an "uncouth" boor. (The standard way to avoid this confusion is to recognize Jesus as a sort of super-mystic, a kind of nebulous "Kahlil Gibran," whose veiled remarks never refer to Christian doctrine as found in the New Testament, but rather refer to the great pagan concept of "the Fatherhood of God and the Brotherhood of Man.")

This interpretation, however, runs into tremendous difficulty, since the passage must be lifted clear out of the setting. Notice that verse 50 says, ["Whosoever shall do the will of MY Father...."]{.class_4258} Jesus never says: "OUR" Father! (Throughout all four gospels He maintains His integrity, calling, source, and mission as [separate ]{.class_4257}from anyone else who claims God as a Father.) The "Our Father" is a misnomer in both churches, Protestant and Catholic, for the Catholic uses it for merit, whereas it is an Old Testament prayer given to Jews under the Law, before the crucifixion. The Protestant does worse, in that he not only superimposes it over the doctrines of divinity for a New Testament Christian, but he calls it the "Lord's Prayer," to imply that Jesus spoke of "Our Father," [as though including Himself.]{.class_4257} This reduction of Christ to a disciple \[and after all, it is a prayer He taught [His disciples ]{.class_4257}to pray (Matt. 6:6--9)\] goes further than the Catholic perversion, in that it strips Christ of His Deity (not "divinity"; you can have "divinity fudge" and spend a "divine" evening with someone. The word is "deity").

Further difficulties are encountered by the rendering of "OUR" Father as inclusive of Christ (who always calls Him: ["My Father"]{.class_4258} or ["your Father"]{.class_4258} or ["thy Father"]{.class_4258} or ["Holy Father"]{.class_4258}) when one considers what the will of God is that He is asking His "brethren" to do. Notice that this "will" is defined in John 6:29, 40, and it includes accepting Christ as the Saviour sent from God, who is able to produce a [physical resurrection]{.class_4257}. The passage also identifies Christ as [the only Author and Giver of life]{.class_4257}. The Catholic again backtracks to John 6:51--56, and by ignoring the context (vs. 57--63) turns the life-giving Saviour into a jug of fermented liquor and a piece of dry bread! This is hardly "the will of the Father"!

["Who are my brethren"]{.class_4258} is finally identified [exactly]{.class_4257}, as a remnant of Hebrew believers who believe on Jesus Christ as "Saviour." Study these definitive passages carefully for the exact doctrinal import of the statement (Heb. 2:11; Matt. 25:40; Heb. 2:13; John 20:17). After all, the final authority on who Christ's "brethren" [are ]{.class_4257}would be Jesus Christ Himself. This seems a little too obvious, but it is amazing how many people there are yet in the world who trust someone else to "dish it out for them," because they are too lazy or too scared to "search the Scriptures."

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml}

# Chapter 13 {.heading_84-0}

13:1 The same day went Jesus out of the house, and sat by the sea side.

2 And great multitudes were gathered together unto him, so that he went into a ship, and sat; and the whole multitude stood on the shore.

3 And he spake many things unto them in parables, saying, Behold, a sower went forth to sow;

To understand why the parables of the mysteries follow, one should carefully review chapter 12 and notice the elements. The Messiah has come (vs. 1), He has identified Himself as the Son of David (vs. 3), He has proved it with signs and wonders (vs. 13), and He has been rejected and called a devil (vs. 24); [and in the middle of the chapter is inserted a warning that God is going to deal with Gentiles ]{.class_4257}(vs. 21).

Nearing the end of the chapter, the unpardonable sin is committed (vs. 31). Christ pronounces a [Gentile judgment on Israel]{.class_4257} (vs. 40--42) and closes by likening the Jewish rejection to a demon-possessed man (vs. 45), whereupon Christ renounces His own family connection with Israel (vss. 49--50) and goes ["out of the house" ]{.class_4258}(13:1). (Compare this with Matt. 23:28 to get the dispensation and eschatological connection.)

The parables of "Christendom" (not "Christianity") follow the rejection of Israel. (The student should be careful of such words as "Christianity," "religion," "Pro­testantism," "Holy Roman Empire," and other such wolf‑words in sheep-letters. The Liberal never uses Bible terms right, but always uses them to enforce something that isn't right.)

["A sower went forth to sow." ]{.class_4258}This is called the ["parable of the sower"]{.class_4258} in the 18th verse. In Luke 8:11, we learn that the seed sown is the word of God and that the sower is Christ Himself, for Luke 8:5 calls the seed ["his"]{.class_4258} seed. The specific "word" that is sown is the word concerning ["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} (Matt 13:19), and Christ makes this sufficiently clear in 13:11. [It is NOT]{.class_4257} ["THE CHURCH."]{.class_4257} (Settle that once and for all and quit fishing for []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_218}red‑snappers in a creek‑bed.)

13:4 And when he sowed, some seeds fell by the way side, and the fowls came and devoured them up:

5 Some fell upon stony places, where they had not much earth: and forthwith they sprung up, because they had no deepness of earth:

6 And when the sun was up, they were scorched; and because they had no root, they withered away.

7 And some fell among thorns; and the thorns sprung up, and choked them:

8 But other fell into good ground, and brought forth fruit, some an hundredfold, some sixtyfold, some thirtyfold.

9 Who hath ears to hear, let him hear.

The first of the kingdom parables, which number twelve in all (the number of Israel), is rich in illustrative material for sermons. Four types of Christian "profession" are present:

1\. The careless (vs. 4).

2\. The temporary (vss. 5--6).

3\. The worldly (vs. 7).

4\. The sincere (vs. 8).

That the parable can be applied to the Church Age in [some ]{.class_4257}respect is obvious in that this first parable is called a ["mystery of the kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} (Mark 4:11), as well as the ["kingdom of heaven."]{.class_4258} (Luke 8:10 says likewise.) In Luke 8 the sower and the seed is a "kingdom‑of‑God mystery" (and also in Mark). Further, in Mark 4, the mustard‑seed parable is said to deal with the ["kingdom of God."]{.class_4258} But the parable of the ["full corn in the ear"]{.class_4258} (Mark 4) does not occur in Matthew as a [kingdom of heaven parable, ]{.class_4257}and the remaining parables in Matthew 13 have no application to the kingdom of God, with the exception of the leaven (see Luke 13:21). This leaves us something by which to go. The kingdom of heaven and the kingdom of God are identical in [three parables: ]{.class_4257}the sower, the mustard seed, and the leaven. In the remaining nine parables (Matt. 13:36, 44--45, 20:1, 22:2, 25:1, 14, 13:47, 18:23), there are no similarities between the two. [On the basis of three similarities, ]{.class_4257}our []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_219}clumsy amillennial and postmillennial friends have synthesized the church, the kingdom of God, and the kingdom of heaven into [one organic unit, ]{.class_4257}and, thereby, have missed the proper interpretation of three-fourths of the New Testament parables and 75 percent of the Old Testament prophecies.

The outstanding thing one can learn from this first parable is that, contrary to scholarly tradition, [every parable has an exact meaning for everything in it. ]{.class_4257}Since most Bible scholars from 1600--1960 were unable to handle the parable of the prodigal son and the parable of the lost coin, they have adopted the curious and apologetic dictum that ["a parable cannot be used to teach doctrine, but only presents a central truth."]{.class_4257} Far from being an honest statement, this is merely a frank admission that [a man with two legs of different length has a hard time walking ]{.class_4257}(see Prov 26:7). Jesus deliberately assigns a DETAILED mean­ing [to every detail ]{.class_4257}He named of the only two parables He ever explained (Matt. 13:18--23, 37--42). Let us "follow the Master."

The sower is Christ. The seed is the word of God. The fowls of verse 4 are demons sent by Satan (see interpreta­tion in vs. 19 and Rev. 18:2). The ground represents the heart of a man by the wayside. He is LOST (Luke 8:12). The "stony place" hearers, where there is no ["earth,"]{.class_4258} are those that "joyfully receive the word" (Matt. 13:20) and believe---without a ["root"]{.class_4258} taking place in the heart (Rev. 22:16). They pass off as Christians until either persecu­tion, affliction, or temptation put them to the test (Luke 8:13; Mark 4:17; Matt 13:21)---the persecution being connected with "standing for THE WORD"---notice: ["because of the word"]{.class_4258} in Matthew 13:20--21.

The third "hearer," Matthew 13:7, is a man who hears and evidently [receives, ]{.class_4257}for he had a [possibility ]{.class_4257}of bearing fruit (John 15:1--7). Further, ["he becometh unfruitful,"]{.class_4258} indicating that at one time [he did bear fruit. ]{.class_4257}Further, the word is [in ]{.class_4257}him (Mark 4:19). Furthermore, he has [some ]{.class_4257}fruit that is not perfected (Luke 8:14). Three things keep this man from being a fruitful Christian, and they are listed by Luke as: ["cares\...riches, and pleasures."]{.class_4258} Mark lists them as:[ "cares," "deceitfulness of riches,"]{.class_4258} and ["other things."]{.class_4258} We recognize immediately the famous temptation‑trinity, so graphically described in the temptation of Eve and the temptation []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_220}of Christ (see Matt. 4:7).

The fourth hearer is described as "ground that has been well‑prepared for planting" (Matt. 13:23; Luke 8:15---["an honest and good heart"]{.class_4258}). He [understands ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 13:23) and "bears fruit" (Matt. 13:23), with both Matthew and Mark showing varieties of "fruitfulness" among the saved---30, 60, 100. This hearer [keeps ]{.class_4257}the word (Luke 8:15; John 15:7--10), and brings ["forth the fruit with patience"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}(Luke 8:15).

The first kingdom parable, then, is unique in that it represents the mediatorial, Davidic, literal kingdom and the spiritual, new‑birth kingdom. It is also unique in that Satan's work is described [outside ]{.class_4257}the believer; from Matthew 13:24 on, [He is inside. ]{.class_4257}In the first parable, we study Christ's work, and in the second parable, beginning at verse 24, we study the DEVIL'S work.

The [first hearer is ]{.class_4257}well represented by HEROD and PILATE.

The [second hearer is ]{.class_4257}personified in SIMON the SORCERER.

The [third hearer is ]{.class_4257}manifest in DEMAS.

The [fourth hearer is ]{.class_4257}represented by three classes of fruit‑bearers (and this classification is mentioned, incidentally, in Rev. 12:11 and 17:14): the twelve disciples bring forth 30‑fold; Peter, James, and John bring forth 60‑fold; but JOHN, and only John, is right next to the Lord's heart at all times, with a clear conscience, and goes clear through the trial to the cross itself (see John 13:23--25, 18:15--17, 19:25--27). "Near to the heart of God!"

A parable, then, is an exact, correct, infallible, and perfect teaching of a doctrine given in a veiled and mysterious form, for the purpose of fouling up those who tend to treat the Bible lightly or indifferently.

13:10 And the disciples came, and said unto him, Why speakest thou unto them in parables?

11 He answered and said unto them, Because it is given unto you to know the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, but to them it is not given.

12 For whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and he shall have more abundance: but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath.

13 Therefore speak I to them in parables: because they seeing see not; and hearing they hear not, neither do they understand.

The truth of the last comment (on vs. 9) is now brought home with devastating authority. The teaching is purposely veiled so that a man who can [see and hear will ]{.class_4257}be rendered [blind and deaf, ]{.class_4257}if his heart is not right (see John 9:40--41: Acts 28:25--28). The truth has far‑reaching implications that account for the damnation of every man who insists that "one religion is as good as another." And it re‑emphasizes a weighty thought: "Light rejected becomes lightning." [God is not under obligation to reveal the truth to a man who doesn't want it ]{.class_4257}(John 7:17). This reduces a lot of philosophy and psychology and "churchianity" to a rubbish‑heap of second‑hand "oscars."

["It is given unto you to know." ]{.class_4258}This statement implies that God the Father Himself is selective and selects and gives to certain people what He does not to others (see John 6:44--45). Since the context is the "word of God" (the sown seed), Romans 10:17 fits appropriately enough. The teaching is simple. If a child learns his arithmetic right, he will be given a chance at math. If he learns this right, he will be given a chance at algebra, etc. If a man goes by the truth God gives him and believes what God has said about fundamental basic things (i.e., sin, death, judgment, and salvation), then God will increase his ability to learn greater truths, and he may go deeper into revelation (John 16:21, 1 Cor. 3:1--4, 2:12). The disciples have obeyed "John's Baptism," and they have followed Christ and listened to Him, thus far, without criticism or "hardness of heart." They are going to learn something the Pharisees are not going to learn.

13:14 And in them is fulfilled the prophecy of Esaias, which saith, By hearing ye shall hear, and shall not understand; and seeing ye shall see, and shall not perceive:

15 For this people's heart is waxed gross, and their ears are dull of hearing, and their eyes they have closed; lest at any time they should see with their eyes and hear with their ears, and should understand with their heart, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_222}should be converted, and I should heal them.

16 But blessed are your eyes, for they see: and your ears, for they hear.

17 For verily I say unto you, That many prophets and righteous men have desired to see those things which ye see, and have not seen them; and to hear those things which ye hear, and have not heard them.

This prophecy of Isaiah is referred to so many times in the New Testament that it merits the closest examination. It is cited in John 12 immediately before the Last Supper and the Crucifixion, and it is cited again in Acts 28, following the rejection of the risen Messiah by Jews of the dispersion. These three places are not Scriptural "misfits." In Matthew 13 the unpardonable sin has been committed and the mystery form of the kingdom is revealed which will fill the gap between Acts 28 and the Second Coming. In John 12, the final rejection of Christ's earthly ministry heralds His crucifixion, and the passage in Isaiah 6, from whence the verses are taken, [heralds the great tribulation ]{.class_4257}(see Isa. 6:6--13). In Acts 28, the citation heralds the spread of the gospel to the Gentiles and the calling out of that great body of Believers known as the Church (1 Cor. 12:13).

["Waxed gross"]{.class_4258} implies grown heavy in the sense of thick layers of fat covering the heart so that it cannot function properly, or rendering it incapable of receiving spiritual truths. [Epachunthem ]{.class_4257}(Greek) will come out literally: "rendered unfeeling," "dull of hearing"; i.e., dull or blunt in the sense of being used to the point where they no longer are interested in hearing anything true (cf. 1 Tim. 4:3). The Greek ["bareos" ]{.class_4257}describes a man so "weighted down" with hearing "big things" that he just gives up and yawns and goes to sleep. ["And should be converted." ]{.class_4258}The sentence again is aimed at Israel, as a nation. [Individuals are converted ]{.class_4257}even in the official rejection (John 6:68; Rom 11:14; Acts 21:20).

The disciples, then, in Matthew 13, stand for the [Jewish remnant ]{.class_4257}who will believe on Christ in spite of the attitude taken by Israel's priests, scribes, rulers, and Pharisees. They are the equivalent of Jehovah's 3,000 in Romans 11:4 and the 144,000 who will stand true in Revelation 7:3--10. ["Many prophets and righteous men" ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_223}had certainly desired to learn the truths that are about to be taught, and Simon Peter adds that they searched their own writings diligently to try to understand "the GAP" between the First Coming in humility and the Second Coming in glory (1 Pet. 1:9--11).

13:18 Hear ye therefore the parable of the sower.

19 When any one heareth the word of the kingdom, and understandeth it not, then cometh the wicked one, and catcheth away that which was sown in his heart. This is he which received seed by the way side.

20 But he that received the seed into stony places, the same is he that heareth the word, and anon with joy receiveth it;

21 Yet hath he not root in himself, but dureth for a while: for when tribulation or persecution ariseth because of the word, by and by he is offended.

22 He also that received seed among the thorns is he that heareth the word; and the care of this world, and the deceitfulness of riches, choke the word, and he becometh unfruitful.

23 But he that received seed into the good ground is he that heareth the word, and understandeth it; which also beareth fruit, and bringeth forth, some an hundredfold, some sixty, some thirty.

This passage is commented on under Matthew 13:3--9. It needs little information further on it, except for the explanation of a few words. ["Anon," ]{.class_4258}in verse 20, can mean "right away," and the Greek ([euthus]{.class_4257})[ ]{.class_4257}indicated "immediately." ["Dureth for a while" ]{.class_4258}is the same for "endures for a while." Clarence Larkin likens the first parable to the effect of the word of God on the world from the first church of Revelation 1 (Ephesus) up to the rapture of the church. That is, we can count on [ONE convert out of six ]{.class_4257}really taking the matter to heart, two converts out of four actually being saved, and one of them living a life that would not indicate its profession. And if the parable is further applied it would indicate that half the people that hear the truth forget it or desert it before it effects any change in their lives or nature.

13:24 Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is likened unto a man which sowed good seed in his field:

25 But while men slept, his enemy came and sowed tares among the wheat, and went his way.

26 But when the blade was sprung up, and brought forth fruit, then appeared the tares also.

27 So the servants of the householder came and said unto him, Sir, didst not thou sow good seed in thy field? from whence then hath it tares?

28 He said unto them, An enemy hath done this. The servants said unto him, Wilt thou then that we go and gather them up?

29 But he said, Nay; lest while ye gather up the tares, ye root up also the wheat with them.

30 Let both grow together until the harvest: and in the time of harvest I will say to the reapers, Gather ye together first the tares, and bind them in bundles to burn them: but gather the wheat into my barn.

In the second parable, Satan is found as an enemy [within the ranks ]{.class_4257}of the believers, trying to destroy the work by a counterfeit. Exodus 12--15 shows a fine example of this work in the ["mixed multitude"]{.class_4258} that departed from Egypt, many of them with no change of heart to match the believers with them (Heb. 4:1, 3:9--18).

We have here a very humorous reading in the [RSV]{.class_4257}, which shows either ignorance of Satan's ["devices"]{.class_4258} or deliberate cooperation with Satan to mess up the Greek New Testament. Our erudite "scholars" of the [RSV ]{.class_4257}have translated ["weeds" ]{.class_4257}for ["tares," ]{.class_4258}thus destroying the entire sense, thought, purpose, and object of the parable. Anyone can detect a [weed. ]{.class_4257}And no one [plants ]{.class_4257}weeds. And anyone knows the difference between a weed and an ear of wheat, and you don't [gather ]{.class_4257}weeds (vs. 30); you [dig them up ]{.class_4257}or pull them out. In their zeal to destroy the [Authorized Version]{.class_4257}, the [RSV ]{.class_4257}translators often violate the precepts of good taste, decency, common sense, natural phenomenon, and natural laws. This is only one example among several dozen.

Following the Scriptural rule that a parable is "a detailed teaching of doctrine to teach a number of specific truths," let us accept Jesus Christ's detailed explanation of the parable and believe the teachings which He assigns to the parable.

The ["sower,"]{.class_4258} again, is the ["Son of man" ]{.class_4258}(Jesus---vs. 37). Verses 38--40 are so explicit and detailed in their application to the parable that it would seem a calamity that anyone could misapply the truths or misread the interpretation. Every element in the parable is named and given a meaning. The only element to which no other meaning is given is [the fire! ]{.class_4257}The fire, therefore, is literal [fire. ]{.class_4257}The children of the wicked one are burned in [literal fire]{.class_4257} (vs. 42). This interpretation can be classified by a liberal as "hell fire and damnation preaching" or "selec­tive dualism" or "crass literalism." But these are only typical attempts to ignore "the teachings of the Master" and denounce His authority. The parable plainly teaches that Satan has "imitation children of God," who are not "children of God" at all, and these live in the world side‑by‑side with the real article until the Second Advent, at which time they are [gathered for burning, ]{.class_4257}while the children of God [go up into the top of the loft. ]{.class_4257}A score of notes could be made on the misapplications and misread­ings of the parable among the Catholic, amillennial factions.

1\. ["The field is the world," ]{.class_4258}not the church. To keep the saved yoked up to the lost, pastors teach that it is wrong to kick a lost person out of church since it will "root up the wheat." This bizarre interpretation comes from reading "the field is [the church," ]{.class_4257}instead of ["the field is the world"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}(see vs. 38).

2\. ["The children of the kingdom," ]{.class_4258}where it occurs, has a reference to saved people in this age, rather than a Jewish remnant in the Tribulation, for ["the children of the kingdom"]{.class_4258} in 8:12 are said to go into ["outer darkness"]{.class_4258}! The ["good seed"]{.class_4258} as children, must be born of that [seed, ]{.class_4257}and that [seed is ]{.class_4257}said to be ["the word of God"]{.class_4258} (Luke 8:11, 1 Pet. 1:22--23).

3\. ["The harvest is the end of the world" ]{.class_4258}(vs. 39) has led Catholic‑Protestant politicians to assume that there is no Tribulation, Antichrist, Armageddon, or thousand‑year reign [before ]{.class_4257}"the end." However, this time the "scholars" would do well to go to their Greek (on which they are always harping to correct the King James) and learn that ["aion" ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_226}(Greek) is "age" and refers to the world, in the sense of the end of the world's course as it runs this present time. (Thus it is used in Rom. 12:2; 2 Cor. 4:4; Gal. 1:4; 1 Tim. 6:17.)

4.[ "While men slept\...tares." ]{.class_4258}The teaching is that "the MAN" (God) did not sleep. [Men slept; ]{.class_4257}that is, those put in charge of the field went to sleep at the switch (1 Thess. 5:1--6). The [tare, ]{.class_4257}as such, is not a [weed ]{.class_4257}at all, but is a perfect imitation of wheat and appears as wheat until it is examined in harvest; then it is apparent that there is no "grain in the EAR." So with the unsaved. Many of them right up to the end of the age [appear ]{.class_4257}to be real, sure‑enough "Christians." The harvest, however, separates the "real McCoy" from the plastic.

5\. The separating is indicated in a unique way, which seems to have escaped the notice of the commentators. ["Gather ye together first the tares."]{.class_4258} This gathering is done with a reference to a future burning, but it takes place [before the burning]{.class_4257} (vs.[ ]{.class_4257}40), and what is more, it takes place [before the wheat goes up into the garner! ]{.class_4257}A study of Hosea 10:10; Matthew 3:12; Zephaniah 3:8; Isaiah 9:11; Zechariah 12:3; and Zechariah 14:2 will help at this point, and will make the student aware of the fact [that the United Nations and the ecumenical movement of the Protestant and Catholic churches is the divine plan of God to prepare the world ]{.class_4257}for [damnation, not salvation, as is supposed. ]{.class_4257}We must always remember that ["Holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost,"]{.class_4258} and therefore, their information is always a good bit ahead of the Associated Press, INS, and UP. "TOGETHERNESS" of [the lost ]{.class_4257}will precede the rapture!

6\. ["Children of the wicked one" ]{.class_4258}is an advanced teaching on the ancient fable of the "Fatherhood of God and the Brotherhood of Man," the latter expression being an archaic relic of folklore which comes from Greek mythology. That everyone is most certainly [not ]{.class_4257}a child of God is evident from the content of both Testaments in the original languages (John 8; Matt. 23; Eph. 2; Gal. 3; John 1, 3; 1 Sam. 1; 2 Sam. 7; Psa. 109). Satan not only has "children," but he has a specific "seed" (Gen. 3:15) to imitate the Messiah.

7\. In verse 40 there is a purposeful omission of the wheat going into the barn for some reason. ["The reapers"]{.class_4258} are angels and are found in Revelation 14 doing the reaping in the last six verses, also in []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_227}Matthew 24:30--31.

8\. One detail of great importance is the fact that what was a [field ]{.class_4257}(the world) becomes the Father's ["kingdom"]{.class_4258} (vss. 41--43). The great change which converts a sprawling mass of heathen, Gentile[ "grasshoppers"]{.class_4258} (see Isa. 40:22), whose nations are less than a drop in the bucket (see Isa. 40:15), into God's kingdom on earth, takes place in Revelation 11:15. It is such an important date that it is named to the year, month, and day on God's calendar so no Bible student can miss it. It is the seventh year of Daniel's Seventieth Week, on [the last day of a thirty‑day month in a series of forty-two months ]{.class_4257}(see Dan. 9:24--27; Rev. 11:2--3, 13:5, 12:6--14).

The event is always spoken of in both Testaments as a cataclysmic event attended by supernatural signs and wonders (Rev. 6; Acts 2; Joel 2; Dan. 2), and there is no CHRISTIAN authority, whatsoever, for the Greek, gnostic mysticism that teaches that the coming Kingdom is a gradual, spiritual effusion of integration, CARE‑packages, "Golden‑rulers," and "brother's‑keepers." The Scrip­tures describe the coming kingdom as a harvest, a war, a battle, a catastrophe, an earthquake, a terror, a victory, a defeat; and it indicates the literalness of the Old Testament prophecies (Isa. 5, 9, 24, 26; Amos 9; Joel 2; Rev. 6, 19, 14; Matt. 24; Mark 13; Luke 21). The gradual conversion of the world to "the Christ," in the light of the clear teaching of 2,000 passages, is an "old wives' fable" for the weak in heart and backslidden in character. In Matthew 13, [somebody goes weeping and wailing into ]{.class_4257}["a furnace of fire"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}(13:42).

13:31 Another parable put he forth unto them, saying, The kingdom of heaven is like to a grain of mustard seed, which a man took, and sowed in his field:

13:32 Which indeed is the least of all seeds: but when it is grown, it is the greatest among herbs, and becometh a tree, so that the birds of the air come and lodge in the branches thereof.

This is one of the "kingdom parables," which has application to both the Kingdom of God and the Kingdom of Heaven, for in Luke 13:18--19, the same similitude is used. Luke is peculiar in that his recording of the parable says ["a garden" ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_228}instead of a field. Since the field is the world, then "the garden" is something else, and again we discover that careful attention to the word of God shows there is [a difference ]{.class_4257}between the two kingdoms.

["And sowed in his field." ]{.class_4258}The mustard seed then is sown [in the world (]{.class_4257}vs. 38). The mustard seed is clearly identified in the Bible, which is its own dictionary of types and interpretations of symbols; a mustard seed is associ­ated with faith (Matt. 17:20).

In Luke, the faith is put in the proper place, ["the kingdom of God," ]{.class_4258}which in [this age ]{.class_4257}turns out to be a kingdom entered by a new birth (John 3:6; Rom. 14:17).

In Matthew, the faith is ["in the world" ]{.class_4258}(John 1:3--10). The roots of this faith, therefore, are ["in the world"]{.class_4258} (Luke 18:8 makes it abundantly clear that there will be little of [true ]{.class_4257}faith on the earth at the Second Coming). This "faith," although classified as an "herb" (Matt. 13:32), becomes a [tree. ]{.class_4257}Trees in Scripture are assorted in ethical values. The wicked is a bay tree. The righteous is a palm. Self‑righteousness is a fig tree. Israel is an olive tree, etc. However, the "birds lodging in the branches" classify this tree very quickly. The birds of the air are [demons ]{.class_4257}according to the exact, detailed, doctrinal teaching of Jesus Himself on parables (Matt. 13:19; Mark 4:4--5, 15; Luke 8:5--12).

This [tree, ]{.class_4257}then, is most clearly identified with a tree like the one in Daniel 4. As a type of Antichrist and the Antichrist's kingdom itself (Rev. 18:1--3), this tree is a [religious monstrosity ]{.class_4257}shading pigs, bears, bats, snakes, and wolves, instead of [sheep ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 10:16). The standard interpretation that the tree is [the church, ]{.class_4257}offering a haven of rest for all, is appropriate, if it be clearly stated that the church here, as the woman in the next two verses, is [a Roman, ]{.class_4257}worldly church, offering a [worldly faith ]{.class_4257}for the world. It is "strictly for the birds," and they find a lodging in it every Christmas and Easter! It bears no resemblance to Bible Christianity, Bible religion, Christian doctrines, or Christian principles. Ezekiel reinforces this thought with the great passage on the "tree" in Ezekiel 31. (Note especially "the birds" in Ezek. 31:6.)

The undying, cheerful optimism with which our "world betterment" societies approach the kingdom parables is not to be imitated. The parables are not "optimistic" for [the world. ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_229}They show the course of this present age as one in which saved and lost are side by side together associated with a church monstrosity ("Christianity") which forever bleats about "faith" and "the faith" and "the old faith," actually referring to a faith which attracts [demons ]{.class_4257}(1 Tim. 4:1--5).

Larkin applies the third parable to the third church of Revelation 2 (the Pergamos church) and matches it to that period where the church joins Rome and settles down in the world.

13:33 Another parable spake he unto them; The kingdom of heaven is like unto leaven, which a woman took, and hid in three measures of meal, till the whole was leavened.

This parable is still quoted in literature published by liberals and the Knights of Columbus as representing the spread of the gospel by the church. Why this blatant falsehood continues to be published is a mystery that only ["the mystery of iniquity"]{.class_4258} can explain. The very briefest study of the parable in the most superficial light would fail to convince anyone with common sense that the gospel is "Leaven" or that the woman is the "Church." Departing from the Bible's definition of Bible terms, the Liberal arrives at a highly fanciful and imaginative theory that the world is going to be converted [by the spread of LEAVEN. ]{.class_4257}Again, we must go to the Bible to understand the Bible. The private interpretation of liberal and Catholic theology that makes the woman the true church and the leaven the true gospel is one of the most outstanding examples of religious extortion and lying ever to blot the printed page. And they will cleave fast to it and will not let it go for love nor money. Now notice:

1\. Leaven is false doctrine, always, in every case, with no exceptions (Matt. 16:6--12; Gal. 5:9; 1 Cor. 5:6--8).

2\. LEAVEN is false doctrine of Sadducees and Pharisees (Matt. 16:6--12).

a\. Teaching to call priests "FATHER" (Matt 23:9).

b\. Teaching that long prayers should be made for the dead (Matt. 23:14).

c\. Teaching the wearing of long robes (Matt. 23:5; Mark 12:38).

d\. Teaching authority of traditions (Mark 7:6--13).

e\. Denying literal resurrection and angels (Acts 23:8).

3\. Thus it becomes apparent why some groups insist that [leaven is ]{.class_4257}the [gospel! ]{.class_4257}Leaven is sour dough, the equivalent of yeast. The gospel is likened to the word of God, which in itself is Meat, Bread, Honey, Milk, and Apples, [but never sour dough.]{.class_4257}

Let us locate "the woman." The church, the bride of Christ, is never called a "woman" anywhere in either Testament. The true church, which is His Body (Eph. 5:24--33), is a Wife or Bride (Rev. 21) or a Chaste Virgin (2 Cor. 11:1--3), [but never "a woman."]{.class_4257}

The private interpretation which makes the woman of Matthew 13:33 the true church is another theological shot in the dark ending in a swamp. There are [women ]{.class_4257}in the Scriptures engaged in religious activities, but the [woman ]{.class_4257}of the Thyatira church (which incidentally matches this fourth parable) is an adulteress (Rev. 2:20), and the woman of Revelation 17:1--5 is a whore. Worse than that, she is defined by the Holy Spirit, apart from private interpretation, as being a [city ]{.class_4257}built on seven mountains, who murders Christians, and whose symbol is a [golden cup ]{.class_4257}and who loves [purple and scarlet ]{.class_4257}(Rev. 17:3--4, 6, 18). No reasonable man in his sound mind would have any trouble identifying the woman from Scripture, if he were allowed the American, Christian privilege of reading it and believing what he reads. [The Woman is that old purple‑clad mother of abominations, the Papal Roman "Church." ]{.class_4257}All reformers understood this. All premillennialists under­stand it. No born‑again Christian in any denomination has any trouble identifying it if the passages are read aloud, without comment. The only way the pope can bury this stinking corpse that clings to his religious organization is to pretend that Revelation 17 [refers to past history and pagan Rome, ]{.class_4257}thus dissecting the passages from the Second Advent. This accounts for the entire Protestant system of amillennialism, which is in line with Roman tradition.

["Three measures of meal." ]{.class_4258}Since the ["meal"]{.class_4258} is made from wheat, we have the key to the interpretation in our hand. The wheat in verse 38 are ["the children of the kingdom,"]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_231} and the children of the Kingdom of God in [this age ]{.class_4257}are likened to a lump of whole meal (1 Cor. 5). The meal then represents the Body of Christ in its three professing groups: Roman Catholic, Greek Orthodox, and Protestant. The teaching of the parable is clear. The old Roman whore (Rev. 17:1--5) will slip her false teachings into the body of Christian doctrines until all three groups are shot through with false doctrine. The prediction then is not optimistic. It is a prophecy of a falling away of the body of professing Christianity and return to the Roman system of corruption, which makes up the "leaven of the Pharisees."

Since the parable is also found in Luke 13:20--21 as applying to ["the kingdom of God,"]{.class_4258} we may learn positively that false doctrine is not only found in the three professing bodies of "Christendom," it is also found [within the body of born‑again believers, ]{.class_4257}in the Body of Christ! There is a remarkable "truth from a remoter context" connected with this parable. In Leviticus 7:13, we find that "LEAVENED bread" is accepted on the part of the believer, where only "unleavened bread" is found where it represents Christ! (Lev. 23:6). Again, where the Holy Spirit prophesies of Pentecost in Leviticus 23, He indicates that [leaven is present ]{.class_4257}even among the New Testament saints of Acts 1--2, for Leviticus 23:17 allows for the offering of bread [with leaven! ]{.class_4257}Even further, one can be absolutely sure that the interpretation is correct, for the leaven the woman hides in the meal in Matthew 13:33 [has no oil in it ]{.class_4257}(Lev. 2:11), and oil represents the Holy Spirit! The teaching of the parable, then, is abundantly clear and plain from the Divine standpoint, apart from interpretation, and any Christian can safely reject any interpretation other than the one given by the Holy Spirit; i.e., Rome will corrupt all Christendom with her false doctrines which are named in Scripture, and within the Body of Christ itself, this false doctrine will often appear. Unfortunately for our gullible liberal and Catholic fellow‑travelers, history bears out that this interpretation is correct, and not the superficial dream‑fantasy that the church is converting the world with "the gospel."

13:34 All these things spake Jesus unto the multitude in parables; and without a parable spake he not unto them:

35 That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by the prophet, saying, I will open my mouth in parables; I will utter things which have been kept secret from the foundation of the world.

The citation is from Psalm 78:1--3. A study of the passage would indicate that the Old Testament "history," far from being a record of man's "quest for God," is a [parable ]{.class_4257}and an exact description of [future events ]{.class_4257}which will take place during the Tribulation. The parables cannot be interpreted by fools (Prov. 26:7--9), for it wounds them. As the lame man of Proverbs 26:7, the sorry systems of amillennialism and postmillennialism can't get the "legs equal"; they lose their balance and only save face by saying: "It is a general truth." 13:36 Then Jesus sent the multitude away, and went into the house: and his disciples came unto him, saying, Declare unto us the parable of the tares of the field.

37 He answered and said unto them, He that soweth the good seed is the Son of man;

38 The field is the world; the good seed are the children of the kingdom; but the tares are the children of the wicked one;

39 The enemy that sowed them is the devil; the harvest is the end of the world; and the reapers are the angels.

40 As therefore the tares are gathered and burned in the fire; so shall it be in the end of this world.

(This passage has been commented on in connection with its original version in vss. 24--30.) The disciples call it ["the parable of the tares," ]{.class_4258}without mentioning [the wheat. ]{.class_4257}Verse 40 teaches plainly that Hell is a literal fire, for the fire of the parable has no counterpart in the interpretation (vss. 37--43). The fire is [fire. ]{.class_4257}All other elements of the parable are defined.

13:41 The Son of man shall send forth his angels, and they shall gather out of his kingdom all things that offend, and them which do iniquity;

42 And shall cast them into a furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth.

43 Then shall the righteous shine forth as the sun in the kingdom of their Father. Who hath ears to hear, let him hear.

The verses have been commented on in part under Matthew 13:24--30. It may be repeated that ["his kingdom"]{.class_4258} is equated with Christ's kingdom, and the action referred to takes place in Revelation 11: 15, with frequent references to it found in Matthew 24; Daniel 2, 7, and other places. ["The Son of man"]{.class_4258} is the title used, which indicates that it is a reference to the advent of Christ to save Israel. [Paul never uses the term ]{.class_4257}["Son of man"]{.class_4258}[ in his epistles when writing to the Christian.]{.class_4257}

["A furnace of fire"]{.class_4258} occurs numerous times in the Scripture and always points to a literal Hell of literal smoke and fire (notwithstanding the superstitious, liberal versions concerning Hell---see 18:8, 5:22, 23:33, 25:41, 46). Unbelief in Hell, of course, always springs from the same source: [refusal to believe what God has revealed about the true nature of fallen man. ]{.class_4257}The only cure for this refusal is [honesty.]{.class_4257}

["Shine forth as the sun in the kingdom" ]{.class_4258}refers to a host of passages in the Old Testament, all of them pointing directly to the coming King in glory in a thousand‑year reign on earth at Jerusalem. This period is always likened to ["the SUN"]{.class_4258} coming up in the morning after night (Isa. 21:11--12; Mal. 4:2; Matt. 24:43; 1 Thess. 5:1--6), and the "RIGHTEOUS" in this period are "LIKE the SUN," for they are LIKE [Christ ]{.class_4257}(Phil. 3:20--21; 1 John 3:1--3; Dan. 12:1--5).

It is odd that the caution on "HEARING" (see vs. 43) would occur here, as the scene is a private conversation with the disciples, who already have been told that they [will be able to hear and understand]{.class_4257} (vs. 16). However, the oddness disappears when the reader takes stock of [who is ]{.class_4257}actually there. All twelve are there---Judas [included! ]{.class_4257}The warning is aimed at him, for he sells Christ out for thirty pieces of silver when he perceives that the "glorious kingdom" is not coming this time! Notice that Judas "holds the bag" []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_234}and goes along with Christ's ministry until the night at Mary's house where the Lord suddenly says, ["Let her alone: against the day of my burying hath she kept this." ]{.class_4258}That was all Judas needed to hear. The true Messiah would "abide forever" (John 12:7; Dan. 7:13--19); therefore, this man was not the true Messiah. Since there was a price on His head, why not pick it up if the man was going to die anyway?

13:44 Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto treasure hid in a field; the which when a man hath found, he hideth, and for joy thereof goeth and selleth all that he hath, and buyeth that field.

Now, for an interlude of humor in the midst of these tremendous truths, let us consult the Greek scholar Alford ([Alford's New Testament]{.class_4257}). He represents perhaps the best in the field of typical interpretations given by men who go to the Greek to avoid the truths of the English. Here is really masterful "cubism" in Bible interpretation, if there is such a thing.

2\. The Christian is the TREASURE!

3\. The MAN (God) hides the Christian in the sacraments!

The only thing wrong with Alford's interpretation (and the standard, liberal, Catholic interpretation) is that it [is pure, arbitrary guesswork, ]{.class_4257}with no foundation in Scripture, fact, history, or sound judgment. When the National Council tries the interpretation, they fare no better than the blinded Pharisees of verses 13--15, for they come out with:

1\. The field is the Church.

2\. The treasure is Christ.

3\. The treasure is the Gospel.

4\. The man is the Christian believer.

The only thing wrong with this interpretation is that the Christian has nothing for sale, and he cannot [buy ]{.class_4257}Christ, and you can't buy the church unless you are [Christ ]{.class_4257}(Eph. 5:25).

It would be refreshing now, just for novelty, to go to the Scriptures to see what they have to say about themselves. There can be no end of interpretations where the interpreter is hobbling along []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_235}on his unequal legs and driving thorns into his hands (Prov. 26:7--9) in an effort to get more people to go to church and take the sacraments.

1\. The ["treasure"]{.class_4258} is most certainly [Israel ]{.class_4257}in all cases, bar none (Deut. 31:17; Isa. 54:8; Exod. 19:5; Psa. 135:4). Furthermore:

2.[ "The field is the world," ]{.class_4258}and the Lord just told you so in verse 38, so there could be no divergence of opinions.

3\. The ["man"]{.class_4258} is God (as He has been in every parable till here: that is, Christ being God in the flesh---Matt. 13:3, 24, 37).

4\. The buying is Christ purchasing not only the church, but the [world ]{.class_4257}(with His blood). The world in this sense will be the land‑surface, not the worldly "system"; though even then, this will convert to Christ at Revelation 11:15. Do not forget that the ["field"]{.class_4258} becomes the kingdom of ["the Son of man"]{.class_4258} in Matthew 13:37, 41.

The meaning of the parable, then, is abundantly clear. Somewhere in the world is hidden a true remnant of believing Israel. God hides His face from Israel as a nation in that day (Deut. 31:17). [Notice the third person, singular, indicative active in Matthew ]{.class_4257}13:44---["he hideth,"]{.class_4258} and notice there is no [object. ]{.class_4257}He can either hide the treasure or hide [Himself! ]{.class_4257}Here the unsearchable riches of the King James English bring out a truth hidden in the obscure Greek text! (This fifth parable matches the Sardis period of history---Rev. 3:1--2.)

13:45 Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a merchant man, seeking goodly pearls:

46 Who, when he had found one pearl of great price, went and sold all that he had, and bought it.

Since our Lord begins with ["again,"]{.class_4258} we may begin by saying, "AGAIN," the interpretations are as deranged as a Chinese fire drill. The "goodly pearl" is not salvation, for the liberals who teach this recognize a dozen ways to heaven, [not ONE. ]{.class_4257}Again, salvation is [without price ]{.class_4257}as it is said to be a gift (Acts 8:20; Eph. 2:8--9).

For clarity of thought and intelligible interpretation, let us again consult the only, accurate, infallible authority---the [Scriptures themselves.]{.class_4257}

1\. A ["pearl"]{.class_4258} in Scriptures stands for a [person ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_236}and usually for a female person. (The pearl is not defined as a woman, but as a "precious stone." The pearl is unique in that it is the only precious stone that is an [organism; ]{.class_4257}all other stones---jewels, jasper, onyx, sapphire, ruby, topaz, beryl, etc.---are inorganic [minerals]{.class_4257}.) Note: Proverbs 31:10, 11:22; Matthew 7:6; Proverbs 3:13--16; Zechariah 9:16; Malachi 3:17.

2\. There is no reason for assuming the ["merchant man" ]{.class_4258}is any other man than the Son of Man, the Lord Jesus Christ, for after all, He has been the anonymous ["man"]{.class_4258} in every parable until NOW. Furthermore, we know that Christ did [buy something, ]{.class_4257}and we know what the price of the purchase was (Eph. 5:25; 1 Pet. 1:18--19; Acts 20:28). Besides this we are clearly WARNED in Scripture to look for the Liberal who denies that Christ did buy anything [with His blood! ]{.class_4257}This warning is found in a book which liberals have tried to exclude from the canon of Scriptures since the first century: 2 Peter 2:1.

Now, since our Lord never uses a similitude of any kind without being cognizant of ALL that the symbol or object involves, we should be prudent and conscientious about studying the similitude, before making application.

a\. A pearl is an [organism.]{.class_4257}

b\. It is the only jewel that would be RUINED by any kind of division (other stones can be divided into smaller stones a dozen times).

c\. A pearl is found on the bottom of the ocean in a dirty or muddy place.

d\. It is found in an oyster and is formed by a diseased secretion, which is put forth to cover [an injury.]{.class_4257}

e\. It is the favorite head or necklace dress for kings and queens.

All considering, the ["pearl of great price," ]{.class_4258}if it represents [people ]{.class_4257}(and all precious stones do---see 1 Pet. 2:5; 1 Cor. 3:10, 12), can only refer to one group of people on the face of the earth in the "mystery" period of the Kingdom of Heaven; it is the church, which is His body. This church is a [living organism ]{.class_4257}(1 Cor. 12), purchased by suffering, and its members are "FISHES" who have been "fished" for the Lord (Luke 5:10). The saved sinner thus comes from a muddy place under the wrath of God; he is covered with Christ's []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_237}righteousness, which was only made available through suffering; thus covered, he is destined to [reign as a king ]{.class_4257}(Rev. 1:1--6)! The Man is Christ. The Pearl is the Church. The Great Price is Christ's Blood.

No other interpretation will come even REASON­ABLY close to satisfying the demands of Scripture; albeit, it may satisfy the carnal and selfish demands of a Catholic‑Socialist‑religio‑politico system gone bugs on "religion."

13:47 Again, the kingdom of heaven is like unto a net, that was cast into the sea, and gathered of every kind:

48 Which, when it was full, they drew to shore, and sat down, and gathered the good into vessels, but cast the bad away.

49 So shall it be at the end of the world: the angels shall come forth, and sever the wicked from among the just,

50 And shall cast them into the furnace of fire: there shall be wailing and gnashing of teeth.

A man, a grain, leaven, a woman, and a merchant man are followed by [a net ]{.class_4257}as picturing the mystery of the Kingdom of Heaven. As the goodly pearl in the last parable can represent the Philadelphia church period in history (Rev. 3), so this last parable may represent the Laodicean period (Rev. 3). It is peculiar indeed that only seven parables are given in this chapter as a warning of the Church Age to come (which no one in the audience would understand until the revelation is given by Paul in Eph. 3), while the remaining five parables occur [later ]{.class_4257}in Matthew. This total of twelve hearkens to Israel, and so the remaining five [parables all deal with Israel's situation in the Great Tribulation at the end of the Church Age.]{.class_4257}

The net could be the "throw net" (mullet net on the Gulf Coast) of Matthew 4:18, but here in Matthew 13:47, a different word is used. This is the "Seine" or sweep‑net used by Bluefish and Mackerel commercial fishermen from Port St. Joe to Pensacola.

"Good and bad fish" are obviously [saved and lost men ]{.class_4257}(Eccl. 9:12; Mark 1:17). Furthermore, they are defined in verse 49 as ["the wicked" ]{.class_4258}and ["the just." "They drew to shore" ]{.class_4258}implies fishermen, yet the fishermen are defined as "angels" in verse 49. The []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_238}passage is easily understandable when taken with 1 Corinthians 6:1--4 and Matthew 22:30. The "soul winners" of this age [judge the unsaved in the age to come and come with Christ, ]{.class_4257}as angels, when He comes (Matt. 22:30, 24:31, 2 Thess. 1:7).

["The end of the world" ]{.class_4258}again is found in the context (and a text without a context is a pretext!) to be the end of this [present world system, aionos, ]{.class_4257}"age," and not the destruction of the land surface of the earth [(ga). ]{.class_4257}Again, the unsearchable riches of the [AV ]{.class_4257}English, in Hebrews 9:26, define the Greek, as well or better than the lexicon does, and we are reminded of the hand of the Holy Spirit on the 1611 translation that is missing from its crude and defective "sequels."

See Matthew 13:30, 42 for the remarks on the literalness of Hell.

13:51 Jesus saith unto them, Have ye understood all these things? They say unto him, Yea, Lord.

52 Then said he unto them, Therefore every scribe which is instructed unto the kingdom of heaven is like unto a man that is an householder, which bringeth forth out of his treasure things new and old.

Upon His conclusion of the parables, our Lord asks the disciples if they have understood [all ]{.class_4257}these things. Their ["yea, Lord,"]{.class_4258} implies that they certainly understood more than the present cult of the interpretators who are always getting the church balled‑up with the kingdom, and the kingdom all balled‑up with the National Council, and the Council all snarled up in Romanism, and Israel all muddled‑up with the church. There lies a certain limitation to their understanding, however, and this limitation is clearly given in John 16:12 and Acts 26:16. The modern "Ebionite" (represented by the "teachings of the Master" sects) stops with the Synoptic Gospels and, thus, loses the key to the mysteries, which explain them exactly and in a way that is even more explicit than the disciples understood.

["Every scribe\..."]{.class_4258} denotes that there are [good scribes ]{.class_4257}(Ezra 7:6) as well as [bad scribes ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 23:23), but the difference is apparent in what follows; the [good ]{.class_4257}scribe is a "householder" and has a good treasure---["new and old." ]{.class_4258}Now, without taking time out to theorize on what Jesus may or may not have meant, let us follow our usual custom of finding out []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_239}["What saith the Scriptures?"]{.class_4257}

The ["householder"]{.class_4258} is a steward (Luke 16:1--12), and as a good steward over a household (Heb. 3:3--5), he has charge of the "treasures" (Psa. 119:162). The treasures are good things in "the heart" (Matt. 12:35). And "old and new treasures" must refer to some kind of a relationship between the Old and New Testaments, although this was not at all clear at the time of the Matthew 13 discourse. None of the New Testament had been written, and more than that, the New Testament had not even been instituted. Nevertheless, the suggestion is found in the words. If a man understands the Old, he can interpret the New. There is material in both which is identical, synonymous, in contrast, parallel, progressive, expansive, illustrative, and explicative of the other. Leviticus 26:10, when spiritualized, produces a nice corollary to the statement of the Lord Jesus. You bring forth the correct doctrines of the Old Testament, [because of the New, ]{.class_4257}and vice versa. ["The old store,"]{.class_4258} here, is likened to food (Lev. 25:22), but since the word of God meets this requirement also (1 Pet. 2:1--3, Heb. 5:12--14), the application is not too far-fetched.

13:53 And it came to pass, that when Jesus had finished these parables, he departed thence.

54 And when he was come into his own country, he taught them in their synagogue, insomuch that they were astonished, and said, Whence hath this man this wisdom, and these mighty works?

55 Is not this the carpenter's son? is not his mother called Mary? and his brethren, James, and Joses, and Simon, and Judas?

56 And his sisters, are they not all with us? Whence then hath this man all these things?

57 And they were offended in him. But Jesus said unto them, A prophet is not without honour, save in his own country, and in his own house.

58 And he did not many mighty works there because of their unbelief.

["His own country"]{.class_4258} implies up around Nazareth again in []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1266.xhtml_page_240}Galilee and back in the synagogues of previous trouble (Luke 4:16--28). This is the third tour through Galilee preceding the crisis at Capernaum (John 6:26--66).

[​]{.class_4259}["Whence hath this man\...." ]{.class_4258}The statement is to be compared with the one following where they say, ["are they not all with us?"]{.class_4258}.[ ]{.class_4258}These two statements sum up the complete doctrinal ATTITUDE of the unbelieving, religious people towards the Deity of Christ. As has been mentioned before, the average, educated American cannot get ONE particular thought through his unregenerate brain, and that is the Bible fact that [God is particular and selective. ]{.class_4257}(He is not a general straw boss for a universe operating by some theory which Einstein imposed on Him.) ["With us" ]{.class_4258}is a masterful summary of mistaken identity, for the Man referred to is ["Emmanuel\...God with us." ]{.class_4258}The title is read by all Liberals, Communists, Modernists, atheists, and pagans alike (without any distinction): "a man [like us, ]{.class_4257}with us." The proof is adduced from the presence of His "brethren" and "sisters," for the leaders of the synagogue have had ample opportunity to observe that "Jesus' brothers and sisters" were ordinary folk like anyone else. They resent His authority, they resent His claim to deity, they resent His power, they resent His teaching and they resent His true followers (Rom. 8:8). Thus has it ever been, and thus shall it always be, world without end, Amen. An unsaved religious leader, by virtue of his sinfulness, is [forced ]{.class_4257}to classify Jesus with Buddha, Lao Tse, Moham­med, Confucius, and Krishna, et al.; for all of these can be matched [when it comes to self‑righteous, good works. ]{.class_4257}This is the root of the "world‑religion" of the liberal.

In addition to the statements which you will find under Matthew 1:25 and Matthew 10:2--4, we find here the curious comparison of ["brethren" ]{.class_4258}with ["country"]{.class_4258} ("kin folks" in Mark 6:4), and "brothers and sisters" with ["his own house"]{.class_4258}---thus settling conclusively the argument about literal brothers and sisters by Joseph and Mary. [Jesus had them.]{.class_4257}

["He did not many mighty works there." ]{.class_4258}The passage suggests that there must be some corresponding amount of faith on the part of the people, as there is power on the part of the God‑Man (see Heb. 11:6). Mark adds that "healing" is not a mighty work (see Mark 6:5).

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1439.xhtml}

# Chapter 14 {.heading_84-0}

14:1 At that time Herod the tetrarch heard of the fame of Jesus,

2 And said unto his servants, This is John the Baptist; he is risen from the dead; and therefore mighty works do show forth themselves in him.

The rumour of the ["mighty works"]{.class_4258} undoubtedly arises from the work of the twelve in Matthew 10, which by this time reaches Herod's ears. The ["risen from the dead"]{.class_4258} indicates that Herod had a guilty conscience from having murdered John (see vs. 10), and it also indicates that he knew something of the prophecies which clearly told of the resurrection and return of Elijah before the coming of the Lord (see John 1:21; Matt. 16:14). The reader should notice closely the relationship of Jeremiah, John, and Elijah; the names are not placed in the text accidentally. All three names are associated with the Great Tribulation.

14:3 For Herod had laid hold on John, and bound him, and put him in prison for Herodias' sake, his brother Philip's wife.

4 For John said unto him, It is not lawful for thee to have her.

5 And when he would have put him to death, he feared the multitude, because they counted him as a prophet.

6 But when Herod's birthday was kept, the daughter of Herodias danced before them, and pleased Herod.

7 Whereupon he promised with an oath to give her whatsoever she would ask.

The characters are all historical and correct in their relationship. This is Herod Antipas who divorced his wife (a daughter of Aretas) in order to marry Herodias. Herodias was the niece of Philip (half brother of Antipas). \[Josephus Ant. XVIII, V. 2.\]

["It is not lawful" ]{.class_4258}is a true Jewish statement with the Law in mind (Lev. 18:16). Herod's fear of the multitude is similar to the Pharisees' fear, recorded in Matthew 21:26. Herod is evidently []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1439.xhtml_page_242}deeply impressed by John's ministry and his ethics, for in Luke 23:7--8, Herod still hopes to see the ["mighty works"]{.class_4258} which he expected of John. Mark also indicates that Herod actually "cleaned up" a little in response to the Baptist's preaching (Mark 6:20). The [RSV ]{.class_4257}(always reliable for a good laugh) mistranslates ["he did many things" ]{.class_4258}as ["he was much perplexed." ]{.class_4257}This passage in Mark is called a copyist's error (Dean Weigle), which, of course, is a typical, biased, subjective opinion with very little thought behind it.

["The daughter of Herodias" ]{.class_4258}is the daughter of Herodias. The believer should note this, for at this junction in Matthew, we find one of the greatest blunders of New Testament Greek scholarship. This blunder puts the Westcott and Hort Greek Text in the correct light---black light! Josephus says that this dancer was "Salome," the [daughter of ]{.class_4257}Philip. Even Goodspeed, who publishes one of the greatest Unitarian translations in print, admits that the reading of the [King James ]{.class_4257}is correct: it is [Herodias' daughter, ]{.class_4257}not [Herod's ]{.class_4257}daughter (Goodspeed, New Testament, p. 76). Yet Westcott and Hort claim this [is Herod's daughter! ]{.class_4257}Common sense should tell Westcott and Hort that no Roman king or governor would allow his daughter to do a belly dance in public! Westcott and Hort, going against the sum total of manuscript evidence, plus history, plus common sense, plus the Holy Spirit, throw out the reading in an effort to prove their private theory that the Byzantine Text of the [Authorized Version ]{.class_4257}is a "conflate" text. Again, the believer may put Westcott and Hort in file 13 and return to learn something from the unsearchable riches of the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611.

The "DANCING" here is not defined, but any person with any experience at all in world traveling or in "exotic adventures" knows perfectly well the suggestive and sensuous character of an oriental Syrian or Arabian dance. The gestures and movements are not confined to graceful poses as we find in Japan and Siam, but include the repertoire of burlesque movements, which are for the sole purpose of [arousing the unregenerate nature. ]{.class_4257}It works on Herod; he is carried away and promises her nothing short of the moon.

14:8 And she, being before instructed of her mother, said, Give me here John Baptist's head in a charger.

9 And the king was sorry: nevertheless for the oath's sake, and them which sat with him at meat, he commanded it to be given her.

10 And he sent, and beheaded John in the prison.

11 And his head was brought in a charger, and given to the damsel: and she brought it to her mother.

12 And his disciples came, and took up the body, and buried it, and went and told Jesus.

There are several tremendous spiritual and moral lessons that can be learned from the study of this incident. In the first place, we find that Herod, as most political leaders, is afraid of everything and everybody [except God ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 10:27--28). He fears his conscience (14:2). He fears John (vs. 4). He fears the multitude (vs. 5). He fears ridicule (vs. 9). And he fears to break his own word (Mark 6:26). Here, then, is a perfect example of the attitude, make‑up, motives, and "religion" of a typical Christ‑rejecting sinner. He fears EVERYTHING he shouldn't, and NOTHING he should. Herod's conscience is [weakened ]{.class_4257}under John's preaching (Mark 6:20), it is [outraged ]{.class_4257}at a dance (Mark 6:22), and it is [dead ]{.class_4257}by the time Herod is confronted with the decision to receive Christ (Luke 23:8--12). It is significant that where Herod and Pilate cannot be tolerant and broadminded and cooperate in political matters, [they can agree that a Scripture-quoting, Bible‑believing Saviour should be "gotton rid of."]{.class_4257}

The ["charger"]{.class_4258} (Greek: [pinax]{.class_4257}) amounts to a "wooden trencher," a sort of deep wooden bowl, much the same as a modern centerpiece "salad bowl."

Among many moral lessons taught in the story, we learn that a man is what he [loves, ]{.class_4257}and that women make a religion out of LOVE. It is obvious that the real objector to the Baptist's preaching (at least where it regarded marriage) was Herodias herself. Look at Matthew 14:11, ["she brought it to her MOTHER." ]{.class_4258}Women have [religious convictions ]{.class_4257}about "love" where they have none about truth or principle. And of course, it is this fact that is taken advantage of by the Roman Catholic Church in building its membership on the contingency of marriages without birth control.

The student of prophecy should notice the reference to the "beheading." This is important, for John the Baptist is said to be a "type of Elijah" (see Luke 1; Matt. 17; and John 1). As a type of Elijah, it is fitting that he should suffer death by decapitation, for when Elijah shows up (Rev. 11:3--10), he dies in this same manner (see Rev. 20:3--6).

14:13 When Jesus heard of it, he departed thence by ship into a desert place apart: and when the people had heard thereof, they followed him on foot out of the cities.

14 And Jesus went forth, and saw a great multitude, and was moved with compassion toward them, and he healed their sick.

The time and place is the setting of Mark 6; Luke 9; and John 6. This is one of the truly remarkable times in the earthly ministry of our Lord, since all four Gospels record the miraculous "feeding of the multitude" which follows; rarely do all four writers describe the same events at any time before the "passion" week. The locality is probably the NORTHEAST coast of the Sea of Galilee.

The expression ["moved with compassion" ]{.class_4258}is found again in 9:36 and 18:27, as well as Mark 1:41 and 6:34. It is one of those short phrases pregnant with significance. The Greek ([splagchnizomai]{.class_4257}) is from the medical term for the viscera. Whereas, modern science refers to the "bowels" as the [lower viscera ]{.class_4257}only, the [AV ]{.class_4257}uses the word ["bowels"]{.class_4258} as originally intended: [the inner core of some­thing. ]{.class_4257}Hence, ["bowels"]{.class_4258} in the New Testament refers not to the lower viscera only, but to the upper as well (1 John 3:17). As such, it denotes the heart, liver, lungs, and stomach, as well as the upper and lower intestines. This ["compassion"]{.class_4258} is to[ ]{.class_4258}be associated with that meaning and, therefore, indicates the exercise of being "pained in the heart" or being "cut to the heart" (expressions found in the New Testament). The idea is that the Lord's [emotional feeling]{.class_4257} is so aroused and disturbed by the pitiful condition of the straying multitudes that it effects Him physically so that He [feels ]{.class_4257}the disturbances in His own human body. (For further comment, see Heb. 4:15.)

14:15 And when it was evening, his disciples came to him, saying, This is a desert place, and the time is now past; send the multitude away, that they may go into the villages, and buy themselves victuals.

16 But Jesus said unto them, They need not depart; give ye them to eat.

17 And they say unto him, We have here but five loaves, and two fishes.

18 He said, Bring them hither to me.

19 And he commanded the multitude to sit down on the grass, and took the five loaves, and the two fishes, and looking up to heaven, he blessed, and brake, and gave the loaves to his disciples, and the disciples to the multitude.

In parallel accounts, we can pick up a number of other details.

1\. The time was far ["passed"]{.class_4258} as well as ["past"]{.class_4258} (Mark 6:35).

2\. The word ["bread"]{.class_4258} is substituted for ["victuals"]{.class_4258}[ ]{.class_4257}(Mark 6:36).

3\. They argued with Jesus in unbelief before He actually commanded them to bring the food (Mark 6:37; John 6:7--9).

4\. The word ["meat" ]{.class_4258}is used in Luke 9:13 for ["bread"]{.class_4258} and ["victuals."]{.class_4258} (Notice how the [King James ]{.class_4257}is self‑definitive in the use of "archaic" words!!)

5\. The desert place turns out to be not a [sandy ]{.class_4257}desert, but a grassy desert (John 6:10).

If this seems strange to the inexperienced traveler (and nearly all Hebrew and Greek scholars are "stay‑at­home" folks!), it must be remembered that the word "desert" is kin to "deserted" and means: empty, barren, void---of any [person. ]{.class_4257}The Gobi desert is largely a [rock ]{.class_4257}desert, the Sahara a [sand ]{.class_4257}desert, and the deserts in Mexico and Arizona [cactus and sage brush deserts; ]{.class_4257}nevertheless they are deserted and qualify as "deserts."

["The time is now past" ]{.class_4258}suggests that "the time to [eat supper" is ]{.class_4257}now past. However, it is apparently still light enough to feed the people in groups (John 6:10, Luke 9:14), so the time would occur between 5 and 7 o'clock. Since they say ["the day is far spent" ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1439.xhtml_page_246}(Luke 24:29), the chances are that it is beginning to turn dark and the sun has set; if the scene is in the summer, this would put the time around 7:30 or 8 in the evening. The fairy tale of the "Robe," portrayed in Hollywood in [broad daylight, is ]{.class_4257}typical of that dream world. Hollywood's polite slander of Christ's Deity, by making the bread appear from [under the robes of the crowd who came, is ]{.class_4257}also very typical of that class of religious hack‑writers who write such scenarios.

Prophetically, the scene is indicative of the feeding of the remnant of Israel in the Great Tribulation, who also will be fed miraculously from Heaven (Rev. 12:1--15, Micah 7:14--15, Jer. 50:19--20, Ezek. 20:35, Lam. 5:9). It is also significant that this miraculous producing of bread in the wilderness is [the first temptation Satan offers Jesus. ]{.class_4257}We thus learn that Satan is a premillennial Bible believer when it comes to the literal fulfillment of prophecy and, as such, is considerably ahead of his "ministers" (2 Cor. 11:10--14), who still idle time away with the worthless Catholic system of amillennialism.

14:20 And they did all eat, and were filled: and they took up of the fragments that remained twelve baskets full.

21 And they that had eaten were about five thousand men, beside women and children.

Again, additional details are found in the other gospels.

1\. They sat down by [companies ]{.class_4257}or ranks (fifty in each rank---Mark 6:39--40).

2\. The loaves are blessed and broken before they are given (cf. Matt. 26:26), and this contains a tremendous spiritual truth, with which all Bible-believing Christians are familiar. The word of God has to be broken to be given out (Luke 24:45). The Christian has to be broken to be a blessing. He has to be blessed by God to endure the breaking which follows (2 Cor. 12:1--8).

3\. John supplies most of the detailed information concerning this feeding of 5,000. Chapter 6 in John's gospel not only supplies a detailed conversation with Phillip and Andrew, preceding the feeding, but also a tremendous discourse on the "Bread of LIFE" subsequent to the feeding (John 6:15--71). The passage is of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1439.xhtml_page_247}tremendous importance as it contains (wrested from the context) [the proof texts ]{.class_4257}of the priest for the pagan Roman Mass (see vss. 52--56). The student will astutely observe that in the first place the discourse is not to saved Gentiles, [but to unsaved Jews. ]{.class_4257}It is [before ]{.class_4257}the crucifixion. Those who receive the bread know they have eternal life as a present possession (vs. 54). The interpretation of the passage is given in verse 57, and this verse is purposely omitted in teaching the Roman catechism. And furthermore, the literal flesh is plainly renounced in the context (vs. 63) as having any magical "saving grace." But who can convince a papist (1 Cor. 14:38)?

4\. John also records the fact that not only was the multitude completely satisfied in the amount that they had to eat from this miraculous feeding, but that the remains were [greater than the original amount ]{.class_4257}(see John 6:13). The feeding is said, by the Holy Spirit, to be a "MIRACLE" (John 6:14), and Fosdick's childish state­ment, that if they had known anything about inductive science they would not have spoken of "miracles," is representative of his class of commentators.

14:22 And straightway Jesus constrained his disciples to get into a ship, and to go before him unto the other side, while he sent the multitudes away.

23 And when he had sent the multitudes away, he went up into a mountain apart to pray: and when the evening was come, he was there alone.

24 But the ship was now in the midst of the sea, tossed with waves: for the wind was contrary.

25 And in the fourth watch of the night Jesus went unto them, walking on the sea.

Luke omits the incident that follows, but it is found in John 6:16--21 and Mark 6:45--51. ["The other side" ]{.class_4258}indicates that the feeding of the 5,000 took place somewhere on the [eastern ]{.class_4257}shore of Galilee, for the ["other side"]{.class_4258} turns out to be Capernaum (John 6:17), which is well located on ancient and modern maps. ["The multitudes away\..."]{.class_4258} turns out to be only a temporary thing, for when they hear of Jesus' presence, the next day they sail after Him to get more bread (John 6:22).

Jesus often withdraws for special seasons of prayer, and many of these seasons are lonely vigils, when no one is praying with Him (Mark 1:35, 6:46; Luke 9:18, 9:28, 22:44).

["A mountain apart\..."]{.class_4258} is a "lead‑in" for students of prophecy, although only the most adept and skillful in the "meat of the word" (Heb. 5) should attempt the application, which is considerably ahead of the investigations of Bullinger, Stam, Larkin, Sauer, Pember, and Pink.

1\. Christ is now "on high," [praying ]{.class_4257}for His saints.

2\. They are in the [nighttime ]{.class_4257}of this dispensation (John 9:4--5; Isa. 21:11; Matt. 13:43; Mark 13:34--35; 1 Thess. 5:1--10).

3\. ["The wind" ]{.class_4258}is contrary to the saints (Eccl. 11:4; Eph 4:14).

4\. [When Jesus Christ returns, He will return in the fourth watch]{.class_4257} (see text), [which is the morning watch ]{.class_4257}(3--6 a.m.). (See Mal. 4; Deut. 32; Matt. 13; 2 Pet. 3:8.)

However, this is "strong doctrine," and it involves a study of the SEA, as the "great deeps" (which is a little beyond the average Bible student of any persuasion). \[See notes on Genesis 1:1--4 in the Commentary on Genesis, 1969, Bible Baptist Bookstore.\]

Practically speaking, the passage makes fine sermonic material, rich in suggestions that deal with the Christian experience. This is especially noticeable in what follows:

14:26 And when the disciples saw him walking on the sea, they were troubled, saying, It is a spirit; and they cried out for fear.

27 But straightway Jesus spake unto them, saying, Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid.

28 And Peter answered him and said, Lord, if it be thou, bid me come unto thee on the water.

29 And he said, Come. And when Peter was come down out of the ship, he walked on the water, to go to Jesus.

The ["walking on the sea"]{.class_4258} has been explained in various ways, the main way being that the water was [frozen ]{.class_4257}and Jesus walked on the [ice! ]{.class_4257}Exactly how He walks on ice, with a strong wind blowing and high waves rolling [in the summertime ]{.class_4257}(see note on the time of year in regard to the evening supper of the 5,000, vss. 1--12), is more of a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1439.xhtml_page_249}mystery than if He actually managed to tip‑toe on the waves. Furthermore, there would have been no consternation in the boat about a ["spirit"]{.class_4258} showing up, if any of the disciples were familiar with ice‑cake walking! If such a practice existed on the shores of Galilee, who would know any more about it than [commercial fishermen? ]{.class_4257}The objections to the miraculous occurrence raise more problems of credulity than are raised by accepting the Biblical account as it stands.

The phenomenon is similar to the post-resurrection appearance of Jesus to the eleven in Luke 24, and it evokes the same response. They are terrified and He reassures them.

The [RSV ]{.class_4257}(always reliable for a good laugh) comes up with ["It is a ghost" ]{.class_4257}([phantasma, ]{.class_4257}Greek); however, the juggling is unnecessary.
