18:10 Take heed that ye despise not one of these little ones; for I say unto you, That in heaven their angels do always behold the face of my Father which is in heaven.

11 For the Son of man is come to save that which was lost.

["Their angels."]{.class_4258} This passage is deduced to mean that everyone has a "guardian" angel. Hence, St. Jude, St. Christopher, St. Joan, St. Fatima, St. Michael, St. Pat­rick, and myriads of "saints" clutter up history, with many of them "cooperation" saints who (following Marx and Darwin and the French Revolution) [adopt and guard "social groups" ]{.class_4257}instead of [individuals. ]{.class_4257}These "patron" saints take care of "mass troubles" in keeping with standard Communist and socialistic doctrines. One looks after the foot soldier, one is staying awake at night worrying about poor people, one is put on your dashboard to keep the highway patrol from getting you, and another sweats blood to collect enough money from Bingo games to litigate aid for Parochial schools in the local govern­mental headquarters.

The Bible believer can recognize the superstition no matter how carefully it is veneered and shellacked with Scripture; although I have given an exaggerated picture, it is by no means beyond the "faith" of the "faithful" who mistake [credulity ]{.class_4257}for "faith."

Certain [facts ]{.class_4257}can be established with the aid of Scripture:

1\. Angels are basically "appearances" (Acts 7:30, 32; Gen 32:30; Judg. 13:1--20).

2\. Wherever they are spoken of, they are spoken of as MALE (Gen. 32, 19; Judg. 13; Luke 24; Acts 1; John 20; Rev. 21:17).

3\. As appearances, angels are said to represent local‑church congregations (Rev. 1--3) or "nations" (Dan. 12:1, 10:21; Isa. 63:9).

4\. They are in heaven in the presence of God (Luke 15:7, 10; Matt. 16:27; 1 Tim. 5:21).

5\. Many of them left this "estate" to come to earth (Jude 6; 2 Pet. 2:3--4; Gen. 6:1--6; Job 38).

6\. Many more will appear in the next fifty years, as "men from outer space" (Job 1--2, 38; Gen 6:1--6; Luke 17:26--30; Rev 12:4--13; Acts 14:11--13).

["Guardian angels" ]{.class_4257}seem to be a reality in one sense, and this is the sense in which, on special occasions, they [minister ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_326}to the saints (Heb. 1:14). One ministers to Hagar in Genesis, to Jesus at the temptation, to Paul in Acts 27 on the ship, and to Peter in jail. However, this does not [assign ]{.class_4257}an angel to each person, nor does it give Rome the right to shuffle a deck of "saints" and deal them out for a good old‑fashioned game of seven-card-stud-Vatican-high.

The child has an "angelin heaven" to represent it, and this angel is either a 33‑year old [male ]{.class_4257}as the other angels of Scripture, like Christ (see Phil. 3:20--21, 1 John 3:1--3; and Matt. 22:30), or else the [appearance of the child ]{.class_4257}is in heaven just like a flash on a TV screen where it can be observed. Either case is "way out" when it comes to exposition, but what we cannot explain, we believe [because God said it! ]{.class_4257}(Not because some church "taught it.")

Matthew 18:11 is omitted in the [RSV ]{.class_4257}(a-dud-a-minute!). This omission is from the writings of Origen (184--254). The "Hesychian" manuscripts of North Africa (which Origen invented) also omit the verse.

18:12 How think ye? if a man have an hundred sheep, and one of them be gone astray, doth he not leave the ninety and nine, and goeth into the mountains, and seeketh that which is gone astray?

The teaching of the parable is:

One: Christ leaves the ninety-nine sheep of the house of Israel, that are obeying the commandments of the Old Testament, and goes out to seek the "sinner" in Israel who has broken them (Matt. 9:13).

Two: the "lost sheep" who will listen are a remnant in Christ's day, and they represent the remnant [who will believe ]{.class_4257}in the Tribulation.

18:15 Moreover if thy brother shall trespass against thee, go and tell him his fault between thee and him alone: if he shall hear thee, thou hast gained thy brother.

17 And if he shall neglect to hear them, tell it unto the church: but if he neglect to hear the church, let him be unto thee as a heathen man and a publican.

Here follows practical instructions on settling disputes between "brethren." Anyone who has had extensive dealing with Christians []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_331}realizes the difficulty involved in obeying the commandment, for it is always easier to gossip to someone else to get him on your side than it is to go to the person involved and "have it out" with him.

The rules are clearly given, however, and [two trips ]{.class_4257}to the person are considered necessary before the matter comes before the assembly. Before he is forgiven, he is supposed to ["repent and turn again to thee."]{.class_4257} 1[ ]{.class_4257}Corinthians 5 and 2 Corinthians 11 discuss this matter in the local church in connection with "church discipline." 2\. Forgiveness in the local church conditioned on the erring party [seeking forgiveness, ]{.class_4257}and the erring party should have had two previous chances to make the matter right---one by the opposing party and the other by the party with two or three witnesses. It then becomes a "church matter," and the forgiveness is [not automatic or unconditional ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 18:18 in context which takes us right back to Matt. 16:16--18).

["Tell it unto the church" ]{.class_4258}(Army version: "tell it to the chaplain") brings about the case where you [have forgiven the man but he has not forgiven you ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 5:23--26), and the matter is therefore still ["bound." ]{.class_4258}The church, as a group (notice the "ye" in vs. 18, commented on before), has this [binding and loosing power, ]{.class_4257}and it most certainly is NOT confined to Simon Peter and his successors---see comments under Matthew 16:16--19.

["Let him be unto thee as an heathen"]{.class_4258} is a strange thing occurring where it does; as in Greek, [ethnikos ]{.class_4257}is the very word for "GENTILE"! []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_332}12). For example, 490 years dates the first and second coming of Christ---the first coming within a year (Sir R. Anderson had it figured to the day) and the Second Coming to within a year, following the tribulation (see Dan. 9:24--27; Gen. 29:27; Neh. 2). (There isn't a bad thing on this earth that is not a good thing twisted.) ]{.class_4257}The existence of the imitation proves the real thing is there. \[Without the [AV]{.class_4257}, no [RSV]{.class_4257}, [RV]{.class_4257}, or [ASV]{.class_4257}. Note: 490 years from the Passover in Egypt and Israel's deliverance to the dedication of the temple under Solomon: 450 years in Judges, 40 in the wilderness, 40 under Eli, 40 under Samuel, 40 under Saul, 40 under David, and 4 for Solomon, 45 for Caleb = 699 years. Of this 699 years, 165 years must be subtracted for the time that Israel served her captors in the book of Judges (thirteen captivities all listed). Another 40 years of wandering in the wilderness must be subtracted, giving 494 for the temple, and an even 490 for the Passover to the ascension of the Son of David to the Throne.

That is not all. There are an even 490 years of 360 days (lunar years) from the dedication of the temple (1 Kings 8:1) to the restoration of Israel after the 70 years of captivity (Ezra 1:1). Thus we find 490 from Joshua to Saul. 490 from Saul to Nebuchadnezzar. 490 from the first dedication in 2 Chronicles 5 (1004--1000 B.C.) to the second dedication (517 B.C.). (The foundation was laid fifty-three years from the destruction of Jerusalem in 634--536 B.C.) If this were not enough, there are 490 years from the covenant that God made with Abraham (Gen. 12) to the covenant Joshua makes on entering the promised land (Josh. 24). There are 483 years from the commandment to rebuild Jerusalem (Neh. 2) to the triumphal entry of the Messiah, Palm Sunday (Dan. 9), and the remaining seven years []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_336}(making a total of 490) are to be found in the seven year period of the Great Tribulation [yet future, ]{.class_4257}preceding the Second Coming.

But this is "too much to swallow," and it remains a mystery to every Seventh‑day Adventist, Jehovah's Witness, Campbellite, Methodist, Presbyterian, Catholic, Buddhist, Lutheran, Mohammedan, Episcopalian, Hindu, and Greek Orthodox on the face of the earth---for [all these groups reject the revelation of Jesus Christ]{.class_4257} (Rev. 1:1)[ as being a record of future events. ]{.class_4257}Without an understanding of Daniel's Seventieth Week (making 490 years), any doctrine in the Bible can be perverted by misplacing the verses dealing with that period; hence, baptismal regeneration, golden‑rule, covenant sprinkling, Roman priesthood, confessional, oil healing, "enduring to the end," "living it," eating "Mass," "Lord's prayer," the Sermon on the Mount, "brotherhood of man," and every other anti‑Christian perversion of the Holy Bible.

Four hundred and ninety, when studied, will straighten out a lot of doctrines that are otherwise obscured, for 490 is the key to the correct placing of the book of Matthew, the book of Acts, and the book of Hebrews. These are the books that all amillennialists and postmillennialists use to teach false doctrine on the above mentioned "anti‑Christian perversions." Only a premillennial system, which takes into account Daniel's "Seventieth Week" (70 times 7---Dan. 2), will ever enable the Christian to avoid the pitfalls into which these other systems plunge.

One is reminded of the chaplain's famous headlong dive into a cellar during a bombardment (Chaplain Engels---Bastonge Bulge, Dec. 23, 1944). It would not have been inappropriate had not the chaplain been in the possession of a bottle of hootch, and as he fell down the stairs he gave out with the battlecry of the Infantry school, "Follow me!" Wrong man, wrong direction, wrong com­pany, and wrong example. The only thing right about it [was a motto that he had stolen from someone else. ]{.class_4257}Thus, Rome and New York and Chicago steal the Christian's book and ruin him with it.

18:23 Therefore is the kingdom of heaven likened unto a certain king, which would take account of his servants.

24 And when he had begun to reckon, one was brought []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_337}unto him, which owed him ten thousand talents.

25 But forasmuch as he had not to pay, his lord commanded him to be sold, and his wife, and children, and all that he had, and payment to be made.

26 The servant therefore fell down, and worshipped him, saying, Lord, have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.

27 Then the lord of that servant was moved with compassion, and loosed him, and forgave him the debt.

["The kingdom of heaven" ]{.class_4258}again plainly locates the subject matter. This time it is not given as a "mystery." Until here the parables have been of a mysterious nature, but this one has a very plain teaching. It can be summed up in a line or two and has already been stated doctrinally in Matthew 6:14--15. The element of forgiveness, [conditional on works, ]{.class_4257}is clearly stated. The ["king"]{.class_4258} is God; the ["SERVANTS"]{.class_4258} are those of Matthew 25:14--26. The servants cannot be forced into the Church Age, [doctrinally, ]{.class_4257}for in Matthew 25:26--30, one of them loses his salvation. Here in Matthew 18, the forgiveness is [conditional, ]{.class_4257}not on the Blood Atonement of Christ, but on the attitude toward their fellowman, exactly as in Matthew 25:30--44!

Notice how closely all this parallels the "Sermon on the Mount." [Hence, Matthew is the favorite gospel for the Christ‑rejecting Modernist and the Bible‑perverting Catholic.]{.class_4257} Both hold the book in reverence as a standard textbook of New Testament church practice for the Christian, [whereas none of the verses in the context have anything to do with the church or the Christian; all are spoken to Old Testament Jews under the Law, awaiting a visible, physical, earthly kingdom ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 3:2, 4:17).

This peculiar admixture of [grace and works ]{.class_4257}has been mentioned before, and there is no use running another page on it. Only the premillennial system can correctly place the verses so they do not contradict the Pauline Epistles. Three classes of people ignore this truth and go ahead, anyway, with full confidence in the bottle‑toting Chaplain mentioned above.

Catholics: they leave it and let the Bible contradict itself and thus drive their communicants [to them ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_338}as the authoritative resolver of an insoluble mess. Modernists: they reject the Pauline grace passages and accept Matthew as the "plan of salvation." Fundamentalists: they insist on putting the passages in with Paul and, thereby, spiritualize the passages, heeding only the inspirational lesson, while [ignoring sound doctrine]{.class_4257} (2Tim 4:1‑6).

18:28 But the same servant went out, and found one of his fellowservants, which owed him an hundred pence: and he laid hands on him, and took him by the throat, saying, Pay me that thou owest.

29 And his fellowservant fell down at his feet, and besought him, saying, Have patience with me, and I will pay thee all.

30 And he would not: but went and cast him into prison, till he should pay the debt.

31 So when his fellowservants saw what was done, they were very sorry, and came and told unto their lord all that was done.

The ["pence"]{.class_4258} of verse 28 is translated "Denari," which reminds us again that the [RSV ]{.class_4257}(as the [RV ]{.class_4257}and [ASV]{.class_4257}) is always particularly careful to give us the correct Greek in a nonessential matter [in order to point out the discrepancies in the AV, ]{.class_4257}but where doctrine is at stake (as in this chapter---18:7, 11), [entire verses are omitted without authority, ]{.class_4257}and changes in doctrine are made with no Greek authority whatsoever.

The joke about all this is that the [RSV ]{.class_4257}occasionally goes to sleep and even violates its own rulings. For example, this word ["pence"]{.class_4258} in Matthew 22:19 [is left untranslated in the RSV! ]{.class_4257}Strange inconsistencies for a professed "scholarly translation!"

The teaching of the parable needs no explanation up to here, as it is plain that a man owed money that he couldn't pay. He begged for a "credit loan" to extend payments. He got it and then refused to give it to someone who owed him. The "debtor's prison" of medieval times is found in verse 30.

18:32 Then his lord, after that he had called him, said []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_339}unto him, O thou wicked servant, I forgave thee all that debt, because thou desiredst me:

33 Shouldest not thou also have had compassion on thy fellowservant, even as I had pity on thee?

34 And his lord was wroth, and delivered him to the tormentors, till he should pay all that was due unto him.

35 So likewise shall my heavenly Father do also unto you, if ye from your hearts forgive not every one his brother their trespasses.

Practically, the teaching is so lucid that it is self­-explanatory (Luke 6:31, 36--37).

The Roman hierarchy has always taught the doctrine of [purgatory after death ]{.class_4257}from the text before us (Matt. 18:32--35). \[The Holiness groups have always taught "losing salvation" from the parable in Matt. 25:1--10, and the Baptists and Methodists and Episcopalians and Presbyterians have been guilty of "pie on the earth bye‑and‑bye" from the parable of Matt. 13:31--33.\]

But the parable of Matthew 18 is to Old Testament Jews. The ["brother,"]{.class_4258} where it occurs (as in Matt. 5:22, 7:11, 12:49), is always applied to [pre-crucifixion, Jewish disciples. ]{.class_4257}Now, if you are going to lift these verses over into the Church Age for a spiritual application, then you are going to have to be doubly careful in your reading, or you are going to mess up a dozen other verses. The verses can be applied [doctrinally ]{.class_4257}to any Jew before the crucifixion and to any [believer in the Tribulation, ]{.class_4257}but if they are going to be set down in the church---where they are usually placed---then ROME is going to step in, and surely enough, she does! Note verses 34--35. Who are the tormentors? (Rev. 9 can answer it if put in the tribulation.) How much is due, and who can pay it if Christ didn't pay it? (See Rom. 5.) The Roman answer is: "The born‑again believer in Christ is not born‑again enough to quite make it, if he has a few 'unpaid for' sins in his life. So he goes to 'Purgatory' [(no such word in Greek!) ]{.class_4257}and burns an appreciable length of time until he has had enough 'condensed hell' to grant him entrance into heaven." This hair‑brain theory is erected on the ruins of Platonic scholarship which reasons that man is not [good enough to make it ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 3), but [not quite bad enough to burn forever! ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw1985.xhtml_page_340}Hence, a "middle state" is made up for the "bad‑good man" to go to, and since everyone is "good‑bad or bad‑good" (according to Plato), then all go to Purgatory to "get rid of the bad."

Plato (422--347 B.C.) and his Roman constituents have overlooked several plain facts:

1\. The passage in 18:34 is not in a "life after death" setting.

2\. The ["tormentors"]{.class_4258} are ["tormentors,"]{.class_4258} not ["the torments."]{.class_4257}

3\. Neither purgatory nor Hell are mentioned in the parable, while a [kingdom parable never ]{.class_4257}fails [to mention the burning when it is there ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 13:42, 50).

4\. ["All that was due"]{.class_4258} was paid by the Lord Jesus at Calvary (Acts 13:38--40; Gal. 3:13; Col. 2:14; Heb. 1:3). The last reference (Heb. 1:3, as well as Heb. 9:14) makes a shambles of the Roman system, for here, as in all places, the word ["purge"]{.class_4258} always is associated with "purging by blood." One immediately recognizes the double perver­sion. Not only has Rome put the believer back under Old Testament Law on [cleansing by water ]{.class_4257}(Heb. 9:13; Acts 22:16), but he has invented a new "presto-chango cleanser" by making [fire ]{.class_4257}a "cleaning agent" (see Num. 31:23).

[Works ]{.class_4257}are purified by fire at the Judgment Seat of Christ (1 Cor. 3), and [objects ]{.class_4257}are purified by fire in the Old Testament (Isa. 6:1--6), but no where in any Bible in print, in any land, in any century, has anyone ever read [that fire (after death) purifies anybody from sin. ]{.class_4257}This is a psychi­atric subject.

The doctrine breaks down at every point of applica­tion and forces the reader to change the Greek and English to prove Rome's theological point. There is NO theological point unless the verse is handled [historically ]{.class_4257}to an OLD Testament Jew, [doctrinally ]{.class_4257}to a Tribulation saint, and [inspirationally ]{.class_4257}to a New Testament believer.

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml}

# Chapter 19 {.heading_84-0}

19:1 And it came to pass, that when Jesus had finished these sayings, he departed from Galilee, and came into the coasts of Judaea beyond Jordan;

2 And great multitudes followed him; and he healed them there.

3 The Pharisees also came unto him, tempting him, and saying unto him, Is it lawful for a man to put away his wife for every cause?

4 And he answered and said unto them, Have ye not read, that he which made them at the beginning made them male and female,

5 And said, For this cause shall a man leave father and mother, and shall cleave to his wife: and they twain shall be one flesh?

6 Wherefore they are no more twain, but one flesh. What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder.

The Lord is now moving south and coming "down the home stretch," so to speak, and to His final Passover journey. Notice that, again, the Pharisees' reaction to miracles is a [doctrinal argument ]{.class_4257}that ignores the miracle (cf. 19:2--3 with 15:37, 16:1, and 14:36--15:1).

["Is it lawful for a man\...every cause?" ]{.class_4258}The quota­tion is from Deuteronomy 24:1--3. To understand what follows, the question must first be understood. This would seem self‑evident, but strangely enough, all the men who teach that there are [no grounds for divorce and remarriage ]{.class_4257}adopt the singularly peculiar idea that Matthew 19:3, 7 refers to [an engaged couple who have not yet married! ]{.class_4257}Why this fanciful notion is indulged in is unexplainable, unless it be that the commentators have failed to read the question of verse 3---["for every cause."]{.class_4258} The citation from Deuteronomy 24:1 is[ "hath found SOME UNCLEAN­NESS in her," ]{.class_4258}not "When he has found that some other man has had an affair with her!" Furthermore, the issue could not possibly be this, as our Lord's answer in verse 9 []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_342}[condones the putting away on those grounds. ]{.class_4257}Here we have a very funny situation in the Fundamentalist camp now, with good reason for the Modernist to pun "funny­mentalist," for the one‑marriage adherents put verses 7, 9 into the Old Testament Jewish setting of Deuteronomy 24, but place verses 3, 6 into a New Testament setting!

To paraphrase it, the teaching is: "Since God allows only one marriage to anyone (19:4--6), except in case of death (Rom. 7:1--4), then a Jew had to marry his betrothed (Matt. 19:7--8) unless he found that she was [not a virgin ]{.class_4257}(19:9)." Moses gave temporary allowance for the Old Testament dispensation. This teaching (accompanied by reams of notes from the "original language") fails to measure up to the Bible for three glaring reasons:

1\. The question is not "can a man put away his wife for fornication? " The question is "can he for [any reason?" ]{.class_4257}(["Every cause?"]{.class_4258}) In plainer words, if he doesn't like her (see Deut. 24:1--2), can he get rid of her? To make the words ["EVERY cause"]{.class_4258} refer to "fornication" is to misread what anyone can read. ["Every,"]{.class_4258} my friend, is not [one specific sin.]{.class_4257}

2\. ["Twain shall be one flesh"]{.class_4258} clearly indicates that the man and wife relationship is a [physical relationship which has taken place.]{.class_4257} To make the ["wife,"]{.class_4258} therefore, of verse 3 refer to an [engaged fiancee ]{.class_4257}(like Mary in Matt. 1) is to ignore what Jesus said! ["They twain shall be one flesh."]{.class_4258} If there is any doubt that the context is discussing a married woman who has already had relations with her husband, look at 1 Corinthians 6:16 where the marriage vow is[ used of an act of fornication. ]{.class_4257}(This brings an entirely new aspect into the picture which is completely hidden to the rejectors of the [AV]{.class_4257}, nor will their Hebrew and Greek lexicons give them any light on it.)

The third glaring reason, which overthrows [all ]{.class_4257}current interpretations of the passage, is found in the following:

19:7 They say unto him, Why did Moses then command to give a writing of divorcement, and to put her away?

8 He saith unto them, Moses because of the hardness of your hearts suffered you to put away your wives: but from the beginning it was not so.

9 And I say unto you, Whosoever shall put away his wife, except it be for fornication, and shall marry another, committeth adultery: and whoso marrieth her which is put away doth commit adultery.

3\. This places the reader face‑to‑face with the fact that [fornication is grounds for re‑marriage, ]{.class_4257}for lo and behold, the rejected ["wife"]{.class_4258} of Deuteronomy 24:1 is given the privilege of going and [becoming the wife of another man while the previous husband is still "living"!! ]{.class_4257}It is objected, "but this is Moses' stipulation under the law"---verse 8. [Wrong again. ]{.class_4257}It is Jesus Christ's doctrine in the next verse---verse 9!

["Fornication,"]{.class_4258} then, becomes grounds for divorce and remarriage, although the remarriage is cautioned against in 1 Corinthians 7:27--28. The fact that Adam and Eve's marriage (an ideal marriage before sin or depravity) represented Christ and the Church and was the proper intent of God, [before the Fall, ]{.class_4257}in no way alters the fact that under [law ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 19:7--8) and under [grace ]{.class_4257}(1 Cor. 7:8--16) permission is granted under "extenuating circumstances." Undoubtedly, one of the most carnal, selfish, and man‑glorifying things that fundamental expositors ever did was to accuse less fortunate Christians of ["living in adultery,"]{.class_4257} because they remarried under occasion. [No Christian ever lived "in adultery" or ever "lived in sin." ]{.class_4257}Every Christian lives ["in Christ." ]{.class_4258}He is exhorted that in view of this fact, he should ["walk\...after the Spirit." ]{.class_4258}["Living in sin" is ]{.class_4257}an expression the Bible reserves only for the unconverted. (Fundamental expositors have fallen into the heresy of applying it to a Christian, by running to the Greek in 1 John 3:9 to explain something already explained in the English in John 3:6--7.)

I heard a famous preacher once explain bombastically, "If you have made a failure of marriage, no matter what else you may have succeeded in, you have made a failure of life!" God had been merciful to this man and taken home his first partner, who was not suited for his ministry, and given him a second wife who saw eye‑to‑eye with him. I thought to myself, "Look at that poor fellow taking credit for the grace of God!"

The Bible clearly lists [three grounds of divorce: ]{.class_4257}no more and no []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_344}less.

1\. DEATH: This is abundantly clear to all in Romans 7:1--4; 1 Corinthians 7:39.

2\. FORNICATION: This is clear to 75 percent in Matthew 19:9; 1 Corinthians 6:16.

3\. DESERTION: This is clear to about 20 percent in 1 Corinthians 7:15. In this case the unbeliever deserts the believer, and the believer is said to be ["not under bondage" ]{.class_4258}(cf. 1 Cor. 7:27) in the context.

What the commentators have missed (Rome in the lead as usual!) is that fornication, death, and desertion [are ]{.class_4257}"divorces." And herein lies the key to the whole situation. In the Bible, "a marriage" and a "divorce" are where flesh [joins ]{.class_4257}flesh or flesh [leaves ]{.class_4257}flesh. (Note the entire context of Matt. 19:7--12 and the entire context of Eph. 5:23--33. Note also that marriage is always a "flesh" business and is never a "ceremony.") The preacher and priest stumble at the stumbling‑stone and assume because [they ]{.class_4257}are involved in the "ceremony" that the [ceremony is the marriage! ]{.class_4257}It is only a step from this blunder into a whole swamp full of theological errors, and the first one that shows up is the mistaken notion that a divorce [decree ]{.class_4257}is a "DIVORCE!" And thus, the "one‑marriage" devotees alike, divest the word of God of all its authority by making a "SACRAMENT" of [marriage ]{.class_4257}and a "ritual" of [divorce; ]{.class_4257}whereas, in God's sight, [neither one is anything of the kind!]{.class_4257}

None have noticed that ["the husband"]{.class_4258} of Romans 7:1--4 is not some man a woman [was ]{.class_4257}married to, but the man who is true to her [now; ]{.class_4257}observe ["hath" ]{.class_4257}present tense (AV!!), not ["had." ]{.class_4257}To ignore this distinction is to teach that a man or woman can [have ]{.class_4257}(present tense) more than one husband (which is denied in John 4:16--18), and it further [protects all people who lived immorally or who are living immorally, as long as they do not get a divorce "decree" or another marriage license! ]{.class_4257}Thus, 1 Timothy 3:2 (in the [RSV]{.class_4257}) is changed to protect an immoral bishop. It reads, "having MARRIED only once"---referring to the license and ceremony! The [AV ]{.class_4257}1611, preserving the accurate Greek of all ancient manuscripts, reads ["husband of one wife," ]{.class_4258}indicating he must be true to [his present wife, as any previous immoral life would already provide him with as many wives as acts of fornication! ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_345}Thus, men make of none effect the word of God. They do it by tradition, and "many other like things" do they! If the reading ["married only once"]{.class_4257} is[ ]{.class_4257}adopted, then no man can qualify for bishop if he has [ever had more than one affair, ]{.class_4257}for in God's sight, the "marriage" is [not ]{.class_4257}"the ceremony." \[Think about that awhile!\]

The Bible teaches that in essence a "MARRIAGE" is where flesh joins flesh and a divorce is where flesh leaves flesh. This is completely contrary to all teaching on the subject, and only goes to prove what has already been observed---that the Bible is still (in the twentieth century) the most radical book in print. Darwin, Marx, Freud, Huxley, and Spillane have a long way to go.

19:10 His disciples say unto him, If the case of the man be so with his wife, it is not good to marry.

11 But he said unto them, All men cannot receive this saying, save they to whom it is given.

12 For there are some eunuchs, which were so born from their mother's womb: and there are some eunuchs, which were made eunuchs of men: and there be eunuchs, which have made themselves eunuchs for the kingdom of heaven's sake. He that is able to receive it, let him receive it.

In the context, we are still driven to recognize that marriage is a matter of [flesh, ]{.class_4257}for all that follows is a discussion of [physical ability to have children. ]{.class_4257}The eunuch is a man who cannot have children. There is no getting around this, for the eunuch is (1) born, (2) made by mutilation, or (3) made by self‑control. The mutilation and the "birth" give the definition of the word away. It settles forever that marriage is a [physical relationship. ]{.class_4257}Someone could say, "Well, the one that is a eunuch for the kingdom's sake just [refuses to get married."]{.class_4257} But the attempt to re‑define marriage into the ritualistic‑sacramental setting is vain, for the other two eunuchs [can get married yet still they cannot have children. ]{.class_4257}Married or unmarried, a eunuch is a [eunuch.]{.class_4257}

Our Lord's answer, therefore, ["All men cannot receive this saying" ]{.class_4258}is the answer to ["it is not good to marry." ]{.class_4258}(Notice vss. 10--11.) The marriage, there­fore, is not a [ceremony ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_346}that a man can take or leave. [It is flesh joining flesh.]{.class_4257}

To repeat: A eunuch can be [born. ]{.class_4257}Therefore, He is not saying that the eunuch is born [to remain unmarried; ]{.class_4257}he is born with the inability to have children in the fleshy relationship. That much is apparent to anyone. A eunuch can be "made a eunuch of men." Therefore, He is not saying that the eunuch is rendered so that he cannot go through a marriage ceremony; any eunuch can do that! It is that he has been mutilated so that he cannot have children in the fleshy relationship. A eunuch can make himself a eunuch; here, it can be the case of a vasectomy, rendering him physically unable to have children, or it can be that he abstains from the [act or the ceremony of marriage, ]{.class_4257}as Paul (1 Cor. 9:1--5). This last case given is the reason why the other "two eunuchs" are thought to be merely "unmarried men"; whereas in both cases, they are [physically incapacitated men. ]{.class_4257}Thus, it is brought home again and again and again that [marriage is a physical transaction.]{.class_4257}

There are no "ceremonies" in the Bible. The immedi­ate reaction to this is: "But what about the law requiring, etc.," and "do you mean to tell me that common law marriage is, etc." Since most of our Bible commentators and scholars are totally ignorant of the answer to these questions (also found in the plain English Bible), it will be useless to waste paper trying to help them out. In the Old Testament, a feast is given, and upon the conclusion of the feast, the father takes the bride to the bridegroom's house and leaves her there. That is it! \[The English "wedlock," of course, is from the Anglo‑Saxon "wed," to pledge, and "lac," a gift. Hence, "giving away the bride." "To tie the knot" comes from the ancient custom of tying the two robe‑sleeves of the bride and groom together as they pledged their "troth" to each other. "Getting hitched" and similar expressions are found, but in the final analysis, the marriage takes place in God's sight [before or after the ceremony. ]{.class_4257}You cannot make a "ceremony" a real "marriage" unless you are a Bible‑rejecting "Catholic."\]

Notice that "engagements" are not the context of Matthew 19. The eunuch of verse 12 is not involved in "engagements," real or unreal. To make the passage in verses 8 and 9 refer to an "engaged" wife (like Mary in Matt. 1) is to pull the old "text‑without‑a‑context" []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_347}business, and of course, this business (as all the devil's business) is practised to prove a lie! For example: ["The tabernacles of robbers prosper" ]{.class_4258}is a lie, but it is [Scripture, ]{.class_4257}which is said to be inspired by the Holy Spirit (2 Tim. 3:16). Ignorance of the Scripture and refusal to study the Scripture result in this type of confusion; and to the mind of the non‑Bible believer, there is no answer except those patented answers handed down to him by his religious leaders, such as: "Oh well, it is probably figurative," "Well, you can't take it all literally," "Well, there is much of the Bible we can't understand," "Why God wouldn't condemn the heathen for not understanding when there are so many knotty problems in the Bible," and so forth and so on. ["The tabernacles of robbers prosper"]{.class_4258} is in Job 12, but like all texts without a context, it teaches nothing but a lie, until you arouse yourself out of your fishing rods, shotguns, TV sets, golf clubs, flower clubs, sewing circles, and literary guilds and read the context to find out what it really does mean.

["He that is able to receive it\...." ]{.class_4258}The closing of the passage is fitting. Our Lord says in effect, "if you are able to stay away from women, do it." This is repeated in 1 Corinthians 7:1­--4. Notice that not all men are [able to do it]{.class_4257} (1 Cor. 7:7), and Jesus recognizes this by saying, ["All men cannot receive this saying." ]{.class_4258}The ["saying"]{.class_4258} that He says not all can receive is the saying of the preceding verse in Matthew 19:10, ["it is not good to marry." ]{.class_4258}It is good [not to marry ]{.class_4257}if you can avoid it; nevertheless, ["a prudent wife is from the ]{.class_4258}[Lord]{.class_4267}[," ]{.class_4258}and ["whoso findeth a wife findeth a good thing" ]{.class_4258}(see Prov. 19:14, and 18:22).

Matthew 19:12, by the way, is the prooftext used by the Roman "Church" to prove that the celibacy rules regarding the priesthood are "Scriptural." This needs some examination, as do all "doctrines" of Rome. \[See publication: [Rome---The Great Private Interpreter,]{.class_4257} 1969.\]

To begin with, it will be noted that celibacy was never the LAW or RULE of the early church. Peter was married (Matt. 8:14); Paul said that he himself had a right to marry (1 Cor. 9:1‑6)! Ezekiel, Moses, Samuel, and David were all married. The council of Ancrya (314) (see Canon 10, Bruns. 1, 68) requires deacons to be married, and this, of course, is the exact teaching of the New Testament passages (1 Tim. 3) also given for [a bishop. ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_348}(Notice 1 Tim. 3:1--3.) \[The EASTERN branch of the church retains this Scriptural Christian position right on through to A.D. 629 (Quinisext Council, Canons 6, 12, 13, 18, Bruns. 1, 39 ff).\]

The Western Branch, however (Rome!), goes into apostasy almost immediately and, as usual, [quotes Scrip­ture to justify the apostasy. ]{.class_4257}Instead of going by that part of the Bible devoted to the requirements for office of deacons and bishops (clearly given in detail in 1 Tim. 3), they turned, of all places, to 1 Corinthians 7:29(!) to prove that if any of the clergy were married they should immediately refrain from natural relations with their wives, and that no more "priests" should take "holy" orders as married men. Leo the Great approved, author­ized, and explained this pagan departure from the New Testament (in Ep. 14, Ad Anastasium, Ep. 167, Ad Rusticum). From here on, the Roman Church enforces its anti‑Christian, man‑made dogma (see 1 Tim. 4:1­--3) into place to supersede the doctrines of divine inspiration. This forcing is called ["seducing spirits, and doctrines of devils" ]{.class_4258}in 1 Timothy 4:1--6. ["Forbidding to marry" ]{.class_4258}(1 Tim. 4) is not a Roman Catholic "Holy Order"; it is a [doctrine of a devil.]{.class_4257}

19:13 Then were there brought unto him little children, that he should put his hands on them, and pray: and the disciples rebuked them.

14 But Jesus said, Suffer little children, and forbid them not, to come unto me: for of such is the kingdom of heaven.

15 And he laid his hands on them, and departed thence.

The passages have been commented on at some length under Matthew 18:2--6. It is remarkable that the disciples could make the same mistake of resenting the children's presence so quickly after the Lord had rebuked them, for it (the time element between 18:1--2 and 19:13) has been less than three months.

The word ["suffer"]{.class_4258} is defined (in the [AV]{.class_4257}) in Acts 21:39, so there is no need of a new translation, except for the ignorant among the seminarians who have difficulty with words. ["Of such is the kingdom of heaven" ]{.class_4258}again tells the reader that the doctrinal import of the passages is [not ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_349}little children "getting into heaven by sprinkling." The ["little children"]{.class_4258} make it to heaven by election on an automatic atonement (Rom. 4:15, 5:13, 9:11) and, thank God, do not have to worry about some rascal in a Halloween suit coming around mumbling Latin and dripping water all over them! ["To come unto me" ]{.class_4258}means what it says and has nothing in common with ["come unto us" ]{.class_4257}(meaning Reformed Christian) or ["come unto church."]{.class_4257} It is funny what a terrible time self‑righteous people have with plain English in an [AV]{.class_4257}!

19:16 And, behold, one came and said unto him, Good Master, what good thing shall I do, that I may have eternal life?

17 And he said unto him, Why callest thou me good? there is none good but one, that is, God: but if thou wilt enter into life, keep the commandments.

18 He saith unto him, Which? Jesus said, Thou shalt do no murder, Thou shalt not commit adultery, Thou shalt not steal, Thou shalt not bear false witness,

19 Honour thy father and thy mother: and, Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself.

What follows is one of the most tremendous studies on religious psychology and human nature found anywhere in literature. (It is so tremendous you might know that the "new translations" would have to change it to cover up what it reveals.)

The reader will observe that the young ruler is asking: ["What good thing shall I do?" ]{.class_4258}His question is not philosophical; it [is practical. ]{.class_4257}It does not take a preacher or priest to tell that the man wants to get eternal life and wants to know what to do to get it. (Since all men want life, before money, food, clothes, or even comfort, the question is a natural, common sense question.) Now Westcott and Hort (1885) substitute their "version" for the Textus Receptus at this point. Where Jesus answers: ["Why callest thou me good?" ]{.class_4258}(which he [did; ]{.class_4257}see verse 16, ["Good Master"]{.class_4258}), Westcott and Hort change the "original Greek" and insert: ["Why askest thou me concerning the good?"]{.class_4257}

No Bible-believing Christian could fail to grasp the peculiar []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_350}jarring effect this has on the dialogue. Nothing could sound more [un‑Christlike ]{.class_4257}than this answer. "Con­cerning the good" is not a subject any Bible-believing Jew ever discussed or a Bible-believing Christian ever discussed. The Bible definitions of good and evil are so clear (and so often and thoroughly repeated) that no Christian would even consider the question worth contemplation. However, there is a class of people who [would ]{.class_4257}consider "the good" a "weighty problem," and these are those peculiar folk who [insist that all truth is relative ]{.class_4257}and that, therefore, [every moral problem is "complex."]{.class_4257} Only[ ]{.class_4257}two people fit that description: [Greek philosophers ]{.class_4257}and [Catholic "scholastics."]{.class_4257} This is their "meat and potatoes." If you doubt this, read Plato and Aristotle, and Aquinas and Loyola, and all doubt will vanish like vapor on glass.

No one in Matthew 19:15--19 is discussing "the good" or "what is the good," and no one has asked about the "good" or "what is good." The question is: ["Good Master, what GOOD THING SHALL I DO, that I may have eternal life?" ]{.class_4258}The Westcott and Hort text (which is Vaticanus in the main) answers with a gnostic depravation that is completely out of harmony and touch with the setting, context, question, and place of the narrative.

Now, approaching the problem critically, we find that the text yields the answer to the flood of "new versions," for all these versions are Westcott and Hort's Vatican‑text (Protestant or Catholic). This text is built on the idea that Matthew CHANGED the original Greek and inserted the [Authorized Version]{.class_4257} reading to "avoid theological difficulties" (see Allen, [Gospel According to Matthew]{.class_4257}, Scribners, 1907, p. 208).

Origen (240), Eusebius (313), Jerome (340), and Augustine (354) favor this "philosophical" perversion of the text. All [are amillennial or postmillennial "Catho­]{.class_4257}lics." Grab that. Don't let it get away!

["Why askest thou me concerning the good" ]{.class_4257}is found in Vaticanus, the Old Latin, some Old Syriac, and Sinaiticus; its appearance [here ]{.class_4257}is an ominous warning (unheeded by scholars) that the ["philosophy," ]{.class_4258}which Colossians 2 speaks against, is in charge of the men who handled the Hesychian (Egyptian type) text [from which all the new Bibles come. A ]{.class_4257}Socratic dialogue yielding up a stale crumb of Greek philosophy (see Hills, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_351}[King James Bible Defended]{.class_4257}) has nothing in common with what Jesus actually [did ]{.class_4257}say to the rich young ruler. The correct reading is undoubtedly the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 Version, exactly as you find it on the dime‑store counter, and the spurious interpolation of Westcott and Hort found in the [ASV, RV, RSV ]{.class_4257}and other Catholic translations is as far from the truth as you could go without offering up your rooster with Socrates (469--399 B.C.) and committing suicide with him!

The passage is far from exhausted. Moffatt steps in with his butcher knife (a dull one at that!) and dissects ["God"]{.class_4258} out of Matthew 19:17. From where here did he get this? Westcott and Hort! Look at their text. Having reduced ["God"]{.class_4258} to a neuter theological discussion, they take their place with the famous "it" people, who say: ["Christianity is a new reality. A new state of things has appeared. It still appears. It is hidden and invisible. It is there and it is here. Accept it. Enter into it. Let it grasp you!!" ]{.class_4257}(See Tillich, [Time]{.class_4257}, March 16, 1959.)

This "it" religion, in the Holiness groups, comes out with the reduction of the third ["Person" ]{.class_4257}of the Trinity to ["have you got it?" ]{.class_4257}In the Catholic, it reduces the [second Person of the Trinity to a piece of bread! ]{.class_4257}Thus, we see that scholarship, philosophy, Catholicism, emotionalism, and humanism are terrifically [simplified systems; ]{.class_4257}and all the pretense to their "complexity" is just like Westcott and Hort trying to mess up the Bible. For when reduced to what they really ["believe," they all ]{.class_4257}(without exception)[ believe that a great neuter "it" is the supreme God. ]{.class_4257}Thus, with Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, Mohammedanism, Brahmanism, Shintoism, Protestantism, Judaism, and others, [all believe a "thing" or "things" can save them. ]{.class_4257}All[ ]{.class_4257}go religiously, nay, "fanatically," about the business of getting "it" or praying to "it" or "doing it," or "believing in it," and [all without exception reject a living Saviour who is a Person, not a piece of bread or a "faith" or a "church."]{.class_4257}

"The men" have been divided from "the boys."

The Westcott and Hort reading of Matthew 19:17 betrays their true position. Like a machine-gunner laying down a trajectory of tracers, the "authoritative text" of Westcott and Hort can be traced to its nest; [it is an unholy, un‑Christian, ungodly, pagan text. ]{.class_4257}Its []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_352}line of fire is laid down to cut Bible-believing Christianity to the ground.

Let us go look into the nest and see what mother eagle has hatched all these pagan speculations. Here he is! Our old friend "Origen!" For, lo and behold, Origen says (A.D. 254---Berlin, [Origenes Werke]{.class_4257}, Vol. 10, Streeter, Four Gospels, p. 123) that Romans 13:8--10 is the complement of the text in Matthew 18, and then begins to "reconcile" the two passages!

Getting back to "mamma eagle"---Origen reasoned that (now read carefully!) if Romans 13 says that the [whole law ]{.class_4257}is summed up in "love your neighbor as yourself" (Rom 13:8, 10), then Jesus could not have said, ["love thy neighbour" ]{.class_4258}in Matthew 19:19. It stands to "reason" (hello, Socrates!) that if He had said this, then the ruler was [already perfect, ]{.class_4257}for he says in verse 20 that he has [kept these commandments. ]{.class_4257}On the basis of this "authoritative reasoning," Origen [changes verse 17 and verse 19. ]{.class_4257}Origen, in Alexandria, Egypt, writes the Hesychi­an, Egyptian‑type text in all his "corrections" of the original "Textus Receptus." He takes half of Matthew 19:19 out and alters Matthew 19:17 to call your attention to the tremendous importance of theologically discussing the great neuter ["thing." "What good THING shall I do, that I may have eternal life?" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 19:16).

Thus, we find the Greek philosophical system thrusting itself into the body of Scripture by Origen at Alexandria (North Africa), taking a good hold under Augustine and Cyprian (North Africa), and coming boldly out (dragging the Scripture with it) in Jerome and Pope Gregory ([Latin church from North Africa]{.class_4257}): black‑robed priests, Platonic scholarship, Easter bunnies, wedding rings, fire purges, sprinkling babies, rosaries, "holy water," "candles," and the works! Purgatory, as the great "it" or "thing," is a perfect example of a reasonable solution to a reasonable question, which a reasonable man would "reason out" from his "reason." Unfortunately, it has nothing to do with the Bible, Christianity, the Revelation of God, the Truth of God, Salvation, Regeneration, or Correct Doctrine. It is just Origen again trying to "figure [things ]{.class_4257}out."

Now that we have cleared the air of the stink and stench of North African Greek "scholarship," let us again examine the text and learn []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_353}from the Holy Spirit what God intended us to learn, without the aid of Origen, Plato, Socrates, Augustine, Aquinas, Aristotle, Jerome, and similar deluded pagans.

One: Men think they can [do something ]{.class_4257}to get eternal life (notice 19:16); the emphasis is on the [do,]{.class_4257} as in Acts 16:31. The question is naturally appropriate. There is something a man [must do ]{.class_4257}before the crucifixion (see Matt. 5:28--31, 7:24, 16:24, ete.). There is something he [must do ]{.class_4257}in the coming tribulation (see Rev. 22:14, 14:12, 12:17). There is something he [must do ]{.class_4257}in the kingdom age (Zech. 14; Matt. 5--7). In this age there is something he must [not ]{.class_4257}do (Rom. 4:5; Gal. 5:4); there is [Someone on whom he must believe ]{.class_4257}(Rom .10:9--13)!

This places the rich young ruler in a choice position, for he is [confronted with that Someone on whom he is to believe. ]{.class_4257}The transposition is that in [this age ]{.class_4257}(pre‑crucifixion), he cannot prove that he [believes ]{.class_4257}without obeying the commandment he is about to get (Luke 6:46), and the commandment he is about to get (Matt. 19:21) is [most certainly not the New Testament gospel of salvation by grace through faith! ]{.class_4257}Read it.

Two: The young ruler is blessed, however, by an answer from Jesus that would give him the clue to post‑resurrection salvation, for the Lord answers: "What are you buttering ME up for? If I am not God, I am no good." And herein we see Origen's (and Westcott and Hort's) objection to the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 text. As Christ states the question in 19:17, He puts the ruler in a position where he either has to admit that Christ is a human man (and, therefore, sinful and fallible as all men) or else admit that [Christ is God. ]{.class_4257}This accounts for the hatred of Westcott and Hort ([ASV, RV]{.class_4257}, and [RSV]{.class_4257}) regarding the text. They would like to do what the rich young ruler did: pat Christ on the back and talk about how wonderful the "Word of God" is, and then in a "tight" emphatically deny the deity of either.

["There is none good but one, that is, God." ]{.class_4258}Westcott and Hort knock ["God"]{.class_4258} out of the text so the all‑important, devasting, crucial, and essential truth of the text is hidden. Moffatt and the [ASV ]{.class_4257}(1901) follow. ["None good" ]{.class_4258}means what it says (see Rom. 3:10--23; Psa. 53:1--3; Ecc. 7:20). This highly objectionable Bible truth is immediately (nay, violently!) rejected by all churches and denomina­tions who teach that man can in any way [earn ]{.class_4257}or [deserve ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_354}his salvation. Whereas the Bible supposes that in this age no man can earn heaven because he is born wrong, thousands of groups continue to teach that it doesn't make any difference; there is still enough good in him so that he can "work it out" in his natural state. Using Matthew 19 as proof, they point out the commandments that Jesus tells the man to keep, [forgetting that He is dealing with an Old Testament Jew under the Law! ]{.class_4257}(Forgetting also that Christ has [not yet made payment for sin ]{.class_4257}so[ ]{.class_4257}that a "new birth" can be appropriated. Again, everyone steps on the word of God and entrusts it to their leaders, who are blinder than they are.)

Although there is ["none good," ]{.class_4258}God still accepts [faith and the works of the law ]{.class_4257}in Matthew 19; Revelation 12; Zechariah 14; and Isaiah 66, but by doing this, the Lord deliberately blinds and confuses the ecclesiastical leaders who are looking for ["thy kingdom come."]{.class_4258} Any postmillennial or amillennial leader will be caught in a cross‑fire with landmines underneath him, for [there are passages that teach works in connection with salvation. ]{.class_4257}Having no place to put them, the postmillennial or amillennial leader [thrusts them into the Church Age and, thereby, develops a teaching that if the born‑again believer does not hold out "to the end," he can lose it. ]{.class_4257}(See additional remarks under Matt. 17:7--9.)

[God only]{.class_4257} is ["good,"]{.class_4258} in the absolute sense. All others have some element of "self" involved in the best works, and of course, the Modernist and Catholic will both deny this vigorously if pinned down. The Catholic will insist that Christ's atonement is not enough to save a man ["by grace\...through faith" ]{.class_4258}(Eph. 2:1--12), but will flee immediately to an epistle written[ "to the twelve tribes"]{.class_4258} of Israel (James 1:1) and start blowing hot air all over the room about James 2:24. The Modernist will go him one better and pick up a pre‑crucifixion passage in Luke 17:20, written to a bunch of Christ‑rejecting Pharisees, and go off spouting about "a spark of divinity in everybody," etc.

Both fail to recognize the essential truth that ["Every man at his BEST STATE is altogether vanity"]{.class_4258} (Psa. 39:5). Some people do good works to pay for sin (Catholic), some do good to appease their conscience, some do good to get recognition (Egotist), some do good because they are afraid they would go to hell if they didn't []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_355}(Catholic), some do good to atone for sins committed in youth, some do good because they found it "pays" (Peale), some do good because they think they can earn heaven (Modernist), and some do good because they are too old or sick to do bad;[ but what does this have to do with BEING good? ]{.class_4257}Nothing. In every case, the first consideration is the person considering himself first. No unregenerate heart ever grasped the truth that a born‑again Bible believer does GOOD because Christ is [inside him doing the good, ]{.class_4257}and He alone is "good" (see Gal. 2:20; Rom. 7).

The rich young ruler, then, is at least "clued in" on the possibilities confronting him.

Three: Nevertheless, the commandments are clearly put forward as part of gaining eternal life (exactly as in Matt. 5--7, 24--25, and all pre-crucifixion passages). ["Keep the commandments."]{.class_4258} (The passage reminds us of the discussion and comments on Matt. 5, which makes the passage peculiarly adaptable to self‑righteous sinners who reject the divine revelation given to Paul. These people are called "Ebionites" in church history and are characterized by paying reverence to "the words of Jesus," while rejecting [all revelation that God gave as it is related to the death of Jesus ]{.class_4257}\[see Romans, Galatians, and Ephesians\]. Such people refer in glowing terms to the "beatitudes" and the "Lord's Prayer," but when confronted with their total depravity and absolute damnation, [apart from saving grace and the new birth, ]{.class_4257}they begin to whimper about "Pauline and Johannine" theology!)

["Which?"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 19:18) is a wild stroke. For here, the ruler gives away his position just like Origen did when he messed up the text. ["Which?" ]{.class_4258}Why ["which it," ]{.class_4257}when[ ]{.class_4257}Jesus just said ["the commandments"]{.class_4258}? You can't "which" a plural, if you were commanded to keep the plural! If you "which" it, it is because [you know you are going to violate one or two, and you want to find out if Jesus will allow you those one or two! ]{.class_4257}Oh boy! Doesn't that book have [your ]{.class_4257}number!? (Someone should write a book called "Who's Which?" Everyone has six or seven they can keep and three or four they can't, and around we go and where we stop nobody knows---unless it is in Hell. A colored man said his congregation was not listed in "Who's Who" but they might be found in "Who's dat?")

Our Lord graciously overlooks the man's hypocrisy (knowing that He is going to get him later on in the conversation) and lists the ["which."]{.class_4258} It is instructive to notice that the ["which"]{.class_4258} [avoids the first and greatest commandment. ]{.class_4257}This is ominous, for it speaks plainly that before He is through, the Lord Jesus is going to suddenly confront His would‑be disciple with that very command­ment. The young ruler passes on six commandments, the sabbath commandment being taken for granted. [But three are purposely omitted, and they all deal with "money"]{.class_4257}! For, lo and behold, when the original ten are listed in Exodus 20, we find [no coveting, no idolatry ]{.class_4257}(see Col. 3:5), [and no other gods. ]{.class_4257}Jesus has read the young man's mind and purposely tested him where He knew he had already [kept the commandments, ]{.class_4257}deliberately avoiding the real ["which"]{.class_4258} that the rich boy had on his mind and heart!

There are two more "unsearchable riches" found in the [AV ]{.class_4257}in verse 18 which are obscured in the Greek of Nestle's text. The first is the fact that the command­ment to ["not kill"]{.class_4258} is interpreted by Jesus as ["murder," ]{.class_4258}thus relieving many a combat veteran from a number of complexes unjustly put on him by some religious groups (and aggravated by a double perversion that if a man dies in combat for his country, he gets an automatic pass into heaven). The second (in the same verse, Matt. 19:18) is the fact that the word ["steal" ]{.class_4258}is put to the ruler as ["defraud" ]{.class_4258}in Mark 10:19, indicating that a good many [business transactions ]{.class_4257}of rich people amount to just that---stealing[.]{.class_4257}

19:20 The young man saith unto him, All these things have I kept from my youth up: what lack I yet?

21 Jesus said unto him, If thou wilt be perfect, go and sell that thou hast, and give to the poor, and thou shalt have treasure in heaven: and come and follow me.

22 But when the young man heard that saying, he went away sorrowful: for he had great possessions.

According to the text, the rich young ruler is in the position that the apostle Paul was in before his conversion to Christ (cf. Phil 3:4--7). He was like Cornelius (Acts 10). He is as righteous as a human being can be [outside ]{.class_4257}of Christ. One might say that he was ["a doer of the law" ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_357}(Rom. 10:1--5), but this does not guarantee his salvation, for the law is said to be a ["schoolmaster to bring us to Christ" ]{.class_4258}(Gal. 3:24). If this young man is not [brought to Christ, ]{.class_4257}then the law has failed to teach him what it should (see Rom. 8:3). Here, then, we have a perfect picture of New Testament salvation given in an Old Testament setting, with a Jew in the transition period seeking righteousness (Rom. 10:1--4), and what follows illustrates Romans 9:30--33.

["What lack I yet?"]{.class_4258} Well, you lack a payment for sins. You lack a sense of guilt. You lack a knowledge of the laws you [have broken. ]{.class_4257}You lack assurance of salvation, and you lack peace of heart and mind. Isn't that enough? He didn't lack morals, money, zeal, interest, health, or common sense. Yet in Mark 10:21, our Lord says, ["ONE THING thou lackest." ]{.class_4258}Strangely enough, the ["one thing" ]{.class_4258}is found in three commandments, and [none of these commandments are remotely connected with observing the sabbath! ]{.class_4257}This is something no Seventh-day Adventist has ever considered or prayed about. Christ says he only lacks ["one thing."]{.class_4258} (In[ ]{.class_4258}the context, the crux of the argument concerns the riches in the man's personal possessions.) At no time does Christ hint, directly or indirectly, that "sabbath observance" was in anyway remotely connected with the original question on ["eternal life" ]{.class_4258}(19:16). Likewise, Paul, in Romans 13:9--12, is careful to omit the sabbath and make it plain that it has nothing to do with New Testament salvation, but who can make an Adventist get out of Matthew 5 and 1 John 2 long enough to find out what is going on?

The three commandments our Lord has omitted (till now) comprise the beginning and end of the original ten. They are 1, 2, and 10. No other gods, no idols, and no coveting. Since the Roman Church is exactly as the covetous rich man, they cut out no. 2 and break no. 10 into [two commandments ]{.class_4257}(any Catholic knows this but submits to it, even though he knows it is HERESY). All Coptic, Aramaic, Syriac, Hebrew, Samaritan, Chaldean, "LXX," and Vulgate editions have it as the [AV]{.class_4257}. Rome has "done showed its hand." [It is a rich, self‑righteous ruler seeking salvation by works. ]{.class_4257}The three commandments all center around money as another "god." And of course, idolatry is "covetousness" in Colossians 3:5, and the money []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_358}is involved in the idolatry of Acts 19:24--27, and both covetousness and idolatry place some "god" ahead of God. Therefore, the passage in Matthew 19 is loaded. No wonder Origen, Augustine, Eusebius, Jerome, and Gregory couldn't keep their hands off it! It is a divine rebuke in the strongest possible terms to every man on the earth who is seeking a religion by which to earn heaven, while harboring in his bosom what [he really loves ]{.class_4257}and would not let go for anybody.

Verse 21 finally flushes the whole covey. The game is over; rack out and cash in; [your heart ]{.class_4257}has been exposed and your religion is revealed for what it is---a gold‑plated garbage can to hide dead cats in! ["Go and SELL that thou hast, and GIVE."]{.class_4258}

The last words penetrate the religious veneer of the rich young ruler like an armor‑piercing shell. ["Sell\...give."]{.class_4258} Rich young men do not sell to [give; ]{.class_4257}they sell to [get ]{.class_4257}(Prov. 10:2, 11:4; Job 22:2). The young man sees in a second that the proposition before him is [bankruptcy; ]{.class_4257}he doesn't exactly drool at the prospect. (Jesus seems to have a habit of demanding bankruptcy from His followers---see Luke 9:57--62.) The young man of our text is suddenly confronted with a situation similar to 1929. Someone was about to cash his meager paycheck when a blind was drawn at the teller's window reading: ["Closed, the bank is busted. ]{.class_4257}" He stammered to the teller, "Busted? B‑b‑b‑bust­ed? What does you all mean 'busted'?" She said, "Well, it's busted. Haven't you ever heard of a bank [busting ]{.class_4257}before?" He replied, "Yes ma'am, I sho has, b‑b‑but ah jes never had one bust right [in mah face befoh!"]{.class_4257}

The words of verse 21 are laid out significantly: "Go, sell, give, come." Notice the contrast to the simple "Come, believe, receive, confess" of the post‑resurrection salvation (Rev. 22:17; Rom. 10:9--10; Acts 16:31; John 1:12). \[Of course, we must always contend with the Campbellite inserting John's "Jewish water baptism" of Acts 2:38 into the "plan of salvation," but really there are no intelligent Bible believers today who believe this nonsense. The Bible is clear on it (see Matt. 3:11 and comments). The verses ignored by the Campbellite are as follows: 1 Corinthians 15:8, Paul born‑again three days [before ]{.class_4257}he was baptized; Acts 10, Cornelius saved [before ]{.class_4257}he was baptized; Colos­sians 2:11--12, the saving baptism is [not ]{.class_4257}made with hands; 1 Peter 3:21, water baptism is only a figure of salvation; no []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_359}record of any apostle being baptized according to Acts 2:38; all were Jewish and baptized by John, and all went to heaven except Judas---proving [water baptism doesn't decide anything.]{.class_4257}\]

But in this pre‑crucifixion setting, it is "go, sell, give, come." The final word indicates that the ruler will do one of two things. He will either [obey ]{.class_4257}and fulfill the law (see Rom. 13:9--11), or else he will [be convicted by the law and come to Christ for salvation ]{.class_4257}(see Gal. 3:22--25). He does [neither. As ]{.class_4257}all covetous, religious people, he breaks the law and deserts the Saviour.

["He went away sorrowful: for he had great posses­sions." ]{.class_4258}The possessions, of course, are material possessions and not "treasures in heaven." Compare Luke 12:15, 21. The passage in Luke mades a good triology with Luke 16, for all three passages (Matt. 19; Luke 12; and Luke 16) deal with a rich man, as a youth---before death---after death.

The summation of the incident is explicit and concise:

1\. You need to come to Jesus to have eternal life.

2\. Jews needed to keep the law in the Old Testament to have eternal life.

3\. If you [cannot ]{.class_4257}keep this law, you will have [to look for a Messiah or Saviour.]{.class_4257}

4\. When He shows up, you [must ]{.class_4257}come to Him (John 6:37).

5\. You do [not ]{.class_4257}keep all the law, even if you kid yourself into thinking you do.

6\. The sabbath is a [ceremony, ]{.class_4257}not a moral command.

7\. Hidden sin is the real reason why men try to "keep the law" instead of coming to Christ.

8\. You can be "near the kingdom" and know every fact necessary to be saved and still be [lost.]{.class_4257}

19:23 Then said Jesus unto his disciples, Verily I say unto you, That a rich man shall hardly enter into the kingdom of heaven.

24 And again I say unto you, It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God.

25 When his disciples heard it, they were exceedingly []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_360}amazed, saying, Who then can be saved?

26 But Jesus beheld them, and said unto them, With men this is impossible; but with God all things are possible.

["That a rich man shall hardly enter." ]{.class_4258}The reason for this is apparent. Riches obscure "faith," for if the man has all that he needs, what does he have to claim in prayer [by faith? ]{.class_4257}Riches hinder humility; for since "money talks," men are quick to find out that it is "power," and why should a powerful man be humble? There is no need; he has plenty of reserve if he does have a reversal.

Furthermore, riches magnify the world, not the Lord. The lust of the eyes and the "pride of life" are obtained [with money. ]{.class_4257}When a man has plenty of it, he is not afraid to go "window shopping."

An interesting theological fact appears in the text to the careful reader---a Jew \[since he is looking for an earthly, literal, physical, visible inheritance (correctly so)\] reasons that if a man has riches, it stands to reason that the blessing of God [must ]{.class_4257}be upon him! Notice their remark in verse 25 and look at the ["exceedingly amazed."]{.class_4258} Reasoning as Job's companions, they presume that outward prosperi­ty must be certain proof of spiritual uprightness; converse­ly, disaster must mean that the recipient is under the wrath of God. This is logical reasoning, and in spite of every profession of faith, men tend naturally to [think ]{.class_4257}in this fashion. A bed‑ridden saint in Baltimore, in the tenement district east of town, told me that her biggest persecutors had been [other Christians. ]{.class_4257}This is especially true of the "divine healer" groups, for it stands to reason that if they teach [sickness is due to "lack of faith,"]{.class_4257} then there is something wrong [spiritually ]{.class_4257}with the sick person! This is the mistake Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar made in the book of Job, and it is the mistake the disciples make in John 9:2--3. It is a common mistake in 2000.

Matthew 19:24 poses another desperate problem to the rejector of the [AV]{.class_4257}. Did Christ really mean ["a camel\...through the eye of a needle"? ]{.class_4258}(Or as one spoonerist said: "Easier for a camel to go through the knee of an idol!") Didn't He mean a ["gate," ]{.class_4257}like the modernist has been teaching since 1850? Didn't He mean a ["rope," ]{.class_4257}like the reconstructed "Q" document of Lamsa says? What did He mean?

Well, He meant what He said!

(Catholic and Liberal alike have one fixed, constant, inflexible, endurable, unending habit---[they can't stand the word of God as written. ]{.class_4257}They must have an Apocrypha "stuck on" or a word Greekified or Hebrewified to fit infidelity, and I suppose there is no cure for this habit; it is more in the nature of a disease. It has been proved incurable for over 1,500 years.)

Observe: The word ["eye"]{.class_4258} is found in Luke 18:25 as not ["the eye of a needle"]{.class_4258} but the ["needle's eye"]{.class_4258} ([tramatos, ]{.class_4257}Greek). The word means a ["hole that was made by boring through something!" ]{.class_4257}It certainly [never ]{.class_4257}means a gate in a city wall, nor could it mean that, nor will it ever mean that, nor [does it say that. ]{.class_4257}Following in the steps of bungling infidel scholarship, we have Bible believers today who go around talking about a camel having to "stoop to enter the gate at Jerusalem called the Needle's Eye!" Rubbish!! You can't muster Edersheim, Pliny, Plutarch, and Josephus forever to attack the Bible. Somewhere, they are going to run out of ammo. And here, they run out! [Neither the Greek nor the English will support the attack, and neither will the "Aramaic,"]{.class_4257} for, lo and behold, the Aramaic Peshitta reads [(gamla) ]{.class_4257}"rope" for "camel"! Our Lord meant what He said. He was being sarcastic deliberately. No one who has read the Old Testament (and believed it) would fail to appreciate the caustic and biting remark. Note for example Malachi 3:15; Amos 4:4, and numerous others.

["Jesus beheld them." ]{.class_4258}The clause indicates that after their question, He did not answer them immediately, but did a little heart‑searching and face‑searching to see if they would take a little "spiritual" talk instead of "physical." \[What follows (vss. 27--30 and 20:1--16) shows that they needed some instruction on these matters, for Peter immediately asks about physical blessings and rewards, and the parable in Chapter 20 deals with the "pay off" for serving the Lord.\]

["But with God all things are possible" ]{.class_4258}(see Gen. 18:14; Jer. 32:27; Mark 10:27; Luke 18:27). The contrast is that a [man ]{.class_4257}cannot figure it out, but God can. A man cannot get to heaven apart from God. Man thinks one way and God thinks another (Isa. 55:5--8). Only God can separate the man from his riches and give him the right []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_362}"slant" on things. There is evidence that although the rich man [rarely ]{.class_4257}is saved, nonetheless, rich men [do get saved; ]{.class_4257}notable examples are Zacchaeus, Nicodemus, Matthew, and Cornelius. It is not "money" that is the root of all evil, but the ["love of money."]{.class_4258} (Of course, the reading: "all kinds of evil," is merely a covetous reading inserted by the idolators of the [ASV ]{.class_4257}who wished to avoid the force of the text in 1 Timothy 6:10. Likewise, "money is A root of evil," etc.; there are as many ways to mess up the Scripture as there are people who love money!) "A little power, a little sway, a belly full of food each day, is all the rich and worldly crave, between the cradle and the grave."

Bill Chrysler (a personal worker in Pensacola, Florida) ran into a businessman whose son was thinking seriously of the ministry. The boy's father was outraged at the thought of "his son" entering such a profession, and stamping his foot, he bellowed: "My son will [never ]{.class_4257}be a preacher! I'll have you know he is my only son, and I have plans for him! He will inherit the business I am now building!!" Ten days later, the man declared bankruptcy. One never knows, does one?

19:27 Then answered Peter and said unto him, Behold, we have forsaken all, and followed thee; what shall we have therefore?

28 And Jesus said unto them, Verily I say unto you, That ye which have followed me, in the regeneration when the Son of man shall sit in the throne of his glory, ye also shall sit upon twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel.

29 And every one that hath forsaken houses, or brethren, or sisters, or father, or mother, or wife, or children, or lands, for my name's sake, shall receive an hundredfold, and shall inherit everlasting life.

30 But many that are first shall be last; and the last shall be first.

["Then answered Peter." ]{.class_4258}Whatever may be said about Peter's character, there is one thing that is certain---he hangs attentively on every word that Jesus says. (Compare John 13:6 and Matt. 16:22.) He is wide awake. Like a German Shepherd on the kitchen floor []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_363}waiting for a bone from the master's table, old Peter is "on the spot" when something is going on. He may blunder and speak out of turn, but he is [there. ]{.class_4257}Like General Puller, Truscott, Patton, and "Howlin Mad Smith," he is "present and accounted for," good or bad. Peter states a fact and asks a question: ["We have forsaken all\..."]{.class_4258} (and this was true); ["What shall we have\...?"]{.class_4258} The question is moderately selfish but it is timely. (You must remember that they have just witnessed a prospective disciple "on the grill," so to speak, and he has [refused ]{.class_4257}to forsake all and follow Christ. The question, then, is theological as well as practical. Our Lord does not hesitate to answer.)

["In the regeneration\..."]{.class_4258} (vss. 28--29) is another one of the great Premillennial verses installed by the Holy Spirit as a road‑block to stop the suicidal tendencies of amillennialists and postmillennialists. Notice that [every word in the sentence is clearly defined within the covers of the Holy Writ, ]{.class_4257}and that the interpretation is so clear and exact that [every possibility of teaching it falsely is avoided. ]{.class_4257}["In the regeneration."]{.class_4258} The word is used [twice ]{.class_4257}in the Scriptures, and two times only. Once here and once in Titus 3:5. In one case (Titus), the word refers to the new birth of the believer by the Holy Spirit, in connection with the [first coming of Christ to die. ]{.class_4257}In the other case (Matthew), the word refers to the new birth of the EARTH at the [second coming of Christ to reign. ]{.class_4257}Nor is there an inch to give to another interpretation. The laws of plain English, here, are as inviolable as the laws of jurisprudence, where two reliable witnesses give eye‑witness accounts of identical facts.

All attempts to pervert the passage and say, "Well, I was taught\..." or "Well, you think you're right and he thinks he's right\..." or "But my faith teaches" or "My church believes that\..." are futile. A man drank a bottle of strichnine thinking it was Pepto‑Bismol. Do you know what happened? (Of course, he was [taught ]{.class_4257}it was Pepto‑Bismol, and he had [faith to believe ]{.class_4257}it, or he wouldn't have taken it.) Do you know what happened to him? (Of course, he [thought it ]{.class_4257}was Pepto‑Bismol and figured it would be Pepto‑Bismol to anyone who [sincerely believed it]{.class_4257}.) Do you know what happened to him? He was buried.

["The throne of his glory," ]{.class_4258}in either Testament, in every passage, without exception, always refers to the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_364}[earthly throne of David.]{.class_4257} If this were debatable, look at the rest of Matthew 19:28--29---["thrones," "houses," "lands."]{.class_4258} You could not possibly get a spiritual "kingdom of God" out of the passage. ["The throne of his glory" ]{.class_4258}is found, verbatim in Matthew 25:31, in a passage on the Second Coming. It is found again in Jeremiah 14:21, defined as David's earthly, physical, literal, visible, Jewish throne at Jerusalem; and this is repeated to Mary (in the New Testament) in Luke 1:30--33, and again at Pentecost in Acts 2:30, and again in 1 Samuel 2:8.

Yet today, in America, 95 percent of all ministers and priests in the Catholic, Presbyterian, Baptist, Methodist, Lutheran, Congregational, Reformed, and Episcopalian churches are teaching their congregations that Christ is now a king on a throne in heaven, spreading a kingdom of which they (the priests and ministers) are the rulers!

This aborted heresy is such a malfit that the teachers of it themselves are gradually adjusting their theology to bring in a [physical, man‑made, visible kingdom ]{.class_4257}without the restoration of Israel or the return of Christ. Originally (1700--1900), they talked of "spiritual reigning," but as time has passed, they have shifted from this position to an actual, [physical, man‑made paradise ]{.class_4257}to be brought about by CARE packages, legislation, litigation, Red Cross, United Drive, United Fund, United States, United Forces, United Nations, united opposition to the Bible, and united opinion that [the kingdom has already come, ]{.class_4257}and Christ is [never coming back to reign over an earthly kingdom. ]{.class_4257}What began as "spiritualizing" of the text before us, Matthew 19:28--29, winds up in taking the passage [literally, ]{.class_4257}while omitting every doctrine taught in the Bible connected with the passage. Thus the "postmillennialist" backslides (with no "post" to lean on) into an "amillennialist," and from here he goes to the [social regeneration ]{.class_4257}position, which culminates in international socialism and religious catholicism; neither of these positions is remotely connected with Biblical Christianity in any way, shape, or form. Catholi­cism (to gain political control, and "bring in the kingdom") adopts the Christian vocabulary and hypocritically uses the Scriptures to justify its political intrigues; but the text, Matthew 19:28, speaks of a [literal ]{.class_4257}kingdom where [Jews sit on twelve thrones, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2117.xhtml_page_365}where Christ sits on an earthly throne, where the earth is regenerated ]{.class_4257}(Acts 3:19--22),[ and where rewards are given as an "inheritance" ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 8:17; 2 Tim. 2:12). What concord hath Christ with Rome? What part hath he that believeth with an amillennialist, and what communion hath light with liberalism, and what agreement hath a Christian with a Bible‑perverter?

Matthew 19:28 is clear. It is the ["Son of man"]{.class_4258} (Christ's Jewish designation in His earthly ministry to Israel) that is used---not the "Son of God." The apostles are with Him (Judas excepted on Psa. 122:5; Luke 22:36; Acts 1) as earthly judges, and the rewards they receive as the "inheritance" (not heaven!) are "HOUSES AND LANDS" (see Luke 19:11--25). This is not ["interpreta­tion."]{.class_4257} This is reading and believing what you read.

The ["wife"]{.class_4258} is not repayed in the "plural" (as we learn from reading the companion passage in Mark 10:29--30), and an additional element is mentioned---persecution! You must observe that the persecution is persecution for "Christ's sake" or the ["gospel's."]{.class_4258} Any attempt by Rome to thrust their martyred nuns and priests into this category is a vainglorious attempt to claim something that belongs to someone else. ("Stealing" is the correct Anglo‑Saxon.)
