Although the Jew was waiting for a real ["son of David"]{.class_4258} to show up and claim the throne (as the legal descendant of the Kings), he was NOT expecting this king to be [God incarnate! ]{.class_4257}(This explains the statements of Peter in Acts 3:17 and Paul in Acts 13:27.) The ["son of David"]{.class_4258} is a Heaven‑sent MESSIAH, sent to take over the entire world, but He comes first to Israel on a mission of [suffering. ]{.class_4257}(Notice, that in the passage in Psa. 89:20--37, the text turns completely "about face" and reverses on every promise in the passage at verse 38! This again is the kind of thing that makes a lazy man quit. It is the often repeated "trick" of the Holy Spirit in the Old Testament---putting both advents together, with no intervening "Church Age" to distinguish them.)

["What these say?"]{.class_4258} is that they recognize the presence of the long‑promised Jewish Messiah, from the Davidic line, who is destined to inherit the throne and set up Israel as the "top nation" according to the prophecies of Daniel 2, 7. The priests are saying acidly, "You know the Old Testament, and you know perfectly well that you are NOT the Messiah. Why do you allow this bunch of fanatics to run around here calling you[ 'the son of David,']{.class_4258} when []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_401}you're nothing but the son of a carpenter named Joseph?" Christ's retort is devastating.

["Yea; have ye never read\...."]{.class_4258} That is, "Is that right? Well, how about you, bright eyes; have YOU never read, yourself?" Notice the sarcastic ["Yea"]{.class_4258} prefacing the question. In colloquial Americana this would be, "Oh, is that so?" or, "Yea, yeah, I know, and how about YOU?" Unfortunately the [RSV ]{.class_4257}and [ASV ]{.class_4257}(being antiquated translations of a dead language) are unable to put this into "living American." (A truly American translation would turn your hair white, for Americans do NOT talk in the language of Phillips, Moffatt, Weymouth, and Goodspeed. They talk the language of the Army barracks, the fishing boats, Hollywood, the dance bands, the garages, the mechanic shops, the underworld, the beaches, the bar­rooms, the salesman, and the sports world.)

["Out of the mouth of babes and sucklings." ]{.class_4258}This penetrating quotation says so much that it says too much. The quotation is from Psalm 8:2, and it tallies with Isaiah 29:12, 28:9; and Psalm 131. The long and short of the matter is that God is not interested in showing a smart‑aleck priest, rabbi, or scholar [anything ]{.class_4257}worth knowing. He is interested in revealing the deep things and the "hidden truths" to [babies ]{.class_4257}(see Luke 10:21). Thus the "babe in Christ" (1 Pet. 2:1--4) has access to wisdom that the Rosicrucians, Christian Scientists, Bodhisattvas, Sadhus, Astronauts, Gurus, Cheldas, Lamas, and nuclear physicists do not have.

In all the passages cited (Isaiah, Psalms, and Luke) the refrain is the same: God reveals doctrinal truth to people who come to Him as little children and simply believe what He tells them, because He said it. By such a standard, we can understand readily why Greek and Hebrew scholarship and "better translations" have bogged down in the mire of apostasy. All these "newer translations" (with their trans­lators) take for granted that they are intelligent, enlightened, highly intellectual jobs. None pay the slightest attention to the fact that God does not reveal things to smart‑alecks. The people, who recognized in Christ ["the son of David"]{.class_4258} and accepted Him as Lord and Christ (Acts 2:34--37), were the common, ordinary, everyday people of the streets (Mark 12:37). The educated class of long‑robed priests and liberal Sadducees [crucify Him on religious grounds ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 26:53--[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_402}68). How can one explain this? There is only one explanation: God gives light and understanding to the ["babes and sucklings"]{.class_4258} who are just "dumb" enough to believe what He says, but He BLINDS the "wise and prudent" on the grounds that [they are stuck on themselves ]{.class_4257}(see John 9:39--41). After all, PRIDE is the sin that keeps any man from believing the Bible or repenting or receiving Christ or leaving an apostate Church, or going ["without the camp, bearing his reproach."]{.class_4258}

21:17 And he left them, and went out of the city into Bethany; and he lodged there.

18 Now in the morning as he returned into the city, he hungered.

19 And when he saw a fig tree in the way, he came to it, and found nothing thereon, but leaves only, and said unto it, Let no fruit grow on thee henceforward for ever. And presently the fig tree withered away.

20 And when the disciples saw it, they marvelled, saying, How soon is the fig tree withered away!

21 Jesus answered and said unto them, Verily I say unto you, If ye have faith, and doubt not, ye shall not only do this which is done to the fig tree, but also if ye shall say unto this mountain, Be thou removed, and be thou cast into the sea; it shall be done.

22 And all things, whatsoever ye shall ask in prayer, believing, ye shall receive.

["He lodged there." ]{.class_4258}The verse clearly places the first night at Bethany as being [Sunday night. ]{.class_4257}This should be noted, for from here on, we run into the peculiar tradition that Christ was crucified [Friday---]{.class_4257}yet there are [only three days accounted for ]{.class_4257}(including Palm Sunday) in the accounts given in Matthew, Mark, and Luke. This ancient Roman heresy is still propagated in Protestant churches, and is so thoroughly deceptive that Premillennial scholars have even bolstered a "Good [Thursday" ]{.class_4257}in an effort to half‑compromise the actual date of the crucifixion, which is Wednesday morning (see Matt. 12:39--40). Matthew 21:1 is Sunday morning. Matthew 21:17 is Sunday night. Matthew 21:18 is Monday morning. Nothing could be []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_403}clearer.

["And found nothing thereon, but leaves only." ]{.class_4258}The episode with the fig tree has caused much comment. Mark 11:12--13 indicates that Jesus does not exercise His divine power of omniscience in dealing with the fig tree; He walks up to it ["if haply he might find any thing thereon." ]{.class_4258}What He is looking for (fruit) is not there. The fact that the tree has [leaves ]{.class_4257}indicates that it should have [fruit, ]{.class_4257}but there are a number of geographical and meteorological factors that enter at this point.

1\. The early figs in Palestine do not ripen unto July. But winter figs are found in March.

2\. If the leaves on a fig tree show in March--April, then the unripe fruit from the Fall has survived the winter and is ripe in April before the next crop in July. (To this day Palestine harvests four crops a year and in the Millennium they will harvest twelve---Ezek. 47:12.)

The reason then for cursing the fig tree is one of two things; either the winter figs should be found on it, or else the unripe fruit from the fall has survived the winter. Either way, the leaves indicate the tree [professes ]{.class_4257}to have fruit. It has ["an apron of fig leaves" ]{.class_4257}with no fruit to back it up---exactly what Adam and Eve had! This is the only tree in Scripture that the Lord ever curses, and in keeping with its own scientifically accurate catalog of correct types, the Holy Scriptures set forth the FIG TREE in the first three chapters of Genesis as a picture of [self‑righteousness; ]{.class_4257}hence, it is used to picture Israel in Jeremiah 24:2, 5, 8; Matthew 24:32--33; Amos 8:2; and Hosea 9:10.

The lesson is obvious. God hates profession that does not bring [fruit ]{.class_4257}with it. The passage in James 2 will not help the Christ‑rejecting, science‑worshipping churchman to avoid the implication. ["Faith without works is dead," ]{.class_4258}but it is also true that [works and faith are both dead and less than worthless if they are placed in a church or a creed or a sacrament for salvation, and not the Lord Jesus Christ Himself ]{.class_4257}(see Rom. 10:1--4; Gal. 3:1--13; Titus 3:5--6). You may have noticed that works are not ["fruit" ]{.class_4258}unless the Holy Spirit in the born‑again Believer is the one bringing forth these ["works"]{.class_4258} (see ["fruit"]{.class_4258} in Gal. 5:22). The self‑righteous, wine‑drinking priest, who is trusting in his "faith" or his "religion" to []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_404}save his soul, has here missed the fundamental truth of the Blood Atonement.

Works that are produced by the individual in [order to merit salvation ]{.class_4257}are called in the New Testament ["dead works"]{.class_4258} (Heb. 9:14) and will merit Hell (Matt. 7:21--23), along with all the religious rags of self‑righteousness, denominated "filth" in Isaiah 64 and ["dung"]{.class_4258} in Philippians 3. The only "works" that are accepted by God (1 Cor. 3:8, 15) are works that the indwelling Holy Spirit "works out" (Phil. 2:12--13) in the believer, after the believer is born of that Spirit. FIG LEAVES are WORKS, but my friend, [they are not "fruit"!! ]{.class_4257}["Faith without works is dead,"]{.class_4258} and works without fruit are dead. ["Works" are not "fruit" ]{.class_4257}(see Matt. 23:3).

["Let no fruit grow on thee henceforward for ever." ]{.class_4258}The incident is strikingly similar to the one found in Luke 13:1--10, where the fig tree planted in the vineyard refuses to yield fruit and the owner orders it cut down. Comparing Matthew 17:1, you will notice that the figure SEVEN is again evident. (Running 1,000 years for each year of Luke 13:1--10, we arrive at an astonishing truth. For 3,000 years God comes seeking fruit from Israel---one‑year‑for‑a‑thousand as one-day-for-a-thousand in Matt. 17:1.) At the end of 3,000 years (1000 B.C., Solomon's dedication of the temple), the owner says ["cut it down."]{.class_4258} The keeper says, ["Let it alone this year]{.class_4258} (1,000 more) [also, till I shall ]{.class_4258}[dig ]{.class_4268}[about it, and ]{.class_4258}[dung ]{.class_4268}[it\...."]{.class_4258} The [digging ]{.class_4257}comes under Sennacherib, who in the next 1,000 year period (1000 B.C. to 4 B.C.) carries away ten tribes into captivity. The ["dunging" ]{.class_4257}follows with Nebuchadnezzar taking away two southern tribes into captivity during the same period (1000 B.C. to 4 B.C.), and still in the time of Christ (representing the fourth year), ["no fruit"]{.class_4258}---[so God cuts Israel down! ]{.class_4257}A.D. 70 is an historical fact, which testifies the truth that God operates history to coincide with the exact wording of Scripture.

No fruit---down goes the fig tree in Luke 13. No fruit---cursed is the fig tree in Matthew 21. God hates religious profession which [refuses to believe Him. ]{.class_4257}This is Cain's way. Cain believed in God (like all Liberals and Catholics), but like all Liberals and Catholics, [he believed in his own faith above what God said. ]{.class_4257}He believed [in ]{.class_4257}God but did not [believe ]{.class_4257}God! (There are a lot of practical atheists like that []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_405}running around loose these days!)

["How soon is the fig tree withered away!"]{.class_4258} The time of this statement is a day later, on Tuesday morning, and since it is very important that we follow the chronology of the Holy Spirit during the last week of Christ's earthly ministry, we need to go to Mark's gospel to pick up the loose ends. In Mark 11:13--14, Jesus curses the fig tree exactly as He does in the passage in Matthew under consideration, but whereas Matthew continues the narrative without an intervening night, Mark writes in 11:19, ["AND WHEN EVEN WAS COME, he went out of the city."]{.class_4258} Then Mark continues in verse 20, ["AND IN THE MORNING, as they passed by, they saw the fig tree dried up from the roots. And Peter calling to remembrance saith unto him\...."]{.class_4258}

The statement in Matthew 21:20 takes place the day AFTER the fig tree is cursed. That is, the tree is cursed Monday and in one day's time (Tuesday), it is completely withered. This explains the remark, ["How SOON is the fig tree withered away!"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 21:20). This chronology should be followed carefully, or we may stumble into the universal Catholic heresy that Jesus was crucified on Friday (see Matt. 12:40 and notes).

["If ye have faith, and doubt not\...whatsoever ye shall ask\...ye shall receive." ]{.class_4258}Verses 21--22 contain a great prayer promise, and although it can be claimed spiritually by anyone with enough faith to claim it, it will be noticed that the mountain to which He is referring (the Mount of Olives) does literally "remove" at the Second Coming (see Zech. 14:4, 7--9).

There is considerable disagreement among Christians about such prayers. Many Christians have claimed a Bible promise and found that it not only did not come in, but the pious brethren remarked that, ["You got an answer; the answer was no."]{.class_4257} But of course, this is a lame way to alibi out, for the other promises are not predicated with the idea that [something will show up that you DIDN'T ASK FOR, ]{.class_4257}if you have faith to believe you would [receive what you ask for. ]{.class_4257}Many of Oral Roberts' healing prayers are a colossal "flop," and a lot of sick saints know it. Many a devotee of "believ­ingism" has believed till he was blue-in-the-face that Mary ascended into Heaven, but according to the Bible, she is still in the grave (see []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_406}comments on Matt. 8:17). I know of a G.I. who believed his entire head had been shot off because his helmet rolled off while he was sleeping, and he woke up yelling, "My head! My head! Oh, my God, my head is blown off!" [He had faith to believe. ]{.class_4257}He just believed something that wasn't so (Battalion Medic, Ardennes, 1944).

["Believing, ye shall receive." ]{.class_4258}The promise stands for whoever can claim it, but it must be remembered that there are two kinds of ["believing"]{.class_4258} in the Scripture (Rom. 6:17; Acts 8:13), and one of them is wholly ineffectual.

21:23 And when he was come into the temple, the chief priests and the elders of the people came unto him as he was teaching, and said, By what authority doest thou these things? and who gave thee this authority?

24 And Jesus answered and said unto them, I also will ask you one thing, which if ye tell me, I in like wise will tell you by what authority I do these things.

25 The baptism of John, whence was it? from heaven, or of men? And they reasoned with themselves, saying, If we shall say, From heaven; he will say unto us, Why did ye not then believe him?

26 But if we shall say, Of men; we fear the people; for all hold John as a prophet.

27 And they answered Jesus, and said, We cannot tell. And he said unto them, Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things.

Notice the interminable reoccurrence of the "theological dispute" following a miracle, where the disputer deliberately [ignores ]{.class_4257}the miracle that has just been performed---here, the cursing of the fig tree.

["By what authority?" ]{.class_4258}The question is appalling. Jesus has just cursed a work of nature and caused it to obey without hesitation. The fig tree dried up from the roots---at the speaking of one sentence! And here come the long‑robed priests (in their Baal vestments) asking, ["By what authority?" ]{.class_4258}The question reminds us of the recent rash of Bible translators all claiming the "[AUTHORIZED VERSION]{.class_4262}" should just be "[KJV]{.class_4262}"---the [King James Version]{.class_4262}. That is, the puny little devotees of the "newer" translations all []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_407}[question the authority ]{.class_4262}of the Book that obviously has more power between its covers than the combined authority of the British Empire, the Vatican State, and the Soviet Republic. ["By what authority?" ]{.class_4258}(They would like to locate Him and classify Him in the proper bracket so they can [explain the miracles away!) ]{.class_4262}["Who gave thee this authority?"]{.class_4258}

Jesus would have no difficulty answering the question at all if He felt inclined to do so, but at this particular moment He is not in the mood to answer with John 5:27, 30. Instead, He evades the question and sets a trap for these griping religious leaders who desire everyone to derive authority from [them.]{.class_4257}

["The baptism of John, whence was it? from heaven, or of men?" ]{.class_4258}This is another loaded question. Looking at it, as it stands, is the equivalent of looking down the bore of a 16‑incher during an off‑shore naval battery bombardment. The men at which it is aimed reason correctly. In plain English (vss. 25--26), they reason, "Man, we've had it now! If we say the baptism is [from Heaven, ]{.class_4257}He is going to come back with, 'Why didn't you get baptized then' and if we say [it is from men, ]{.class_4257}then this crowd will mop us up, because they're just dumb enough to believe that John got his directives from Heaven!" So they turn and (in the standard scientific, broadminded, and tolerant fashion) they reply, ["We are agnostics. It may be from heaven and then again it may not be. We wish to be fair and open‑minded to all. We can't tell from where it was."]{.class_4257}

"Neither tell I you by what authority I do these things."

In plainer words, if you are going to claim ignorance, when you are (in actuality) [deliberately hiding ]{.class_4257}from the truth of the word, then you can keep right on being ignorant. If these seem like harsh words for a commentary, let the reader take heed, for in the context, at verse 44, the "agnostic" of verse 27 is told that he will not only be "broken," but he will be ground ["to powder." ]{.class_4258}The same audience addressed [knows ]{.class_4257}to whom He is speaking! (see vs. 45).

The repartee of our Lord shows a number of things. It shows first of all that even in the midst of a vicious and murderous attack on His character and work, the Lord has a [sense of humour. ]{.class_4257}Look at the proposition He gave them in verse 24---["IF."]{.class_4258} Then after all their attempts to evade the issue they []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_408}[never do get their question answered. ]{.class_4257}They become so involved in trying to protect their own worthless hides and religious prestige that they forget the original information they went out to seek! They not only didn't get an answer, they were publicly insulted and prevented from asking the question again. The repartee shows that the Lord's thinking is far ahead of theirs. He maintains His social equilibrium in the heat of the argument. It shows, also, God's method of dealing with religious "wheels" who are always worried about "who has the authority besides us?" One can almost hear the modern Greek scholars brooding over the Westcott and Hort "text" and saying, ["The Greek is uncertain in this passage, at least the expression is highly figurative," or ]{.class_4257}one can hear the liquor‑headed priest leaning back, smiling like a cat entering a butcher shop, and saying, ["Well, when people forsake the true interpreter of the Bible, the one true church, to whom Christ gave authority to administer the sacraments, then there can be a variety of interpretations!"]{.class_4257}

They are both ignorant. One took a lexicon instead of the Holy Spirit, and the other took 1,000 years of tradition passed down from one bachelor to another.

The chief priests and elders have so saturated the world with this ["willful ignorance" ]{.class_4257}(a hypocritical pretense) that you cannot KNOW anything about absolute truth and authority---see Isaiah 29:12. Door‑to‑door visitation will reveal the philosophy entrenched in the minds of twentieth century people, from mechanics to bankers. You ask a man, "Are you saved?" and he replies, "I don't believe that a man can know whether or not he's saved until he dies." Why does he answer in this fashion? [Nothing in the Bible would lead him to believe this. ]{.class_4257}Peter, James, John, Moses, David, and Paul ALL knew where they were going when they died, and [three of them murdered men! ]{.class_4257}What do you suppose it is that possesses a man to follow an agnostic religious leader, who had NO assurance about life after death, when the man has before him the written revelation of God that assures him he CAN know (1 John 3:2--5, 14, 19, 22, 24)? We have to pick up the "chief priests and elders" to understand this "possession."

If the man you ask about salvation answers, "Yes, I am saved," []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_409}then he will be obligated to spend a little time in Christian fellowship with you around the word of God (which of course all born‑again people believe and love). If he answers, "No, I am not," then you will pull out the word and deal with him about salvation. Let's face it. The man [does not want to talk about the Bible or hear it or read it or study it or have it "put to him."]{.class_4257} So[ ]{.class_4257}he deceitfully and evasively answers, "I don't believe any man can know\..." exactly as the "chief priests" taught him. As in the case of Matthew 21:23--26, the modern answer is on the same premises as the "chief priests." The premises are self‑preservation, hatred for any truth that threatens self, love of man‑made authority, and intense dislike for anything that God has revealed [contrary to religious upbringing. ]{.class_4257}Agnostics are agnostics for these four good religious reasons, and it accounts for the peculiar New Testament revelation that agnostics will be ["punished with everlasting destruction from the presence of the Lord\...In flaming fire" ]{.class_4258}(2 Thess. 1:7--9). An agnostic is a willful rejector of any truth that conflicts with his own ideas (which he has learned from other people like himself).

21:28 But what think ye? A certain man had two sons; and he came to the first, and said, Son, go work to day in my vineyard.

29 He answered and said, I will not: but afterward he repented, and went.

30 And he came to the second, and said likewise. And he answered and said, I go, sir: and went not.

31 Whether of them twain did the will of his father? They say unto him, The first. Jesus saith unto them, Verily I say unto you, That the publicans and the harlots go into the kingdom of God before you.

32 For John came unto you in the way of righteousness, and ye believed him not: but the publicans and the harlots believed him: and ye, when ye had seen it, repented not afterward, that ye might believe him.

["But what THINK ye?" ]{.class_4258}Since they are always coming to Him and asking ["What THINKEST thou?"]{.class_4258} (desiring to hear an opinion instead of the Divine Judgment already revealed about the matter), He tosses it back in their lap. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_410}["But what think YE?"]{.class_4258}

The parable which follows is a simple test of correct judgment and sound reasoning. It wouldn't take a genius to figure it out. Like the question Jesus puts to the lawyer in Luke 10:36 and the Pharisee in Luke 7:42, this question, at the end of the parable---["Whether of them twain did the will of his father?"---]{.class_4258}has only one acceptable answer. One boy said, "I'm going," and went off bass‑fishing. The other said, "Nothin' doin', I ain't working today," and then repented and put up his rod and reel and went out into the vineyard. Obviously, the boy that wound up doing the work is the one that ["did the will of his father."]{.class_4258} The common people in front of Jesus have no trouble with the problem; they answer, ["The first" ]{.class_4258}(meaning the boy who said, "I'm not going," and then changed his mind and went).

The ["will of his father"]{.class_4258} in this little story is the will expressed also in the parable of the vineyard in the same chapter, later, at verses 33--40. Throughout the chapter, the picture is consistent. The entire chapter is dealing with Christ's lawful claims to the vineyard (the Palestinian territory promised to Israel) and the [recognition ]{.class_4257}of this fact by the common people (the publicans and harlots) and its [denial ]{.class_4257}by the religious leaders. Notice that the chapter opens with the Son of David coming as rightful heir to the throne and the land of Palestine. The religious leaders are set up to resist this coming (vss. 12--15), and their resistance is likened to a hypocritical fig tree which professes to have fruit but has none (vss. 17--22). The baptism of John is connected with this coming‑to‑reign, as John is a type of Elijah (see notes on Matt. 17:10--11), so this baptism is the [testing point ]{.class_4257}on which Jesus leans when arguing about His Messianic authority (vss. 23--26).

Continuing further into the chapter, it is pointed out that the publicans and harlots, who submitted to John's baptism, have correctly analyzed the person and work of the Messiah, Jesus Christ, while the religious leaders (who wanted to keep the authority for themselves) have rejected Him. Again, John's baptism (vs. 32) is the testing ground since John's water baptism for the remission of sins (as Peter's in Acts 2) is specifically said to be for the purpose of manifesting [Christ to Israel ]{.class_4257}(John 1:31). Nothing is remotely connected []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_411}[with the spiritual salvation of a Gentile from Hell, in any way, shape, or form.]{.class_4257}

["Publicans and harlots go into the kingdom." ]{.class_4258}Notice that it is here the spiritual kingdom which is to appear at the Second Advent (see Luke 19:11). ["For John came\...and ye believed him not." ]{.class_4258}The message of John's preaching was, ["Repent ye: for the kingdom of HEAVEN is at hand." ]{.class_4258}But in Mark 1, the Holy Spirit records ["the kingdom of God," ]{.class_4258}thereby deceiving the amillennial and postmillennial interpreters into thinking that they are [the same. ]{.class_4257}Yet in no case in 66 books are they [ever ]{.class_4257}the same, except in the persons of Adam and Christ. Both of these are "sons of God" and yet "earthy" (see comments under Matt. 3:2, 4:23).

The Pharisees rejected John's message of the coming kingdom on the grounds that they would wait and see who the Messiah was first, and then [if they approved of him ]{.class_4257}(and could control him!), they would submit to John. If [not, ]{.class_4257}they would keep on ignoring John. Notice that when the Pharisees refused to submit to John's baptism (Matt. 21:32), John read their mind and made the same reference to the "stones crying out" which Jesus makes at the triumphal entry (see Luke 3:8, 19:40). All of this ties together perfectly.

1\. A kingdom is coming to Israel in Palestine.

2\. This kingdom is to be ruled by the "Son of David."

3\. This Son of David shows up in Matthew 21 as rightful ruler.

4\. He is rejected by the leaders on the grounds that His teaching and conduct conflicts with their Talmudic traditions, which they have invented themselves, to retain power themselves.

5\. To show their disapproval, they refuse to acknowledge the authority of John and refuse to admit that his baptism is from "heaven."

6\. They further refuse Christ's authority on the grounds that they want to run Palestine themselves (Matt. 21:38) and continue to be religious bigshots in the eyes of the common people.

7\. Every verse in Matthew 21 points this out and brings it rapidly to a head.

[At no time in the Scriptures does Christ intimate or suggest that He has not come to set up a physical, literal, earthly, visible, Davidic, Jewish, Messianic kingdom in Palestine. ]{.class_4257}This is the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_412}fictitious theorizing of the Roman Catholic apostates under the guidance of St. Augustine, Irenaeus, Cyprian, Origen, Jerome, Constantine, Callixtus, and Eusebius.

Calvin and other dead‑orthodox intellectuals in the Reformation subscribed to this theorizing, and taught it as [Bible doctrine; ]{.class_4257}hence, they adopted at least the Roman Catholic [method ]{.class_4257}of interpreting the New Testament. And of course, a church's view on the Second Coming [determines ]{.class_4257}the method it uses in placing the verses found in Matthew, Acts, and Hebrews. Furthermore, if the premillennial system is presented to this bunch of deluded agnostics as the [solution ]{.class_4257}for the misplaced verses, the system is assailed and reviled with, "Oh, that's just YOUR interpretation," "Oh, you think YOU'RE right and everyone else is wrong," "Well, my mother was as godly a woman as ever lived, and she\..." etc. All of which proves that if a man loves this world system, he will find an alibi and a church and a priest and a preacher and a religion and a Bible that will "go along with him," and let him thumb his nose at the revelation of God. (Prayerfully, of course!)

No, it is not that the Pharisees [misunderstand the nature of the kingdom.]{.class_4257} No, for goodness sake, don't cover their God‑defying sin under a lexicographer's blanket and say, "Poor boys, they didn't understand." God help you, brother, THEY UNDERSTOOD PERFECTLY (look at 21:25). [They were just a bunch of conceited, hypocritical liars!]{.class_4257}

The nature of the kingdom promised Israel has never changed and will never change. The only change that has been made is [temporary ]{.class_4257}(Rom. 11:1--29), and this change reveals a mystery given to the apostle Paul, which was unknown to the Jews of Christ's day (Eph. 3:1--10). ["The way of righteousness" ]{.class_4258}is connected with this intermediate ["kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 21:32; Rom. 14:17), and as noted before, it implies a [moral ]{.class_4257}quality that must be attained by Israel before ["the kingdom of heaven"]{.class_4258} comes in (see Matt. 6:33).

["When ye had seen it, repented not." ]{.class_4258}The charge brought in verse 32 is the charge that could be brought against CAIN. Cain saw his brother's sacrifice accepted. He knew [what ]{.class_4257}to bring (Gen. 4:4), he knew [when ]{.class_4257}to bring it (Gen. 4:3), and he knew [where ]{.class_4257}to bring it (Gen. 4:16). [He just refused to do it, after he knew it was right to do. ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_413}This is the exact case of the Pharisees who went into a huddle in Matthew 21:25. They came out of the huddle and had the quarterback call the signals, "We are agnostic. Absolute truth is unattainable. All truth is relative. We have an open mind." What was the truth? The truth is, that like Cain, they were jealous and HATED their brother and had already made plans to kill him (see Matt. 12:14)!

["When ye had seen it," ]{.class_4258}has further implications in the context. The Pharisee, as the second son of the parable (vs. 30) and as Cain, was the first one to profess to do the right thing. Cain brought the offering before Abel did! [Cain was more religious. ]{.class_4257}The Pharisees were, at the time, leading the people and trying to get them to hold to the Mosaic Law and promises, while waiting for the Messiah (John 9:28--29; 7:51--52). While Israel "went to pot," the religious leaders were at least [attempting, ]{.class_4257}or at least [professing ]{.class_4257}to be, "ready to go to work when the Messiah came." But up He shows, and what happens? The people, "going to pot," believe John and go down and submit themselves to his baptism and "go to work in the vineyard" (vs. 29), while the gang who said, "I go," did NOT go to John for baptism and, furthermore, did NOT repent when they saw [what was taking place! ]{.class_4257}(Not any more than Cain did when he saw Abel's sacrifice going up in smoke off the altar! See Commentary on Genesis, Gen. 4:1--6.)

Let's square off. They [saw ]{.class_4257}and [knew ]{.class_4257}(John 12:37, 3:1--2), yet claimed to be agnostics to protect their own hides and preserve their ecclesiastical pomp. John 12:42--43 reduces to a sentence the entire worth and value of their knowledge and morality in the sight of God. ["They loved the praise of men" ]{.class_4258}(John 12:43; Luke 16:14--15). Most "agnosticism" amounts to just that. If a Catholic communicant ever went to his priest and showed him from the Scriptures how he [knew ]{.class_4257}he was saved, he would be excommunicated or slandered. If an Episcopalian or a Jew ever went to his "rector" or "rabbi" and showed him from the Scripture how he [knew ]{.class_4257}Jesus was coming again to reign on earth 1,000 years, he would be socially and religiously "blackballed." As [long as he remains "agnostic" he will receive the praise of men and will retain his "standing." ]{.class_4257}This accounts for all unsaved college professors in one fell swoop. They instinctively resent, despise, [and reject ]{.class_4257}any Bible truth which gives []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_414}[absolute knowledge of future events. ]{.class_4257}In a word, they are practical Atheists, parading with such serial numbers as "Catholic," "Protestant," "Jew," "Buddhist," "Taoist," "Confucianist," "Hindu," "Intellectual," "Conservative," and "Prag­matist" plastered on their helmeted minds.

21:33 Hear another parable: There was a certain householder, which planted a vineyard, and hedged it round about, and digged a winepress in it, and built a tower, and let it out to husbandmen, and went into a far country:

34 And when the time of the fruit drew near, he sent his servants to the husbandmen, that they might receive the fruits of it.

35 And the husbandmen took his servants, and beat one, and killed another, and stoned another.

36 Again, he sent other servants more than the first: and they did unto them likewise.

37 But last of all he sent unto them his son, saying, They will reverence my son.

["Another prabable" ]{.class_4258}implies that the story of the two sons in verses 28--31 is a parable, although it is not listed as one of the twelve parables of the "kingdom of heaven."

["A certain householder." ]{.class_4258}This is obviously God the­ Father in the parable. We should always keep clearly in mind that [every detail of a parable always has an exact, specific meaning.]{.class_4257} (I realize this is not taught in a single college or seminary in the United States, but since Jesus Himself taught this so plainly that no one could miss it, all we can do is just ignore the colleges and seminaries and graduate to the Higher Criticism of Biblical Christianity, as taught by the Holy Spirit.) You will notice that in the entire ministry of Jesus, He only explained two parables, and both times He gave [an exact, detailed explanation of every detail in both of them ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 13:18--23; 36--43). Exactly how Protestant scholars and teachers began to teach the theory that parables only illustrate "general truths" is not hard to see, if one remembers that ["a parable in the mouth of fools"]{.class_4258} is []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_415}like a thorn going up into the hand or a man walking on two legs of unequal length (Prov. 26:7, 9). Therefore, to avoid the embarrassment of being classified with "the blind" (Matt. 13:12--17), they crawfish out of it by saying, "You must not look for a specific meaning to every part, as a parable only teaches a [general truth."]{.class_4257} More agnosticism. What they mean to say is, "Our church's method of placing the verses makes this parable teach a general truth, [before we analyze it; ]{.class_4257}therefore, we cannot analyze it too closely, or it [might develop into a doctrine running contrary to what we have been taught, and then---ah oo! There goes our authority!"]{.class_4257}

Thus has arisen the unsightly and unholy doctrine that a parable only teaches a "general truth." The people who believe this and teach it are compelled to do so once they adopt the Catholic, Liberal, or dead-Orthodox method of interpreting Revelation. This system of interpretation will not allow ANY parable to have an exact meaning. Once the parable becomes exact, the truth pops out like the cork on a champagne bottle, and the horrible truth is that [every parable that deals with this present age teaches that it is going to end in ecclesiastical dictator­ship, false doctrine, demon-possession, and total apostasy. ]{.class_4257}This is in direct conflict with the Catholic, Liberal, and dead‑Orthodox church teachings; all three of these teach that in the present age we are "spreading a kingdom" and that the only [literal ]{.class_4257}kingdom to come is the One‑world­-Catholic‑Socialistic‑Scientific Kingdom of Humanitarian Materialism.

["Which planted a vineyard." ]{.class_4258}The Bible, as usual, defines its own terms and interprets each detail of its own parables. The vineyard in Isaiah 5:1--7 is the house of Israel and Judah in the land. It is the same in Isaiah 27:1--6 in the land, in the Millennium. This house of Israel and Judah is planted "as a vine" in the vineyard, which is called the [land itself ]{.class_4257}in Psalm 80:8. In the parable under surveillance, it is also called "the inheritance" (Matt. 21:38).

Putting all this together, we find the vineyard represents Israel's earthly inheritance in the kingdom of God, which comes on earth [visibly ]{.class_4257}at the return of the Lord. Verse 44 cinches the interpretation past the place where Admiral Nelson could untie it. ["It will grind him to powder" ]{.class_4258}(see Dan. 2:35, 45). Such a thing never happened []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_416}at the First Coming, and not even the Catholic or the Modernist would go this far in trying to interpret the parable. Unwilling and unable to digest the terrific "meat" of the passage, he will have to come off lamely with a "spiritual grinding" to infidels in the Church Age.

["When the lord\...cometh" ]{.class_4258}(21:40) again places the time of the "inheritance" of the vineyard.

["Into a far country" ]{.class_4258}(vs. 33) can then be located exactly. The Householder is God the Father. He lets out the land of Palestine to the Jewish Pharisees, scribes, and elders to take care of until the "time of fruit" (see Luke 13:7). The Far Country is Heaven, as in the parable of Luke 19:12.

["And when the time of the fruit drew near."]{.class_4258} ["When the fulness of the time was come," ]{.class_4258}Paul writes (Gal. 4:4), indicating a period of time B.C., approaching the First Coming of Christ. ["He sent his servants." ]{.class_4258}These are defined in Revelation 22:9 and Amos 3:7 as ["the prophets," ]{.class_4258}so we are not surprised to find the prophets pleading with Israel to bring forth the fruit, preceding the First Coming of the Saviour (Isa. 5:1--7). Surely enough, they [beat one]{.class_4262} (1 Kings 22:24), [killed another ]{.class_4262}(Jer. 26:23), and [stoned another ]{.class_4262}(2 Chron. 24:21). ["Again, he sent other servants"---]{.class_4258}and there they come, [Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Haggai, Malachi, ]{.class_4262}etc. And they did unto them likewise (see Matt. 23:34). ["But last of all he sent unto them his son, saying, They will reverence my son."]{.class_4258}

The parable is all too clear. The householder is God the Father and the ["son"]{.class_4258} is God the Son, who deserves [" reverence"]{.class_4258} (Psa. 2:11--12; Heb. 12:28--29). ["He sent his son" ]{.class_4258}(John 3:16, 1:10--13).

21:38 But when the husbandmen saw the son, they said among themselves, This is the heir; come, let us kill him, and let us seize on his inheritance.

39 And they caught him, and cast him out of the vineyard, and slew him.

40 When the lord therefore of the vineyard cometh, what will he do unto those husbandmen?

41 They say unto him, He will miserably destroy those wicked men, and will let out his vineyard unto other []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_417}husbandmen, which shall render him the fruits in their seasons.

42 Jesus saith unto them, Did ye never read in the scriptures, The stone which the builders rejected, the same is become the head of the corner: this is the Lord's doing, and it is marvellous in our eyes?

["They said among themselves, This is the heir." ]{.class_4258}One cannot fail to recognize the similarity between this passage and the affair in Genesis which takes place between Joseph and his brethren. In Genesis 37, Israel sends his firstborn (by Rachel) to see "his brethren" and before Joseph ever came near, ["They conspired against him to slay him" ]{.class_4258}(Gen. 37:18).

["This is the heir." ]{.class_4258}The statement in the parable is astonishing, as it would indicate that the husbandmen [know the rightful heir when they see him! ]{.class_4257}This is remarkable when one considers that the ["husbandmen"]{.class_4258} are the [chief priests and Pharisees to whom He is now talking, and they know that it is they to whom He is now referring ]{.class_4257}(see 21:45). Notice again, that "agnosticism" is just a plain, out‑and‑out, hypocritical lie for WICKEDNESS. The "agnostics" of Christ's audience, who refuse to acknowledge absolute revealed truth, are even called ["wicked"]{.class_4258} by the crowd hearing the parable (21:41), and Jesus does not correct their estimation! An agnostic is a lying fool. No man can be an agnostic and be [honest ]{.class_4257}(John 7:17).

["And cast him out of the vineyard." ]{.class_4258}This will match Hebrews 13:12--13. He is crucified [outside ]{.class_4257}the city (Gordon's Calvary, not the Catholic Calvary inside the city wall).

["When the lord therefore of the vineyard cometh." ]{.class_4258}The reference has immediate and prospective fulfillment at the Second Coming exactly as all the passages up to Acts 7. Prospectively, it will be the future coming of Revelation 11:1--15. But [immediately, ]{.class_4257}the reference deals with the impending destruction of Jerusalem in A.D. 70. [Historically, ]{.class_4257}Jesus, in the parable, is referring to the coming destruction in A.D. 70 and the Church Age to follow (21:43). [Doctrinally, ]{.class_4257}He is referring to the Second Coming to take over the inheritance (21:44). [Spiritually, ]{.class_4257}the passage is teaching a man to receive Jesus, as God's Son, in whom He is well pleased.

["He will miserably destroy those wicked men." ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_418}Thus in A.D. 70, Titus is responsible for a pillage and sacking that saw 500 Jews crucified outside the city walls on trees. The ["miserably destroy"]{.class_4258} is such an historical fact that comment on it would be superfluous.

[​]{.class_4259}["Other husbandmen, which shall render...." ]{.class_4258}The other husbandmen is interpreted in the passage in verse 43 (["a nation"]{.class_4258}). If this nation is the nation of 1 Peter 2:9, then it is the Church, for Israel in Exodus 19:5 has different technology assigned to it. However, both Israel and the church are called ["a nation."]{.class_4258} In Exodus 19:5--6, it is a ["holy nation,"]{.class_4258} and in 1 Peter 2:9, it is a ["holy nation."]{.class_4258}

This has opened the possibility to the idea that 1 Peter 2:9 may be referring to a [future remnant of Israel saved in the Tribulation, ]{.class_4257}which is entirely true. (Note that Matt. 21:44 with Dan. 2 clearly bespeaks of the Second Advent.) But ["the lord\...of the vineyard" ]{.class_4258}who ["cometh" ]{.class_4258}(21:40) is NOT the Second Coming of God the [Son, ]{.class_4257}for the "Lord of the vineyard" was God [the Father ]{.class_4257}(see notes on vs. 33), and the "coming" of verse 41 is to destroy the [Pharisees THEN present---]{.class_4257}see verses 41, 45.

["Did ye never read in the scriptures?" ]{.class_4258}The quotation which follows is found again in 1 Peter 2:1--11, and it is a quotation from Isaiah 28:13--16 and Psalm 118:22. We would say, "Is it marvelous in our eyes?" The question is put, ["It is marvellous?"]{.class_4258}

["The stone which the builders rejected" ]{.class_4258}contains tremendous historical and doctrinal themes. This is the smiting stone of Daniel 2; it is the living Rock of 1 Corinthians 10:1--4 and the stone of Zechariah 3:9. It is the Rock that is rejected by the popes in favor of the "little rock" (Simon Peter), who of course is the [wrong foundation, ]{.class_4257}according to Deuteronomy 32:31. ["The build­ers rejected" ]{.class_4258}indicates that the "builders of the kingdom" are the ones who are so stupid that they do not even know what material to build with; like the Babel builders of Genesis 11, they have "slime" for mortar and brick instead of the true [rock. ]{.class_4257}(Brick is man‑made; rocks are God‑made.)

["The head of the corner" ]{.class_4258}strikingly calls the reader's attention to the fact that the building that is under discussion is not []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw2352.xhtml_page_419}an ordinary building. It is a PYRAMID. A pyramid is the only structure in the world where a "corner stone" is the HEAD stone. (All buildings with corner stones have them on the bottom or the top; there would be four on the bottom and four at the top. But a pyramid is peculiar in that it has not four, but [five ]{.class_4257}corners, and ONE corner has the preeminent place, being the [top ]{.class_4257}corner. Any stone placed on this fifth corner or "head," would be the HEAD STONE.) The builders, then, have laboured to construct a pyramid and are now near completion of the project. With joyful exclamations and manful jests, they slap each other on the back and say, "Well, ole' buddy, it's been a booger, but there she is! Slap on the capstone and we got 'er made!" In comes the capstone. The builders take one look and turn up their noses and say, "Man, that mess? Do you call [that ]{.class_4257}a headstone? Why, a termite could get a meal off that. We want a headstone, man, not a bean bag." In plainer words, the Builders (see vs. 45) rejected the structure. On close inspection, they said, "It's grade C, unfinished, slightly warped, bad grain\...can't use it."

["His own received him not"]{.class_4258} (John 1:11--12). [This capstone to the pyramid is found on the back‑side of an American one dollar bill, suspended, waiting for the Advent; ]{.class_4257}but what money-crazy, college graduate in America would know anything about the symbols involved?

21:43 Therefore say I unto you, The kingdom of God shall be taken from you, and given to a nation bringing forth the fruits thereof.

44 And whosoever shall fall on this stone shall be broken: but on whomsoever it shall fall, it will grind him to powder.

45 And when the chief priests and Pharisees had heard his parables, they perceived that he spake of them.

46 But when they sought to lay hands on him, they feared the multitude, because they took him for a prophet.

(See the comments under the first verses, vss. 33--38, for the integration of the nation ["bringing forth the fruits thereof"]{.class_4258} and the ["kingdom of God"]{.class_4258} in relation to this.)
