37 And he took with him Peter and the two sons of Zebedee, and began to be sorrowful and very heavy.

38 Then saith he unto them, My soul is exceeding sorrowful, even unto death: tarry ye here, and watch with me.

39 And he went a little farther, and fell on his face, and prayed, saying, O my Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me: nevertheless not as I will, but as thou wilt.

40 And he cometh unto the disciples, and findeth them asleep, and saith unto Peter, What, could ye not watch with me one hour?

41 Watch and pray, that ye enter not into temptation: the spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak.

42 He went away again the second time, and prayed, saying, O my Father, if this cup may not pass away from me, except I drink it, thy will be done.

43 And he came and found them asleep again: for their eyes were heavy.

44 And he left them, and went away again, and prayed the third time, saying the same words.

45 Then cometh he to his disciples, and saith unto them, Sleep on now, and take your rest: behold, the hour is at hand, and the Son of man is betrayed into the hands of sinners.

46 Rise, let us be going: behold, he is at hand that doth betray me.

The entire passage has been produced at length, since all parts of it must be connected in proper commentation.

The place is GETHSEMANE: an "olive press." Since olive oil is a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_571}type of the Holy Spirit, the typology of this place is that the very life of the Son of God is being [squeezed out of Him ]{.class_4262}in a travailing agony of prayer. (This is the case in Luke 22, where we read, ["And there appeared an angel unto him from heaven, strengthening him. And being in an agony he prayed more earnestly: and his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down to the ground."]{.class_4258})

This is real prayer. No "saying of prayers" or sweating out "an act of contrition" when an 88 lands too close. No reading from the prayer book or quoting some mumbo‑jumbo from Francis of Assisi. There is no hurried "Hail Mary" or "Our Father" here or a dignified address to the Deity. It is ["not my will, but thine, be done," ]{.class_4258}and a man who never has reached this point has never really [prayed.]{.class_4257}

This, dear reader, is where the psychiatrist enters, making notes and mumbling something about the poor fellow being "psychotic" or a "manic depressive" type. There he is, stretched out on the floor. He has not shaved today. He hasn't eaten. His face is down. His eyes are closed; the fingernails grip the floor boards. The tongue­-in‑groove is wet with tears. The man's nose is stuffed with mucous from crying, so he cannot pray distinctly; and between moans and sobs, an occasional word comes through---"Lord, if there is any way it can be done\...?" "Lord, is there no other way\...?" "Lord, for Christ's sake, have mercy on me and hear me!" "Lord, why can it not be answered...?" At this point the rituals of the Catholic Church and the theological brainwashing of Westcott and Hort seem tritely academic. Here in Geth­semane, the blood, sweat, and tears roll; here, one verse in the book of Job is of greater comfort than a thousand masses, missals, meetings, and mother-confessors. Jesus is praying.

" 'Neath the old olive tree, walked the Sa­viour of light.

All alone, 'neath the dew‑laden trees. Where no star could be found, Jesus knelt on the ground: [and He prayed ]{.class_4257}'neath the old olive trees!"

It was a solitary [prayer. ]{.class_4262}Those who accompanied Him remained at the perimeter of the prayer, and those three who went with Him were overcome by fatigue and the lateness of the hour. It was a [submissive prayer: ]{.class_4262}["not my will, but thine, be done." ]{.class_4258}Notice the progression in the prayer. The first time, it is: "O my Father, []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_572}['if it be possible, let this cup pass\...!'" ]{.class_4258}In these short sentences is summed up the agonizing burst of all the frustrations and sorrows that ever fell on mortal man, and as one has so aptly said: "God sent one Man into this world without sin, but none without sorrow. The Sinless One was ['a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief.'"]{.class_4258} For those of you who find life a miserable and desperate alliance with disease, frustration, suffering, and death\...["Behold the man!"]{.class_4258}

He prayeth!

Jesus is on His face, downward (Matt. 26:39; Luke 22:41). He has fallen under the burden of what is about to take place, and the blood comes oozing through the pores to run on the ground and stain His clothes, inaugurating the beginning of the [New Testament ]{.class_4257}(Matt. 26:28). The blood is shed first by Jesus Himself, before a soldier puts a whip to Him.

The prayer is a [Scriptural prayer; ]{.class_4257}it is addressed to God the Father. Jesus is careful not to mention Mary anywhere in the prayer, and He is very careful not to mention her directly or indirectly to anyone in the garden, including the Father. He is praying to God (Acts 12:5). He is praying for a definite answer, hoping to get it, yet recognizing that it may be contrary to God's purpose at this time. (Here, John R. Rice, the great soul-winner and kindly saint, makes a terrible blunder in exegesis and off go forty or fifty preachers with him. Brother Rice picked up the mistaken notion from Hebrews (!) that Jesus was worried about DYING in the garden and was praying that He could go on to the cross and NOT die in the garden. This peculiar twist would mean that the ["CUP"]{.class_4258} He is praying about is [physical death, ]{.class_4257}and that the prayer being heard was answered in the affirmative (Heb. 5:7). That is, [the cup did pass, ]{.class_4257}and Jesus did [not have to drink of it. ]{.class_4257}There are five things wrong with this interpretation, and since there is nothing right about it, [nothing will ]{.class_4257}be listed in the affirmative.

1\. Jesus never had to worry about dying [anywhere. ]{.class_4257}No one could kill Him (John 7:32, 45, 7:30, 8:59; Luke 4:29--30; John 10:18). The devil could not have killed Him in Gethsemane if he had tried all night.

2\. Jesus never had to worry about death on the cross, for He faced it joyfully and triumphantly (Heb. 12:2; Col. 2:15).

3\. He, therefore, is not praying to be released from crucifixion or from physical death; [He is praying to be released from drinking a cup. ]{.class_4257}Now Scripture with Scripture (that is the only way to do business, boys!), ["What is a ]{.class_4257}cup?"

4\. God's cup, that a man would not want to drink, is clearly defined up until Matthew 26 as the "cup of God's wrath" on sin (note Jer. 25:15; Isa. 51:17; Psa. 75:8). The cup that He is praying to be delivered from is the cup of God's wrath, which is to be poured out on Him while He is being crucified, thus making Him ["to be sin for us"]{.class_4258} (2 Cor. 5:21)!

5\. The prayer is not answered in the affirmative! John 18:11 dispels all doubts about matters of interpretation: ["The cup which my Father hath given me, shall I not drink it?" ]{.class_4258}He does. He drinks it to the dregs. He is not answered with a "Yes"; [He is answered with a "No."]{.class_4257} To make the cup an elementary thing like "physical death" is to knock the meaning clean out of the passage and the heart clean out of the Atonement.

John R. got in trouble where all Arminians, Catholics, Liberals, Campbellites, and Premillennialists get in trouble---MATTHEW, ACTS, and HEBREWS. You cannot take Hebrews 5:7 to interpret Matthew 26:39, for Matthew 26:39 is dealing with "A CUP," and there is NO cup mentioned in Hebrews 5. (One says, "BUT Dr. Ruckman, do you have to be so exact?" Yes, always, when dealing with the infallible, authoritative, perfect, inerrant, everlasting, divine word of the Living God; yes, always.)

["My soul is exceeding sorrowful, even unto death." ]{.class_4258}This is His nearness to "soul‑perishing," not "body‑perishing." Where the second death of Revelation 20 will be the death of the soul, Jesus is getting a foretaste here. \[Without going into the doctrines involved, let it briefly be noted that animals have [dead souls ]{.class_4257}in the Bible (Num. 31:28; Lev. 22:11), and the resumption of the animal form (Isa. 34) occurs in eternal damnation; in this case, the WORM (see crucifixion passages in Psa. 22:6). But once more, I see several patients in the ward whose fever charts are not high enough to contain the rising graphline, so we will go get the ice‑pack of standard exposition and a []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_574}few denominational aspirins. If you want to study it further, it's there, but who studies anything these days, except "translations"? \]

"What, could ye not watch with me one hour?"

Peter does not answer, but the answer is: "Sorry, Lord, I can't make it." A little later Peter is standing badly in need of prayer (vss. 69--75), but prayer puts a lot of people to sleep. A man recommended it as a sure cure for insomnia.

["The spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak." ]{.class_4258}The statement comes to have tremendous doctrinal implications in the later Pauline epistles which deal with the two natures; notice in particular Romans 8:3, 13, 7:24--25; Galatians 5:24--25, 16--17.

["Pray, that ye enter not into temptation." ]{.class_4258}This is nearly verbatim the instruction given in the disciple's prayer of Matthew 6:13. James 1:12 is a blessing promised for enduring temptation, but the gist of the thing is that you will be tempted enough without asking for it; you had better ask to avoid it (see 1 Cor. 10:12--14 and James 1:13--15).

The word ["watch"]{.class_4258} in the passage is a favorite word of New Testament Bible-believing Christians (1 Thess. 5:1--10; 1 Cor. 16:13; Rev 3:2, 16:15; 2 Tim. 4:5; 1 Pet. 4:6--7; Eph. 6:18; Heb. 13:17). A man said that WATCH means "W"---watch your Words. "A"---watch your Actions. "T"---watch your Thoughts. "C"---watch your Companions. And "H"---watch your Heart. There is a Scripture verse to match each of these "watches," and they make excellent preaching material.

"Sleep on now, and take your rest\...the hour is at hand\...Rise, let us be going\...the Son of man is betrayed."

Our Lord finally resigns Himself to the Father's will, and having done so, He tells them to go on and sleep. (They undoubtedly do not hear until later when He says: ["Rise, let us be going,"]{.class_4258} for when they RISE, they rise in sleepy confusion, swinging swords, missing heads, running off naked, and stumbling over olive branches and stones.)

The last time He comes back to them, Luke says, ["He found them sleeping for sorrow," ]{.class_4258}which would literally mean, ["to His sorrow He found that they were still sleeping,"]{.class_4257} but the old English does not botch the text any. A little prayer and study can handle the []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_575}difficulties better than running to Westcott and Hort for a morsel of baked dead‑cat served up as the "bread of life." Arthur Katt, for example ([RSV ]{.class_4257}translator), says that the ["TARRY"]{.class_4258} of Mark 14:34 is "completely archaic," so the [RSV ]{.class_4257}has "remain here." ([What about "Go to bed," said Sleepy Head. "TARRY A WHILE," said Slow, "Put on the pot," said Greedy Glut, "Let's sup before we go!" ]{.class_4257}Do you[ ]{.class_4257}suppose Arthur Katt ever graduated from first grade?)

Adam is lost in a garden. The last Adam (1 Cor. 15:45) created the garden. Adam lost by disobedience, but this One lying on His face is [obeying. ]{.class_4257}Where Adam hides from God in the day, Jesus seeks His face at night.

["Not as I will, but as thou wilt." ]{.class_4258}Reversal of this turned a paradise with God into a desert of thorns. Gethsemane is the next greatest thing in the New Testament outside of the bloody crucifix itself. Here Jesus overcomes self‑will, conquers the temptation to quit, resists the devil to the limit, and emerges bloody, submissive, victorious, and above all---sinless! (see Prov. 16:32).

["Rise, let us be going." ]{.class_4258}There is an interval between verse 41 and verse 42 in Mark 14, as there is between verses 45 and 46 in Matthew 26. (The interval is not given, but the supper took from say 7 until 9 to complete, and the trip to Olivet and the praying from 9 until after midnight; the hour is now somewhere between 1 A.M. and 3 A.M. on Wednesday morning.)

26:47 And while he yet spake, lo, Judas, one of the twelve, came, and with him a great multitude with swords and staves, from the chief priests and elders of the people.

48 Now he that betrayed him gave them a sign, saying, Whomsoever I shall kiss, that same is he: hold him fast.

49 And forthwith he came to Jesus, and said, Hail, master; and kissed him.

50 And Jesus said unto him, Friend, wherefore art thou come? Then came they, and laid hands on Jesus, and took him.

51 And, behold, one of them which were with Jesus stretched out his hand, and drew his sword, and struck a servant of the high priest's, and smote off his ear.

52 Then said Jesus unto him, Put up again thy sword into his place: for all they that take the sword shall perish with the sword.

From here on the narrative moves rapidly. As the disciples stretch and kick the stiffness out of their legs and brush the leaves off the back of their heads, they hear the tramp of feet. Lights flicker through the underbrush, voices are heard, and in a few minutes they find themselves surrounded in a semi‑circle by dimly lit faces, the torches weaving and flickering shadows across them like ripples of water. Angry and scared voices are heard. In the darkness a few faces are recognized, but it is difficult with the wind blowing the torches. Suddenly, the crowd stops. There is a distance of about twenty feet between the front rank and the disciples of Jesus. Before the action of Matthew 26:48--50 takes place, something else goes wrong. John tells about it.

John 18:4--9---["Jesus therefore, knowing all things that should come upon him, went forth, and said unto them, Whom seek ye? They answered him, Jesus of Nazareth. Jesus saith unto them, I am he. And Judas also, which betrayed him, stood with them. As soon then as he had said unto them, I am he, they went backward, and fell to the ground. Then asked he them again, Whom seek ye? And they said, Jesus of Nazareth. Jesus answered, I have told you that I am he: if therefore ye seek me, let these go their way: That the saying might be fulfilled, which he spake, Of them which thou gavest me have I lost none."]{.class_4258}

Then, Peter starts the sword play!

The picture is melodramatic in the extreme. Here are a hundred or more armed men with lanterns and torches, thrashing through the underbrush on a cool spring night, hunting a Jewish Prophet who never carried so much as a pocket knife under His robes. Professional soldiers are in the group, religious leaders (John 18:3), rabble, and officers of the temple. They have quarter‑staves and swords for weapons and a devil for a guide. They interrupt their victim at a prayer meeting in the middle of the night, without a warrant, subpoena, or summons, and they come to arrest Him.

Held at bay, they suddenly seem paralyzed with indecision, and a quiet voice from the figure ahead of them says: "If you're looking for Jesus of Nazareth\..., I               AM\...." \[Note the ["he"]{.class_4258} in italics (John 18:6, 8) showing the King James is still the only honest translation on the market, and showing also that Jesus is speaking the Jehovistic [tetra­grammation---]{.class_4257}["I am"]{.class_4258} (Exod. 3:14).\]

Upon the utterance of these words---["I am"]{.class_4258} the posse stumbles backwards over each other and, to their own amazement and embarrassment, fall all over each other as they step back. [From the ground, ]{.class_4257}they are asked again for whom they are looking (John 18:7). They get the same answer. They still cannot move forward until Peter breaks the spell. What has happened in the interval is that Judas, having been knocked flat by the pronouncement of the Deity he is about to betray, gets up, whisks the dust off him, comes forward, and completes the pre‑arranged transaction with a kiss!

Again, it is brought most forcibly to our attention that a man can KNOW what he is doing and know the result of it and go ahead knowing that it is willful sin and merits only death (Rom. 1:32). Judas knows the One he is facing is not merely a Jewish Prophet, but his own Creator, the ["I AM"]{.class_4258} who spoke on eternity's morning and struck off sparks from the wheel of divine fire that threw blazing solar systems into their orbits. This One he is betraying is the One who slept under stars at night that were no more than the glittering bands wherewith He clasped the robes of His glory. Still, Judas gets up, goes forward (my, what an effort!), [and sells Him out. ]{.class_4257}Anything for a buck!

Jesus is calm as ever: ["Friend, wherefore art thou come?"]{.class_4258}[ "What are you doing here, Judas? Did you come to have prayer with me, friend?"]{.class_4257}

["Wherefore art thou come?" ]{.class_4258}[The New Bible Commentary ]{.class_4257}says charitably about the passage: "The [Authorized Version]{.class_4257} is most certainly wrong," and makes the question a command. We may add: "[The New Bible Commentary ]{.class_4257}is most certainly wrong and should never be considered worthy of intelligent comment or study."

"Betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?"

Open goes the big hairy hand of Simon Peter. Down goes the bronzed and weather‑beaten fist on the sword haft---ssswish! Out []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_578}comes a side‑weapon in the hand of a six‑foot man, who weighs over 200 pounds, and SWWAPPP! Every man for himself! In the dark, Peter doesn't pick a target. He just swings at the first man near (John 18:10). Malchus ducks---but not far enough, and a stroke, intended to decapitate him like a chicken, goes flying across his shoulder and takes the right ear clean off. Blood spouts into the air, and whoever had the other sword among the disciples (Luke 22:38) draws it and prepares to develop the situation ("mount the attack," I believe, is the expression).

At this crucial moment, when the soldiers opposite Peter, James, and John unsheath their swords and the quarter‑staves drop to the guard position, Jesus says: ["Suffer ye thus far" ]{.class_4258}(Luke 22:51). He turns to Peter and says: ["Put up thy sword into the sheath" ]{.class_4258}(John 18:11), and immediately He turns and heals the stump of the ear that was just amputated. The implication is that right there before them the blood stopped and a new ear popped onto the head!

Everyone is concreted with shock. There they stand, knives drawn, swords unsheathed, staves lowered, shaking from head to foot; and before them, the ["I am"]{.class_4258} of Exodus puts an ear back on without a splint, compress, penicillin, morphine, plasma, sulpha pills, or an oxygen tent. No one is going to take the initiative again, for a while at least. Judas is breathing heavily, looking wildly in all directions, expecting at any moment for a bolt of lightning to send him home to the pit. Peter is standing with his weapon half put‑up, glowering like Vulcan at the rabble ahead of him. The priests and rabbis are licking their lips, glassy‑eyed at the miracle that has just taken place, and the soldiers are debating whether to start a hand‑to‑hand match in the dark, or just forget the whole church‑split and go home and go to bed.

Jesus speaks again: ["Put up again thy sword into his place: for all they that take the sword shall perish with the sword. Thinkest thou that I cannot now pray to my Father, and he shall presently give me more than twelve legions of angels? But how then shall the scriptures be fulfilled, that thus it must be? Be ye come out, as against a thief, with swords and staves? When I was daily with you in the temple, ye stretched forth no hands against me: but this is your hour, and the power of darkness" ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_579}(Matt. 26:52--54; Luke 22:52--53).

The Pharisee recognizes his dog‑tag: ["Your hour\...the power of darkness!" ]{.class_4258}That's him! That's the long‑robed father who compassed land and sea to get converts twice as damned as he is! He knows his name! ["Take Him!" ]{.class_4257}one of them yells. "Grab Him!" screams another. ["Then took they him" ]{.class_4258}(Luke 22:54), and all His disciples ["forsook him, and fled"]{.class_4258} (Mark 14:50; Matt. 26:56).

There goes Peter, there goes James, there goes John, there go Philip, Bartholomew, Thomas, James, Judas (not Iscariot), Simon the Canaanite, Andrew, and Matth­ew over the hill, A.W.O.L., like ducks on a pond dodging lightning. Mark nearly gets caught and slips out of his overcoat, like a sailor getting out of a Mae West to go through a ventilator on a submerged boat. Thomas is running with both hands over his head and muttering, "I knew it! I knew it! I knew it was too good to last!" There goes Matthew, wondering what he is going to tell Zacchaeus when he sees him, and there goes the Big Fisherman, running, not because of fright, but because he saw that it was suicide to stay where he could not use a sword. He had gotten to the 38th parallel, and the government had called off his assault. What was the use? Simon Peter was "put out" by his Lord.

26:53 Thinkest thou that I cannot now pray to my Father, and he shall presently give me more than twelve legions of angels?

54 But how then shall the scriptures be fulfilled, that thus it must be?

55 In that same hour said Jesus to the multitudes, Are ye come out as against a thief with swords and staves for to take me? I sat daily with you teaching in the temple, and ye laid no hold on me.

56 But all this was done, that the scriptures of the prophets might be fulfilled. Then all the disciples forsook him, and fled.

57 And they that had laid hold on Jesus led him away to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and the elders were assembled.

58 But Peter followed him afar off unto the high priest's []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_580}palace, and went in, and sat with the servants, to see the end.

Before the final "bolt for the bushes," the Lord states to the disciples that He is surrendering [on purpose. ]{.class_4257}This is what defeats Simon Peter (see Matt. 16:22). Pete figures if you CAN win, do it. Most commercial fishermen figure the same way; so do West Point Generals. ["Twelve legions of angels"]{.class_4258} would bring on a massacre that would make two world wars look like a fire sale. One angel is capable of killing 185,000 men (2 Kings 19:35). Twelve legions would be about 72,000 angels, each killing 185,000 men apiece. This catastrophe, if it had occurred, could have neatly obliterated the entire population of the world several times---over 13 billion men. Jesus does not need Peter's sidearm.

["As against a thief\...." ]{.class_4258}That is exactly how they DID come out against Him, and to prove it, they wind up crucifying Him between two THIEVES. Christ's charge that they had not taken Him in the temple is mortifying if one examines it. What it all amounts to is that religious devilment is always carried on [undercover ]{.class_4257}(Mark 14:1--2). As it was, so shall it ever be. Today, the real devilment is carried on behind closed doors by select minority groups that railroad devices in, against the knowledge and will of the majority. The trick is to "bring the man before the council," [where the people cannot hear his defense. ]{.class_4257}Most ordinary people are fair‑minded and have common sense when it comes to matters of beliefs. The trouble is always with the [minority leadership ]{.class_4257}that wishes to control the crowd for the purposes of obtaining their [money. ]{.class_4257}(See remarks under Matt. 23:13--29.) Thus, we find the Lord being dragged off [at night, ]{.class_4257}with no [official judge present, ]{.class_4257}two [bribed witnesses ]{.class_4257}against Him, [no witness ]{.class_4257}allowed for His [defense, ]{.class_4257}no lawyer to [represent Him, ]{.class_4257}and [violence unchecked ]{.class_4257}in the courtroom. Thus, religious bigots and sacramental hypocrites cannot stand the open, public display of comparative reasoning from the Scriptures. It is absolutely essential that they shut off the masses from hearing and seeing their own unbelief and ignorance of the Scriptures.

Jesus is saying (and He deliberately [aims ]{.class_4257}at the scribes, Pharisees, and Sadducees), ["I sat daily WITH YOU teaching\...." ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_581}And He had. Why did they not answer Him then? Because they were ignorant of the word, hated it, and neglected it (Matt. 22:29), [and, therefore, could not answer. ]{.class_4257}Their only "answer" was swords and staves under the cover of darkness. This is the answer Romanism has given to Bible‑believing Christianity for 1,500 years. In the [New York Herald ]{.class_4257}(May 7, 1901), you will find: "I do not doubt, if they were strong enough, that the Catholic people would hinder, [even by death, ]{.class_4257}if necessary, the spread of heretical errors among the people, and I say [rightly so. Certainly the church does consider Protestants heretics." ]{.class_4257}This is the statement of Father Harney (a Paulist father in St. Peter's RCC in New Brunswick, New Jersey). The modern ecumenical move­ment is for the purpose of putting the Catholic Church into [that position ]{.class_4257}of strength.

["But Peter followed him afar off." ]{.class_4258}Mark says, ["And Peter followed him afar off." ]{.class_4258}Up until here Peter had been following so closely that he occasionally stumbled over a truth too near to see (John 13:6, 8). Now, he sort of has "the ginger taken out of him." He is following afar off to see the end. It is his [heart ]{.class_4257}that is compelling him to do so.

Somewhere out in the garden, Peter ditched his sidearm, and looking back over his head many times, he made his way down the west side of the hill and across Kidron back to Jerusalem. If he hadn't yet begun to "curse and swear" (Matt. 26:74), he was thinking seriously about it. He had only been trying to protect his Lord. Had not Jesus told them that very night to sell something and BUY a sword? Why all this tripe about "perishing by the sword?" Who cares anyway? How are you going to enthrone Jesus as King and bring in the Kingdom unless you do it with violence (Matt. 11:12)? Isn't that what He said? What was He doing anyway, trying to kid them?

The mind of Simon Peter would have been something to look at that night as he hid behind the eastern gate and then followed the rabble to Caiaphas' house. Did you ever read the mind of a [commercial fisherman ]{.class_4262}who wanted to get in a fight [and had to back out? ]{.class_4262}(Yes, there is plenty you can't get from the Greek---just plenty! Coy Rafield or Captain Anderson in Panama City, Florida, could give you a greater insight into the text than a volume of notes by Trench and Alford four inches thick.) ["But Peter followed him afar []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_582}off\...to see the end."]{.class_4258}

26:59 Now the chief priests, and elders, and all the council, sought false witness against Jesus, to put him to death;

60 But found none: yea, though many false witnesses came, yet found they none. At the last came two false witnesses,

61 And said, This fellow said, I am able to destroy the temple of God, and to build it in three days.

62 And the high priest arose, and said unto him, Answerest thou nothing? what is it which these witness against thee?

63 But Jesus held his peace. And the high priest answered and said unto him, I adjure thee by the living God, that thou tell us whether thou be the Christ, the Son of God.

64 Jesus saith unto him, Thou hast said: nevertheless I say unto you, Hereafter shall ye see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven.

65 Then the high priest rent his clothes, saying, He hath spoken blasphemy; what further need have we of witnesses? behold, now ye have heard his blasphemy.

66 What think ye? They answered and said, He is guilty of death.

67 Then did they spit in his face, and buffeted him; and others smote him with the palms of their hands,

68 Saying, Prophesy unto us, thou Christ, Who is he that smote thee?

The entire scene must be read in the light of the Deuteronomic laws that are supposed to govern the trial and condemnation of an offender. In Deuteronomy 19:17, not only are priests to be present, [but judges are to preside at official trials; ]{.class_4257}that is, the [religious ]{.class_4257}leaders are not capable of giving a fair decision without consulting the civil leaders. It is the [judge ]{.class_4257}who is to make the inquisition, [not the priests ]{.class_4257}(as in the case of the Spanish Inquisition---see Deut. []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_583}19:18). This inquisition is first to hear of it, then to enquire diligently, then make certain (Deut. 17:4). Two or three witnesses are necessary (Deut. 17:6), and if their witness is found to be false, [they are to receive the sentence ]{.class_4257}that was passed out to the man on trial (Deut. 19:18--21).

["False witness against Jesus\...But found none: yea, though many false witnesses came, yet found they none." ]{.class_4258}The explanation for this apparent discrepancy is found in Mark 14:56, where the false witnesses are nullified by the fact that they can't get together on their testimonies. The testimonies contradict each other and, therefore, cannot be accepted as positive evidence in the trial. Even the two who finally were used to "cinch" the case did not agree (Mark 14:58), for one added: ["without hands" ]{.class_4258}(which is a rebound from being under conviction after hearing Matt. 21:42--45). (Dan. 2---the ["stone\...cut out without hands."]{.class_4258}) The witness has this passage eating away at his vitals and ADDS it to the real statement in John 2:19 about "raising the temple up." Thus, one witness cites John 2:19, and the other cites John 2:19 with the addition of part of Daniel 2:45. They cannot get together on what He said, so the High Priest asks sternly: ["Answerest thou nothing? what is it which these witness against thee?" ]{.class_4258}(see Mark 14:60). ["But Jesus held his peace" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:63). Like a lamb before its shearers, so He opens not His mouth. They finally aggravate Him to open it so they can slap it shut. This is done by asking Jesus about [His doctrine.]{.class_4257}

Now the whole scene is out‑of‑hand. No charges are being brought, and none are being preferred, and no one is trying to prove them. What they have done is, they have arrested an innocent Man without a warrant, have hailed Him to a private Gestapo meeting (without due process of the law), and in order to kill the Man, they are going to make Him talk about [His religious beliefs so ]{.class_4257}they can hang Him on a prejudice. ["Jesus answered him, I spake openly to the world ]{.class_4258}(true)[;]{.class_4258} [I ever taught in the synagogue, and in the temple ]{.class_4258}(true)[,]{.class_4258} [whither the Jews always resort; and in secret have I said nothing" ]{.class_4258}(true), if the essentials of His teaching are considered (see Matt. 24:3; Mark 9:28). ["Why askest thou me? Ask them which heard me, what I have said unto them: behold, THEY KNOW what I said" ]{.class_4258}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_584}(see John 18:20--21). Jesus' answer is spoken politely and quietly, but in the words are the bite and bitterness of a rattlesnake: ["They know what I said!" ]{.class_4258}What a thing to say to a long‑robed priest who prided himself on how much [he knew ]{.class_4257}about Christ!

Every word that Jesus said is true, but it is said in such a way that an officer of the temple standing near responds by slapping Jesus in the face with the palm of his hand. ["Answerest thou the high priest so?" ]{.class_4258}\[Paul answers the same way (Acts 23:1--3), except Paul, being a sinner, temporarily loses his temper; Jesus keeps His.\] ["Jesus answered him, If I have spoken evil, bear witness of the evil: but if well, why smitest thou me?"]{.class_4258} (John 18:23).[ ]{.class_4258}There is no way to answer the question. Either way the officer has sinned and broken the law he is pledged to uphold; either way, the court in charge of the decision is guilty of breaking the law. (An excellent case is Oxford, Mississippi, 1962, where the civil rights of 8,000 people were violated and the government forced its citizens to expend \$20,000 a day to give ONE man his rights. It's like the man who finally shot the rabbit, but he killed three beagles, two cows, a man, and a horse before he got the rabbit; but he got the rabbit, and his name was Meredith when he got him \[a black man who was killed then\]!)

The whole trial is a farce. The victim has been officially condemned by the judge, jury, and witnesses [before ]{.class_4257}He arrives in the courtroom. He is now at Caiaphas'; the high priest arises from his ex cathedra place and says solemnly (no doubt crossing himself while he does it!), ["I adjure thee by the living God, that thou tell us whether thou be the Christ, the Son of God" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:63). ["Art thou the Christ, the Son of the Blessed?" ]{.class_4258}(Mark 14:61). The question is direct examination, and the courtroom waits with bated breath and bared fangs for the answer. It comes, and when it comes, it comes so suddenly and so plainly that it puts the courtroom in an uproar, and no order is ever restored to it.

["And Jesus said, I am"]{.class_4258} (Mark 14:62).

That settles it. He professed to be what He was, and He either was lying or telling the truth. If He was lying, He deserved what He got, for He plainly blasphemed the name of Jehovah, ["I am,"]{.class_4258} and all []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_585}understood that this is what He had done (John 10:21--36). If Jesus was [not lying, ]{.class_4257}then every Liberal and Modernist in the country is insulting God Almighty every Sunday by standing in a Trinitarian pulpit and presenting Christ as anything less than [God manifest in the flesh, dying for the sins of His creation and creatures. ]{.class_4257}(See 1 Tim. 3:16; John 3:16.)

["Thou hast said\...I am"]{.class_4258} (Matt. 26:64; Mark 14:62).

There it is in plain English: "How about it, are you really the Son of God the Blessed, the Saviour who is the Son of God?" "You [said it. ]{.class_4257}I'M IT." ("You told it right, buddy, I'm it and no one else!")

["Nevertheless I say unto you, Hereafter shall ye see the Son of man sitting on the right hand of power, and coming in the clouds of heaven" ]{.class_4258}(Matthew and Mark). The reference is clearly to the Second Coming of Christ, discussed in detail under Matthew 24:30--31 and 17:2, 5. (The thing nearly takes place in Acts 7, but upon the official rejection of the King, the gospel moves on to the Gentiles and the Church Age grows rapidly into an extensive period of 1,950 years before the fulfillment of the verse \[Matt. 26:64\]. See comments under 3:2, 4:17, 10:6--7.)

Sitting helplessly before His accusers and betrayers, the Lord says this quietly, slowly, earnestly, and majestically. ["Thou hast said\...I am." ]{.class_4258}From here on in the courtroom, it is too wet to plow. RIPPP! go the robes of the high priest (as he puts on a little religious playlet for the passion week), ["He hath spoken blasphemy; what further need have we of witnesses? Behold, now ye have heard his blasphemy. What think ye? They answered and said, He is guilty of death!" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:65--66), and they ALL condemned Him to be guilty of death (Mark 14:64).

(Weirdly enough \[by some strange stroke of the pen\], the verse on which the false priest, Caiaphas, accuses the true High Priest, Jesus, of BLASPHEMY is verse 65 \[5 times 13\], containing 26 words \[2 times 13\] in Chapter 26 \[2 times 13\]; but after all, the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 is ["just a translation," isn't it? ]{.class_4257}Or is it?)

Now come the blows. Some spit on Him, some slap His face, some pluck out His beard. "Oh, so you're the Son of God, eh? Then surely you must know everything! WHO HIT YOU? Blind man's bluff!"

SMACK! SMACK! SMACK!

They have tied a towel around His face so He cannot see (Mark 14:65), and then they paste Him with their palms and fists until the effect is as described in Isaiah 52:14 and 50:6. Jesus, sitting patiently under the abuse, could have called out the name, rank, and serial number of every man in the outfit, but He ["reviled not again" ]{.class_4258}(1 Pet. 2:21--23), nor answered (Isa. 53:7).

This is the hour when the "Prince of this World" vents his hatred and wrath on the rightful heir to the throne (John 14:30). (Since the main subject of the Bible is a Kingdom, and that Kingdom has two claimants \[Luke 4:6\], it is remarkably fitting that at this juncture, the usurper should "pour it on" and try to the limit of his strength to get his Rival to quit.) There is more here than a room full of shouting, cursing, venomous Pharisees "taking it out" on a Prophet named Jesus. There in the early morning light, amidst the slappings, buffetings, blows, and spittle, a soul sits enthroned in the Sinless Son of God and faces another soul in the spirit realm and says to him: ["He is near that justifieth me; who will contend with me? let us stand together: who is mine adversary? LET HIM COME NEAR TO ME!!"]{.class_4258} (Isa. 50:8---note the context!)

Here is an open challenge to ["the prince of the power of the air,"]{.class_4258} the ruler of spiritual darkness, to come into close quarters and "try Jesus for size!" ["Let him come near!" ]{.class_4258}To those who have read Jude 9 and 1 Peter 5:8 (and believed them), the challenge is blood‑curdling. Jesus is stepping "into the ring" with the most powerful being in the universe, outside the Godhead. He is challenging the heavy‑weight for the title, and this heavy‑weight [has never lost a battle until this time. ]{.class_4257}Ten billion championship bouts---ten billion K.O.'s in the first round. Count them!

Adam, hiding.

Eve, lying.

Noah, drunk.

Joseph, lying.

Jacob, swindling.

David, adultery.

Moses, murder.

Daniel, idolatry.

Paul, resisting God's will.

Peter, cursing.

Lot, backslidden.

Isaac, lying.

Abraham, lying.

Jeremiah, not trusting.

Job, complaining.

COUNT THEM!

That old veteran of ancient days, his majesty "the Devil," never had any trouble K.O.‑ing the greatest, best, wisest, and holiest men who ever lived. And now a Man sits before him, blindfolded with spit and blood running down His face, and says: ["Who is mine adversary? let him come near!" ]{.class_4258}[In he comes, both fists flailing, and brass knuckles on both hands ]{.class_4257}(Job 40:18). Heaven, earth, and hell look on as the greatest conflict that ever took place in human history begins (Col. 2:15).

26:69 Now Peter sat without in the palace: and a damsel came unto him, saying, Thou also wast with Jesus of Galilee.

70 But he denied before them all, saying, I know not what thou sayest.

71 And when he was gone out into the porch, another maid saw him, and said unto them that were there, This fellow was also with Jesus of Nazareth.

72 And again he denied with an oath, I do not know the man.

73 And after a while came unto him they that stood by, and said to Peter, Surely thou also art one of them; for thy speech bewrayeth thee.

74 Then began he to curse and to swear, saying, I know not the man. And immediately the cock crew.

75 And Peter remembered the word of Jesus, which said unto him, Before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice. And he went out, and wept bitterly.

As Satan and the Lord Jesus close for combat and the gunnery of Hell opens in the face of the Sinless Son of God, the favorite disciple is [warming his hands with the enemy ]{.class_4257}(see Mark 14:54)! Even though []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_588}he has just been called a "friend" and NOT a "servant" (John 15:15), he is with the ["servants"]{.class_4258} (Mark 14:54). He is in the palace, but since it contains an outer courtyard, he is actually outdoors and is warming himself.

For a moment let us go back and pick up the trail of Simon Peter after he left Gethsemane in a frustrated rage.

Peter waited at the gate until the crowd passed through dragging Jesus; he then followed the crowd first to Annas, then to Caiaphas (John 18:13). Both evidently lived in the same quarters for Peter is without both inquisitions while they are taking place (John 18:15, 26). Whereas Peter stayed outside in the open hallway to warm himself, John goes right on in where the accusations are taking place (John 18:15). True to the end, the beloved disciple not only goes through the judgment with Jesus, but right on up to the cross (John 19:26) and to the tomb (John 20). Old John [has it, ]{.class_4257}whatever "IT" is!

Peter is experiencing mixed emotions, like the Scotchman who saw his mother‑in‑law drive off the cliff in his new Cadillac. Too ashamed to flee, he loves Jesus to the end; yet too big a coward to go in and be taken with Him, [he warms himself without ]{.class_4262}(John 18:18). But the word "coward" is not exactly the right word. Peter never was a coward and is not one here. He drew his sword, expecting to fight to the death, and if Jesus hadn't stopped him, he would have gone down threshing out human lives like a power‑mower. Peter is not a coward in [physical combat. ]{.class_4262}He is a coward when it comes to sitting still and taking a beating, when he is unable to retaliate! And that is what he [sees ]{.class_4262}going on inside the palace; and he hears it, and his heart must have been so shaken and torn that he was nearly in tears. He wanted to help, but he knew if he tried, the Lord would stop him. In despair, the Big Fisherman stands over the fire gazing into its depths, oblivious to everything except a deep, consuming desire to FIGHT. At this moment, a shy maiden's voice queries: ["Art not thou also one of this man's disciples?"]{.class_4258}

John has sicked a servant girl on Pete!

(Look at John 18:15--17 and see what happened. John knew where Peter's heart was, and he had no doubt at all that old Pete would just step in with him and see the thing out. But to make sure that it is Peter's free will, he asks the girl to just ASK if Peter is one of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_589}the disciples.)

["Art not thou also one of this man's disciples?" ]{.class_4258}Without taking his eyes off the fire or looking up, Peter growls: ["I am not!"]{.class_4258} [(He almost said: "H‑‑‑ no!") ]{.class_4257}Peter was telling the truth. Salvation is one thing (John 1:12--14), and discipleship (Acts 11:26) is something else. Peter responded to salvation before discipleship, and so must it ever be (John 1:41--42; Luke 5:1--10). You can't live the "Sermon on the Mount" until you've visited Mount Calvary. Peter is a saved man, but his discipleship isn't worth the pen and ink it would take to describe it.

The girl says: ["Thou also was with Jesus of Galilee" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:69), ["but he denied before them all."]{.class_4258} What Peter says is: ["I know not, neither understand I what thou sayest." ]{.class_4258}This is almost the truth. He is no longer a true disciple, and the old boy has so much on his mind, it could be truthfully said that he didn't know what she was saying, but Peter never intended all this. He only intended to say: "Get the --- outta here! I don't know Him, never did, got nothin' to do with Him; don't wanna ever hear of Him again, and any man that would do all that talking and then when the showdown came\...!" That is what he intended.

You say I am reading between the lines?

Well, you see, [knowing commercial fishermen ]{.class_4257}is better than [knowing Greek ]{.class_4257}when you get to Matthew 26. Peter wheels on his foot and walks a few steps (Mark 14:68) and in so doing is unaware of the fact that a rooster has just crowed the 3 o'clock call, preceding the 6 o'clock (Gallicinum Matuti­num) call. Lost in his own thoughts and torn with conflicting desires, Peter forgets the Scripture---Matthew 26:31, 34.

Peter has no sooner gone out onto the porch when another maid sees him and turns to the other folks around the fire and says: ["This fellow was also with Jesus" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:71). Luke 22:58 states,[ "Thou art also of them," ]{.class_4258}and John 18, ["Art not thou also one of his disciples?" ]{.class_4258}The Lord is pouring salt into Peter's wounded heart. "You were with Him? You are one of them. [Are you not one of them?" ]{.class_4257}God is trying desperately to use the devil to get His own child right. He wants Peter to confess, even if it does mean a shameful, humiliating, sheep‑like death. But Peter has too much wolf left in []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_590}him to go down without a bleat.

["I am not!"]{.class_4258} (John 18:25). Man, I swear by God Almighty (Matt 26:72), ["I do not know the man!"]{.class_4258}

Thus, with an oath, Peter figures he has shut everybody up. (This is why men swear anyway. They want to emphasize the AUTHORITY by which they speak.)

But things do not go well at all. Far from shutting them up, this last additional statement gets some of their heads together, and they figure things out. "Two‑and‑two is four, man; this bird has a Galilaean accent. I can spot it anywhere. My daddy fished up there for G.C. Stein and Company for nearly ten years and he says\...." And so it goes.

The next time Peter nears the fire, those that stand by say: ["Surely\...thou art a Galilaean, and thy speech agreeth thereto" ]{.class_4258}(Mark 14:70), and another chimes in with: ["Surely thou also art one of them; for thy speech bewrayeth thee!" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:73). Before Peter can answer, a skeleton comes out of the closet (John 18:26) and says: ["Did not I see thee in the garden with him?" ]{.class_4258}(This is a kinsman to Malchus, who was nearly decapitated by Peter's sword.)

There they stand like a pack of fice terriers around a great dane. All Peter has to do is sail into them, and he can probably floor three of them before they pin him down. But what is the use? He had a sword, and Jesus wouldn't let him use it. They are going to kill Jesus now, and if he starts anything this late, they'll get him too.

A fierce, frustrated rage wells up in the old fisherman, and Matthew 12:34 fulfills[ ]{.class_4257}itself in a violent outburst of filthy language and unholy railing: "YOU \*+t!!@=t!+t get off my back! I don't know a \*&++t\*11 thing about this man, of whom you speak!" He is not merely SWEARING (defined in Heb. 6:13); [he is cursing. ]{.class_4257}And believe me, construction men, infantrymen, and commercial fishermen [can curse. ]{.class_4257}Peter is so busy staring down the shocked faces in front of him that he barely hears it, but he does hear it faintly---A ROOSTER CROWS.

At this tense and terrific moment, Jesus is being led through the hall, past the fire, from Caiaphas (John 18:28) to ["the hall of judgment."]{.class_4258} The big fisherman slowly raises his head like a bulldog []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3314.xhtml_page_591}that has just dropped a bone, and with the cock‑crow ringing in his ears, he sees staring at him the [eyes of his Lord ]{.class_4257}(Luke 22:6 1).

"I told you so, Peter---[before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice]{.class_4258}" (Luke 22:61).

That look brings home the truth with horrible reality (Mark 14:72). In a daze, the poor, bewildered, old rascal looks at the circle around him (who are still paralyzed by the profuse stream of profanity they have just heard), and he says in a pitifully soft voice: "Excc‑cc‑use me, folks. I gotta g‑gg‑go home." He steps out of the circle, nearly knocking three people down, and walks dumb-struck to the gate and pushes it open. Once outside, a bitter and horrible cry is heard as Peter turns his face up to Heaven, covers his eyes with hands, and runs back to the Mount of Olives TO PRAY.

["And he went out, and wept bitterly" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 26:75).

While Jesus won the battle inside the palace, seated with Satan, Peter lost the battle in the courtyard, standing with the servants.[ If you can't get your feet warm anywhere but at the Devil's fire, GO AHEAD AND GET COLD FEET.]{.class_4257}

[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml}

# Chapter 27 {.heading_84-0}

27:1 When the morning was come, all the chief priests and elders of the people took counsel against Jesus to put him to death:

2 And when they had bound him, they led him away, and delivered him to Pontius Pilate the governor.

3 Then Judas, which had betrayed him, when he saw that he was condemned, repented himself, and brought again the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders,

4 Saying, I have sinned in that I have betrayed the innocent blood. And they said, What is that to us? see thou to that.

5 And he cast down the pieces of silver in the temple, and departed, and went and hanged himself.

6 And the chief priests took the silver pieces, and said, It is not lawful for to put them into the treasury, because it is the price of blood.

7 And they took counsel, and bought with them the potter's field, to bury strangers in.

8 Wherefore that field was called, The field of blood, unto this day.

In describing the final hours of Christ's earthly ministry to Israel (before the crucifixion), the Holy Spirit has seen fit to give us a remarkable "flash‑back" into the lives of two other men---Peter and Judas. Right here at the end (where Jesus and Pilate take the spotlight), in the most terrible and trying times of their lives, Matthew has just devoted seven verses to Peter's crisis and breakdown; he now swings the camera to the left and picks up Judas (described in eight verses) before the gospel account is through with him forever.

["When the morning was come." ]{.class_4258}To pick up the exact details here, we must go to Luke 23; John 18--19, and since so much is found in these passages that describe what went on after ["they led him away\...to Pontius Pilate the governor" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 27:2), we shall []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_593}spare comment until Matthew 27:11 to pick up the dramatic scene.

["Judas\...when he saw that he was condemned." ]{.class_4258}There is no surface explanation for Judas' remorse at this point. It looks as though he had hoped that Christ would NOT be condemned, for he confesses in verse 4: ["I have sinned\...I have betrayed the innocent blood." ]{.class_4258}This is the confession of a criminal caught in the act, [knowing ]{.class_4257}that he has done wrong. Yet Judas knew their intention was to [kill Jesus, ]{.class_4257}months before he ever closed the bargain (Matt. 26:15, 21:39, 45, 12:14). There is only one avenue of thought that is left open to follow: Judas must have thought that before condemnation could be passed, Jesus would have done a miracle and [brought in the kingdom, ]{.class_4257}thereby overcoming His enemies. But this idea is unstable and insecure. If Christ HAD brought in the kingdom, what would He have done to JUDAS? Did Judas think that Christ would be exposed as a mere human when cornered by the council, and that they would turn Him loose in disgrace after a good "chewing out"? Whatever shady ramblings went on in Judas' mind, three things were apparent. One, he had made a mistake. Two, he had made a [bad ]{.class_4257}mistake. Three, he had innocent blood on his hands as a murderer.

["Repented himself." ]{.class_4258}The wording gives a splendid illustration of one of the kinds of repentance defined in 2 Corinthians 7:10. One is called ["godly sorrow,"]{.class_4258} and the other is ["the sorrow of the world." ]{.class_4258}Peter illustrates the first type perfectly. His sorrow is of a godly sort. Judas illustrates the second type---it is an earthly, human thing. Peter's is a grief‑stricken heart for being wrong. Judas' is a despair for messing up a plan. The source of Peter's repentance is [a look from Jesus Christ. ]{.class_4257}The source of Judas' repentance is [himself ]{.class_4257}(27:3). Both Peter and Judas were sorrowful, both wanted to do right; both made restitution. But the PLACE of repentance was wrong; while Peter confessed to his Lord, Judas confessed to the priest. I said: ["Judas confessed to the priest."]{.class_4257} I said: ["Judas confessed to the priest!"]{.class_4257} Did you get that? If you didn't, you will find it in Matthew 27:3 in a Protestant or Catholic Bible---["chief priests." ]{.class_4258}Wrong place (1 Tim. 2:5).

(Like O'Malley asked the father: "Father, what is consumption?" The black‑robed Baalite on the other side of the partition thought: []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_594}"Boy, I've got him now; I'll get him confessing things he wouldn't tell anyone!"

"Patrick, my boy," the father said, "consumption is something that folks get from running around late at night and living like the devil and drinking and carousing; that's what it is---why?"

"Well, I was just wonderin," said Patrick O'Malley, "I read in the paper this morning where [the Pope ]{.class_4257}had consumption.")

Judas is going to the wrong "father."

The results of Peter's repentance and Judas' repent­ance show an obvious dissimilarity in their quality. Peter reaps a broken spirit, a contrite heart, and a humbleness of mind (see John 21:15). Judas reaps a suicide by hanging and a bottomless pit for a "home."

["I have sinned." ]{.class_4258}When David says this (2 Samuel) and the Prodigal says it (Luke 15), it is the real thing, but when Pharaoh and Balaam say it (Exod. 10:16; Num. 22:34), it means little more than with Judas here. It simply means, "I've made a mistake. I'm sorry for what I've done." This clearly points out the difference between [evangelical repentance ]{.class_4257}taught by the Bible and [church repentance ]{.class_4257}(penance) taught by Campbellites and Catholics.

Whereas the Catholic and Campbellite show how they are so sorry for what they have done that they submit to a sacrament to make it right, the true Believer is so sorry for [what he is ]{.class_4257}that he turns to Christ [only ]{.class_4257}for salvation. In a nut‑shell, being sorry for what [you've done ]{.class_4257}is the sorrow of this world that worketh death. Being sorry for what you [are will ]{.class_4257}turn you, heart and soul, to Jesus as your own Saviour (see Rom. 10:9­--10).

There is all the difference in the world between these two men. Both follow Christ, both work miracles, both take communion, both are members of the local church, both are baptized, and both profess "CHRISTIANITY." One is a saint; the other is a devil.

"What is that to us? see thou to that."

This is Judas' welcome home from his fine-feathered friends who had "flocked together." Instead of "We are so sorry, let us pray for you," it is "Tough apples, old buddy. That's [your ]{.class_4257}affair." In reality, they could have said little else, for they were [in on it themselves. ]{.class_4257}If they got him right, they would have to confess what he had confessed; thus, there are two sides to every confessional booth, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_595}it might be amusing and interesting to ask the "father" about his private life the next time you go!

["And he cast down the pieces of silver in the temple." ]{.class_4258}(Hence Zech. 11:13---["the house of the ]{.class_4258}[Lord]{.class_4267}[."]{.class_4258}) It is said to be "cast unto the potter," because although it is cast down on the floor of the temple, it is immediately picked up and transferred to a deed for the potter's field in the valley of Gehenna (Jer. 19:1--2, 18:1--10). This is HOT money because of the stipulation in Deuteronomy 23:18. (It is amazing how scrupulous the Pharisees are to obey the law while carrying out a murder of the [Author of the Law! ]{.class_4257}If the sale of a dog or the hire of a whore could not come into the treasury, then certainly money for the sale of a MAN could not make it!) Jesus had been "sold down the river," by Judas.

["And went and hanged himself." ]{.class_4258}Judas rushes out of the temple, head covered with a hood, clawing and clutching at his throat. He runs immediately to the southern promontory overlooking Gehenna, and seeking out a gnarled and twisted tree---just beginning to bud---he pulls the rope out of the robe and ties the knot. He climbs the tree, slides out on the limb, places the noose on his neck. He adjusts the knot. His actions are deliberate, premeditated, and intentional; there is nothing panicky about them. Lying on the limb, he suddenly loosens his grip, and the entire body slides off and comes to a sickening jerk at the full length of the rope. The neck is broken in the fraction of a second, and there hangs Judas---the follower of Christ, who "imitated the master" and "confessed to the priests!"

(Acts 1:18--20 gives additional material and tells what happened after the earthquake of Matthew 27:50--54. While Jesus is also hanging, the earthquake comes, and with it there is the crunching and grinding of boulders and strata on the south rim of Zion. Over goes the tree on which Judas hangs, and down it goes with its burden onto the rocks 50‑100 feet below it. Judas lands on a jagged rock formation, and the body bursts asunder and the intestines and stomach slush out on the ground in ["the field of blood."]{.class_4258}

"When the bough breaks the cradle will fall; down will come rock‑a‑bye baby and all!!")

Before leaving the passage, it might be well to observe one critical note on verse 4. The [RV, ASV, RSV]{.class_4257}, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_596}[New Bible Commentary ]{.class_4257}(in keeping with the gnostic‑pagan traditions of anti‑Christian scholarship) have omitted the word ["the,"]{.class_4258} thus making the passage: ["I have betrayed innocent blood." ]{.class_4257}This, of course, detracts not only from the enormity of crime but calls attention to the fact that Christ's blood was like the blood of [any ]{.class_4257}innocent man; which, of course, [it was not. ]{.class_4257}["The innocent blood" ]{.class_4258}is a much more probable reading in keeping with [the majority of verses ]{.class_4257}that deal with the blood. That is, the internal witness of the Holy Spirit Himself to the passages that deal with the blood (Col. 1:14, 20; Eph. 1:7, 2:13; 1 Pet. 1:2, 19; Heb. 9:14, 13:12) is a better criterion for determining the reading of Matthew 27:4 than a manuscript transmitted from one corrupt scholar to another. If it is to be objected that the article is not in the Greek, it may be stated that [the RSV, RV, and ASV all leave the article untranslated when it is in the Greek ]{.class_4257}in Matthew 8:14, 18, 22, 9:9, 19, 28, 35; 12:1, 15, and dozens of other places. You see, under the pretences of "accurate translations" and "being true to the original," another deathly blow is aimed at the Deity of Jesus Christ.

Christ's blood is [the ]{.class_4257}innocent blood, since it was not a [man's ]{.class_4257}blood. It was the sinless blood of a sinless life that came from a sinless God (Acts 20:28). This is the chief and main objection of the Christ‑rejecting Greek scholar to the [AV ]{.class_4257}1611. The [AV ]{.class_4257}1611 Bible is the orthodox Christian text of the New Testament church of Antioch and Asia Minor; all its manuscripts (of the Byzantine period) manifest an orthodox, fundamental, God‑honoring character. The [RSV, ASV]{.class_4257}, and [RV ]{.class_4257}all manifest the heretical, gnostic character of the depraved scholarship of Alexandrian Greeks.

Note, for example, in the classic passage on the blood (Acts 20:28), Lamsa's Aramaic reads: "Christ\...his own blood."

"Blood of his own" in the other translations is a corruption unworthy of any translation.

The [ASV ]{.class_4257}says: "Lord\...his own blood," and the believer will be careful to notice how all [three ]{.class_4257}readings are geared and designed to avoid the implication [that God has blood.]{.class_4257}

The [AV ]{.class_4257}says: ["God\...his own blood." ]{.class_4258}God's blood! You see, the truth is so shockingly fundamental it cannot be tolerated by []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_597}Westcott and Hort and Nestle. When one chooses up sides to sling mudballs, one finds again that remarkable UNITY of scholars against the [AV]{.class_4257}.

Notice the men who RESENT the implication that God has blood---they are Origen, Westcott and Hort, Irenaeus, Eusebius, Constantine, Jerome, Wetstein, and Griesbach. This is the North African line of Egyptian apostasy. Augustine sides with these dissenters, as does Lachmann, Tischendorf, and Tregelles. All of these men favor "B," VATICANUS, as superior to the Textus Receptus. All favor the seven apocryphal books added by Rome to the Bible, in violation of Jesus' canonical statements (Matt. 23:35; Luke 24:44), and ALL hated the Syrian text of the Textus Receptus with undying hatred. These men are opposed in history by men like Polycarp, Tertullian, Chrysostom, Dwight Moody, Sam Jones, Billy Sunday, and David Livingstone.

The change of the word ["GOD"]{.class_4258} to "LORD" by Eusebius, Irenaeus, Augustine, Constantine, Jerome, Griesbach, Tregelles, Tischendorf, and Lachmann is one of the most remarkable things that took place in the notorious history of textual bungling; for the reading ["God"]{.class_4258} of the King James is this time found in [both "Sinaiticus" and "Vaticanus,"]{.class_4257} which these scholars worshipped. But because the manuscript, this time, [backed up the Deity of Christ, ]{.class_4257}they were rejected!

"The heart is deceitful above all things, and desperately wicked: who can know it?"

This manhandling of Acts 20:28 in regard to ["the innocent blood" ]{.class_4258}shows a betrayal far more treacherous than anything Judas pulled off. These men who used Vaticanus to manufacture the Westcott and Hort Greek text (used by every Bible school in America!) [would not even stick by their own convictions on manuscript evidence, if one of their manuscripts accidentally bragged on Jesus Christ!!! ]{.class_4262}While they stuck with Vaticanus through every passage that [slandered Jesus ]{.class_4262}(1 Tim. 3:16; Luke 2:33), they abandoned it when it read [as the King James: ]{.class_4262}["God\...with his own blood!"]{.class_4258}

Nestle's critical edition, used by every translator in the world, pulls off perhaps the dirtiest trick of all in an effort to make a fool out of the [AV ]{.class_4257}[]{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_598}Bible. In the footnote on page 365, you will read in the critical apparatus that the Receptus has "Lord and God." But the Receptus does not say that at all; it says what it says---["God."]{.class_4258} Having gone backwards completely on their own critical theories, the [ASV, RV, RSV,]{.class_4257} and [New Bible Commentary ]{.class_4257}reject Nestle's text reading of the Vaticanus and Sinaiticus and adopt a [later ]{.class_4257}reading so they can stamp on Christ's Deity, while Nestle's (loathing to admit that the word should be GOD) inserts ["GOD"]{.class_4258} in the text, but slanders the [AV ]{.class_4257}in the footnote by saying it said something it did not say! There is no doubt whatsoever about the "Christianity" of Westcott and Hort and Nestle. They are North African infidels.

["I have betrayed the innocent blood." ]{.class_4258}The [AV ]{.class_4257}is correct and the theorizing of Irenaeus, Eusebius, Constantine, Jerome, Augustine, Tischendorf, Tregelles, Alford, Trench, A. T. Robertson, Westcott and Hort, Griesbach (and the Jehovah Witness translation!) is just as corrupt as you can get without hanging yourself on a limb at the south end of Jerusalem. (Robertson, Trench, and Alford all professed allegiance to the "Virgin Birth" and "resurrection"; that much may be said in their favor.)

27:9 Then was fulfilled that which was spoken by Jeremy the prophet, saying, And they took the thirty pieces of silver, the price of him that was valued, whom they of the children of Israel did value;

10 And gave them for the potter's field, as the Lord appointed me.

The words are not [written in ]{.class_4257}Jeremiah, but are written in Zechariah 11. However, Zechariah 7:7 is a plain warning to remember not only what Jeremiah [wrote, ]{.class_4257}but what he said. Jeremiah 18:1--8 and 19:2--3 connect the potter and his field with the valley of Hinnom (Gehenna), running around the east side of Jerusalem to the south end, where the city dump was located.

A similar confusion among Bible‑rejecting scholars is found in Jude 14. Here the citation is [spoken ]{.class_4257}from Zechariah 14:5 and Deuteronomy 33, and scholars have to go thrashing around in the Dead Sea Scrolls to find a "Book of Enoch" (so they can prove that "Tobit," "Judith," and "Bel and the Dragon" belong in the Bible also!). []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_599}Nowhere in the Bible is there any reference to any of these or to any "Book of Enoch." All are the childish fancies of fickle brats.

The citation is quoted from an oral speech, [not ]{.class_4257}a [written one.]{.class_4257}

27:11 And Jesus stood before the governor: and the governor asked him, saying, Art thou the King of the Jews? And Jesus said unto him, Thou sayest.

12 And when he was accused of the chief priests and elders, he answered nothing.

13 Then said Pilate unto him, Hearest thou not how many things they witness against thee?

14 And he answered him to never a word; insomuch that the governor marvelled greatly.

15 Now at that feast the governor was wont to release unto the people a prisoner, whom they would.

16 And they had then a notable prisoner, called Barabbas.

17 Therefore when they were gathered together, Pilate said unto them, Whom will ye that I release unto you? Barabbas, or Jesus which is called Christ?

18 For he knew that for envy they had delivered him.

Before proper comment can be made on the passage, it is necessary to get the other gospel accounts together, for a number of things take place before this Barabbas­--Jesus choice is offered to the people. ["And Jesus stood before the governor."]{.class_4258}

While He is standing there, this kind of accusation is heard behind Him: ["We found this fellow perverting the nation, and forbidding to give tribute to Caesar, saying that he himself is Christ a King\....He stirreth up the people, teaching throughout all Jewry, beginning from Galilee to this place"]{.class_4258} (Luke 23:1--5).

The Jews are OUTSIDE the judgment hall of Pilate, and they have made Pilate come out to them. This was done because the Pharisees were such consecrated, dedicated, sacramental, ritualistic "fathers," they thought it was "A MORTAL SIN" to go into the wrong building (see John 18:28). (While they had no conscience about murdering an innocent Bible Preacher, they got the holy terrors at the thought of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_600}being caught in the wrong building---they might get "defiled"!)

["Perverting the nation." ]{.class_4258}The charge is a lie; He was HEALING the nation (see Matt. 8--11). ["Forbidding to give tribute." ]{.class_4258}Another lie, for He paid it Himself (Matt. 17:24--27). ["He stirreth up the people." ]{.class_4258}Another lie. The Pharisees followed Him from Dan to Beersheba to stir up the people against Him (see Matt. 8--18). Jesus stands silent under the blast (Matt. 27:14).

Pilate responds practically: ["What accusation bring ye against this man?"]{.class_4258} (John 18:29). ["If he were not a malefactor, we would not have delivered him up unto thee."]{.class_4258}

They evade the question, for they cannot get a single accusation planted, with proof, that would convince Pilate He was a malefactor. Pilate senses their indecision and evasiveness and says: ["Take ye him, and judge him according to your law. The Jews therefore said unto him, It is not lawful for us to put any man to death: That the saying of Jesus might be fulfilled, which he spake, signifying what death he should die."]{.class_4258}

Here, the hand of God intervenes, and it is seen how Christ is destined to die a ["Roman" ]{.class_4257}form of execution (Acts 2:23). The Jew, as a dominated satellite, has lost the power to execute capital punishment; Rome retains this power.

Pilate goes back into the hall and calls Jesus into him privately and says: ["Art thou the King of the Jews?" ]{.class_4258}Jesus says quietly (but with that touch of irony in His voice that made an officer strike Him in John 18:21): ["Sayest thou this thing of thyself, or did others tell it thee of me?" ]{.class_4258}(This is exactly what He did to Caiaphas! The implication is clear. "Are you really interested in whether or not I am? Do you really WANT to know or are you just playing cat‑and‑mouse with some second‑hand information some gossip gave you over a bottle of Port?")

Pilate bristles: ["Am I a Jew?"]{.class_4258}

He almost spits it out. "What do you think I am, some sorry JEW, like YOU! How would I, [a Roman, ]{.class_4257}know anything about all this mess!" ["Thine own nation and the chief priests have delivered thee unto me: what hast thou done?" ]{.class_4258}(John 18:35).

In other words: "Let's don't talk about whether you are really a 'KING.' Let's get practical. What have you done?" Pilate is losing his sincerity by the minute. While stating []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_601}["the chief priests have delivered thee unto me," ]{.class_4258}we already know that he knew WHY they had delivered Him unto him---envy! (Mark 15:10).

The two men stand and look at one another. Pilate cannot tear his eyes away, and Jesus has no intention of looking anywhere except straight into the eyes of His judge. ["My kingdom is not of this world: if my kingdom were of this world, then would my servants fight, that I should not be delivered to the Jews: but now is my kingdom not from hence" ]{.class_4258}(John 18:36).

The most important word by far, in the sentence, is the little word ["now,"]{.class_4258} carefully taken out by the [Amplified Version]{.class_4257}! In the [Amplified ]{.class_4257}perversion (as the [RV, ASV]{.class_4257}, and [RSV]{.class_4257}), every attempt is made to force home to the reader---contrary to Scriptures---the ancient amillennial and postmillennial systems of Augustine, Jerome, and Calvin. By omitting the word ["now,"]{.class_4258} the reader is left saturated with the thought that Christ's ["KINGDOM"]{.class_4258} is [only ]{.class_4257}a spiritual kingdom and will [never be ]{.class_4257}any other kind of a kingdom. This depraved attack on the word of God is carried out without a blush or an apology by the reformed theologians of Holland, Michigan. Without batting an eye, they force the reader to accept a doctrine denied in 500 passages of Scripture. For as surely as day follows night, ["the kingdoms of this world"]{.class_4258} [are going to become ]{.class_4257}["the kingdoms of our Lord and of his Christ"]{.class_4258} (Rev 11:15), and they are going to become so following an armed battle of wrath and vengeance, conducted by Jesus Christ Himself in Person (Rev. 19, 14; Joel 2; Isa. 63; Matt. 24; Zech. 14).

The word ["now"]{.class_4258} is a fair warning to Pilate that [some day ]{.class_4257}Christ's kingdom will [be ]{.class_4257}["of this world."]{.class_4258} To remove the ["now"]{.class_4258} and leave the verse stand as:[ "My kingdom is not of this world: if my kingdom were of this world, then would my servants fight, that I should not be delivered to the Jews: but\...my kingdom is not from hence,"]{.class_4258} is as rotten and mean a mistranslation as was ever seen on a sheet of paper. The word ["now"]{.class_4258} in this passage is going to make the difference between correct doctrine and incorrect doctrine in the book of Revelation; as it has been demonstrated before, this will determine the method of handling the Old Testament, which is the key to Matthew, Acts, and []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_602}Hebrews.

["Pilate therefore said unto him, Art thou a king then? Jesus answered, Thou sayest that I am a king" ]{.class_4258}(John 18:37). (This brings us in our text up to Matt. 27:11.) ["To this end was I born, and for this cause came I into the world, that I should bear witness unto the truth. Every one that is of the truth heareth my voice. Pilate saith unto him, What is truth? And when he had said this, he went out again unto the Jews, and saith unto them, I find in him no fault at all" ]{.class_4258}(John 18:37--38).

Jesus admits that He is a King, but admits only that He is the King of a Kingdom that is not from this world (["now!"]{.class_4258}). ["Now"]{.class_4258} His kingdom is a spiritual kingdom characterized by TRUTH.

"What is truth?"

Pilate did not wait for the answer. (He not only did not wait for the answer, he didn't really even [ask ]{.class_4257}the question!) If you will read the passage carefully, you will find that it does not say: ["When he had asked this, he went out\...]{.class_4257}," but ["When he had SAID this\...." ]{.class_4258}Pilate wasn't really [asking. ]{.class_4257}He didn't really want to know. If he had wanted to know, he could have [asked ]{.class_4257}and then waited for the answer; but Pilate (as his entire retinue---Socrates, Plato, Aristotle, Russell, Dewey, Kant, Berkeley, Hobbes, Renan, Strauss, Spinoza, Hegel, Jung, Marx, Darwin, Huxley, and Dave Gardner) was raised in the rigid, inflexible, hide‑bound tradition that "all truth is relative" and "there is no absolute truth."

This narrow‑minded bigotism is common among "the educated class," and it is symptomatic of one‑sidedness in investigation and a definite failure to consider all points of view. Any man who considered [all ]{.class_4257}the evidence would say what Jesus said about the word of God, ["thy word is truth." ]{.class_4258}For the agnostic, John 7:17 is nicely‑placed so that any man can know the absolute truth if [he is willing. ]{.class_4257}Pilate, as his brood, was [unwilling. ]{.class_4257}He died thinking Truth is a relative matter, after first having crucified the One who was ["the way, THE TRUTH, and the life" ]{.class_4258}(1 Cor. 2:8).

["What is truth?" ]{.class_4258}And then he walks away from it, firm in his own conviction (Prov. 26:12) that he knows as much about the answer as Jesus, since it is all a relative matter anyway. You could []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_603}have stood Pilate alongside Einstein, Bertrand Russell, H. G. Wells, John Dewey, and Adolph Hitler; and you couldn't have told them apart: [they all believed there was no such thing as absolute truth.]{.class_4257}

["What is truth?" "Yea, hath God said?" ]{.class_4258}(Gen. 3:1).

"How do you [know ]{.class_4257}you're saved?"

"Well, that's just a [translation, ]{.class_4257}isn't it?"

"Well, I still think that this is the way it is."

"That may be so, but I was raised to believe THAT, and I'll stick with it till I die."

"But suppose a man is going down the street and\...."

And so it goes.

With the absolute, inerrant, infallible truth on every dime‑store counter in the world, man goes his stupid, self‑righteous way, following the steps of the Roman who said, ["What is truth?" ]{.class_4258}and then didn't wait for an answer (Prov. 14:6).

["What is truth?" ]{.class_4258}["Quid est veritas?"]{.class_4257}

The Latin poses an interesting anagram, for the ["Quid est veritas" ]{.class_4257}can be re‑arranged (without the loss or addition of a letter) to spell: ["Est vir qui adest"---]{.class_4257}"IT IS THE MAN BEFORE YOU!"

Thus: ["By thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned" ]{.class_4258}(Matt. 12:37).

"I find in him no fault at all."

Three times Pilate pronounces an official [judicial ]{.class_4257}deci­sion: John 18:38, 19:4, 19:6, and then goes ahead and has the Man executed! The entire proceeding is crooked from the moment they clapped a hand on Jesus to the sponge put in His mouth on the cross. Here is a judge who gives the verdict [three times ]{.class_4257}and then allows the courtroom to [reverse ]{.class_4257}the verdict! What a mess!

"I find in him no fault at all."

["And they were the more fierce, saying, He stirreth up the people, teaching throughout all Jewry, beginning from Galilee to this place. When Pilate heard of Galilee, he asked whether the man were a Galilaean. And as soon as he knew that he belonged unto Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him to Herod, who himself also was at Jerusalem at that time" ]{.class_4258}(Luke 23:5--7).

Pilate had already tried [carelessness ]{.class_4257}in an effort to rid himself of []{#I9B3O0KMSoa6V5WRoptyGw3631.xhtml_page_604}Jesus Christ. He had said: "You take Him!" This brush‑off was intended to make the execution a Jewish responsibility. The ruse failed, and now that he has Jesus on his hands again, he tries a second stratagem: [cowardice. ]{.class_4257}Pilate passes the buck. He pushes Jesus off on Herod and figures: "Well, that's that; I won't have to worry about this mess any more!" It's the old army game where the Colonel tells the Lt. Col., and he calls the Major, who orders the Captain, who tells the First Looey, who gets the Shavetail to order the Staff Sergeant to have the Buck Sergeant tell the Corporal to get the Private to move the garbage can over two feet behind the mess hall.

People, in 2000, resort to both these devices to avoid the decision of receiving the Lord Jesus (John 1:10--13). They try [carelessness ]{.class_4257}FIRST and say: "Well, I believe there is a time and place for that thing," or "I don't exactly accept Him, but I don't [reject ]{.class_4257}Him!" But carelessness does not brush Him off. Jesus is the hardest Person you ever had to get off your hands in all your life, for ["He ever liveth." ]{.class_4258}[Cowardice ]{.class_4257}fails, and I have seen many a six‑footer with white lips, wet palms, and a mouth as dry as a cactus in Needles, California, say: "Not tonight preacher; I'm just not ready tonight." But the Lord is still dealing with him. You can't turn‑tail and run‑out on Jesus. Jesus goes to Herod and comes right back to Pilate, and he has Him on his hands again.

["Then he questioned with him in many words; but he answered him nothing. And the chief priests and scribes stood and vehemently accused him. And Herod with his men of war set him at nought, and mocked him, and arrayed him in a gorgeous robe, and sent him again to Pilate. And the same day Pilate and Herod were made friends together: for before they were at enmity between themselves" ]{.class_4258}(Luke 23:9--12).

Herod's presence in Jerusalem can only be explained by the fact that hundreds of Romans and Greeks knew that the Jew's religion was something superior and supernatural.
